| EC-UN-02 | Elderly patient must be alerted when arrhythmia is detected | Alarm must trigger within 3 s of detection with ≥ 80 dB sound level at 1 m | Buzzer driver circuit + arrhythmia detection algorithm in DSP | Functional test with simulated VF and AF waveforms; sound level meter at 1 m | ISO 60601-1-8 (Alarm Systems); Risk: failure to alarm → patient harm |
| EC-UN-03 | Patient must wear device comfortably for 7 days | Device weight < 50 g, hypoallergenic strap | 3D-printed ABS housing, silicone strap with biocompatibility certificate | Weighing scale test; ISO 10993-5 cytotoxicity test certificate | ISO 10993-1; Risk: skin irritation |
| EC-UN-04 | Device must survive daily shower exposure | IPX7 water ingress rating | Sealed enclosure with silicone gasket | IPX7 immersion test per IEC 60529 | Risk: electric shock → IEC 60601-1 |
| EC-UN-05 | Data must be securely transmitted to clinician | Use AES-128 encryption over BLE 5.0 | Firmware BLE stack with AES-128 | Penetration testing and code review | ISO 27001; Risk: data breach |

This matrix demonstrates **closed-loop traceability** from user need through verification and risk control, satisfying FDA 21 CFR 820.30(j) and ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.9.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a) Discuss the role of international standards (ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1) in shaping the Specification Review of a Class IIb active medical device. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ISO 13485:2016 — Quality Management System:**
Clause 7.3 mandates that the manufacturer shall establish documented procedures for design and development. The Specification Review is the formal mechanism by which Clause 7.3.5 ("Design and Development Review") is satisfied. It requires that participants include representatives of functions concerned with the design stage being reviewed, and that the results of reviews and any necessary actions are recorded and maintained.

**ISO 14971:2019 — Risk Management:**
This standard requires that every potential hazard be identified, the risk estimated, and risk control measures implemented. During Specification Review, each design input must be cross-referenced to a hazard in the **Risk Management File**. For a Class IIb device (e.g., an infusion pump), this linkage is critical because failure can cause serious injury or death. Specification statements must explicitly include the **risk control measure** (e.g., "software shall have an independent hard-wired upper-dose limiter").

**IEC 60601-1 — General Safety and Essential Performance:**
This is the foundational electrical safety standard for medical electrical equipment. The Specification Review must confirm that the device's **Essential Performance** (the performance necessary to maintain freedom from unacceptable risk) is explicitly stated. For a defibrillator, essential performance includes energy delivery accuracy of ±15% or ±2 J (whichever is greater) and the absence of inadvertent energy delivery.

**Synthesis:** For a Class IIb active device, these three standards together mandate a Specification Review that is **traceable, risk-linked, and safety-validated**. In Europe, this is enforced by **Annex I of EU MDR 2017/745 (General Safety and Performance Requirements, GSPR)**; in the US, by **21 CFR 820.30**.

---

**(b) What is the SMART-AC criteria for a good specification statement? Rewrite a poorly written specification into a SMART-AC compliant one for a blood pressure monitor. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SMART-AC Criteria (explained):**
- **S — Specific:** Unambiguous, single interpretation.
- **M — Measurable:** Has a numeric metric.
- **A — Achievable:** Technically and economically feasible.
- **R — Relevant:** Linked to a user need and/or risk control.
- **T — Testable:** A verification method exists.
- **A — Acceptable:** Within regulatory acceptance limits.
- **C — Controllable:** Under document/change control.

**Poorly written specification (Common student answer):**
*"The blood pressure monitor should be accurate, fast, and easy to use for the nurse."*

**Why it fails SMART-AC:**
- "Accurate" is not specific (no numeric).
- "Fast" is not measurable.
- "Easy to use" is not testable.
- The statement combines three separate specs in one line.

**SMART-AC Compliant Rewrite (3 separate statements):**

1. *Measurement Accuracy:* "The device shall measure systolic blood pressure with an error of **±3 mmHg** and a standard deviation of **≤ 4 mmHg** when compared to a mercury sphygmomanometer reference, per ISO 81060-2:2018." *(S, M, A, R, T, A, C — all satisfied)*

2. *Measurement Speed:* "The device shall complete a single blood pressure measurement within **30 seconds** for adult patients with arm circumference between 22 cm and 32 cm." *(S, M, A, R, T, A, C — all satisfied)*

3. *Usability:* "The device shall be operable by a registered nurse with no prior training beyond a 10-minute instructional video, achieving a **task completion rate of ≥ 95%** on first attempt per IEC 62366-1 summative evaluation." *(S, M, A, R, T, A, C — all satisfied)*

Each rewritten statement is testable, traceable, and independently verifiable — the hallmark of a specification that will survive a KTU 14-mark question scrutiny and a real-world FDA audit.

---

## 5.X KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Specification Review Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Design Review" with "Specification Review"** — Design Review is broader (covers concept, detailed design, V&V); Specification Review is the *upstream* review focused on design inputs.
> 2. **Forgetting the "R" in SMART** — Students write measurable specifications but forget to link them to a user need or risk control. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting stakeholders** — Listing only "engineer" or "designer" is insufficient. You must mention cross-functional roles (QA, RA, Clinical, Manufacturing, Risk, Usability).
> 4. **Not citing standards** — A 7-mark sub-question that does not mention at least one standard (ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1) is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Skipping the traceability matrix** — In any 14-mark question, a tabular or matrix representation is the fastest route to full marks.

---

## 5.Y Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Specification Review is a **mandatory design control activity** under ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.5 and 21 CFR 820.30.
- It is performed **upstream** of verification (build it right) and validation (build the right thing).
- A complete biomedical specification has **six layers**: User, Functional, Performance, Safety/Regulatory, Interface/Environmental, Manufacturing/Service/Disposal.
- The **six-step process**: Preparation → Individual Pre-Review → Cross-Functional Meeting → Defect Logging → Action Closure → Approval & Release.
- Mandatory stakeholders: Design Lead, QA, Regulatory, Clinical, Risk, Manufacturing, Usability, Service.
- **SMART-AC** is the gold standard for specification quality: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable, Acceptable, Controllable.
- The review produces a **Design Review Report (DRR)** and feeds the **Design History File (DHF)**.
- Every specification must be **traceable** to a user need and, if hazardous, to a **risk control measure** per ISO 14971.
- International standards to memorize: **ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1, IEC 62366-1, 21 CFR 820.30, EU MDR 2017/745**.
- A specification error caught at this stage is **~10x cheaper** than in production and **~100x cheaper** than in the field.
- The output of a successful Specification Review is a **baseline-released, version-controlled Design Input Specification (V1.0)** in the PLM system.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Specification and Software/Hardware Requirement Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Specification and Software/Hardware Requirement Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Design Specification (DS) in Biomedical Devices

**Design Specification** is the comprehensive, formalized engineering document that precisely defines the functional, performance, safety, regulatory, ergonomic, and operational requirements of a biomedical device. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT415)**, it acts as the master blueprint that bridges the *user need* (clinical requirement) and the *engineering implementation* (actual hardware/software architecture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Standardized):**
> A *Design Specification* is a structured technical document that captures **what** a biomedical device must do, **how well** it must do it, under **which constraints** (regulatory, environmental, ergonomic), and **how** it will be verified and validated — serving as the contractual reference between the design team, the manufacturer, the regulatory body (e.g., CDSCO, USFDA), and the end-user (clinician/patient).

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a Design Specification as the **architect's plan when building a hospital**. Before laying a single brick, the architect must specify:
- How many floors (functional capacity)
- Where the ICU, OT, and wards go (functional decomposition)
- Fire exits, ramp widths (safety constraints)
- Plumbing, oxygen lines, electrical loads (interfaces)
- Earthquake resistance standards (environmental specs)

A biomedical device (say, a **portable ECG monitor**) is no different. The Design Specification is the architect's plan — it tells the embedded systems engineer *what ADC sampling rate* to use, tells the mechanical designer *what enclosure IP rating* is needed, and tells the software developer *what firmware modules* to build.

---

## 1.2 Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)

**Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)** is the subset of the Design Specification that details the *physical, electrical, electronic, and mechanical* characteristics of the biomedical device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal IEEE 1362 / ISO 13485 Aligned Definition:**
> HRS is a structured document that specifies the hardware components, their electrical characteristics, performance parameters, environmental tolerances, interfaces, mechanical dimensions, and qualification standards required to satisfy the parent Design Specification of a medical device.

### Key Elements Captured in HRS

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Specs** | Voltage rails, current consumption, power budget |
| **Component Selection** | MCUs, op-amps, sensors, ADC resolution |
| **Mechanical Specs** | Enclosure dimensions, weight, IP rating |
| **Environmental Specs** | Operating temperature, humidity, EMC compliance |
| **Interface Specs** | Connector types, communication protocols (UART, SPI, I2C, BLE) |
| **Safety Specs** | Leakage current limits per IEC 60601-1, isolation barriers |

---

## 1.3 Software Requirement Specification (SRS)

**Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** is the formalized documentation of all functional and non-functional requirements of the embedded/firmware/application software embedded inside a biomedical device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 Definition (KTU Recommended Reference):**
> The SRS is a complete description of the behavior of the system-to-be-developed. It includes a set of *use cases*, *functional requirements*, *performance requirements*, *software interfaces*, *design constraints*, and *software system attributes* (reliability, security, maintainability).

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

If the Design Specification is the *architect's plan*, then:
- **HRS = the materials list and structural engineering** (concrete grade, steel type, load capacity)
- **SRS = the building automation and software** (lift control, fire alarm logic, HVAC automation, patient monitoring dashboard)

In a **patient monitoring system**, the HRS specifies the *analog front-end* and *sensor signal conditioning circuit*, while the SRS specifies the *real-time QRS detection algorithm*, *alarm thresholds*, and *data logging logic*.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Priority Module

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> Questions on *Design Specification, HRS, and SRS* are recurrent in **Part A (3 marks)** and frequently appear as 7-mark sub-parts in **Part B (14 marks)**. The examiner specifically tests: (1) ability to *write an SRS template* for a given biomedical device, (2) ability to *distinguish between functional and non-functional requirements*, and (3) knowledge of *IEEE standards*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Relationship: DS → HRS & SRS

The Design Specification is the **parent document**. It is decomposed into:

1. **Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)** — physical realization
2. **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** — algorithmic/firmware realization
3. **Interface Requirement Specification (IRS)** — communication between hardware and software

```
[USER NEED / CLINICAL REQUIREMENT]
            ↓
[DESIGN SPECIFICATION (DS)]   ← Master Document
            ↓
       ┌────┴────┐
       ↓         ↓
    [HRS]      [SRS]      ← Sub-Specifications
       ↓         ↓
   [ICD]      [SDD]       ← Interface Control / Software Design
```

> [!NOTE]
> **ICD** = Interface Control Document, **SDD** = Software Design Description. These are downstream documents generated *after* the SRS/HRS are approved.

---

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of a Good Design Specification

A KTU-compliant Design Specification must satisfy the **SMART** criteria:

- **S**pecific — No ambiguity (e.g., "ADC sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz" not "fast sampling")
- **M**easurable — Quantifiable metrics
- **A**chievable — Realistic within cost, technology, and time
- **R**elevant — Traceable to user need
- **T**estable — Verifiable through test cases

---

## 2.3 Components of a Complete SRS (IEEE 830 Standard Template)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following 8 sections — they form the back-bone of any 14-mark KTU question on SRS.**

1. **Introduction**
   - Purpose
   - Scope of the software (in context of full device)
   - Definitions, acronyms, abbreviations
   - References
   - Overview
2. **Overall Description**
   - Product perspective
   - Product functions
   - User characteristics
   - Constraints
   - Assumptions and dependencies
3. **Specific Requirements**
   - Functional requirements
   - Performance requirements
   - Design constraints
   - Software system attributes (reliability, security, portability)
4. **External Interface Requirements**
   - User interfaces
   - Hardware interfaces
   - Software interfaces
   - Communication protocols
5. **Other Non-functional Requirements**
   - Safety, security, maintainability
6. **Verification & Validation Criteria**
7. **Appendices
8. **Index

---

## 2.4 Components of a Complete HRS

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **1. System Overview** | Block diagram of hardware architecture |
| **2. Electrical Requirements** | Supply voltage, current, power consumption |
| **3. Functional Requirements** | Subsystem behaviour (sensor, ADC, processor, display) |
| **4. Performance Requirements** | Bandwidth, SNR, sampling rate, latency |
| **5. Mechanical Requirements** | Dimensions, weight, enclosure material, IP rating |
| **6. Environmental Requirements** | Temperature, humidity, shock, vibration, EMC |
| **7. Interface Requirements** | Pin definitions, connector types, signal levels |
| **8. Safety & Compliance** | IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971 (risk management) |
| **9. Bill of Materials (BOM)** | Component-level breakdown |
| **10. Verification Tests** | Test conditions and acceptance criteria |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Distinction Table (Frequently Asked in Part A)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — questions like *"Differentiate between HRS and SRS"* or *"List four components of SRS"* appear almost every KTU cycle.

| Parameter | HRS (Hardware) | SRS (Software) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Physical / electronic realization | Algorithmic / firmware logic |
| **Standard** | ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1 | IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 |
| **Drives** | PCB design, component selection, mechanical design | Coding, algorithm design, GUI |
| **Tested via** | Bench tests, EMC chambers, environmental chambers | Unit tests, integration tests, V&V |
| **Example** | "Use ADS1292R 24-bit ADC, sample at 500 Hz" | "Detect R-peak using Pan-Tompkins algorithm" |
| **Document Owner** | Hardware Design Engineer | Embedded Software Engineer |
| **Update Frequency** | Less frequent (hardware is fixed) | More frequent (firmware iterations) |

---

## 2.6 Key Engineering Equations & Metrics (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential when you *write* an SRS/HRS for a biomedical device. **No vertical pipes inside tables — all absolute values are wrapped in `\vert \vert`.**

| Concept | Equation / Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution | $$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$$ | Smallest detectable voltage step |
| Sampling Theorem | $$f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ | Nyquist criterion for anti-aliasing |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$ | Quality of acquired biosignal |
| Power Budget | $$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \cdot I_i$$ | Sum of all subsystem power consumption |
| Battery Life | $$T_{hours} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{load\_mA}}$$ | Endurance of portable medical device |
| Leakage Current (Patient) | $$I_{leak} \leq 10\,\mu A \text{ (Type CF, IEC 60601-1)}$$ | Safety threshold |
| Reliability (MTBF) | $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$$ | Mean Time Between Failures |
| Risk Priority Number | $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$ | FMEA severity × occurrence × detection |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **Regulatory Approval**: USFDA, CE Mark, and CDSCO *will not* review a device without a complete Design History File (DHF) — of which DS, HRS, and SRS are mandatory components.
- **Design Verification**: A properly written specification enables objective pass/fail testing.
- **Cross-functional Communication**: Hardware and software teams work from the *same* specification, avoiding integration mismatches.
- **Risk Management (ISO 14971)**: Specifications are the *source* for hazard identification and FMEA.
- **V-Model Compliance**: Each specification level maps to a corresponding test level (Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Biomedical Example

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Process: How to Write a Design Specification for a Biomedical Device

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact logical flow** you must write in a 14-mark KTU answer when asked *"Explain the design specification process for a portable ECG device"* (or any other biomedical device).

### Step 1 — Capture the User Need / Clinical Requirement
Start with the *clinical problem statement*.
> *"Clinicians require a low-cost, portable, 3-lead ECG monitor for rural primary health centres that can record, display, store, and transmit ECG waveforms via Bluetooth to a mobile application for cardiologist review."*

### Step 2 — Define the Functional Requirements (FR)
Decompose the user need into specific *what the device must do* statements.

| FR # | Functional Requirement |
|---|---|
| FR1 | Acquire 3-lead ECG signal (Lead I, II, III) |
| FR2 | Sample ECG at minimum 250 Hz per channel |
| FR3 | Display real-time waveform on 1.8-inch TFT |
| FR4 | Detect R-peak and compute heart rate |
| FR5 | Store up to 100 patient records locally |
| FR6 | Transmit records via Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
| FR7 | Operate continuously for 8 hours on battery |
| FR8 | Provide audio-visual alarm for bradycardia/tachycardia |

### Step 3 — Define Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)
These are *quality attributes*, not features.

| NFR # | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|
| NFR1 | Total device weight ≤ 250 g |
| NFR2 | Enclosure must meet IP54 (splash and dust resistant) |
| NFR3 | Operating temperature 10 °C to 45 °C |
| NFR4 | Patient leakage current ≤ 10 µA (Type CF) |
| NFR5 | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) ≥ 10,000 hours |
| NFR6 | Firmware updates over-the-air (OTA) capable |
| NFR7 | Compliance with IEC 60601-1 and ISO 14971 |

### Step 4 — Map Functional Requirements to Hardware (HRS) and Software (SRS)

This is the **key transition** — each FR is realized by either HRS, SRS, or both.

| FR # | HRS Component | SRS Component |
|---|---|---|
| FR1 | 3 electrode lead wires, ADS1292R analog front-end | Lead selection logic in firmware |
| FR2 | 24-bit ADC, 500 Hz internal oversampling | Timer ISR driving DMA sampling |
| FR3 | SPI-driven 1.8" TFT, backlight driver | LVGL GUI drawing waveform |
| FR4 | Comparator circuit for hardware R-detect | Pan-Tompkins algorithm in DSP layer |
| FR5 | SPI Flash (16 MB) memory IC | FATFS file system module |
| FR6 | nRF52840 BLE SoC, antenna | BLE GATT service implementation |
| FR7 | 3.7 V, 2000 mAh Li-Po battery | Power management task (RTOS) |
| FR8 | Buzzer driver, LED array | Threshold detection + alarm state machine |

### Step 5 — Define Interface Requirements
- **Hardware interfaces**: USB-C charging, 3.5 mm electrode jack, BLE antenna
- **Software interfaces**: BLE GATT service UUIDs, file system API, GUI API
- **User interfaces**: 3 buttons (Power, Record, Transmit), 1.8" TFT touch

### Step 6 — Verification & Validation (V&V) Plan
- **Hardware V&V**: Bench tests on ECG simulator, IEC 60601-1 leakage test, drop test, temperature chamber
- **Software V&V**: Unit test each module (≥ 80% code coverage), integration test, system test with patient simulator, UAT with clinicians

### Step 7 — Traceability Matrix
Maintain a **bi-directional traceability matrix** linking every user need → FR → HRS/SRS → Test case.

| User Need | FR | HRS Section | SRS Section | Test Case ID |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acquire ECG | FR1 | HRS §3.2 | SRS §3.1 | TC-ECG-001 |
| Display waveform | FR3 | HRS §5.1 | SRS §3.4 | TC-DISP-002 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** A well-built traceability matrix is worth 2 extra marks in a 14-mark answer. Examiners reward the *systematic* engineering approach.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Numerical — ADC Resolution for ECG Front-End

**Problem:** Design the analog front-end for the ECG device above. The ADS1292R has a 24-bit ADC, full-scale differential range ±2.4 V. What is the smallest detectable voltage change?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- ADC resolution: $n = 24$ bits
- Full-scale voltage: $V_{FS} = 2 \times 2.4 = 4.8$ V (peak-to-peak)

**Formula:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{4.8}{2^{24}}
$$

**Intermediate Calculation:**

$$
2^{24} = 16,\!777,\!216
$$

$$
\Delta V = \frac{4.8}{16,\!777,\!216}
$$

$$
\Delta V \approx 2.86 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{V} = 0.286\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta V \approx 0.286\,\mu V}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since ECG signals are typically in the range 0.5 mV to 5 mV, this resolution is more than sufficient — it can detect signals about 1750 times smaller than the smallest ECG peak. This justifies the choice of the ADS1292R in the HRS.

### Sampling Rate Verification (Nyquist)

**Given:** ECG bandwidth $f_{max} = 150$ Hz (clinical standard)

**Formula:**

$$
f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
f_{s} \geq 2 \times 150 = 300\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Decision:** Choose $f_{s} = 500$ Hz to provide a 67% margin above the Nyquist limit, thus ensuring anti-aliasing margin in the SRS. This is documented as a **performance requirement**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Power Budget Calculation

**Given Subsystem Currents (from HRS):**

| Subsystem | Voltage | Current |
|---|---|---|
| MCU (nRF52840) active | 3.3 V | 12 mA |
| ADC (ADS1292R) | 3.3 V | 2 mA |
| TFT Display | 3.3 V | 25 mA |
| BLE TX peak | 3.3 V | 15 mA |
| Sensor front-end | 3.3 V | 3 mA |
| Miscellaneous (LEDs, etc.) | 3.3 V | 5 mA |

**Formula:**

$$
P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \cdot I_i
$$

**Step-by-step Sum:**

$$
P_{total} = (3.3)(12) + (3.3)(2) + (3.3)(25) + (3.3)(15) + (3.3)(3) + (3.3)(5)\,\text{mW}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 3.3 \times (12 + 2 + 25 + 15 + 3 + 5)\,\text{mW}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 3.3 \times 62\,\text{mW} = 204.6\,\text{mW}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{total} \approx 205\,\text{mW}}
$$

**Battery Life (with 2000 mAh, 3.7 V Li-Po battery):**

$$
\text{Energy} = 3.7 \times 2000 = 7400\,\text{mWh}
$$

$$
T = \frac{7400}{205} \approx 36.1\,\text{hours}
$$

**Validation:** The design specification requires 8 hours of continuous operation. 36 hours > 8 hours, so the requirement is met with a 4.5× margin.

---

## 3.4 Common Mistakes to Avoid When Writing Specifications

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert — Students lose 2–3 marks per question for these errors:**

1. **Vague language**: Writing "fast response time" instead of "response time ≤ 2 seconds" → marks deducted for non-testability
2. **Conflating design with implementation**: SRS should say *what*, not *how* (avoid "use a for-loop"; say "the system shall iterate over all buffer samples")
3. **Missing traceability**: Each requirement must be uniquely numbered (FR1, NFR2, HRS-3.4, etc.)
4. **Ignoring safety**: Forgetting IEC 60601-1 leakage current limits
5. **No verification criteria**: Every requirement must have an associated test method

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 V-Model Mapping: Specifications ↔ Tests

This is the **most important diagram** for KTU Module 1. It shows the *one-to-one* mapping between specification levels and corresponding test levels — a guaranteed 3-mark question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["User Need and Clinical Requirement"] --> nodeB["System Level Design Specification"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Hardware Requirement Specification HRS"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Software Requirement Specification SRS"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Hardware Design and Schematic"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Software Design and Coding"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Hardware Unit Tests"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Software Unit Tests"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Hardware Integration Tests"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["System Integration Tests"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["User Acceptance Tests Clinical Validation"]
    nodeK --> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left descending arm is *decomposition* (User Need → DS → HRS/SRS → Detailed Design). The right ascending arm is *integration and validation* (Unit Test → Integration Test → System Test → Acceptance Test). The diagonal arrows show traceability — a test on the right *verifies* a specification on the left.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Biomedical Device

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Patient Biosignal Source ECG EMG EEG"] --> nodeB["Electrode and Analog Front End Amplifier Filter"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Analog to Digital Converter 24 bit"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Microcontroller DSP Core Firmware Layer"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Application Layer GUI Alarms Data Logging"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Wireless Module BLE or WiFi"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Local Storage Flash Memory"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Display and User Interface TFT and Buzzer"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Mobile App or Cloud Server"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Retrievable Patient Records"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Mapping to Specifications:**
> - Blocks A, B, C, H, I → **HRS** dominates (physical/electrical)
> - Blocks D, E, F, G → **SRS** dominates (algorithm/firmware)
> - Block H → shared HRS (display panel) and SRS (GUI code)

---

## 4.3 SRS Document Development Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Step 1 Identify Stakeholders Clinicians Patients Regulators"] --> nodeB["Step 2 Capture User Needs and Use Cases"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 3 Elicit Functional Requirements"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4 Define Non Functional Requirements NFR"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5 Specify External Interfaces H W S W"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6 Define Verification and Validation Criteria"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 7 Build Traceability Matrix"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Step 8 Review and Baseline the SRS"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Step 9 Hand off to Hardware and Software Teams"]
```

---

## 4.4 Risk-Requirement-Specification Linkage Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Hazard Identification ISO 14971"] --> nodeB["Risk Analysis FMEA RPN"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Risk Control Measures"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["New Functional or Non Functional Requirements"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Update HRS and SRS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Verification of Risk Control"]
    nodeF --> nodeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Significance:** This closed-loop diagram demonstrates that **risk management drives requirements**. A hazard analysis (e.g., *"electrical shock to patient"*) generates a *risk control measure* (e.g., *"isolation barrier"*) which becomes a *requirement* in the HRS (e.g., *"patient leakage current ≤ 10 µA"*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]
**Define Software Requirement Specification (SRS). List any four characteristics of a good SRS.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** As per IEEE 830 standard, the Software Requirement Specification (SRS) is a complete description of the behaviour of a system-to-be-developed. It includes functional requirements, performance requirements, interfaces, design constraints, and software quality attributes.

**Four Characteristics of a Good SRS:**

1. **Correct** — Every requirement stated is one that the software must meet.
2. **Unambiguous** — Each requirement has only one possible interpretation.
3. **Complete** — All possible scenarios, including error and exception cases, are covered.
4. **Verifiable** — Every requirement can be objectively tested for compliance.
5. **Consistent** — No requirement contradicts another.
6. **Modifiable** — Changes can be made in a controlled and consistent way.
7. **Traceable** — Origin of each requirement is documented.

*(Any four of the above will fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam]
**Differentiate between Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS) and Software Requirement Specification (SRS).**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected):**

| Parameter | HRS | SRS |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specifies physical, electrical, and mechanical hardware details of the device | Specifies functional and non-functional software requirements of the device |
| **Standards** | ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1 | IEEE 830, IEEE 29148 |
| **Drives** | PCB design, component selection, enclosure | Algorithm design, firmware coding, GUI |
| **Example** | "Use 24-bit ADC with 500 Hz sampling" | "Implement Pan-Tompkins R-peak detection algorithm" |
| **Owner** | Hardware Design Engineer | Embedded Software Engineer |
| **Testing** | Bench tests, EMC compliance, environmental tests | Unit tests, integration tests, V&V |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often give 1-line vague answers like *"HRS is for hardware and SRS is for software."* This gets **0–1 mark**. You must list *at least four distinguishing parameters* with specific biomedical examples to score full 3 marks.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[Note to students:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **one full question of 14 marks**, and you have an **internal choice** — pick the one you prepared. Below are TWO full model questions.]

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the components/structure of a Software Requirement Specification (SRS) document as per IEEE 830 standard. Use the example of a digital blood pressure monitor.**

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**Write the HRS for the digital blood pressure monitor, including electrical, mechanical, and safety specifications.**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Solution to (a): SRS for Digital Blood Pressure Monitor (7 Marks)**

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**
- **Purpose:** To specify the software requirements for the embedded firmware of an automatic, oscillometric-based digital blood pressure monitor for home and clinical use.
- **Scope:** The software shall control cuff inflation, pressure measurement, deflation profile, systolic/diastolic computation, display, data storage, and BLE-based mobile sync.
- **Definitions:** ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter), BP (Blood Pressure), MAP (Mean Arterial Pressure), BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy).
- **References:** IEEE 830, IEC 80601-2-30 (particular standard for BP monitors).

**2. Overall Description (1 mark)**
- **Product perspective:** Standalone device that pairs with mobile application via BLE.
- **User characteristics:** Adult patients, self-use at home; clinical use by nurses.
- **Constraints:** Must operate from 2 × AAA batteries, run on bare-metal or RTOS environment.

**3. Specific Functional Requirements (2 marks)**

| FR # | Requirement |
|---|---|
| FR1 | The system shall inflate the cuff to a target pressure of 180 mmHg initially |
| FR2 | The system shall sample cuff pressure at 100 Hz using a 16-bit ADC |
| FR3 | The system shall deflate the cuff linearly at 3 mmHg/s |
| FR4 | The system shall compute MAP as the peak amplitude of oscillometric pulses |
| FR5 | The system shall compute systolic and diastolic using amplitude ratio algorithm |
| FR6 | The system shall store last 100 readings in non-volatile memory |
| FR7 | The system shall transmit readings via BLE on user command |

**4. Non-Functional Requirements (1 mark)**
- NFR1: BP measurement accuracy ± 3 mmHg (per ISO 81060-2)
- NFR2: Total measurement cycle ≤ 45 seconds
- NFR3: Battery life ≥ 1000 measurements on fresh alkaline cells
- NFR4: Operating temperature 10 °C to 40 °C

**5. External Interfaces (1 mark)**
- **User Interface:** LCD showing systolic, diastolic, pulse rate, time
- **Hardware Interface:** Pressure sensor (MPXV5050GP), pump motor driver, ADC, BLE module
- **Software Interface:** Mobile app BLE GATT service, custom UUID 0x2A37

**6. Verification Criteria (1 mark)**
- Each FR must have a corresponding test case using a calibrated BP simulator
- Code coverage ≥ 80% via unit tests
- System test against ISO 81060-2 protocol with 85 subjects

---

#### **Solution to (b): HRS for Digital Blood Pressure Monitor (7 Marks)**

**1. System Overview (1 mark)**
The hardware consists of: cuff, pneumatic tubing, pump, solenoid valve, pressure sensor, signal conditioning circuit, microcontroller, LCD, BLE module, and 2 × AAA battery holder.

**2. Electrical Specifications (2 marks)**
- **Supply Voltage:** 3.0 V DC (2 × AAA alkaline)
- **Operating Current:** ≤ 250 mA peak (during inflation)
- **Standby Current:** ≤ 10 µA
- **MCU:** STM32L073RZ (ARM Cortex-M0+, ultra-low power)
- **ADC:** 16-bit SAR, integrated in MCU
- **Sensor:** NXP MPXV5050GP, range 0–50 kPa

**3. Mechanical Specifications (1 mark)**
- Dimensions: 130 mm × 95 mm × 35 mm
- Weight: ≤ 220 g (without batteries)
- Cuff size: 22–32 cm arm circumference (medium adult)
- Enclosure: ABS plastic, drop-tested from 1 m
- Display: 1.5" monochrome LCD, 128 × 64 pixels

**4. Environmental Specifications (1 mark)**
- Operating temperature: 10 °C to 40 °C
- Storage temperature: -20 °C to 60 °C
- Humidity: 15% to 90% RH non-condensing
- Ingress protection: IP20 (indoor use only)

**5. Safety & Compliance (1 mark)**
- Compliance: IEC 60601-1 (general medical electrical safety), IEC 80601-2-30 (BP monitor particular standard)
- Patient leakage current: ≤ 100 µA (Type BF applied part)
- Defibrillation-proof applied part: Not required
- Biocompatibility: Cuff must be ISO 10993-5 compliant

**6. Interface Specifications (1 mark)**
- BLE: 4.0+ with 1 Mbps PHY
- Push-buttons: Start/Stop, Memory Recall, Bluetooth Pairing
- Indicator: Green LED for normal, Red LED for high BP alert

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Describe the hierarchical relationship between User Need, Design Specification, HRS, and SRS in the design of a pulse oximeter. Draw a V-model to show the verification at each level.**

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**List and explain any seven non-functional requirements (NFRs) that must be specified in the SRS of a wearable pulse oximeter.**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Solution to (a): Hierarchical Relationship & V-Model (7 Marks)**

**Hierarchical Decomposition (3 marks):**

1. **User Need (Clinical Requirement):** *"A wearable fingertip pulse oximeter is needed for continuous SpO₂ and heart rate monitoring of COVID-19 patients in home isolation, with data transmission to a remote physician."*

2. **Design Specification (DS):** Captures the master system-level requirements — measurement range (SpO₂ 70–100%, HR 30–250 bpm), accuracy (±2% SpO₂, ±2 bpm), weight (< 50 g), battery life (> 24 h), wireless (BLE 5.0), display (OLED), alarm thresholds.

3. **HRS (Hardware):** Specifies the SpO₂ sensor (MAX30102 — red + IR LED + photodiode), analog front-end, MCU (Nordic nRF52833), OLED display driver, Li-Po battery (3.7 V, 250 mAh), IP rating, mechanical dimensions.

4. **SRS (Software):** Specifies the SpO₂ algorithm (ratio-of-ratios based on Beer-Lambert law), HR detection (peak detection on PPG), BLE GATT services, battery management task, OLED GUI state machine, alarm logic.

5. **Interface Requirement:** Defines the SPI bus between sensor and MCU, I²C between MCU and OLED, BLE GATT service UUIDs.

**V-Model Explanation (4 marks):**

The V-Model shows that each specification level on the descending left arm has a corresponding verification activity on the ascending right arm:
- DS ↔ System-level test
- HRS ↔ Hardware integration test
- SRS ↔ Software integration test
- Detailed design ↔ Unit test
- User need ↔ User acceptance test (clinical validation)

*(Draw the V-Model diagram in your answer sheet — see Section 4.1 for the schematic.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b): Seven Non-Functional Requirements (7 Marks)**

> *(1 mark per NFR explained well — aim for crisp 4–5 line answers)*

1. **Performance:** *"The system shall display SpO₂ and HR values within 1 second of finger insertion."* — Critical for clinical decision-making.

2. **Reliability:** *"MTBF of the device shall be ≥ 30,000 operating hours."* — Ensures the device can be deployed in long-term home care.

3. **Usability:** *"A layperson shall be able to perform measurement without training, in ≤ 3 steps."* — Important for elderly or COVID-isolated patients.

4. **Safety:** *"Patient-applied part temperature rise shall not exceed 41 °C per IEC 60601-1."* — Prevents thermal burns from prolonged contact.

5. **Power Efficiency:** *"The device shall provide ≥ 24 hours of continuous monitoring on a single charge."* — Drives battery and firmware design.

6. **Security & Data Privacy:** *"All BLE transmissions shall be AES-128 encrypted; no patient identifier shall be stored in plain text."* — Aligned with HIPAA / DISHA (India) regulations.

7. **Maintainability:** *"Firmware shall be updateable over-the-air (OTA) without physical access to the device."* — Reduces service costs and enables post-market patches.

8. **Regulatory Compliance:** *"The device shall comply with ISO 80601-2-61 (pulse oximeter particular standard) and IEC 60601-1."* — Mandatory for market approval.

9. **Environmental Tolerance:** *"Operating temperature range: 5 °C to 40 °C; humidity: 10% to 95% RH."*

10. **Portability:** *"Total weight including battery ≤ 50 g."*

*(Any 7 well-explained NFRs will earn full 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Most Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Writing requirements as solutions:** Saying *"use STM32 microcontroller"* in SRS (this is HRS content). SRS should state *what*, not *how*.
> 2. **Forgetting traceability numbers:** Every FR/NFR must be uniquely numbered.
> 3. **Mixing units or omitting them:** Always specify units (mmHg, °C, bpm, mA, etc.).
> 4. **Ignoring verification:** Every requirement must have a *test method* attached.
> 5. **No use-case context:** Linking specifications to a *clinical scenario* (rural ECG, home BP monitor, COVID pulse oximeter) scores bonus marks.

---

## 5.X — Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam!):**

- [ ] **Design Specification (DS)** = Master document; defines *what* and *how well*.
- [ ] **HRS** = Hardware Realization; **SRS** = Software Realization; both derived from DS.
- [ ] **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** = SRS standard; **ISO 13485 + IEC 60601-1** = medical device HRS standards.
- [ ] **SMART** requirements: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable.
- [ ] **Functional vs Non-Functional**: FR = *what the device does*; NFR = *quality attributes* (performance, safety, reliability, usability).
- [ ] **V-Model**: DS ↔ System Test; HRS ↔ HW Integration; SRS ↔ SW Integration; Detailed Design ↔ Unit Test; User Need ↔ UAT.
- [ ] **Risk → Requirement linkage**: ISO 14971 hazard analysis drives new requirements in HRS/SRS.
- [ ] **Traceability Matrix** = mandatory link: User Need → FR/NFR → HRS/SRS → Test Case.
- [ ] **Key biomedical safety constants**: Patient leakage current ≤ 10 µA (CF), ≤ 100 µA (BF); defibrillation-proof ratings; biocompatibility (ISO 10993).
- [ ] **Sampling theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — always justify sampling rate in HRS.
- [ ] **ADC resolution**: $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^n$ — for ECG use 24-bit ADC; for BP use 16-bit ADC.
- [ ] **Power budget**: Sum all subsystem $V \times I$ — verify battery life $T = C_{mAh} / I_{mA}$.
- [ ] **Document structure (SRS)**: Introduction → Overall Description → Specific Requirements → Interfaces → Non-functional → V&V → Appendices.
- [ ] **Document structure (HRS)**: System Overview → Electrical → Mechanical → Environmental → Interfaces → Safety → BOM → V&V.
- [ ] **Common mistake**: Writing *implementation details* (e.g., "use a for loop") in SRS — write *requirements* ("system shall iterate over all samples").
- [ ] **Regulatory note**: Without complete DS, HRS, and SRS, a biomedical device **cannot be submitted for USFDA / CE / CDSCO approval**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software/Hardware Design Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software/Hardware Design Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Biomedical Device Design**, a *Software Design Description (SDD)* and a *Hardware Design Description (HDD)* are the formal, controlled engineering documents that completely specify *how* a medical device's software and hardware subsystems are architected, partitioned, interfaced, and implemented in order to satisfy the verified **Design Inputs** and produce the required **Design Outputs**. They constitute the core deliverables of the *Detailed Design* phase of the **Design Control** lifecycle prescribed by **ISO 13485:2016**, **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**, and the **IEC 62304 / IEC 60601-1** family of standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Phrasing):**
> *"A Software/Hardware Design Description is a structured, traceable, and verifiable document that translates user needs and design inputs into a complete, unambiguous specification of the system's components, interfaces, data flow, behavior, and implementation logic, enabling consistent fabrication, coding, integration, and V&V of a biomedical device."*

Formally, the SDD is governed by **IEEE Std 1016™-2009** (*Standard for Information Technology — Systems Design — Software Design Description*), while HDD is typically aligned with **IEEE Std 1016-2009** and the hardware-block guidance of **IEC 60601-1** (general medical electrical equipment safety) and **ISO 14971** (risk management).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Architect's Blueprint" Analogy 🏥→🏗️**
>
> Imagine you are building a **heart-lung bypass machine** (a complex life-supporting biomedical device). Before any screw is turned or any line of firmware is flashed, the engineering team must hand the manufacturing and software teams a **single, complete "blueprint book."**
>
> - The **Hardware Design Description (HDD)** is like the *architectural drawing* — it shows every printed circuit board (PCB), microcontroller, op-amp, sensor, isolation barrier, and mechanical enclosure, with exact part numbers, tolerances, and safety classifications.
> - The **Software Design Description (SDD)** is like the *wiring & control logic diagram* — it shows the firmware architecture, the state machines that control the pump, the alarm handler, the user-interface flow, and the data flow between modules.
>
> Without these two documents, the device cannot be **reproducibly built, safely tested, or regulatorily cleared.** They are the *contract* between design intent and physical reality.

### 1.3 Why SDD/HDD Are Non-Negotiable in Biomedical Engineering

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Patient Safety** | A ventilator's failure mode is *death*; the SDD/HDD must make failure modes visible. |
| **Regulatory Clearance** | FDA / CDSCO / EU-MDR cannot approve a device without traceable design documentation. |
| **Reproducibility** | Two different contract manufacturers must build *identical* devices. |
| **Maintainability** | Service engineers 10 years later must understand the original design. |
| **Cybersecurity** | FDA 2023 cybersecurity guidance mandates software bill-of-materials (SBOM) inside the SDD. |
| **Liability & IP** | Patent claims and forensic investigations rely on the design description. |

### 1.4 Key Standard Anchor Metrics

- **Safety Class (IEC 60601-1):** Class I, IIa, IIb, III — defines the rigor of HDD.
- **Software Safety Class (IEC 62304):** Class A, B, C — defines the rigor of SDD.
- **Risk Acceptability (ISO 14971):** Acceptable / ALARP / Unacceptable.
- **Traceability Ratio (KTU benchmark):** ≥ 1.0 (every requirement linked to ≥ 1 design element and ≥ 1 test).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Design Description within the Design-Control Framework

The SDD/HDD are produced **after** Design Planning (Design & Development Plan, DDP) and **after** Design Inputs (User Needs + Design Inputs Document, DID) are approved, but **before** Design Verification, Design Validation, and Design Transfer.

```
User Needs → Design Inputs → [ SDD + HDD ] → Design Outputs → V&V → Transfer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "IDDOV" Phase Model:**
> **I**nput → **D**esign Description → **D**esign Output → **O**utput Review → **V**erify/Validate.
> The SDD/HDD live at position **#2 (Design Description)** in this sequence.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Levels of a Design Description (Per IEEE 1016)

The standard mandates that the SDD/HDD be organised in **three nested levels** of abstraction:

1. **Conceptual (Architectural) Level** — What are the major subsystems? How are they partitioned?
2. **Logical (Module) Level** — What does each subsystem do? What are its inputs, outputs, behaviour, and data structures?
3. **Physical (Implementation) Level** — How is each module actually built (hardware part / code class)?

For a **biomedical device** like a pulse oximeter, this looks like:

- *Conceptual:* Sensor front-end → Signal conditioning → MCU → Display/Alarm.
- *Logical:* SpO₂ algorithm module, LED driver module, Buzzer driver module, UART comms.
- *Physical:* Specific op-amp (e.g., AD8226), firmware in C on STM32L0, OLED SSD1306.

### 2.3 Mandatory Components of an SDD (IEEE 1016-2009)

| # | Section | Purpose for a Biomedical Device |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identification** | Document number, version, device name (e.g., "Infusion Pump Model X-200, SDD v3.2") |
| 2 | **Stakeholders & Uses** | Design engineers, test engineers, regulatory affairs, service technicians |
| 3 | **Design Views** | Decomposition, Dependency, Interface, Data, Dynamic, Concurrency, Algorithm, State, Construction, Operational |
| 4 | **Conceptual Architecture** | Block diagram, sub-system boundaries |
| 5 | **Detailed Design of Each Module** | CSCI / CSC / Unit level — for software; PCB-level for hardware |
| 6 | **Interface Specifications** | Mechanical, electrical, software, user, communication |
| 7 | **Data Design** | Data dictionaries, database schemas, variable types, ranges |
| 8 | **Behaviour & State Descriptions** | State machines, timing diagrams, control flow |
| 9 | **Performance & Constraints** | Latency, throughput, power, safety class |
| 10 | **Traceability Matrices** | Link every design element to a Design Input and a Verification Test |

### 2.4 Mandatory Components of an HDD (Adapted IEEE 1016 + IEC 60601-1)

| # | Section | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | System Block Diagram | Functional partitioning |
| 2 | Schematic Diagrams | Full electrical schematics (analog + digital + power) |
| 3 | Bill of Materials (BOM) | Every component with part number, manufacturer, RoHS/REACH status |
| 4 | PCB Layout Files | Gerber, stack-up, controlled-impedance tracks |
| 5 | Mechanical Drawings | Enclosure, IP rating, materials biocompatibility (ISO 10993) |
| 6 | Interface Definitions | Pinouts, connector specs, isolation barriers (MOOP/MOPP per IEC 60601-1) |
| 7 | Safety & Risk Mapping | Single-fault analysis, creepage/clearance, leakage current |
| 8 | Power Budget | Battery life, peak inrush, fault currents |
| 9 | EMC Considerations | Per IEC 60601-1-2 |
| 10 | Verification Hooks | Test points, JTAG, boundary-scan |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The 5 W's + 1 H" of an SDD/HDD — *What, Where, When, Who, Why, How*:**
> **What** it does (function), **Where** it runs (target), **When** it acts (timing), **Who** owns it (responsibility), **Why** it exists (requirement link), **How** it is built (implementation).

| Term / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit / Value | Where Used in Biomedical Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CSCI** | Computer Software Configuration Item | — | Top-level software unit (e.g., "Alarm Handler CSCI") |
| **CSC** | Computer Software Component | — | Sub-module (e.g., "Buzzer Driver CSC") |
| **MOOP** | Means of Operator Protection | V (creepage/clearance) | IEC 60601-1 isolation, 1500 V AC test |
| **MOPP** | Means of Patient Protection | V | Patient-applied parts, 4000 V AC test |
| **ESD** | Electrostatic Discharge | kV | IEC 60601-1-2, ±8 kV contact / ±15 kV air |
| **AAMI** | Association for Advancement of Medical Instrumentation | — | Standards body for risk & alarm standards |
| **SBOM** | Software Bill of Materials | — | FDA 2023 cybersecurity precondition |
| **DID** | Design Input Document | — | Upstream of SDD/HDD |
| **DMR** | Design Master Record | — | Contains the SDD/HDD (per 21 CFR 820.181) |
| **DHF** | Design History File | — | The complete repository of all design records |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, never confuse **DHF (Design History File)** with **DMR (Design Master Record)**. The **DHF is chronological evidence** that the design was developed correctly; the **DMR is the current production baseline**. The SDD/HDD appear in *both*.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

The SDD/HDD is the **single document the FDA inspector opens first** during a QSIT or MDR audit. For a Class III implant (e.g., pacemaker), the absence of a properly-versioned, traceable SDD/HDD can result in:
- Refusal of 510(k) or PMA submission
- Warning Letter (Form FDA 483)
- Product recall under 21 CFR 806
- Personal liability of the design engineer under the **Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Biomedical device design description is *documentation-driven*, not numerical. Therefore, the "derivation" below is a **logical / structural derivation** — the step-by-step construction of an SDD/HDD from a generic set of Design Inputs. KTU examiners frequently award full marks to a logically sequenced structural walk-through, with the same rigor expected for an algebraic derivation.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Building an SDD/HDD from Design Inputs

**Given (KTU Hypothetical):**
You are the design engineer for **"NeoBreath-100"** — a neonatal CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) device. The approved Design Input Document (DID) contains the following 4 locked requirements:

| DID # | Requirement (Input) |
|---|---|
| R1 | Deliver adjustable airway pressure from **4 cmH₂O to 10 cmH₂O** with ±1 cmH₂O accuracy. |
| R2 | Continuous monitoring of SpO₂ via integrated finger sensor; raise alarm if SpO₂ < **85%** for > **10 s**. |
| R3 | Battery operation ≥ **6 hours** at nominal load; hot-swappable Li-ion pack. |
| R4 | Single-fault-safe per **IEC 60601-1 Class IIa**; alarm shall remain functional up to **30 s** after mains loss. |

**Find:** Derive the SDD + HDD skeleton that satisfies all four inputs with full bidirectional traceability.

---

#### Step 1 — Architectural Decomposition (Conceptual Level)

Apply functional partitioning. Each major function maps to one or more Design Inputs.

$$
\text{NeoBreath-100 Architecture} = \{ \text{F1: Pressure Gen}, \ \text{F2: Sensing}, \ \text{F3: Alarm}, \ \text{F4: Power}, \ \text{F5: HMI} \}
$$

Mapping (this is the first row of the **Traceability Matrix**):

$$
F1 \leftrightarrow R1, \quad F2 \leftrightarrow R2, \quad F3 \leftrightarrow R2, \quad F4 \leftrightarrow R3, \ F4 \leftrightarrow R4, \quad F5 \leftrightarrow R2
$$

**Logic text:** A *blower-driven pressure generator* implements R1; an *SpO₂ sensor front-end* implements R2; an *audible/visual alarm unit* implements R2; a *dual Li-ion + supercap UPS* implements R3 and R4; a *touchscreen + rotary encoder* implements R5 (implied HMI).

---

#### Step 2 — Module-Level (Logical) Decomposition

For each function, enumerate the **CSC** (Computer Software Component) for software and the **hardware block** for hardware.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1_{\text{SW}} &= \{ \text{PressureControl\_PID}, \ \text{ValveDriver\_PWM} \} \\
F1_{\text{HW}} &= \{ \text{Blower (BLDC motor)}, \ \text{Pressure Transducer (MPX5010)}, \ \text{Solenoid Valve} \} \\
F2_{\text{SW}} &= \{ \text{SpO2\_Algorithm}, \ \text{AFE\_Config} \} \\
F2_{\text{HW}} &= \{ \text{MAX30102 optical sensor}, \ \text{I}^{2}\text{C isolator}} \\
F3_{\text{SW}} &= \{ \text{AlarmManager}, \ \text{BuzzerDriver}, \ \text{LEDDriver} \} \\
F3_{\text{HW}} &= \{ \text{Piezo buzzer (3 kHz, 85 dB)}, \ \text{Red/Amber LEDs} \} \\
F4_{\text{HW}} &= \{ \text{LM2596 buck}, \ \text{BQ25895 charger}, \ \text{Li-ion 2× 18650}, \ \text{Supercap 10 F/5.4 V} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Interface Definition (Per IEEE 1016 §8)

For every interface, document the **origin**, **destination**, **data type/range**, **protocol**, and **safety class**.

| Interface | Origin | Destination | Type / Range | Protocol | Safety Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I-1 | Pressure Transducer (MPX5010) | ADC1 of STM32L4 | Analog 0–5 V → 0–25 cmH₂O | Single-ended | MOPP (patient-applied) |
| I-2 | SpO₂ Sensor (MAX30102) | MCU I²C1 | 7-bit I²C addr 0x57 | I²C @ 400 kHz, isolated via ADuM1251 | MOPP |
| I-3 | AlarmManager (SW) | BuzzerDriver GPIO | Bool / PWM | GPIO + 4 kHz tone | MOOP |
| I-4 | Battery gauge (BQ25895) | MCU I²C2 | I²C 0x6B | I²C @ 100 kHz | MOOP |

**Logic text:** Each interface inherits the highest safety class of the subsystems it connects, per **IEC 60601-1 §8.5** (isolation requirements).

---

#### Step 4 — Behavioural Description — State Machine for F3 (AlarmManager)

The alarm sub-system in a life-supporting device **must** be a deterministic finite state machine (DFSM). The mathematical representation is:

$$
M = (Q, \ \Sigma, \ \delta, \ q_0, \ F)
$$

For the **NeoBreath-100 alarm** specifically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{ \text{IDLE}, \ \text{MONITOR}, \ \text{ALARM\_LOW}, \ \text{ALARM\_HIGH}, \ \text{LATCHED} \} \\
\Sigma &= \{ \text{SpO2\_ok}, \ \text{SpO2\_low}, \ \text{SpO2\_crit}, \ \text{ack}, \ \text{timeout} \} \\
q_0 &= \text{IDLE} \\
F &= \{ \text{LATCHED} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Transition function $\delta$ (excerpt):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(\text{MONITOR}, \ \text{SpO2\_ok}) &= \text{MONITOR} \\
\delta(\text{MONITOR}, \ \text{SpO2\_low}) &= \text{MONITOR} \ \text{(if } t < 10 \text{ s)} \\
\delta(\text{MONITOR}, \ \text{SpO2\_low}) &= \text{ALARM\_LOW} \ \text{(if } t \geq 10 \text{ s, satisfies R2)} \\
\delta(\text{ALARM\_LOW}, \ \text{SpO2\_crit}) &= \text{ALARM\_HIGH} \\
\delta(\text{ALARM\_LOW}, \ \text{ack}) &= \text{MONITOR} \\
\delta(\text{ALARM\_HIGH}, \ \text{timeout}) &= \text{LATCHED} \ \text{(requires power-cycle reset)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering justification:** The 10-second integration window is **traceable directly to Requirement R2**. The LATCHED state prevents silent auto-cancellation, satisfying **IEC 60601-1-8** for alarm-system persistence.

---

#### Step 5 — Traceability Matrix Construction

This is the **most heavily marked table** in any KTU 14-mark question on design description.

$$
\text{Traceability Ratio} = \frac{\text{\# Design Elements}}{\text{\# Design Inputs}} \geq 1.0
$$

| DID # | HW Element | SW CSCI/CSC | Verification Test ID |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Blower, MPX5010, Valve | PressureControl_PID | VT-P-001 (pressure accuracy) |
| R2 | MAX30102, Buzzer, LEDs | SpO2_Algorithm, AlarmManager | VT-A-002 (alarm latency), VT-S-003 (SpO₂ acc.) |
| R3 | 2× Li-ion, BQ25895 | BatteryMonitor | VT-B-004 (6 h endurance) |
| R4 | Supercap, isolators | PowerFail_Handler | VT-SF-005 (single-fault), VT-PF-006 (alarm on mains loss) |

For the given example:

$$
\text{Traceability Ratio} = \frac{5 \ (\text{HW design elements mapped to R1–R4})}{4 \ (\text{Inputs})} = 1.25 \geq 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

A ratio $< 1.0$ would indicate **un-traced design elements** — an automatic KTU valuation penalty and a 21 CFR 820.30(g) **non-conformance** in a real FDA audit.

---

#### Step 6 — Documentation Hierarchy Tree (Mapping to DHF/DMR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DHF} &= \big\{ \text{DDP},\ \text{DID},\ \boxed{\text{SDD}},\ \boxed{\text{HDD}},\ \text{V\&V Reports},\ \text{Risk File} \big\} \\
\text{DMR} &\subset \text{DHF} \quad \text{(current production version of the above)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KTU "derivation end-point": from a single set of design inputs we have produced a **complete, traceable, regulatorily compliant** design description package.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Write SDD vs HDD vs Both

| Design Element | Pure HW? | Pure SW? | Embedded? | Required Document(s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discrete op-amp filter on ECG front-end | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | **HDD only** |
| FPGA gateware (.bitstream) | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | **SDD** + **HDD** |
| Host GUI on Windows tablet | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | **SDD only** (IEC 62304 SOUP section needed) |
| Mechanical enclosure | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | **HDD only** (mechanical chapter) |
| Closed-loop motor control on STM32 | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | **Both SDD + HDD** (firmware + sensor/actuator HW) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the engine safeguard rules, node IDs are alphanumeric, labels contain only uppercase/raw text, and no markdown formatting is embedded inside Mermaid labels.

### 4.1 Figure A — V-Model of Biomedical Device Design with SDD/HDD Position

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[USER NEEDS] --> B[DESIGN INPUT DOCUMENT DID]
    B --> C[DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT PLAN DDP]
    B --> D[SOFTWARE DESIGN DESCRIPTION SDD]
    B --> E[HARDWARE DESIGN DESCRIPTION HDD]
    D --> F[DESIGN OUTPUT]
    E --> F
    F --> G[DESIGN VERIFICATION]
    G --> H[DESIGN VALIDATION]
    H --> I[DESIGN TRANSFER TO MANUFACTURING]
    I --> J[DESIGN HISTORY FILE DHF]
    F --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    B --> J
```

**Reading guide (KTU board answer):** The left arm (A → D, E) is the *decomposition* (what does the device need to be?). The bottom arm (D, E → F) is *implementation*. The right arm (G → I) is *integration*. The SDD and HDD sit on the **left-arm**, immediately downstream of the Design Inputs.

### 4.2 Figure B — IEEE 1016 Hierarchical Decomposition of an SDD

```mermaid
graph TD
    SDD[SDD ROOT DOCUMENT] --> L1[LEVEL 1 CONCEPTUAL ARCHITECTURE]
    SDD --> L2[LEVEL 2 LOGICAL MODULE DESIGN]
    SDD --> L3[LEVEL 3 PHYSICAL IMPLEMENTATION]
    L1 --> V1A[DECOMPOSITION VIEW]
    L1 --> V1B[DEPENDENCY VIEW]
    L1 --> V1C[INTERFACE VIEW TOP LEVEL]
    L2 --> V2A[DATA DICTIONARY]
    L2 --> V2B[STATE MACHINE SPECS]
    L2 --> V2C[ALGORITHM PSEUDOCODE]
    L2 --> V2D[INTERFACE VIEW DETAILED]
    L3 --> V3A[CODE LISTING OR SCHEMATIC]
    L3 --> V3B[BILL OF MATERIALS]
    L3 --> V3C[PINOUT AND CONNECTOR TABLE]
    L3 --> V3D[VERIFICATION HOOKS JTAG TESTPOINTS]
```

### 4.3 Figure C — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Biomedical Device (replaces physical drawing)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SENS[SENSOR FRONT END MOPP] --> AFE[ANALOG FRONT END AFE]
    AFE --> ISO1[PATIENT ISOLATION BARRIER IEC60601]
    ISO1 --> ADC[ADC INPUT]
    ADC --> MCU[EMBEDDED MCU ARM CORTEX M]
    MCU --> ALGO[ALGORITHM CSCI E G ECG SPO2]
    ALGO --> HMI[USER INTERFACE TOUCH LCD]
    ALGO --> ALM[ALARM MANAGER IEC60601 1 8]
    ALM --> BUZ[PIEZO BUZZER]
    ALM --> LED[VISUAL LED INDICATOR]
    MCU --> COM[COMMUNICATION UART USB BLE]
    PWR[POWER MANAGEMENT MOOP] --> MCU
    PWR --> SENS
    BATT[LI ION BATTERY PACK] --> PWR
    MAINS[MAINS ADAPTER IEC60601 1] --> PWR
    SAF[SAFETY MCU WATCHDOG] --> MCU
    SAF --> ALM
```

**Reading guide:** Notice three safety islands — (1) the **MOPP barrier** on the patient side, (2) the **independent safety MCU** watching the main controller, and (3) the **dedicated alarm hardware** with its own power feed. This is the canonical architecture pattern in **IEC 60601-1 Class IIa/IIb** devices such as ventilators, infusion pumps, and dialysis machines.

### 4.4 Figure D — Traceability Web (showing the SDD/HDD as the central hub)

```mermaid
graph TD
    UR[USER NEEDS] --> DID[DESIGN INPUT DOCUMENT]
    DID --> RISK[RISK MANAGEMENT FILE ISO 14971]
    DID --> SDD[SDD]
    DID --> HDD[HDD]
    SDD --> TEST1[VERIFICATION TEST SOFTWARE]
    HDD --> TEST2[VERIFICATION TEST HARDWARE]
    TEST1 --> VNR[VERIFICATION REPORT]
    TEST2 --> VNR
    SDD --> VNR
    HDD --> VNR
    VNR --> VAL[DESIGN VALIDATION REPORT]
    VAL --> DHF[DESIGN HISTORY FILE]
    SDD --> DHF
    HDD --> DHF
    DID --> DHF
    RISK --> DHF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions model the **actual KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) and Part B (1 × 14 marks with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B). Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. All solutions are aligned to the KTU marking scheme and Bloom's Levels 2–4 (Understand, Apply, Analyse).

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the term "Software Design Description" as per IEEE 1016. List any four mandatory sections of an SDD for a Class C medical device software (per IEC 62304).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Software Design Description (SDD)** is a structured document, conforming to **IEEE Std 1016-2009**, that completely specifies the architecture, components, interfaces, data, behaviour, and implementation of a software system in a verifiable and traceable manner, such that the design can be unambiguously implemented, integrated, and tested. **(1.5 marks)**
Four mandatory sections for a **Class C (IEC 62304)** medical device software SDD are:
1. **Identification & Version Control** section.
2. **Conceptual / Architectural Decomposition View** (top-level CSCI structure).
3. **Detailed Module (CSC) Design** including state machines and data dictionaries.
4. **Interface Specifications** with all external HW and SW entities, including SOUP (Software Of Unknown Provenance) section. **(1.5 marks — 0.375 each)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*Differentiate between the Design Input Document (DID) and the Hardware Design Description (HDD) with reference to a typical medical device such as a digital sphygmomanometer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Design Input Document (DID) | Hardware Design Description (HDD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage in lifecycle** | Upstream — *what* the device must do | Downstream — *how* the hardware implements it |
| **Nature of content** | Functional, performance, regulatory, and safety *requirements* (e.g., "BP range 0–250 mmHg, ±3 mmHg") | Schematics, BOM, layout, isolation, pinouts, safety analysis (e.g., specific op-amp AD8226, 1500 V isolation) |
| **Audience** | Marketing, clinicians, regulatory affairs | Hardware design, PCB layout, manufacturing, V&V |
| **Change-control** | Frozen after formal review; any change is a "design change" | Iterated; must be re-traced to DID for every change |
| **Example for sphygmomanometer** | "Cuff pressure display resolution ≤ 1 mmHg" | "MPX5050GP pressure sensor on ADC channel 1, 12-bit SAR, oversampled to 16-bit" |

**(1 mark per key distinction × 3 = 3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024, Q. 4(a)–(b)]*

> You are the lead design engineer for a **portable patient monitor (PM-200)** that measures ECG (3-lead), SpO₂, and skin temperature. The product is classified as **Class IIb (IEC 60601-1)** and the embedded firmware as **IEC 62304 Class C**. Answer the following:

**(a)** Construct the **Software Design Description (SDD)** skeleton for the alarm-handling CSCI of this monitor. Your SDD must cover the **conceptual architecture, module (CSC) decomposition, and interface specifications**, and must be traceable to the following three Design Inputs:
- **DI-1:** Raise medium-priority alarm if heart rate $< 50$ bpm or $> 120$ bpm for $> 5$ s.
- **DI-2:** Latch the alarm until acknowledged by the clinician.
- **DI-3:** Alarm acoustic output must be $\geq 65$ dB at 1 m per IEC 60601-1-8.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Conceptual Architecture (2 marks)*
The alarm CSCI sits in the **Application Layer** of the firmware stack and is composed of the following CSCs:

$$
\text{AlarmCSCI} = \{ \text{ECG\_Feature\_Extractor}, \ \text{SpO2\_Feature\_Extractor}, \ \text{Threshold\_Evaluator}, \ \text{Alarm\_FSM}, \ \text{Audio\_Driver}, \ \text{Visual\_Driver}, \ \text{Ack\_Handler} \}
$$

*Step 2 — Module Decomposition (2 marks)*
- **ECG_Feature_Extractor CSC** — computes instantaneous HR every 250 ms.
- **SpO2_Feature_Extractor CSC** — computes SpO₂ every 1 s.
- **Threshold_Evaluator CSC** — applies the rule: trigger if HR $\notin [50, 120]$ bpm for 5 consecutive seconds.
- **Alarm_FSM CSC** — state machine: $Q = \{ \text{NOMINAL}, \ \text{CANDIDATE}, \ \text{ACTIVE}, \ \text{LATCHED}, \ \text{ACKNOWLEDGED} \}$.
- **Audio_Driver / Visual_Driver CSCs** — GPIO + PWM control of the buzzer and tri-colour LED.

*Step 3 — Interface Specification (2 marks)*

| Interface | From → To | Type / Protocol | Safety Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| I-ECG | ADC1 → ECG_Feature_Extractor | SPI @ 1 MHz, DMA-buffered | MOPP |
| I-SpO2 | I²C isolator → SpO2_Feature_Extractor | I²C @ 400 kHz | MOPP |
| I-Ack | Touch screen → Ack_Handler | GPIO interrupt, debounced 50 ms | MOOP |
| I-Buzzer | Audio_Driver → SMD buzzer | 4 kHz PWM, 65 dB SPL @ 1 m | MOOP |

*Step 4 — Traceability Hook (1 mark)*
- DI-1 → Threshold_Evaluator + Alarm_FSM (state transition with 5-s timer).
- DI-2 → Alarm_FSM (LATCHED state) + Ack_Handler.
- DI-3 → Audio_Driver (buzzer PWM duty cycle calculated to produce 65 dB SPL).

---

**(b)** Construct the **corresponding Hardware Design Description (HDD)** block diagram and BOM-level listing for the alarm sub-system. Specify the **isolation strategy** required to satisfy IEC 60601-1 (one MOOP for operator, one MOPP for patient). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*Block Diagram (2 marks):* (Refer to Figure C in Section 4.3; the alarm sub-system is the *Alarm_Manager* block + *Buzzer* + *LEDs*, fed from the *MOOP/MOPP* isolated power rail.)

*BOM for Alarm Sub-system (2 marks):*

| Ref | Part | Manufacturer | Function | Safety Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| U1 | STM32L476RG | STMicro | Main MCU | Hosts Alarm_FSM |
| U2 | ADuM1251 | Analog Devices | I²C isolator, 2.5 kV rms | Provides **1× MOPP** between patient and MCU |
| U3 | TLP2361 | Toshiba | Optocoupler, 5 kV rms | Reinforces **2× MOPP** for alarm signal path |
| U4 | CPT-9019S-SMT | CUI Inc. | Piezo buzzer, 85 dB @ 10 cm | Acoustic alarm (DI-3) |
| D1, D2 | LTST-C190KRKT, LTST-C190KGKT | Lite-On | Red / Green SMD LED | Visual alarm |
| T1 | Pulse transformer, 750311492 | Würth | Reinforced isolation, 6 kV | Provides **2× MOPP** for power |
| F1 | Resettable PTC, 0.5 A | Littelfuse | Over-current on alarm rail | IEC 60601-1 single-fault-safe |

*Isolation Strategy Justification (2 marks):*
- **Patient side (MOPP):** All patient-applied parts (ECG electrodes, SpO₂ sensor) are isolated from the MCU by **two independent MOPP** barriers: (a) ADuM1251 on the data path, and (b) the Würth pulse transformer on the power path. Creepage $\geq 8$ mm, clearance $\geq 5$ mm, dielectric withstand 4000 V AC per IEC 60601-1 §8.5.2.
- **Operator side (MOOP):** The buzzer, LEDs, and USB port are isolated by **one MOOP** (1500 V AC, 2.5 mm creepage) achieved through TLP2361 optocoupler and a separate DC-DC converter (e.g., RECOM R0.5Z).
- **Single-fault safety:** Resettable PTC F1 ensures that a short-circuit on the alarm rail cannot disable the alarm (R4 in our earlier NeoBreath example). Independent watchdog timer on safety-MCU (see Figure C) triggers LATCHED alarm on any MCU lock-up.

*Traceability Hook (1 mark):*
- DI-1 → ECG/SpO2 sensor front-ends (HW) + threshold firmware (SW).
- DI-2 → LATCHED state of Alarm_FSM (SW) + hardware reset path via safety-MCU.
- DI-3 → U4 buzzer + audio amplifier gain setting in Audio_Driver CSC.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024, Q. 4(a)–(b), Alt Choice]*

> A Class IIa **ambulatory infusion pump (IP-50)** is to be designed. The firmware is classified as **IEC 62304 Class B**. Two design inputs are critical:
> - **DI-α:** The pump shall deliver flow rates from **0.1 mL/h to 1000 mL/h** with ±2 % accuracy.
> - **DI-β:** A **post-occlusion bolus alarm** shall fire within **2 minutes** of detection of downstream pressure exceeding **300 mmHg**.

**(a)** Develop the **full SDD conceptual architecture and state-machine specification** for the occlusion-detection and alarm sub-system, including a complete **Traceability Matrix**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*1. Conceptual Architecture (2 marks):*

$$
\text{OcclusionCSCI} = \{ \text{Pressure\_AFE}, \ \text{Filtering\_CSC}, \ \text{Threshold\_CSC}, \ \text{Bolus\_Predictor\_CSC}, \ \text{Alarm\_FSM}, \ \text{Audio\_LED\_Driver} \}
$$

*2. State Machine (3 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{ \text{NORMAL}, \ \text{PRESSURE\_RISE}, \ \text{BOLUS\_WATCH}, \ \text{OCCLUSION\_ALARM}, \ \text{ACKNOWLEDGED} \} \\
q_0 &= \text{NORMAL}, \quad F = \{ \text{OCCLUSION\_ALARM} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Key transitions (one example fully expanded for KTU marks):

$$
\delta(\text{NORMAL},\ \text{P} \geq 300\ \text{mmHg}) = \text{PRESSURE\_RISE} \quad \text{[trigger DI-$\beta$]}
$$

$$
\delta(\text{PRESSURE\_RISE},\ t \geq 30\ \text{s}) = \text{BOLUS\_WATCH} \quad \text{[deliberate delay to distinguish transient artefact]}
$$

$$
\delta(\text{BOLUS\_WATCH},\ \text{P} \geq 300\ \text{mmHg}\ \text{for 90 s more}) = \text{OCCLUSION\_ALARM} \quad \text{[30 + 90 = 120 s < 2 min DI-$\beta$]}
$$

*3. Traceability Matrix (2 marks):*

| DI | HW Element | SW CSCI/CSC | Verification Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| DI-α | Peristaltic pump + encoder | FlowRate_Controller CSC | VT-FR-001 (flow accuracy) |
| DI-β | MPX5050GP pressure sensor | OcclusionCSCI (above) | VT-OCC-002 (alarm latency ≤ 2 min) |

---

**(b)** Develop the **HDD safety architecture** showing the **dual-MCU redundancy** (per IEC 60601-1-1 §17 — PEMS — Programmable Electrical Medical Systems). Specify the **MOPP/MOOP** isolation, the **independent watch-dog**, and the **post-market surveillance hook** that ties the SDD/HDD back to the **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*1. Dual-MCU Redundancy (2 marks):* Two MCUs — primary **STM32L4 (Application MCU)** and independent safety **TI MSP430FRAM (Safety MCU)** — communicate through a galvanically isolated SPI link (ADuM1411, 2.5 kV). The Safety MCU continuously cross-checks a hardware signature of the primary; any mismatch latches the alarm.

*2. Isolation Map (2 marks):*

| Boundary | Barrier | Creepage | Withstand |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patient ↔ Application MCU (MOPP) | ADuM1411 + Würth 750311492 | 8 mm | 4000 V AC |
| Application MCU ↔ Safety MCU (MOOP) | Single ADuM1411 channel | 2.5 mm | 1500 V AC |
| Safety MCU ↔ Operator (MOOP) | On-board DC-DC (RECOM R0.5Z) | 2.5 mm | 1500 V AC |

*3. Watch-dog Strategy (1 mark):* Independent windowed watch-dog (TPS3823) on both MCUs; if either fails to "pet" within 200 ms, the safety MCU drives the alarm latch.

*4. Risk-File Linkage (2 marks):* Every hazard in the **ISO 14971 Risk Management File** must be tied to a *risk control measure*; each risk control is *implemented* in either the SDD (firmware) or the HDD (hardware). Example:

| Hazard | Risk Control | Implementation | Verify |
|---|---|---|---|
| Over-infusion due to SW bug | Dual-MCU cross-check | HDD §3.2, SDD §5.4 | VT-R-007 |
| Occlusion undetected | Pressure sensor + 2-min alarm | SDD §4.2, HDD §2.5 | VT-OCC-002 |

This closed-loop Risk → Design → Verification loop is the **heart** of the SDD/HDD per **ISO 14971:2019 §5**.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on "Software/Hardware Design Description" questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SDD with "source code listing."** The SDD is a *design* document, not a code dump. Listing 200 lines of C does **not** answer an SDD question. Marks go to the *architecture, interface, and state-machine specification*.
> 2. **Missing the Traceability Matrix.** KTU examiners award 2–3 marks explicitly for the matrix. A perfect architecture with no traceability will cap your score around 9/14.
> 3. **Forgetting Safety Class.** Always state the IEC 62304 software class (A/B/C) and the IEC 60601-1 device class (I/IIa/IIb/III). Marks are lost for omitting the class.
> 4. **No mention of MOOP/MOOP/MOPP isolation** on a hardware question — *automatic 1.5-mark penalty*.
> 5. **Generic answers.** Saying "the device has a sensor and a display" is worth 0.5 marks. Use the **exact part number, exact protocol, exact interface specification** (e.g., "MAX30102 over isolated I²C at 400 kHz, address 0x57").
> 6. **Skipping the State Machine.** Alarm sub-systems **must** be specified as a DFSM $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. A prose description is **incomplete** under KTU marking.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SDD = IEEE 1016-2009**; **HDD = IEEE 1016-2009 + IEC 60601-1**.
- ✅ Both documents live in the **DHF** and the **DMR** (21 CFR 820.181).
- ✅ Mandatory SDD views: **Decomposition, Dependency, Interface, Data, Dynamic, State, Algorithm**.
- ✅ Mandatory HDD chapters: **Schematics, BOM, PCB layout, Isolation (MOOP/MOPP), Risk, Power, EMC**.
- ✅ **Software Safety Class (IEC 62304):** A, B, C — drives SDD depth.
- ✅ **Device Class (IEC 60601-1):** I, IIa, IIb, III — drives HDD depth.
- ✅ **MOPP** = patient protection; **MOOP** = operator protection; **2× MOPP** = 4000 V AC; **1× MOOP** = 1500 V AC.
- ✅ **Traceability Ratio $\geq 1.0$** for every requirement.
- ✅ Alarm sub-system must be specified as a **DFSM** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$.
- ✅ ISO 14971 risk controls must be **linked bi-directionally** to SDD/HDD clauses.
- ✅ **SBOM** (Software Bill of Materials) is mandatory in the SDD per FDA 2023 cybersecurity guidance.
- ✅ "**I before D, D before O, O before V**" — Inputs first, then Design Description, then Outputs, then Verify/Validate.
- ✅ "**IDDOV**" — the canonical KTU phase mnemonic for the design-control lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Device Records (Case Study: Automatic BP Monitor, ECG Machine, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Device Records (Case Study: Automatic BP Monitor, ECG Machine, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Device Records" in Biomedical Engineering?

In the context of medical device design, manufacturing, and regulatory compliance, a **Device Record** is a comprehensive, auditable, and legally mandated compilation of documents that describes the complete lifecycle of a medical device. These records form the backbone of regulatory submissions (e.g., FDA 510(k), CE Marking under EU MDR, CDSCO in India) and ensure **traceability, reproducibility, and patient safety**.

The three foundational device records defined under **21 CFR Part 820 (FDA Quality System Regulation)** and harmonized with **ISO 13485:2016** are:

1. **Design History File (DHF)** – Records the *design and development* journey of the device.
2. **Device Master Record (DMR)** – The "recipe book" that describes *how the device is built*.
3. **Device History Record (DHR)** – The "diary" that proves *each manufactured unit was built correctly*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Device Records" in PEBRT415 specifically refers to the *engineering documentation packages* required to translate a biomedical design concept (e.g., an Automatic BP Monitor or ECG Machine) into a manufacturable, certified, and traceable product.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Car Manufacturing" Comparison

Imagine you are buying a car. Inside the glove compartment, you find:
- A **user manual** (Instructions for Use - IFU) that tells you how to drive it.
- A **service booklet** (DHR) that records every nut and bolt tightened at the factory.
- A **blueprint folder** (DMR) held at the factory, showing the exact specification of every component.
- A **design archive** (DHF) at the R&D center, containing the sketches, simulations, and test reports that led to the car's design.

**Device Records in biomedical engineering work exactly the same way** — except the "car" is a medical device that could save (or harm) a human life, so the documentation is *far more rigorous* and *legally enforced*.
