### 1.3 Formal Definitions Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 1 – Design History File (DHF):**
> *"A compilation of records which describes the design history of a finished device, including its design controls, design inputs, design outputs, design reviews, design verification, design validation, and design changes."* — Derived from **21 CFR 820.30(e)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 2 – Device Master Record (DMR):**
> *"A compilation of records containing the procedures and specifications for a finished device, including its intended use, device description, materials, production processes, quality assurance procedures, packaging, and labeling."* — Derived from **21 CFR 820.181**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 3 – Device History Record (DHR):**
> *"A compilation of records containing the production history of a finished device, including dates of manufacture, quantities, acceptance records, and unique device identifiers."* — Derived from **21 CFR 820.184**.

### 1.4 Why are Device Records Critical? (The 4 Pillars)

| Pillar | Engineering Significance | Patient Safety Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Traceability** | Every component can be traced to its supplier and lot | Enables rapid recall of faulty components |
| **Reproducibility** | Device #1 and Device #10,000 are functionally identical | Prevents device-to-device variation |
| **Accountability** | Roles and signatures are documented | Establishes legal chain of responsibility |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Satisfies ISO 13485, FDA, CE, CDSCO audits | Mandatory for market approval |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO1** (Describe the design considerations of biomedical devices) and lays the foundation for **CO5** (Apply regulatory and quality standards in device design).

### 1.5 Scope of Module 1 Device Records

In Module 1, the focus is on **two flagship case studies**:
- **Case Study A:** Automatic Blood Pressure (BP) Monitor (Oscillometric method)
- **Case Study B:** Electrocardiogram (ECG) Machine (Bioelectric signal acquisition)

For each, we will examine the **DMR, DHR, and DHF** contents as per KTU 2024 design documentation requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Device Master Record (DMR)

The DMR is the **master technical specification** of a finished device. For any medical device, the DMR *must* contain the following structured sections, in this exact hierarchical order:

### Hierarchical DMR Structure
1. **Device Description & Intended Use**
   - Product name, model number
   - Indication for use (e.g., "for non-invasive measurement of systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial blood pressure in adults")
   - Target user population (clinician, patient, lay-user)
   - Contraindications and warnings

2. **Classification Information**
   - Risk class per **EU MDR Annex VIII** (Class I, IIa, IIb, III) or **FDA Class I/II/III**
   - GMDN code (Global Medical Device Nomenclature)

3. **Bill of Materials (BOM)**
   - Hierarchical listing of every sub-assembly, component, raw material
   - Approved supplier list (ASL) for each part
   - Material certificates (e.g., biocompatibility per **ISO 10993**)

4. **Engineering Drawings & Schematics**
   - PCB layout files (Gerber, Altium, KiCad)
   - Mechanical CAD models (STEP files, SolidWorks)
   - Block diagrams, wiring diagrams, pneumatic/hydraulic schematics

5. **Software Documentation** (if applicable)
   - Software Requirements Specification (SRS)
   - Software Architecture Document
   - Source code repository references
   - Software Level of Concern (per FDA Guidance)

6. **Production & Process Specifications**
   - Manufacturing Work Instructions (WI)
   - Process Flow Diagrams (PFD)
   - Critical Process Parameters (CPP)
   - Sterilization procedures (if applicable)

7. **Quality Assurance Procedures**
   - Incoming Quality Control (IQC) test methods
   - In-Process Quality Control (IPQC) checkpoints
   - Final Quality Control (FQC) acceptance criteria
   - Statistical sampling plan (e.g., **AQL = 0.65** per ISO 2859-1)

8. **Labeling & Packaging Specifications**
   - Primary label (device body)
   - Secondary label (outer carton)
   - Instructions for Use (IFU)
   - Symbols per **ISO 15223-1**

9. **Installation, Maintenance & Service Manual**

10. **Regulatory Approvals & Standards Compliance Matrix**
    - List of harmonized standards applied (e.g., **IEC 60601-1**, **ISO 14971**)

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Device History Record (DHR)

The DHR is **device-specific** (one DHR per lot or batch) and is generated **during and after production**. It *demonstrates* that the device was built per the DMR.

### DHR Contents
- Date and time of manufacture
- Quantity manufactured
- Lot number / UDI (Unique Device Identifier)
- Components and sub-assemblies used (with supplier lot traceability)
- Manufacturing process records (e.g., soldering profile, sterilization batch record)
- In-process and final test results
- Inspection signatures and dates
- Labeling control records
- Any deviations and their disposition (Concession, Rework, Reject, Scrap)

## 2.3 Anatomy of a Design History File (DHF)

The DHF is **project-specific** and is generated **during the design phase**. It is the *engineering evidence* that the design was developed using a controlled, verified, and validated process.

### DHF Contents (mapped to Design Controls per 21 CFR 820.30)
| Design Control Element | Typical Record |
|---|---|
| **Design & Development Plan** | Project timeline, deliverables, responsibilities |
| **Design Inputs** | User Needs, Functional Requirements, Regulatory Requirements |
| **Design Outputs** | Drawings, BOM, software code, risk analysis |
| **Design Review** | Meeting minutes, action items, approval signatures |
| **Design Verification** | Test protocols and reports (Does design meet inputs?) |
| **Design Validation** | Clinical/simulated-use evaluation (Does design meet user needs?) |
| **Design Transfer** | Documentation transferred to manufacturing |
| **Design Changes** | Change requests, impact analysis, approval |
| **Risk Management File** | Per **ISO 14971:2019** |

## 2.4 The DMR – DHR – DHF Inter-relationship

The three records are not independent silos — they form a **closed-loop V-Model** in medical device development:

```
DHF (Design)  →  DMR (Specification)  →  DHR (Production)
   ↑                                            |
   └────────── Feedback from Field Use ←────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The DHF creates the DMR. The DMR drives the production process. The DHR proves production was correct. Field complaints trigger design changes, which update the DHF. This is the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** applied to medical devices.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **key quantitative metrics** used in Device Records, especially for the BP Monitor and ECG Machine case studies. All formulas are **mathematically verifiable** and based on standard medical device engineering principles.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** | $\text{MAP} = P_d + \dfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)$ | Estimate of average arterial pressure | **mmHg** |
| 2 | **Pulse Pressure (PP)** | $\text{PP} = P_s - P_d$ | Difference between systolic and diastolic | **mmHg** |
| 3 | **Cuff Pressure Decay (Oscillometric)** | $P_c(t) = P_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | Exponential pressure release from cuff | $\tau \approx 0.5\text{–}2\,\text{s}$ |
| 4 | **Amplitude Ratio (Oscillometric)** | $R(t) = \dfrac{A_{\text{osc}}(t)}{A_{\max}}$ | Normalized oscillometric pulse amplitude | **dimensionless** (0 to 1) |
| 5 | **Systolic Detection** | $R_s \approx 0.45$ to $0.55$ | Amplitude ratio at systolic pressure | **empirical constant** |
| 6 | **Mean Arterial Detection** | $R_{\text{MAP}} \approx 1.00$ | Maximum amplitude ratio | corresponds to true MAP |
| 7 | **Diastolic Detection** | $R_d \approx 0.70$ to $0.85$ | Amplitude ratio at diastolic pressure | **empirical constant** |
| 8 | **ECG Heart Rate** | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{R\text{-}R\,\text{interval}}$ | Heart rate from R-R interval | **beats per minute (bpm)** |
| 9 | **ECG QRS Amplitude** | $V_{\text{QRS}}$ | Peak-to-peak R-wave voltage | **0.5 – 2.0 mV** (typical) |
| 10 | **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio** | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Ability to reject 50/60 Hz interference | **≥ 90 dB** (clinical ECG) |
| 11 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Quality of acquired signal | **≥ 40 dB** (diagnostic ECG) |
| 12 | **Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | Minimum sampling to avoid aliasing | **≥ 500 Hz** for ECG; **≥ 100 Hz** for BP oscillometric |
| 13 | **ADC Resolution** | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable voltage step | **n = 12 to 24 bits** |
| 14 | **Acceptable Quality Limit (AQL)** | $\text{AQL}$ | Max % defective in sampling plan | **0.65** (typical for medical devices) |
| 15 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\,\dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Manufacturing process capability | **≥ 1.33** (medical devices) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When writing formulas in a table, students often use the vertical pipe symbol `|` for absolute value (e.g., `|x|`), which **breaks the markdown table**. In KTU 2024 exams and answer scripts, always use `\vert` or `\mid` notation.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

- **For BP Monitors:** The oscillometric amplitude ratio (R) is the *engineering core* of every automated cuff-based BP monitor. A documented algorithm (typically patented) in the DMR is the manufacturer's primary differentiator and IP.
- **For ECG Machines:** The CMRR specification in the DMR directly determines whether the device can be used in **electrosurgical (ESU) environments** — a critical clinical safety feature.
- **For Regulatory Submission:** The risk management file (per **ISO 14971**) bridges the DHF and the clinical evaluation, and is mandatory in CE Marking Technical Files.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU students:** Every biomedical device you will design (from a pulse oximeter to an MRI) will require a structured DMR/DHR/DHF. Mastering this in Module 1 sets the foundation for **regulatory affairs careers** in India's growing MedTech industry.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Oscillometric BP Algorithm (Theoretical Foundation)

The **oscillometric method** is the dominant principle in modern automatic BP monitors. The mathematical model is derived from the *cuff–artery interaction* during controlled deflation.

### Step 1: Cuff Pressure Modeling

Let $P_c(t)$ be the cuff pressure as a function of time. During controlled linear or exponential deflation:

$$
P_c(t) = P_{\text{init}} - k \cdot t \quad \text{(linear deflation)}
$$

where $P_{\text{init}}$ is the inflation pressure (typically 180–200 mmHg) and $k$ is the deflation rate (typically 2–3 mmHg/s).

### Step 2: Oscillometric Pulse Amplitude

As the cuff pressure sweeps through arterial pressure, the arterial wall pulsates, superimposing small oscillations on the cuff pressure:

$$
P_{\text{measured}}(t) = P_c(t) + A_{\text{osc}}(t)
$$

where $A_{\text{osc}}(t)$ is the time-varying oscillation amplitude. This is the **raw signal** captured by the pressure transducer.

### Step 3: Envelope Extraction

The oscillometric envelope $A_{\text{osc}}(t)$ is extracted via **peak detection** on the filtered cuff-pressure signal:

$$
A_{\text{osc}}(t_n) = \max\!\left\{\vert P_{\text{measured}}(t) - P_c(t) \vert\right\} \quad \text{within cardiac cycle } n
$$

### Step 4: Normalization

The envelope is normalized by its maximum value to obtain the **amplitude ratio**:

$$
R(t) = \dfrac{A_{\text{osc}}(t)}{\max A_{\text{osc}}}
$$

By definition, $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$.

### Step 5: Feature Point Identification

- **MAP** is identified at the peak of the envelope: $R(t_{\text{MAP}}) = 1$.
- **Systolic** is identified at the rising edge: $R(t_s) = \alpha_s \approx 0.45$.
- **Diastolic** is identified at the falling edge: $R(t_d) = \alpha_d \approx 0.75$.

The corresponding cuff pressures are reported as the BP values:

$$
P_s = P_c(t_s), \quad P_{\text{MAP}} = P_c(t_{\text{MAP}}), \quad P_d = P_c(t_d)
$$

### Step 6: MAP Verification using Arithmetic Mean

The **arithmetic mean** derived from these features should match the **physiological MAP formula** for consistency:

$$
\text{MAP}_{\text{physio}} = P_d + \dfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)
$$

For a healthy adult, $\text{MAP}_{\text{physio}} \approx 93$ mmHg when $P_s = 120$ mmHg and $P_d = 80$ mmHg.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – ECG Heart Rate Calculation

The ECG signal contains characteristic waves (P, QRS, T). The **R-peak** is the most prominent feature and is used for HR computation.

### Step 1: R-Peak Detection

After bandpass filtering (typically 5–40 Hz) and baseline wander removal, the R-peaks are identified. Let the timestamps of consecutive R-peaks be $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n$.

### Step 2: R-R Interval Calculation

The R-R interval in seconds is:

$$
\Delta t_{R\text{-}R} = t_{n+1} - t_n
$$

### Step 3: Heart Rate Derivation

The heart rate in beats per minute (bpm) is:

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{\Delta t_{R\text{-}R}}
$$

### Step 4: Average Heart Rate

For better accuracy, an average is taken over $N$ R-R intervals:

$$
\overline{\text{HR}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot N}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{i+1} - t_i)}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
If 12 R-R intervals are detected in a 10-second window, $N = 12$ and total time = 10 s:

$$
\overline{\text{HR}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 12}{10} = 72 \text{ bpm}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – CMRR Derivation for ECG Amplifier

The **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** is the heart of an ECG front-end.

### Step 1: Define Differential and Common-Mode Gains

- Differential gain: $A_d = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_d}$ where $V_d = V_1 - V_2$
- Common-mode gain: $A_{cm} = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{cm}}$ where $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$

### Step 2: CMRR Definition

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert
$$

Expressed in decibels:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Typical ECG Design

For a 3-lead ECG using an INA with $A_d = 1000$ and $A_{cm} = 0.1$:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1000}{0.1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \cdot 4 = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

A clinical-grade ECG requires **CMRR ≥ 90 dB** (per IEC 60601-2-25), necessitating a **right-leg drive (RLD) circuit** to actively cancel common-mode interference.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Design Record Mapping (BP Monitor vs ECG Machine)

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam resource** — it directly maps design elements to the relevant device record.

| Design Element | BP Monitor (DMR) | ECG Machine (DMR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Oscillometric method | Bioelectric potential measurement |
| **Primary Transducer** | Strain-gauge / piezoresistive pressure sensor (0–300 mmHg) | Ag/AgCl wet electrodes (10 kΩ impedance) |
| **Signal Bandwidth** | 0.5 – 5 Hz (oscillometric pulses) | 0.05 – 150 Hz (diagnostic ECG) |
| **ADC Resolution** | 12-bit (≥ 0.1 mmHg) | 16–24 bit (≥ 1 μV LSB) |
| **Sampling Rate** | 100–200 Hz | 500–1000 Hz |
| **Safety Standard** | IEC 60601-1 | IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-2-25 |
| **Risk Class (EU MDR)** | Class IIa | Class IIa |
| **Biocompatibility Standard** | ISO 10993 (cuff material) | ISO 10993 (electrode gel) |
| **Key Validation Test** | ISO 81060-2 (clinical accuracy) | ANSI/AAMI EC11 (ECG performance) |
| **Software Level of Concern** | Moderate | Major |
| **Critical Process Parameter** | Deflation rate (2–3 mmHg/s) | Lead-off detection threshold |
| **DHR Critical Record** | Calibration certificate of pressure sensor | Calibration certificate of ADC gain |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A patient uses an automatic BP monitor. The cuff is inflated to 180 mmHg and deflates linearly at $k = 3$ mmHg/s. The oscillometric envelope peaks at $t_{\text{MAP}} = 35$ s with amplitude $A_{\max} = 1.2$ mmHg. The systolic and diastolic thresholds are at $R = 0.50$ and $R = 0.75$, occurring at $t_s = 18.5$ s and $t_d = 48$ s respectively.

**Find:** (a) Systolic pressure, (b) Diastolic pressure, (c) MAP, (d) Pulse pressure, (e) Physiological MAP, (f) % error between measured and physiological MAP.

**Solution:**

### Part (a) – Systolic Pressure

The cuff pressure at $t_s = 18.5$ s is:

$$
P_s = P_{\text{init}} - k \cdot t_s = 180 - 3 \cdot 18.5
$$

$$
P_s = 180 - 55.5 = 124.5 \text{ mmHg}
$$

### Part (b) – Diastolic Pressure

The cuff pressure at $t_d = 48$ s is:

$$
P_d = 180 - 3 \cdot 48 = 180 - 144 = 36 \text{ mmHg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — this is abnormal!** A diastolic of 36 mmHg is life-threateningly low. In a *real* BP monitor, the algorithm would reject this and trigger a re-measurement. For this academic example, we continue.

### Part (c) – MAP (from oscillometric peak)

The cuff pressure at the envelope peak is:

$$
P_{\text{MAP}} = 180 - 3 \cdot 35 = 180 - 105 = 75 \text{ mmHg}
$$

### Part (d) – Pulse Pressure

$$
\text{PP} = P_s - P_d = 124.5 - 36 = 88.5 \text{ mmHg}
$$

### Part (e) – Physiological MAP

$$
\text{MAP}_{\text{physio}} = P_d + \dfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d) = 36 + \dfrac{1}{3}(88.5) = 36 + 29.5 = 65.5 \text{ mmHg}
$$

### Part (f) – Percentage Error

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert P_{\text{MAP}} - \text{MAP}_{\text{physio}} \vert}{\text{MAP}_{\text{physio}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert 75 - 65.5 \vert}{65.5} \times 100 = \dfrac{9.5}{65.5} \times 100 \approx 14.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The 14.5% error exceeds the **ISO 81060-2 acceptance criterion of ≤ 5 mmHg mean error**. This indicates either an algorithm calibration issue or a pathological patient condition. In a **Device History Record (DHR)**, such an outlier would be flagged for **Non-Conformance Report (NCR)** investigation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the terms "Device Master Record (DMR)" and "Device History Record (DHR)" as per 21 CFR Part 820. List four essential contents of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Device Master Record (DMR):** As per **21 CFR 820.181**, the DMR is a *compilation of records containing the procedures and specifications for a finished device*.

**Four essential DMR contents:**
1. Device description and intended use
2. Bill of Materials (BOM) with material specifications
3. Production and process specifications (work instructions)
4. Quality assurance procedures and acceptance criteria
5. Labeling and packaging specifications

> **Device History Record (DHR):** As per **21 CFR 820.184**, the DHR is a *compilation of records containing the production history of a finished device*.

**Four essential DHR contents:**
1. Date(s) of manufacture and quantity produced
2. Lot/serial numbers and Unique Device Identifier (UDI)
3. Components and sub-assemblies used (with supplier traceability)
4. Final test and inspection results
5. Release signature of the QA representative

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a Design History File (DHF)? Name the six design controls that must be documented in a DHF per 21 CFR 820.30.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Design History File (DHF):** A compilation of records which *describes the design history of a finished device*, demonstrating that the device was developed under a controlled design process. (3 marks)

**Six design controls per 21 CFR 820.30 (to be listed):**
1. Design and development planning
2. Design inputs
3. Design outputs
4. Design review
5. Design verification
6. Design validation
7. Design transfer
8. Design changes

*(Any six of the above are acceptable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **Automatic Blood Pressure Monitor** based on the oscillometric principle. Identify the role of each block in the DMR and the corresponding calibration record in the DHR.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An automatic BP monitor is inflated to $P_{\text{init}} = 170$ mmHg and deflates at $k = 2.5$ mmHg/s. The oscillometric envelope peaks at $t_{\text{MAP}} = 32$ s. Using the empirical algorithm, the systolic and diastolic thresholds are detected at $t_s = 20$ s and $t_d = 44$ s respectively. Calculate: (i) Systolic pressure $P_s$, (ii) Diastolic pressure $P_d$, (iii) MAP from the algorithm, (iv) MAP from the physiological formula, (v) Pulse pressure, and (vi) % error between the two MAP values.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The automatic BP monitor (oscillometric) consists of the following blocks (mapping to DMR and DHR):

| Block | Function | DMR Reference | DHR Record |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cuff** | Applies controlled pressure to brachial artery | Mechanical drawing, biocompatibility certificate | Lot number of cuff material |
| **Motor Pump** | Inflates cuff to target pressure | Pump specification (max 250 mmHg) | Pump activation cycles log |
| **Deflation Valve (Solenoid)** | Controls linear/p-step deflation | Valve specification, deflation rate WI | Deflation rate verification |
| **Pressure Transducer** | Converts cuff pressure to voltage | Sensor datasheet, calibration procedure | Calibration certificate (NIST-traceable) |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies and filters transducer output | Op-amp circuit, filter design | Gain calibration record |
| **ADC (12-bit)** | Converts analog to digital | ADC spec, sampling rate = 200 Hz | ADC linearity test |
| **Microcontroller** | Runs oscillometric algorithm | Firmware version, algorithm flowchart | Algorithm version log |
| **LCD Display** | Shows BP and HR | Display spec, symbol library | Display test record |
| **Power Supply (Battery)** | Provides power | Battery spec (IEC 62133) | Battery lot traceability |

The **DHR calibration record** for the pressure sensor must include:
- Calibration date
- Reference standard (e.g., mercury manometer or digital pressure calibrator)
- Calibration curve (voltage vs. mmHg)
- Linearity and hysteresis values
- Calibration engineer's signature

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Systolic Pressure $P_s$:**

$$
P_s = P_{\text{init}} - k \cdot t_s = 170 - 2.5 \cdot 20 = 170 - 50 = 120 \text{ mmHg}
$$

**(ii) Diastolic Pressure $P_d$:**

$$
P_d = 170 - 2.5 \cdot 44 = 170 - 110 = 60 \text{ mmHg}
$$

**(iii) MAP from Algorithm (at envelope peak):**

$$
P_{\text{MAP}} = 170 - 2.5 \cdot 32 = 170 - 80 = 90 \text{ mmHg}
$$

**(iv) Physiological MAP:**

$$
\text{MAP}_{\text{physio}} = P_d + \dfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d) = 60 + \dfrac{1}{3}(120 - 60) = 60 + 20 = 80 \text{ mmHg}
$$

**(v) Pulse Pressure:**

$$
\text{PP} = P_s - P_d = 120 - 60 = 60 \text{ mmHg}
$$

**(vi) % Error:**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert 90 - 80 \vert}{80} \times 100 = \dfrac{10}{80} \times 100 = 12.5\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (BP Monitor Question):**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing MAP from the algorithm with the physiological MAP** — these are two different quantities. State both clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — always write **mmHg** in the final answer.
> 3. **Not commenting on whether the result is clinically plausible** — a 12.5% error is *high* and would fail **ISO 81060-2**. Comment on this for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up $P_s$ and $P_d$ definitions** — $P_s$ corresponds to the *rising* edge of the envelope (earlier time), $P_d$ to the *falling* edge (later time).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **ECG Machine**. Identify the role of the Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) and explain why CMRR is a critical DMR specification. What is the minimum CMRR value mandated by **IEC 60601-2-25** for clinical-grade ECG?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ECG machine samples a 12-lead ECG at 1000 Hz with 16-bit ADC. The full-scale input range is $\pm 5$ mV. (i) Calculate the LSB resolution in $\mu$V. (ii) If the QRS amplitude is 1.5 mV peak-to-peak, calculate the SNR (in dB) assuming RMS noise of 5 $\mu$V. (iii) For 8 R-R intervals measured over 8 seconds, calculate the heart rate. (iv) Identify three records from the DHR for this ECG machine.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**ECG Machine Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.2):
1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** – Bioelectric interface with skin
2. **Lead Wires** – 10-lead cable set (12-lead ECG)
3. **Defibrillation Protection** – High-voltage transient suppression
4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** – First-stage amplification with high CMRR
5. **Bandpass Filter (0.05–150 Hz)** – Removes DC drift and high-frequency noise
6. **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz)** – Rejects power-line interference
7. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** – Adjusts gain for ADC input range
8. **ADC (16–24 bit)** – Digital conversion
9. **Microcontroller/DSP** – Pan-Tompkins R-peak detection
10. **Display & Printer** – User output

**Role of INA:** The INA provides:
- High differential gain ($A_d \approx 100$–$1000$)
- High common-mode rejection (rejects 50/60 Hz mains interference picked up equally by both electrodes)
- High input impedance ($> 10^{10}$ $\Omega$) to avoid loading the high-impedance electrode-skin interface

**Why CMRR is critical in DMR:** ECG signals are extremely small (0.5–5 mV) and are contaminated by large common-mode noise (50/60 Hz from power lines, electromagnetic interference). A high CMRR ensures the **differential ECG signal is preserved** while **common-mode noise is rejected**.

**Minimum CMRR per IEC 60601-2-25:** **≥ 90 dB** for diagnostic-grade ECG machines.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) LSB Resolution:**

Total ADC range = $5\text{ mV} - (-5\text{ mV}) = 10\text{ mV} = 10{,}000\ \mu\text{V}$

$$
\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10{,}000\ \mu\text{V}}{2^{16}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{65{,}536} \approx 0.153\ \mu\text{V}
$$

**(ii) SNR Calculation:**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)
$$

Taking peak-to-peak QRS as signal and RMS noise as noise:

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1500\ \mu\text{V}}{5\ \mu\text{V}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(300) = 20 \cdot 2.477 = 49.54 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) Heart Rate Calculation:**

Average R-R interval = 8 s / 8 intervals = 1.0 s

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{1.0} = 60 \text{ bpm}
$$

**(iv) Three DHR records for ECG Machine:**
1. **Serial number and UDI** of the manufactured unit
2. **Calibration certificate** of the ADC gain and offset (using a calibrated ECG simulator)
3. **Final test report** showing standard ECG waveform printout (e.g., 1 mV, 1 Hz calibration pulse)
4. **Lead-off detection test record** (verifying all 10 leads are functional)
5. **Battery test record** (charge/discharge cycle test)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (ECG Machine Question):**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing CMRR (in dB) with the ratio** — always convert: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}})$.
> 2. **Using peak-to-peak vs. RMS inconsistently** in SNR. Be explicit about which you are using.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\pm$ sign in ADC range** — a $\pm 5$ mV range means full-scale = 10 mV, not 5 mV.
> 4. **Wrong sampling theorem check** — for ECG at 1000 Hz sampling, the Nyquist frequency is 500 Hz, well above the 150 Hz ECG bandwidth. State this for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Device Records (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Three core device records:** DHF (design), DMR (specification), DHR (production). They form a **closed-loop PDCA cycle**.
- [ ] **DHF is project-specific; DMR is product-specific; DHR is batch-specific.**
- [ ] **Regulatory anchor:** 21 CFR Part 820 (US FDA) and ISO 13485:2016 (international).
- [ ] **Design Controls (21 CFR 820.30):** Plan, Inputs, Outputs, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes.
- [ ] **Risk Management:** Governed by **ISO 14971:2019** — bridges DHF and DMR.
- [ ] **BP Monitor — Operating Principle:** Oscillometric, using cuff pressure oscillation envelope.
- [ ] **BP Monitor — Key Algorithm Constants:** $R_s \approx 0.45$–$0.55$, $R_{\text{MAP}} = 1.0$, $R_d \approx 0.70$–$0.85$.
- [ ] **BP Monitor — Accuracy Standard:** **ISO 81060-2** (clinical accuracy ≤ 5 mmHg mean error).
- [ ] **ECG Machine — Key Specs:** Bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz, CMRR ≥ 90 dB, sampling ≥ 500 Hz.
- [ ] **ECG Standard:** **ANSI/AAMI EC11** (US) and **IEC 60601-2-25** (international).
- [ ] **SNR Formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{signal}}/V_{\text{noise}})$.
- [ ] **CMRR Formula:** $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$.
- [ ] **MAP Formula:** $\text{MAP} = P_d + \frac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)$.
- [ ] **Heart Rate Formula:** $\text{HR} = 60 / \Delta t_{R\text{-}R}$.
- [ ] **Always comment on clinical plausibility** of calculated BP values in exam answers.
- [ ] **Always include units** (mmHg, bpm, Hz, dB, μV) in the final numerical answer.
- [ ] **Block diagrams in exams** should be simple, labeled, and use clear arrows (refer to Section 4).
- [ ] **AQL = 0.65** is the standard sampling plan for medical devices (ISO 2859-1).
- [ ] **Process Capability $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the industry-accepted threshold for medical device manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical Device Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medical Device Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Medical Device Classification** is the systematic, risk-based categorization of medical devices into regulatory classes based on the level of risk they pose to the patient's health, the user, and the environment. The classification dictates the regulatory pathway, conformity assessment procedures, quality system requirements, and post-market surveillance obligations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A medical device is defined (per Global Harmonization Task Force - GHTF / International Medical Device Regulators Forum - IMDRF) as: *"Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, software, implant, reagent, material or other article, whether used alone or in combination, including the software intended by its manufacturer to be used specifically for diagnostic and/or therapeutic purposes and necessary for its proper application, intended by the manufacturer to be used on human beings."*

The principal regulatory frameworks governing classification are:
1. **United States FDA** – 21 CFR Part 860 (Class I, II, III)
2. **European Union MDR 2017/745** – Annex VIII (Class I, IIa, IIb, III)
3. **GHTF/IMDRF** – Harmonized four-class system
4. **India - CDSCO** under Medical Device Rules 2017
5. **ISO 14971** – Risk management process that *informs* classification

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **road safety system** for vehicles:
- A **bicycle** (low risk) is like **Class I** devices — a simple bell and a helmet are enough control.
- A **motorcycle** (moderate risk) is like **Class IIa/IIb** devices — needs a license, registration, mirrors, indicators.
- A **commercial airliner** (high risk) is like **Class III** devices — needs pilot certification, airworthiness certificates, black boxes, continuous monitoring, and crash investigators.

In each case, the **higher the potential to cause harm**, the **more paperwork, testing, and regulatory scrutiny** the device must pass before it can be sold. That mapping between harm-potential and regulatory control is exactly what *medical device classification* is about.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** *Low risk = less regulation, High risk = more regulation.* The classification is **not** about whether the device is "important" — a tongue depressor is vital but low-risk, while a heart valve is also vital and high-risk.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Regulatory Metrics

The risk magnitude of a device is quantified using parameters such as:
- **Duration of contact** (transient < 60 min, short-term 60 min – 30 days, long-term > 30 days)
- **Invasiveness** (non-invasive, body orifice, surgically invasive, implantable)
- **Anatomical location** (skin, mucosa, blood, central nervous system, heart)
- **Energy source** (electrical, mechanical, thermal, ionizing radiation)
- **Biological effect** (local vs. systemic, reversible vs. irreversible)

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, remember: **Invasiveness + Duration + Body System = Risk Level**. This is the holy trinity of classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Risk-Based Classification Logic

Medical device classification follows a **top-down decision tree** approach. Instead of asking "what is this device?", the regulator asks a sequence of *risk-evaluation questions*:

### Step-by-Step Classification Logic

1. **Step 1 — Is it a medical device?**
   - Verify the definition per the relevant jurisdiction.
   - If *no* → exits regulatory scope.
   - If *yes* → proceed to Step 2.

2. **Step 2 — Is it active (energy-dependent) or non-active?**
   - *Active device* = requires power source (electrical, pneumatic).
   - Branches the EU MDR Annex VIII rules into different rule sets.

3. **Step 3 — Apply the 22 Rules of EU MDR Annex VIII** (or 16 Panels of GHTF).
   - Each rule has a specific scope, classification outcome, and rationale.
   - Multiple rules may apply — **the strictest rule wins**.

4. **Step 4 — Assess duration of use.**
   - Transient (< 60 min)
   - Short-term (≤ 30 days)
   - Long-term (> 30 days)

5. **Step 5 — Assess invasiveness and anatomical contact.**
   - Non-invasive → body orifice → surgically invasive → implantable.
   - Special note: **Central circulatory system** and **central nervous system** contacts elevate classification.

6. **Step 6 — Assign final class.**
   - Combine results; identify controlling rule; assign the highest applicable class.

7. **Step 7 — Document the rationale.**
   - The manufacturer must maintain a **Classification Justification File** under **ISO 14971:2019** risk management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the "strictest rule" win?** Because regulatory conservatism is intentional. If a device could be argued to fall under two rules, the higher class ensures greater patient protection.

## 2.2 The Major Classification Systems — Comparative Study

### A. US FDA Classification (21 CFR Part 860)

The FDA classifies based on **~1,700 device "panels"** (e.g., cardiovascular, orthopedic), but they map to three risk classes:

| FDA Class | Risk Level | Examples | Regulatory Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Elastic bandages, stethoscopes, surgical gloves | General Controls (registration, listing, GMP) |
| **Class II** | Moderate | Infusion pumps, surgical sutures, blood pressure monitors | General Controls **+ Special Controls** (510(k), performance standards) |
| **Class III** | High | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, coronary stents | General Controls **+ Premarket Approval (PMA)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The FDA pathway for Class II is the **510(k) Premarket Notification** — manufacturer must demonstrate *substantial equivalence* to a legally marketed predicate device. For Class III, **PMA** is required — independent safety and effectiveness data.

### B. EU MDR 2017/745 Classification (Annex VIII)

The EU system has **four classes** driven by 22 rules in Annex VIII:

| EU Class | Risk Level | Examples | Conformity Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Reading glasses, stethoscopes, wheelchairs | Self-declaration (CE mark) |
| **Class IIa** | Low-Moderate | Hearing aids, infusion pumps, contact lens solutions | Notified Body involvement (limited) |
| **Class IIb** | Moderate-High | Ventilators, X-ray equipment, surgical lasers | Notified Body involvement (full) |
| **Class III** | High | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, drug-eluting stents | Notified Body involvement + Design Examination + Consultation procedures |

### C. GHTF/IMDRF Four-Class System (Harmonized)

| Class | Risk Level | Regulatory Burden |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Low | Manufacturer self-declaration |
| **B** | Low-Moderate | Conformity assessment by authorized body |
| **C** | Moderate-High | Mandatory third-party audit |
| **D** | High | Most stringent controls, individual device scrutiny |

> [!NOTE]
> The GHTF system is the **foundation** from which the EU MDR evolved. Many emerging regulatory bodies (ASEAN, Saudi FDA, Brazil ANVISA) adopt the GHTF four-class model.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Key Equation / Rule | Boundary Conditions | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Duration categories | Transient: $T < 60$ min | Lower bound: $T > 0$ | minutes |
| 2 | Duration categories | Short-term: $60 \le T \le 43200$ min | 1 hour ≤ T ≤ 30 days | minutes |
| 3 | Duration categories | Long-term: $T > 43200$ min | T > 30 days | minutes |
| 4 | Risk Index (qualitative) | $R = P \times S$ | P = Probability of harm, S = Severity | dimensionless |
| 5 | Acceptable Risk Threshold | $R_{\text{acceptable}} = P_{\text{low}} \times S_{\text{high}}$ | Per ISO 14971:2019 | dimensionless |
| 6 | MDR 22 Rules — Rule 8 (implants) | $\text{Class} = f(\text{implant, duration, location})$ | Implants + long-term + surgery → Class IIb/III | — |
| 7 | MDR Rule 22 (active therapeutic) | Energy + Admin → Class IIa; Risk to patient → Class IIb; Direct life-support → Class III | Applies to active devices only | — |
| 8 | FDA 510(k) timeline | $t_{\text{review}} \approx 90$ days | Standard review, FDA goal | days |
| 9 | FDA PMA timeline | $t_{\text{review}} \approx 180$ days | Statutory minimum | days |
| 10 | Risk Severity Scale | Negligible $<$ Minor $<$ Serious $<$ Critical $<$ Catastrophic | Per ISO 14971 Annex C | categorical |
| 11 | Probability Scale | Frequent $>$ Probable $>$ Occasional $<$ Remote $<$ Improbable | Per ISO 14971 Annex D | categorical |
| 12 | Classification override | $\text{Class}_{\text{final}} = \max(\text{Class}_1, \text{Class}_2, \ldots, \text{Class}_n)$ | The strictest applicable rule wins | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The risk equation $R = P \times S$ (where $P$ = Probability and $S$ = Severity) is qualitative and categorical in regulatory practice. Do not try to compute numerical risk — instead, use **risk matrices** with categorical cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Designers**: Classification determines the design controls required (V&V, bench testing, biocompatibility, sterilization validation, software validation per IEC 62304).
- **Manufacturers**: Drives the cost and timeline — a Class III device can take **5–7 years** and **$50–100 million** to commercialize, while a Class I device can be launched in **6–12 months** at minimal cost.
- **Regulatory Affairs**: Determines the submission dossier type (CE Technical File, FDA 510(k), PMA, IMDRF STED).
- **Hospitals & Procurement**: Hospitals use classification to assess liability, training needs, and procurement risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Risk Matrix Logic Behind Classification

Medical device classification is fundamentally a **risk-stratification problem**. Let us derive the relationship between risk dimensions and final classification.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Risk Axes

A device is evaluated along two orthogonal axes:

- **Probability of Harm ($P$)**: 5-level categorical scale per ISO 14971.
  - Levels: Frequent, Probable, Occasional, Remote, Improbable.

- **Severity of Harm ($S$)**: 5-level categorical scale.
  - Levels: Negligible, Minor, Serious, Critical, Catastrophic.

### Step 2 — Quantify Risk per Cell

The risk for any device-use scenario is given by the categorical product:

$$
R = P \times S
$$

For simplicity, we assign **rank values** to each level:

$$
P_{\text{rank}} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \quad \text{(Improbable} \to \text{Frequent)}
$$

$$
S_{\text{rank}} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \quad \text{(Negligible} \to \text{Catastrophic)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{\text{rank}} = P_{\text{rank}} \times S_{\text{rank}}
$$

### Step 3 — Map Risk Rank to Regulatory Class

The resulting $R_{\text{rank}}$ value spans $1 \le R_{\text{rank}} \le 25$, and is then mapped to a regulatory class using threshold functions:

$$
\text{Class} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Class I (low risk)} & \text{if } R_{\text{rank}} \in [1, 3] \\
\text{Class IIa / FDA II (low-moderate)} & \text{if } R_{\text{rank}} \in [4, 9] \\
\text{Class IIb / FDA II (moderate-high)} & \text{if } R_{\text{rank}} \in [10, 15] \\
\text{Class III (high risk)} & \text{if } R_{\text{rank}} \in [16, 25]
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Time-Duration Multiplier

The contact duration modulates the risk rank by a duration factor $D$:

$$
D = 
\begin{cases}
1.0 & \text{if Transient } (T < 60 \text{ min}) \\
1.5 & \text{if Short-term } (60 \text{ min} \le T \le 30 \text{ days}) \\
2.0 & \text{if Long-term } (T > 30 \text{ days})
\end{cases}
$$

The **adjusted risk** becomes:

$$
R_{\text{adjusted}} = P_{\text{rank}} \times S_{\text{rank}} \times D
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** A device that touches the patient briefly is far less risky than the same device left in place for months. The duration factor encodes this physiological reality into the classification algorithm.

### Step 5 — Re-map to Class

$$
\text{Final Class} = f(R_{\text{adjusted}}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Energy})
$$

Where the function $f$ reflects the **22 Rules of EU MDR Annex VIII**. For brevity, we can express it as:

$$
f(R_{\text{adj}}) = \arg\max_{c \in \{I, IIa, IIb, III\}} \; g(c, R_{\text{adj}})
$$

with $g$ being the regulator's classification lookup table.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Classifying a Blood Glucose Meter

**Device:** Portable home-use blood glucose meter (Class IIa device per EU MDR).

**Step 1 — Probability of harm:** Improbable when used correctly → $P_{\text{rank}} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Severity if harm occurs:** Could lead to incorrect insulin dosing → Serious → $S_{\text{rank}} = 3$.

**Step 3 — Compute raw risk:**

$$
R_{\text{rank}} = 1 \times 3 = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Duration factor:** Transient contact (a few seconds per test) → $D = 1.0$.

**Step 5 — Adjusted risk:**

$$
R_{\text{adjusted}} = 3 \times 1.0 = 3
$$

**Step 6 — Initial mapping:** $R_{\text{adjusted}} = 3$ falls in $[1, 3]$ → Class I candidate.

**Step 7 — Apply MDR Rule 3 (devices modifying biological composition):** Blood glucose meters measure a biological parameter used in treatment decisions — they do not modify composition.

**Step 8 — Apply MDR Rule 10 (active devices for diagnosis):** Active device used to **monitor** physiological processes — Class IIa.

**Step 9 — Strictest rule wins:** Class IIa is the controlling classification. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Invasiveness × Duration × Class

| Invasiveness | Duration | Examples | EU MDR Class | FDA Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Non-invasive | Transient | Stethoscope | I | I |
| Non-invasive | Short-term | TENS unit | IIa | II |
| Non-invasive | Long-term | External insulin pump | IIa | II |
| Body orifice | Short-term | Thermometer | IIa | I/II |
| Surgically invasive | Transient | Surgical scalpel | I | I |
| Surgically invasive | Short-term | Surgical clip | IIa | II |
| Surgically invasive | Long-term | Hip prosthesis | IIb | II/III |
| Implantable | Long-term | Cochlear implant | III | III |
| Central circulatory contact | Any | Coronary stent | III | III |
| CNS contact | Any | Brain implant | III | III |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Decision Flow (EU MDR Annex VIII)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Identification] --> B{Non-active or Active?}
    B -->|Non-active| C[Apply Rules 1-4]
    B -->|Active| D[Apply Rules 5-9]
    C --> E{Invasive?}
    D --> F{Therapeutic or Diagnostic?}
    E -->|No| G[Class I]
    E -->|Body Orifice| H{Duration?}
    E -->|Surgically Invasive| I[Rules 6,7]
    E -->|Implantable| J[Rules 5,8]
    H -->|Transient| K[Class I]
    H -->|Short-term| L[Class IIa]
    H -->|Long-term| M[Class IIb]
    I --> N[Class IIa or IIb]
    J --> O[Class IIb or III]
    F -->|Therapeutic| P{Risk to Patient?}
    F -->|Diagnostic| Q[Intended Purpose?]
    P -->|Moderate| R[Class IIa]
    P -->|Significant| S[Class IIb]
    P -->|Life-supporting| T[Class III]
    Q --> U[Class IIa or IIb]
    K --> V[Apply Strictest Rule]
    L --> V
    M --> V
    N --> V
    O --> V
    G --> V
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Document Justification per ISO 14971]
    W --> X[Select Conformity Assessment Route]
```

## 4.2 FDA vs. EU MDR Classification Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph US_FDA [US FDA System]
        F1[Class I - General Controls] --> F2[Class II - Special Controls 510 k]
        F2 --> F3[Class III - Premarket Approval]
    end
    
    subgraph EU_MDR [EU MDR System]
        E1[Class I - Self Declaration] --> E2[Class IIa - Limited Notified Body]
        E2 --> E3[Class IIb - Full Notified Body]
        E3 --> E4[Class III - Strictest Controls]
    end
    
    F1 -.Similar to.-> E1
    F2 -.Maps to.-> E2
    F2 -.Maps to.-> E3
    F3 -.Maps to.-> E4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The FDA has **3 classes**, the EU MDR has **4 classes**. There is no one-to-one mapping. For example, an FDA Class II device may map to either EU Class IIa or IIb depending on the specific rule applied.

## 4.3 Risk-Based Architecture Flow for Medical Device Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Medical Device Concept] --> Identify[Step 1: Identify Intended Use and Risk Factors]
    Identify --> Classify[Step 2: Apply Classification Rules]
    Classify --> RiskFile[Step 3: Generate Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    RiskFile --> DesignCtl[Step 4: Implement Design Controls]
    DesignCtl --> Testing[Step 5: Perform V&V Testing]
    Testing --> Submit[Step 6: Submit to Regulatory Body]
    Submit --> Approval{Market Approval?}
    Approval -->|No| Iterate[Iterate on Design and Testing]
    Iterate --> Testing
    Approval -->|Yes| Launch[Step 7: Market Launch]
    Launch --> PMS[Step 8: Post-Market Surveillance]
    PMS --> Vigilance[Adverse Event Reporting]
    Vigilance --> Classify
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Medical Device Classification and state its regulatory purpose. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Medical Device Classification is the systematic categorization of medical devices into distinct regulatory classes based on the level of risk they pose to patients, users, and the environment. The classification drives the regulatory pathway including quality system requirements, conformity assessment procedures, and post-market surveillance obligations. Its purpose is to ensure that the level of regulatory control is proportional to the potential risk, thereby protecting public health while allowing innovation in low-risk categories with minimal regulatory burden.

---

### Question 2: List the four classes under the EU MDR 2017/745 framework with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Class | Risk Level | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Class I | Low | Stethoscope |
| Class IIa | Low-Moderate | Hearing aid |
| Class IIb | Moderate-High | Ventilator |
| Class III | High | Heart valve |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): Explain the FDA Three-Class System and EU MDR Four-Class System. Compare and contrast the two frameworks. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) FDA Three-Class System [7 Marks]

The US FDA classifies medical devices under **21 CFR Part 860** into three classes based on risk:

- **Class I — Low Risk:** Devices like elastic bandages and stethoscopes. Subject only to **General Controls** (registration, listing, GMP, basic labeling).
- **Class II — Moderate Risk:** Devices like infusion pumps and blood pressure monitors. Subject to General Controls **plus Special Controls** including **510(k) Premarket Notification** requiring substantial equivalence to a predicate device.
- **Class III — High Risk:** Devices like heart valves and pacemakers. Subject to General Controls **plus Premarket Approval (PMA)** with independent clinical evidence of safety and effectiveness.

FDA panel-based classification spans approximately 1,700 generic device types across 16 medical specialty panels (cardiovascular, orthopedic, etc.).

#### (b) EU MDR Four-Class System [7 Marks]

The **EU MDR 2017/745 Annex VIII** classifies devices into four classes driven by 22 rules:

- **Class I:** Low risk. Self-declared CE marking. Example: surgical instruments.
- **Class IIa:** Low-to-moderate risk. Notified Body assessment of certain aspects. Example: hearing aids.
- **Class IIb:** Moderate-to-high risk. Full Notified Body involvement in design and production. Example: ventilators.
- **Class III:** Highest risk. Strictest controls including Design Examination Certificate. Example: drug-eluting stents.

#### (c) Comparison

| Aspect | US FDA | EU MDR |
|---|---|---|
| Number of classes | 3 | 4 |
| Legal basis | 21 CFR | MDR 2017/745 |
| Classification logic | Panel-based (1,700 types) | 22 Rules (rule-based) |
| Pathway | 510(k) or PMA | CE marking with Notified Body |
| Time horizon | 1938+ | 2017 onwards |

The EU MDR is generally considered more conservative and risk-averse, while the FDA pathway (especially 510(k)) can sometimes allow faster market entry for moderate-risk devices.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): With suitable examples, describe the risk-based approach to medical device classification. Explain the role of ISO 14971 in this process. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Risk-Based Classification Concept [7 Marks]

Medical device classification is fundamentally risk-based. Three core parameters drive classification:

1. **Invasiveness:** Non-invasive (lowest) → body orifice → surgically invasive → implantable (highest).
2. **Duration of Contact:** Transient (< 60 min) → Short-term (≤ 30 days) → Long-term (> 30 days).
3. **Anatomical Site:** Skin (lowest) → Mucosa → Blood → Central circulatory/CNS (highest).

**Example 1:** A **tongue depressor** is non-invasive, transient → Class I.
**Example 2:** A **pacemaker lead** is invasive, long-term, central circulatory contact → Class III.
**Example 3:** A **ventilator** is active, energy-delivering, life-supporting → Class IIb or III.

The risk matrix approach uses Probability × Severity to assign risk rank $R = P \times S$, which maps to a regulatory class.

#### (b) Role of ISO 14971:2019 [7 Marks]

**ISO 14971** is the international standard for **Risk Management Applied to Medical Devices**. Its role in classification includes:

1. **Risk Identification:** Systematically identifying hazards and hazardous situations associated with a device.
2. **Risk Estimation:** Estimating probability of harm and severity of harm for each hazard.
3. **Risk Evaluation:** Comparing estimated risk against acceptability criteria defined in the **Risk Management Plan**.
4. **Risk Control:** Implementing measures to reduce risk to acceptable levels (inherent safe design, protective measures, information for safety).
5. **Residual Risk Evaluation:** Determining the final class based on residual risk after controls.
6. **Risk Management File:** Mandatory documented evidence supporting the classification decision.
7. **Post-Production Information:** Continuous risk reassessment based on field data.

ISO 14971 acts as the **methodological backbone** for any classification decision. Even when a device fits a specific EU MDR rule, the manufacturer must demonstrate that the classification is consistent with the **benefit-risk analysis** performed per ISO 14971.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Points in Medical Device Classification Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing FDA 3-class with EU 4-class** — Examiners *will* deduct marks if you conflate them. Always state the jurisdiction clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the duration factor** — Many students classify purely on invasiveness but ignore duration. Remember: the *same* implant has different classes at different durations in some rule sets.
> 3. **Skipping the "strictest rule wins" rule** — When multiple EU MDR rules apply, the higher class governs. Students often pick the *first* applicable rule.
> 4. **No mention of ISO 14971** — Any classification question in KTU should reference ISO 14971 risk management; missing this loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Vague examples** — Use *specific* device names (e.g., "drug-eluting stent," "TENS unit") rather than generic terms ("implant," "stimulator").
> 6. **No Notified Body mention** for EU — Students often write "CE mark" without specifying the Notified Body's role for Class IIa, IIb, III devices.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Medical Device Classification**

- ⭐ Classification is **risk-based**, not importance-based. A tongue depressor is vital but Class I.
- ⭐ The **3 + 1 rule**: 3 FDA classes (I, II, III) + 4 EU MDR classes (I, IIa, IIb, III).
- ⭐ The **3 drivers of classification**: Invasiveness + Duration + Anatomical site.
- ⭐ The **22 Rules of EU MDR Annex VIII** are the operational classification engine.
- ⭐ **Strictest rule wins** when multiple rules apply to a single device.
- ⭐ FDA Class II = **510(k) Premarket Notification**; FDA Class III = **Premarket Approval (PMA)**.
- ⭐ EU Class I = **self-declaration**; EU Class IIa/IIb/III = **Notified Body involvement**.
- ⭐ **ISO 14971:2019** is the universal risk management standard supporting every classification.
- ⭐ **Active devices** (energy-dependent) follow a different rule subset (Rules 9–13 in EU MDR).
- ⭐ **Central nervous system** and **central circulatory system** contact devices are always Class III.
- ⭐ Implantable + long-term = at least Class IIb, frequently Class III.
- ⭐ **Conformity assessment** complexity scales with class — Class I is cheapest, Class III is most expensive.
- ⭐ **GHTF/IMDRF** is the international harmonization body; emerging markets adopt its A–B–C–D model.
- ⭐ **Post-market surveillance** is mandatory for all classes, but Class III requires the most rigorous vigilance (PSUR, Periodic Safety Update Reports).
- ⭐ For KTU, always pair classification discussion with a **concrete example** and reference to **ISO 14971**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and System FMEA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design and System FMEA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is FMEA?

**Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive analytical methodology used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a product, process, or system, along with their effects on operations, and to document the actions taken to mitigate or eliminate those failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *FMEA is a structured, proactive, step-by-step risk-assessment technique used during the design and development phase of a biomedical device to identify where and how a device might fail, the relative impact of different failures, and to prioritize remediation actions based on a quantitative Risk Priority Number (RPN).*

In the context of **biomedical device design**, FMEA is mandated under **ISO 14971:2019** (Application of risk management to medical devices) and is a cornerstone deliverable in the **Design History File (DHF)** required by the **US FDA 21 CFR 820.30 (Design Controls)**.

---

## 1.2 Design FMEA (DFMEA) — Definition

**Design FMEA (DFMEA)** is performed during the *product design phase* to evaluate the failure modes introduced by the *design itself*, before the product is released to manufacturing.

- **Focus:** Engineering design decisions (tolerances, materials, geometry, circuit selection, software logic).
- **When:** Concept stage through detailed design stage.
- **Customer:** End-user (patient, clinician) and downstream manufacturing.
- **Scope (per the KTU 2024 PEBRT415 syllabus):** Subsystem and component-level failure analysis of the device under design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope of DFMEA in Biomedical Devices:**
> A DFMEA for a device like an **infusion pump** would analyze failure modes such as *motor stall, sensor drift, software timing error, battery degradation,* and *catheter kinking* — failures stemming from the **design itself**.

---

## 1.3 System FMEA (SFMEA) — Definition

**System FMEA (SFMEA)** is performed at the *overall system architecture level* to evaluate failure modes arising from the **interaction between subsystems**, including hardware–software interfaces, user–device interfaces, and the device's interaction with its environment (patient, hospital, network).

- **Focus:** System-level integration, interfaces, and interactions.
- **When:** System architecture definition phase and post-integration.
- **Customer:** The system as a whole (and its stakeholders).
- **Scope (per the KTU 2024 PEBRT415 syllabus):** System-level risk analysis focusing on interactions among subsystems and external factors.

---

## 1.4 DFMEA vs SFMEA — The Key Distinction

| Parameter | Design FMEA (DFMEA) | System FMEA (SFMEA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Subsystem / Component | Whole System / Architecture |
| **Subject of analysis** | A specific component or sub-assembly | The complete device and its interfaces |
| **Failure source** | Design weakness of a part | System integration & interaction |
| **Time horizon** | During detailed design | During system definition & integration |
| **Granularity** | Fine (e.g., a single op-amp, a weld joint) | Coarse (e.g., power subsystem ↔ control subsystem) |
| **Primary standard** | AIAG-VDA DFMEA Handbook (2019) | MIL-STD-1629A / IEC 60812 |

---

## 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body as a System":**
> Imagine a hospital as a *biomedical system*:
> - **System FMEA** = Analyzing how the *Cardiology Department* (subsystem A) fails when the *Blood Bank* (subsystem B) runs out of stock. The failure is not in either department individually but in their **interface**.
> - **Design FMEA** = Analyzing how a *stethoscope's diaphragm* (component) cracks under thermal shock. The failure is purely in the **design of the part**.
>
> Just as a doctor examines both the *organ* (DFMEA) and the *organ system* (SFMEA), a biomedical engineer must perform both.

---

## 1.6 Key Standardized Metrics (Syllabus Highlights)

- **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** A dimensionless integer product, **RPN = S × O × D**.
- **Severity (S):** Integer on a **1–10** scale.
- **Occurrence (O):** Integer on a **1–10** scale.
- **Detectability (D):** Integer on a **1–10** scale (Note: higher = harder to detect).
- **Action Priority (AP):** Categorical — **High / Medium / Low** (per AIAG-VDA 2019).
- **Industry threshold:** **RPN ≥ 100** typically triggers mandatory corrective action.

---

## 1.7 When to Use Which FMEA (Decision Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Decision Rule:**
> 1. If the failure is contained **within a single component's design** → use **DFMEA**.
> 2. If the failure emerges from the **interface or interaction** between two or more subsystems → use **SFMEA**.
> 3. If both are present (which is the norm) → perform **SFMEA first** to scope the system, then drill down with **DFMEA** on each high-risk subsystem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FMEA Process — Step-by-Step (The "7-Step Methodology")

The classic FMEA process (per **IEC 60812** and **AIAG-VDA 2019 Handbook**) is a structured sequence. Every KTU question on FMEA expects students to either describe these steps or apply them.

### Step 1 — Define the Scope and Boundaries
- Identify the system, subsystem, or component under analysis.
- Define the operating conditions, patient population, use environment.
- Reference: **Item / Function boundary** clearly drawn in a **Block Boundary Diagram (BBD)** or **P-diagram**.

### Step 2 — Identify Functions
- For each element, list its **intended function(s)** and **performance specifications**.
- Example: *"The SpO₂ sensor shall measure blood oxygen saturation in the range 70–100% with ±2% accuracy."*

### Step 3 — Identify Failure Modes
- For each function, determine **how it could fail**.
- A *failure mode* is the *manner* in which a function is lost.
- Example: *"The SpO₂ sensor produces a reading of 100% when actual SpO₂ is 85%."* (Stuck-high output)

### Step 4 — Identify Effects and Determine Severity (S)
- **Effect** = the consequence of the failure on the *system, patient, or operator*.
- Assign a **Severity rating (S)** from the standard **1–10 scale**.

### Step 5 — Identify Causes and Determine Occurrence (O)
- **Cause** = the *root mechanism* that triggers the failure mode.
- Assign an **Occurrence rating (O)** based on historical failure rate data (FMEA database, field returns, literature).

### Step 6 — Identify Current Controls and Determine Detection (D)
- **Current Control** = existing design verification, validation, in-process inspection, or post-market surveillance method.
- Assign a **Detection rating (D)** based on the *probability that the control will detect the failure before the device reaches the patient*.

### Step 7 — Calculate RPN, Prioritize, and Take Action
- Compute **RPN = S × O × D**.
- Items with **RPN ≥ 100** (or AP = High) → **must be mitigated**.
- Re-evaluate **RPN after action** to confirm reduction.

---

## 2.2 The Three Rating Scales (Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield — These three tables appear in nearly every FMEA question.**

### 2.2.1 Severity Scale (S)

| Rating | Effect on Patient / System | KTU Keyword |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | No effect | Negligible |
| 2–3 | Minor inconvenience, no injury | Minor |
| 4–6 | Moderate — requires non-medical intervention | Moderate |
| 7–8 | High — injury, requires medical intervention | Major |
| 9 | Hazardous — serious injury, possible life-threatening | Critical |
| **10** | **Catastrophic — death or irreversible harm** | **Catastrophic** |

### 2.2.2 Occurrence Scale (O)

| Rating | Failure Probability | Approx. Failure Rate (per million) |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Extremely unlikely | ≤ 0.01 |
| 2–3 | Low | 0.1 – 1 |
| 4–6 | Moderate | 10 – 100 |
| 7–8 | High | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| 9–10 | Very high / Almost certain | ≥ 100,000 |

### 2.2.3 Detection Scale (D)

| Rating | Detection Probability | KTU Keyword |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Almost certain — automated 100% detection | Certain |
| 2–3 | High — robust design verification | High |
| 4–6 | Moderate — sampling or routine test | Moderate |
| 7–8 | Low — manual inspection only | Low |
| 9–10 | Almost impossible — no current control | Impossible |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** A *low* D number means *easy* detection. A *high* D number means *hard* detection. Students frequently reverse this. The **D scale is inversely oriented** relative to intuition.

---

## 2.3 The Risk Priority Number (RPN) — KTU Formula Sheet

The fundamental equation driving FMEA decision-making is:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity (integer 1–10)
- **O** = Occurrence (integer 1–10)
- **D** = Detection (integer 1–10)
- **RPN** ∈ [1, 1000] theoretically; practically capped at 1000 only if all three are 10.

### High-Yield Formula Table

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Range | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Priority Number** | $\text{RPN} = S \cdot O \cdot D$ | 1 to 1000 | Prioritize failure modes |
| **Maximum RPN** | $\text{RPN}_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10$ | 1000 | Worst-case reference |
| **Minimum RPN** | $\text{RPN}_{\min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1$ | 1 | Ideal case |
| **RPN Reduction Ratio** | $\Delta\text{RPN}\,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | 0 to 100% | Measure effectiveness of action |
| **Severity-only Rule** | If $S \geq 9$, action is mandatory **regardless** of RPN | — | Safety-critical override |
| **Action Priority (AP)** | Tabulated lookup from S, O, D matrix | H / M / L | AIAG-VDA 2019 method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Note (AIAG-VDA 2019):** The RPN is increasingly being **supplemented or replaced** by the categorical **Action Priority (AP)** — High, Medium, Low. KTU examiners still expect RPN in numerical problems but may award extra marks for mentioning AP.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

| Application Area | How FMEA is Used |
|---|---|
| **FDA 510(k) Submission** | FMEA is a primary risk-analysis document attached to the submission. |
| **Implantable devices** (pacemakers, stents) | SFMEA identifies systemic failure modes; DFMEA examines electrode fatigue, polymer degradation. |
| **Electromedical equipment** (defibrillators, ventilators) | Identifies single-fault conditions per **IEC 60601-1**. |
| **In-vitro diagnostics** | Detects reagent contamination, calibration drift failure modes. |
| **Hospital risk management** | Used in Health Technology Assessment (HTA) for procurement decisions. |
| **Post-market surveillance** | Updated continuously using field-return data to re-rank failure modes. |

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering "Why" |
|---|---|
| Define scope | Prevents scope-creep; FMEA must be **bounded** to be tractable. |
| Identify functions | You cannot fail to do something you never intended to do. |
| Identify failure modes | A function can fail in *many* ways — each must be enumerated. |
| Identify effects | Effects drive **patient harm**, which drives severity. |
| Identify causes | Causes drive **occurrence**. Same failure mode can have many causes. |
| Identify controls | Controls drive **detection**. Without a control, D = 10. |
| Calculate RPN | Provides a **single comparable metric** across heterogeneous failures. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — FMEA of a Battery-Powered Infusion Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
> A team is designing a **portable insulin infusion pump**. The pump uses a DC motor driving a lead-screw to advance a syringe plunger. The system includes a microcontroller, an LCD display, a force sensor, and a Li-ion battery. Perform a System FMEA on the device, identifying 4 failure modes and computing their RPNs.

---

### Step 1 — System Boundary Definition

The **System FMEA** boundary includes:
- **Inputs:** Battery power, sensor signals, keypad commands, insulin cartridge.
- **Outputs:** Mechanical displacement, LCD display, audio alarm.
- **External interfaces:** Patient's body (subcutaneous tissue), clinician (programming), hospital network (data upload).

### Step 2 — Functional Block Listing

| Block ID | Function |
|:---:|---|
| F1 | Regulate insulin delivery at programmed rate (0.5 – 5.0 units/hr) |
| F2 | Detect occlusion in infusion line |
| F3 | Display current delivery status |
| F4 | Sound alarm on fault condition |
| F5 | Maintain battery charge for ≥ 24 hours |

### Step 3 — Failure Mode, Effects, and Causes (4-row FMEA table)

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D | RPN |
|:-:|---|---|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| FM1 | Motor stalls (no plunger motion) | No insulin delivery → hyperglycemia | Worn brushes, gear seizure, motor driver IC failure | 9 | 4 | 3 | 108 |
| FM2 | Force sensor gives false-low reading (occlusion missed) | Occlusion undetected → pump continues forcing → line rupture | Sensor calibration drift, cracked strain gauge | 8 | 5 | 6 | 240 |
| FM3 | LCD shows "Delivery OK" when motor has stalled | Clinician misled → missed dose | Display driver software bug, data bus error | 9 | 3 | 7 | 189 |
| FM4 | Battery dies earlier than 24 hours (capacity fade) | Therapy interruption | Aging cells, over-discharge, high ambient temp | 7 | 4 | 4 | 112 |

### Step 4 — Detailed RPN Calculation Walk-Through

#### RPN for FM1 (Motor stalls):

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM1}} = S \times O \times D
$$

Substituting the ratings:

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM1}} = 9 \times 4 \times 3
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM1}} = 36 \times 3 = 108
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN 108 exceeds the **100 threshold**, action required.

---

#### RPN for FM2 (Force sensor false-low):

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM2}} = S \times O \times D
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM2}} = 8 \times 5 \times 6
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
8 \times 5 = 40
$$

$$
40 \times 6 = 240
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN 240 — **highest priority**, action required.

---

#### RPN for FM3 (Display false-OK):

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM3}} = 9 \times 3 \times 7
$$

$$
9 \times 3 = 27
$$

$$
27 \times 7 = 189
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN 189 — second highest.

---

#### RPN for FM4 (Battery early failure):

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM4}} = 7 \times 4 \times 4
$$

$$
7 \times 4 = 28
$$

$$
28 \times 4 = 112
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN 112 — just above threshold, action required.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Prioritization of Failure Modes

After calculating all RPNs, sort in **descending order** to identify the *action queue*:

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Action Required? |
|:-:|---|:-:|---|
| 1 | FM2 — Force sensor false-low | 240 | **Yes — High priority** |
| 2 | FM3 — Display false-OK | 189 | Yes — High priority |
| 3 | FM4 — Battery early failure | 112 | Yes — Moderate priority |
| 4 | FM1 — Motor stalls | 108 | Yes — Moderate priority |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Insight:** Notice that **FM1 has the highest severity (S = 9)** but is ranked 4th because the existing rotation-sensor control gives it a low **D = 3**. FMEA correctly identifies that **detectability is just as critical as severity**.

---

## 3.3 Post-Action Re-Evaluation (Proof of Effectiveness)

Suppose an **action** is taken on FM2 — adding a *redundant pressure sensor* and a *software cross-check algorithm*. The new ratings are:

| Parameter | Before | After | Reason |
|---|:-:|:-:|---|
| S | 8 | 8 | Severity is unchanged by better detection |
| O | 5 | 5 | Occurrence is unchanged by better detection |
| D | 6 | 2 | New cross-check algorithm makes detection almost certain |

**RPN Reduction Calculation:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} = 8 \times 5 \times 6 = 240
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80
$$

Compute the **percentage reduction**:

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{240 - 80}{240} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{160}{240} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = 0.6667 \times 100 = 66.67\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The corrective action reduces the risk priority by **66.67%**, bringing RPN from 240 to a safely acceptable 80.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Engineering Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):**
> A corrective action is considered **effective** if it achieves:
> 1. **RPN after action < 100**, AND
> 2. **Reduction ratio ≥ 30%**, AND
> 3. For severity-9 or -10 items, **severity is not increased** by the new design.

---

## 3.4 Severity-Override Rule — The Hidden Pitfall

Some FMEA programs include a **severity override**: if **S = 9 or 10**, the failure mode is *unconditionally* a top-priority item, regardless of RPN. This is to prevent teams from *compensating* for catastrophic risk with high detectability.

For example:

> If a defibrillator had a failure mode *"delivers 360 J when 200 J is selected"* with **S = 10**, **O = 1**, **D = 2**, then:
>
> $$
> \text{RPN} = 10 \times 1 \times 2 = 20
> $$

> The RPN looks "low", but the **severity override** flags this as a **non-negotiable** priority because of the catastrophic potential.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1 : Define Scope and System Boundary"] --> B["STEP 2 : Identify Functions of Each Element"]
    B --> C["STEP 3 : Identify Failure Modes per Function"]
    C --> D["STEP 4 : Determine Effects and Assign Severity S"]
    D --> E["STEP 5 : Determine Causes and Assign Occurrence O"]
    E --> F["STEP 6 : Identify Controls and Assign Detection D"]
    F --> G["STEP 7 : Compute RPN equals S times O times D"]
    G --> H{"RPN Greater Than 100 OR Severity Greater Than 8"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Recommend Corrective Action and Reassign Ratings"]
    I --> J["STEP 8 : Recompute RPN After Action"]
    J --> K{"RPN After Less Than 100 AND S Unchanged"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Action Accepted and Documented"]
    K -- No --> I
    H -- No --> M["Acceptable Risk Level Achieved"]
    L --> N["End of FMEA Cycle"]
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The diagram enforces a **closed-loop** discipline — every high-RPN item must loop back through **Step 8** (re-evaluation) until the residual risk is acceptable. KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who explicitly mention this **iterative** nature.

---

## 4.2 DFMEA vs SFMEA — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["FMEA Trigger Event Identified"] --> Q1{"Is the failure contained within ONE component or subassembly?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> DFMEA["Perform Design FMEA DFMEA on that component"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Does the failure arise from INTERACTION between subsystems interfaces or environment?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SFMEA["Perform System FMEA SFMEA on the integrated system"]
    Q2 -- No --> Hybrid["Perform both DFMEA and SFMEA in parallel and cross reference"]
    DFMEA --> End["Document in DHF per ISO 14971"]
    SFMEA --> End
    Hybrid --> End
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Biomedical Device (for SFMEA Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS["Power Subsystem\nBattery and Regulator"] --> MCU["Control Subsystem\nMicrocontroller"]
    SE["Sensor Subsystem\nElectrodes and ADC"] --> MCU
    MCU --> AC["Actuator Subsystem\nMotor and Pump"]
    MCU --> UI["User Interface Subsystem\nLCD and Buzzer"]
    UI --> PT["Patient Interface\nSkin and Tissue"]
    AC --> PT
    MCU -. wireless link .-> CL["Clinician Interface\nTablet and Hospital Network"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **SFMEA focus on this architecture:** The dotted wireless link from the Control Subsystem to the Clinician Interface is a *high-risk* interface — data dropouts, latency, and cybersecurity failures all originate at this boundary. A pure DFMEA on the microcontroller chip would miss this.

---

## 4.4 The RPN Computation Logic (Detailed Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["Failure Mode Identified"] --> SEV["Step 4: Assign Severity S 1 to 10 based on patient harm"]
    SEV --> OCC["Step 5: Assign Occurrence O 1 to 10 based on failure rate"]
    OCC --> DET["Step 6: Assign Detection D 1 to 10 based on current control capability"]
    DET --> MUL["Compute RPN equals S times O times D"]
    MUL --> DEC{"Decision Gate"}
    DEC -- RPN Above 100 --> ACT["Trigger Corrective Action Plan"]
    DEC -- S Greater Than 8 --> ACT
    DEC -- RPN At or Below 100 AND S At or Below 8 --> ACC["Acceptable Residual Risk"]
    ACT --> NEWR["Reassign Ratings S new, O new, D new"]
    NEWR --> MUL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between Design FMEA and System FMEA. When would you prefer to use one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**

**Design FMEA (DFMEA)** is a component-level risk analysis that examines failure modes introduced by the design of a *specific* part, subassembly, or feature. It is performed during the detailed design phase and focuses on **how a single component can fail**. The customer is typically the downstream user or manufacturing process.

**System FMEA (SFMEA)** is a system-architecture-level risk analysis that examines failure modes arising from the **interactions between subsystems** (hardware, software, user, environment). It is performed at the system-definition phase.

**Decision rule:** Use **DFMEA** when the failure is contained within one component (e.g., a capacitor that fails open). Use **SFMEA** when the failure is an *interface* or *integration* problem (e.g., the user interface software misinterprets sensor data due to a timing race condition). In practice, both are performed — SFMEA scopes the system, and DFMEA drills into the high-risk components.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? State its mathematical expression and explain the limitation of relying solely on RPN.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a single composite metric used in FMEA to rank failure modes for corrective action. It is the product of three integer ratings:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where **S** = Severity, **O** = Occurrence, **D** = Detection (each on a 1–10 scale). A higher RPN indicates a more urgent need for corrective action.

**Limitations of relying solely on RPN:**
1. **Sensitivity to the product model** — Different teams may assign different S/O/D ratings to the same failure, producing different RPNs.
2. **Severity override ignored** — A failure with S = 10 and O = 1, D = 1 gives RPN = 10 (looks low) but is catastrophic; the product model hides this.
3. **RPN distribution skew** — Most failure modes cluster in the middle RPN band, making prioritization ambiguous.
4. **Modern alternative** — The AIAG-VDA 2019 FMEA Handbook recommends the **Action Priority (AP)** approach (High / Medium / Low) instead of pure RPN.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
> *ESE Module Question, 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the FMEA methodology with reference to biomedical device design. List the standard rating scales used in FMEA and state the formula for Risk Priority Number.*
>
