> **(b)** [7 Marks] *For a blood-glucose monitoring system (glucometer), identify any four potential failure modes at the system level and compute their RPNs. Suggest two corrective actions for the highest-RPN failure mode.*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**FMEA Methodology (5 marks):**

FMEA is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive risk-analysis method performed during the design phase. The **seven steps** are:

1. **Define the scope** — Identify the device, its boundary, operating conditions, and patient population.
2. **Identify functions** — List the intended functions and performance specifications of each element.
3. **Identify failure modes** — For each function, enumerate the *ways* it could fail (e.g., open circuit, short circuit, drift, lock-up, no output).
4. **Identify effects and assign Severity (S)** — Determine the consequence on the patient / user and rate 1–10.
5. **Identify causes and assign Occurrence (O)** — Determine the root mechanism and rate based on failure rate data, 1–10.
6. **Identify current controls and assign Detection (D)** — Rate the *probability that the existing control will detect the failure* before it reaches the patient, 1–10 (higher = harder to detect).
7. **Compute RPN and prioritize action** — RPN = S × O × D; items with RPN ≥ 100 or S ≥ 9 require action.

**Standard Rating Scales (1 mark):** S, O, D each on a 1–10 integer scale (tables from SECTION 2.2 above).

**RPN Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D, \quad S,O,D \in [1, 10]
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Four System-Level Failure Modes of a Glucometer:**

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D | RPN |
|:-:|---|---|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| FM1 | Strip connector gives false-low reading | Patient under-doses insulin → hyperglycemia | Corroded contacts, bent pins | 8 | 5 | 4 | 160 |
| FM2 | Display backlight fails | Patient cannot read result → wrong action | LED open circuit, driver failure | 5 | 3 | 2 | 30 |
| FM3 | Battery low not detected | Device switches off during use | Voltage divider drift, ADC error | 7 | 4 | 5 | 140 |
| FM4 | Temperature compensation fails | Reading inaccurate in cold weather | NTC sensor detach, firmware error | 8 | 4 | 6 | 192 |

**Sample Calculation for FM1:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM1}} = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160
$$

**Sample Calculation for FM4:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM4}} = 8 \times 4 \times 6 = 32 \times 6 = 192
$$

**Highest RPN:** FM4 (RPN = 192).

**Two Corrective Actions for FM4:**

1. **Design action:** Add a *redundant on-board temperature sensor* (independent of the strip sensor) and a *software cross-check*. This will reduce D from 6 to 2.
2. **Process action:** Add an *environmental test* in production (each unit tested at 10 °C and 40 °C) to detect NTC detach. This further reduces D.

**Re-evaluation after action (sample):**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 8 \times 4 \times 2 = 64 \quad (\text{Reduction} = 67\%)
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] *With a neat block diagram, describe the step-by-step procedure of conducting a System FMEA for a wearable cardiac monitor (Holter device).*
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] *Discuss the limitations of RPN. Explain the concept of Action Priority (AP) as introduced in the AIAG-VDA 2019 FMEA Handbook.*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** Refer to the SFMEA flow from SECTION 4.1 of these notes. The student should describe:

- **Inputs to the SFMEA:** ECG electrodes, battery, wireless link, patient activity.
- **System boundary:** Holter device + body surface interface + clinician app.
- **Functional blocks:** Acquisition, Processing, Storage, Wireless, Power.
- **Outputs:** Stored ECG, wireless transmission, alarm buzzer.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Define scope and interfaces** (electrodes, skin, Bluetooth link, app).
2. **List functions** (acquire ECG, denoise, store, transmit).
3. **Identify failure modes** (e.g., electrode-skin contact loss, ADC saturation, Bluetooth drop, clock drift).
4. **Identify effects** (missed arrhythmia, false alarm, lost data).
5. **Assign S, O, D** for each.
6. **Compute RPN**, prioritize.
7. **Recommend actions** (e.g., add redundant electrode, use redundant wireless link, add motion sensor).
8. **Re-evaluate** RPN after action and document in Design History File.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Limitations of RPN (4 marks):**

1. **Subjectivity in rating** — Different FMEA team members may assign different S, O, D values, making RPN unreliable for cross-team comparison.
2. **Equal weighting is mathematically incorrect** — Treating S, O, D as equal-weight multiplicative factors ignores the fact that severity is intrinsically more important than occurrence or detection.
3. **Ignores severity override** — A high-severity (S = 10) failure can appear to have a low RPN if O and D are small, leading teams to under-treat catastrophic risks.
4. **RPN distribution clustering** — Many failure modes fall in the 30–100 RPN band, leading to ambiguous prioritization.
5. **Single-number summary hides trade-offs** — Two failure modes with RPN = 100 may have very different S-O-D profiles (e.g., 10×1×10 vs 5×5×4) requiring different mitigations.

**Action Priority (AP) Concept (3 marks):**

The **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (1st edition, June 2019)** introduced the **Action Priority (AP)** approach as a replacement for raw RPN prioritization. The AP is a **categorical** rating — **High (H), Medium (M), or Low (L)** — determined by a lookup matrix of S, O, and D values. The key principles are:

- The AP **table** is built into the standard and is **deterministic** — no mathematical ambiguity.
- **Severity is weighted more heavily** — high severity always leads to high AP, regardless of O and D.
- **Detection gets a dedicated dimension** — many action categories are driven by poor detectability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Reversing the D scale** — A student writes *"D = 2 means hard to detect"*. This is **wrong**. D = 2 means *easy* to detect (low probability of escape). Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the severity override** — When S = 9 or 10, action is mandatory **regardless of RPN**. Omitting this in a 14-mark answer is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Design FMEA with System FMEA** — Using the terms interchangeably is a structural error that loses 1–2 marks in any FMEA question.
> 4. **Failing to recompute RPN after action** — Examiners specifically look for the **post-action re-evaluation** step; omitting it loses up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Using invented S/O/D values** — Students sometimes quote S = 12 or D = 0. The valid range is **1 to 10 inclusive** for each.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FMEA (Module 2):**

- [x] **FMEA =** Failure Mode and Effects Analysis — systematic, inductive, bottom-up risk method.
- [x] **DFMEA focus =** single-component / subassembly design failures.
- [x] **SFMEA focus =** system-level interfaces, interactions, integration failures.
- [x] **Seven steps:** Scope → Functions → Failure Modes → Effects+S → Causes+O → Controls+D → RPN+Action.
- [x] **RPN formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ — integer, range 1 to 1000.
- [x] **S, O, D scales:** 1 to 10 each.
- [x] **D scale is INVERSE** — high D = hard to detect; low D = easy to detect.
- [x] **RPN ≥ 100** OR **S ≥ 9** → mandatory corrective action (severity-override rule).
- [x] **Action must be re-evaluated** — compute $\text{RPN}_{\text{after}}$ and the **reduction ratio** $\Delta\text{RPN}(\%)$.
- [x] **Effective action rule:** $\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} < 100$ AND $\Delta\text{RPN} \geq 30\%$.
- [x] **AIAG-VDA 2019 update:** categorical **Action Priority (AP)** = High / Medium / Low is the modern alternative to raw RPN.
- [x] **Standards reference:** ISO 14971:2019 (medical device risk management), IEC 60812 (FMEA standard), FDA 21 CFR 820.30 (Design Controls).
- [x] **Document in DHF** — the FMEA is part of the regulatory submission package (FDA 510(k), CE Mark under MDR 2017/745).
- [x] **Decision rule:** *Single-component failure → DFMEA. Interface/integration failure → SFMEA. Both present → SFMEA first, then DFMEA on high-risk subsystems.*
- [x] **Reduction ratio formula:** $\Delta\text{RPN}(\%) = \dfrac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardware Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Design (Biomedical Devices):** The systematic engineering discipline involving the conceptualization, schematic design, component selection, printed circuit board (PCB) layout, prototyping, and validation of the electronic and electromechanical subsystems that constitute a medical device. It encompasses the **Analog Front-End (AFE)**, **Digital Back-End**, **Power Management Unit (PMU)**, and **Patient Isolation Barrier** required to safely acquire, condition, digitize, process, and display physiological signals.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Design of Biomedical Devices*, hardware design refers specifically to the **electronic hardware architecture** that bridges the biological signal source (the human body) and the computational/display unit. It is governed by strict medical safety standards, primarily **IEC 60601-1**, and must account for **leakage currents below 10 µA** (Type CF, direct cardiac contact) and **defibrillation-proof protection**.

The four canonical blocks of any biomedical hardware design are:

1. **Sensor / Transducer Interface** — Electrodes (Ag/AgCl), optical sensors (PPG), pressure transducers, etc.
2. **Analog Signal Conditioning** — Instrumentation Amplifier (INA), filters, level shifters.
3. **Digital Conversion & Processing** — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), microcontroller / DSP.
4. **Isolation, Power & Safety** — Optocouplers, isolation transformers, medical-grade DC-DC converters.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Stethoscope Meets the Smartphone"**

Imagine you want to record the sound of a heartbeat using your phone. The raw heart sound is *extremely weak* (microvolts in ECG, microvolts-to-millivolts in EEG/EMG), buried inside electrical noise (50 Hz mains hum, muscle artifact, electrode DC offset), and absolutely must not let dangerous currents flow back into the patient.

**Hardware design is the engineering "translator"** sitting between the patient and the digital world. It performs three jobs:

- **Boosts** the tiny bioelectric signal (which can be as low as **0.5 mV** for ECG, **10 µV** for EEG).
- **Cleans** it by removing noise and interference.
- **Protects** the patient from any electrical fault in the mains-powered circuitry.

Think of it like a *custom-built high-fidelity recording studio microphone preamp* — but with the additional constraint that if anything goes wrong (a short circuit, a power surge, a defibrillator discharge), the patient must remain unharmed. That is the unique ethical and engineering constraint that distinguishes biomedical hardware design from ordinary consumer electronics design.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Biomedical Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Signal Range** | $V_{in}$ | 0.5 mV – 10 mV (ECG) | Determines required gain |
| **Common-Mode Voltage** | $V_{CM}$ | ±300 mV (electrode offset) | Sets INA CMRR requirement |
| **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio** | CMRR | **≥ 80 dB** minimum, **≥ 100 dB** preferred | Rejects 50 Hz mains interference |
| **Leakage Current (Type CF)** | $I_{leak}$ | **< 10 µA** under normal & single-fault conditions | Patient safety (cardiac) |
| **ADC Resolution** | $n$ | **12 – 24 bits** | Captures low-amplitude bio-signals |
| **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s$ | **≥ 2 × f_max** (Nyquist) | Preserves signal bandwidth |
| **Input Impedance** | $Z_{in}$ | **≥ 10 MΩ** | Prevents signal attenuation |
| **Supply Voltage (analog)** | $V_{DD}$ | **±5 V, ±3.3 V, or 3.3 V single** | Rails for op-amps / ADC |

---

## 1.4 The Hardware Design Workflow (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepwise Engineering Process (Module 2 — KTU 2024):**
> 1. **System specification** — derive from clinical/biological requirement.
> 2. **Block-level architecture** — partition into analog, digital, power, isolation.
> 3. **Component selection** — INA (e.g., AD620, INA128), op-amps, ADC (e.g., ADS1292), MCU.
> 4. **Schematic design** — draw the complete circuit.
> 5. **Simulation** — SPICE / LTspice verification.
> 6. **PCB layout** — 2-layer / 4-layer, ground plane, creepage-clearance rules.
> 7. **Prototype bring-up & EMC testing** — IEC 60601-1 compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Biomedical Hardware Block Diagram

A biomedical instrument universally decomposes into a **signal-flow chain**. Understanding this is the most repeated concept in KTU examinations.

The flow is:

**Electrode → Protection Network → INA → High-Pass Filter → Gain Stage → Low-Pass Filter → 50/60 Hz Notch → ADC → MCU/DSP → Display/Storage**

Each block has a specific role:

- **Protection Network**: Series resistor (10 kΩ) + gas-discharge tube / TVS diode. Limits defibrillator-induced current.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)**: Provides high CMRR, high $Z_{in}$, and adjustable differential gain.
- **High-Pass Filter**: Removes DC electrode offset (typically 0.05 Hz cutoff).
- **Low-Pass Filter**: Anti-aliasing before ADC (e.g., 150 Hz for ECG, 40 Hz for EEG).
- **Notch Filter**: Surgical removal of 50 Hz power-line interference (Q ≈ 2 to 10).
- **Isolation Barrier**: Galvanic isolation between patient-connected circuitry and mains-powered circuitry.

---

## 2.2 Core Theoretical Concepts

### 2.2.1 Why an Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)?

A standard op-amp difference amplifier has a serious limitation: its input impedance is set by the resistor network, and any mismatch in the four resistors destroys the CMRR. The INA solves this with a **three-op-amp topology** that buffers both inputs before the difference stage.

**Three-Op-Amp INA Topology:**
- Stage 1: Two non-inverting buffers with a shared gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between them.
- Stage 2: Unity-gain difference amplifier.

The differential gain of the INA is:

$$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$

For the standard case $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_3 = R_4$ (or simply equal feedback resistors):

$$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition — Why this matters for KTU:**
> In KTU problems, you are frequently asked to choose $R_G$ for a desired gain. The exam expects you to know that $R_G$ is the *single external resistor* that sets gain, while all other resistors are internal and precision-matched inside the INA chip (e.g., AD620, INA128). This is why the INA achieves **CMRR > 100 dB** at DC — the matched internal resistors track each other across temperature.

### 2.2.2 Filter Theory (First-Order Active Filters)

A first-order **active low-pass filter** has transfer function:

$$H(s) = \frac{-R_f / R_{in}}{1 + sR_fC}$$

The **−3 dB cutoff frequency** is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$$

A first-order **active high-pass filter**:

$$H(s) = \frac{-sR_fC}{1 + sR_fC} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C}$$

### 2.2.3 The Twin-T Notch Filter (50/60 Hz Rejection)

The classic passive/inactive Twin-T notch has a transfer function whose **null frequency** (where output = 0) is:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

The **Q-factor** is set by a feedback ratio. High Q = narrow notch = selective 50 Hz removal without affecting nearby ECG frequencies (which extend up to ~150 Hz).

### 2.2.4 ADC and Sampling

**Resolution (LSB voltage):**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3}{4096} = 0.806\ \text{mV}$$

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

For ECG ($f_{max} = 150$ Hz), the minimum sampling rate is **300 Hz**. In practice, biomedical designers use **500 Hz – 1 kHz** for ECG and **256 Hz – 512 Hz** for EEG.

### 2.2.5 Patient Safety and Leakage Current

Per **IEC 60601-1**:

- **Type B** (body): $I_{leak} < 100\ \mu A$ (normal), $< 500\ \mu A$ (single fault).
- **Type BF** (body, floating): $I_{leak} < 100\ \mu A$ (normal), $< 500\ \mu A$ (single fault).
- **Type CF** (cardiac, floating): $I_{leak} < 10\ \mu A$ (normal), $< 50\ \mu A$ (single fault).

This is why **galvanic isolation** (using optocouplers, isolation amplifiers like ISO124, or transformer-isolated DC-DC converters) is mandatory between the patient-side analog front-end and the mains-connected digital/host side.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Reference Table** — Memorize before the ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical KTU Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | INA Differential Gain | $A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$ | $R_G$ = external gain resistor | dimensionless |
| 2 | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain | dB |
| 3 | First-Order LPF Cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | R, C in filter network | Hz |
| 4 | First-Order HPF Cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | R, C in filter network | Hz |
| 5 | Notch Filter Null Frequency | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Twin-T components | Hz |
| 6 | ADC LSB Voltage | $V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | n = resolution bits | Volts |
| 7 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = signal bandwidth | Hz |
| 8 | Power Dissipation in Op-Amp | $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{supply}$ | Quiescent current draw | Watts |
| 9 | Input-Referred Noise | $V_{n,in} = \frac{V_{n,out}}{A_d}$ | After INA | V/√Hz |
| 10 | Patient Leakage Limit (CF) | $I_{leak} \leq 10\ \mu A$ | Cardiac float, normal | Amperes |
| 11 | Sallen-Key LPF Cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | 2nd-order active LPF | Hz |
| 12 | Defibrillator Protection | $V_{clamp} \leq V_{max,INA}$ | TVS diode clamps | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Notation Convention:** Vertical bars (absolute value, magnitude) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the markdown table structure. Example: $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ — not $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Knowledge

The hardware design framework described above is used in:

- **Patient monitors** (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE) — ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, temperature.
- **Holter / ambulatory recorders** — battery-powered, low-noise, 24-hour storage.
- **Implantable devices** — pacemakers, ICDs (require ultra-low-power, hermetic packaging).
- **Defibrillators** — high-voltage hardware with patient isolation.
- **Neural recording implants** — multi-channel INA arrays (Intan Technologies RHD2000).
- **Diagnostic imaging** — front-end electronics for ultrasound transducers, MRI coils.

In industry, the KTU Module 2 concepts directly translate to schematic design using **Altium Designer**, **KiCad**, or **Cadence Allegro**, with **SPICE simulation** in LTspice or Multisim.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **exhaustive**. Every algebraic transition is explicitly written so that the student can follow without consulting any external material. The three derivations below are the highest-yield KTU Module 2 derivations and appear in nearly every previous ESE question paper.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Differential Gain of the Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

**Given Topology:** Two input buffers (op-amps $A_1$, $A_2$) feeding a unity-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$, $A_4$). A single resistor $R_G$ is connected between the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$. Each input buffer has a feedback resistor $R_1$.

**Step 1 — Identify the input voltage across $R_G$.**

Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the non-inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$. The inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ are virtual shorts to $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively. Therefore the voltage appearing across $R_G$ is:

$$V_{R_G} = V_1 - V_2 = V_{d,IN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the current through $R_G$.**

The current flowing through $R_G$ is:

$$I_{R_G} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G} = \frac{V_{d,IN}}{R_G}$$

**Step 3 — Note that the same current flows through both $R_1$ feedback resistors.**

By Kirchhoff's Current Law, the current $I_{R_G}$ must flow through $R_1$ of $A_1$ (from $V_1$ node to the output of $A_1$), and also through $R_1$ of $A_2$ (from output of $A_2$ to $V_2$ node), since no other path exists for it at the virtual short nodes.

**Step 4 — Express outputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$.**

Output of $A_1$:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + I_{R_G} \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{V_{d,IN}}{R_G} \cdot R_1$$

Output of $A_2$:

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - I_{R_G} \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{V_{d,IN}}{R_G} \cdot R_1$$

**Step 5 — Compute the differential output of the first stage.**

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + 2 \cdot \frac{V_{d,IN}}{R_G} \cdot R_1 = V_{d,IN} \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the unity-gain difference amplifier stage.**

The second stage has $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (matched), giving a differential gain of 1 with the rejection of any common-mode component $V_{CM}$:

$$V_{out} = \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_{o1} - V_{o2})$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and obtain the final transfer function.**

With $R_3 = R_2$ (and equal resistors in the second stage), and with the matched-resistor constraint that any common-mode voltage $V_{CM} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ is cancelled by the difference amplifier, the final differential gain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_d &= \frac{V_{out}}{V_{d,IN}} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right) \\
\\
\boxed{A_d} &= \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \quad \text{(when the second stage has unity gain, } R_2 = R_3 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical example for KTU exam practice.**

Suppose we want a differential gain of **1000** and the INA is AD620 with internal $R_1 = 24.7\ \text{k}\Omega$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1000 &= 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 24700}{R_G} \\
999 &= \frac{49400}{R_G} \\
R_G &= \frac{49400}{999} \approx 49.45\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard **49.9 Ω** resistor would be used. This is a frequent KTU calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter Cutoff Frequency

**Given Topology:** A non-inverting op-amp configuration where the feedback network consists of $R_f$ in parallel with $C$. The input series resistor is $R_{in}$.

**Step 1 — Write the feedback impedance.**

The impedance of $R_f \parallel C$ is:

$$Z_f = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{sC}}{R_f + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + sR_fC}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the non-inverting amplifier gain formula.**

The closed-loop gain is:

$$A(s) = 1 + \frac{Z_f}{R_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}(1 + sR_fC)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the pole.**

The transfer function has a single pole at $s = -\dfrac{1}{R_f C}$.

**Step 4 — Convert the pole to the −3 dB cutoff frequency in Hz.**

Set $s = j\omega$ and find where $\vert A(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{R_f C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi}$$

$$
\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}}
$$

**Step 5 — Design example (ECG anti-aliasing filter).**

For ECG, we need $f_c = 150$ Hz. Choose $C = 100$ nF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_f &= \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 150 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}} \\
R_f &= \frac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 10.61\ \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard **10 kΩ** resistor would give a slightly higher cutoff (~159 Hz) — acceptable for ECG.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CMRR in dB and Its Practical Significance

**Step 1 — Define the common-mode and differential gains.**

For a differential amplifier:

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{d,IN} + A_{cm} \cdot V_{CM}$$

where $A_d$ is the differential gain and $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain.

**Step 2 — Define CMRR.**

By definition:

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \quad \text{(dimensionless ratio)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR}_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Suppose $A_d = 1000$ and $A_{cm} = 0.1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR} &= \frac{1000}{0.1} = 10000 \\
\text{CMRR}_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(10000) = 80\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret for noise rejection.**

If a **50 Hz** mains interference of **1 V** appears as common-mode on both electrode leads, and $\text{CMRR} = 80$ dB = factor of 10000, then the residual interference at the output is:

$$V_{out,interference} = \frac{1\ \text{V}}{10000} = 100\ \mu\text{V}$$

This residual is now well below the ECG signal (~1 mV), confirming the INA's effectiveness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Implication:** A 1% mismatch in the four resistors of a simple difference amplifier can degrade CMRR from 80 dB to as low as **40 dB** — that is why precision-matched, laser-trimmed INA packages (e.g., AD620, INA118) are mandatory in biomedical hardware.

---

## 3.4 Quick Analytical Matrix: Choice of Hardware Sub-System

| Subsystem | Component Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Front-end Amplifier | **AD620 / INA128 / INA333** | High CMRR (>100 dB), low noise, single $R_G$ gain setting |
| Anti-Aliasing Filter | **2nd-order Sallen-Key Butterworth LPF** | Flat passband, sharp roll-off |
| Notch Filter | **Twin-T with Q-enhancement op-amp** | Removes 50/60 Hz line interference |
| ADC | **ADS1292 / AD7730 / STM32 internal ADC** | 16–24 bit, simultaneous sampling, medical-grade |
| Microcontroller | **STM32 / MSP430 / PIC** | Low power, DSP instructions, real-time capable |
| Isolation | **ISO124 / ADuM1411 / optocoupler 6N137** | 5 kV isolation for patient safety |
| Power (Patient Side) | **Battery only** (e.g., 2× AA) or isolated DC-DC | Eliminates leakage path |
| Power (Host Side) | **Medical-grade AC-DC, UL/IEC 60601 listed** | Reinforced insulation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Biomedical Hardware Signal-Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Patient Body]:::patient --> E[Electrodes AgAgCl]:::sensor
    E --> PR[Protection Network 10k resistor and TVS diode]:::protect
    PR --> INA[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620 Gain 1000]:::analog
    INA --> HPF[High Pass Filter fc 0.05 Hz]:::analog
    HPF --> G[Variable Gain Stage 1 to 50]:::analog
    G --> LPF[Low Pass Filter fc 150 Hz]:::analog
    LPF --> NF[Notch Filter 50 Hz Twin T]:::analog
    NF --> ISO[Isolation Barrier ISO124]:::isolation
    ISO --> ADC[ADC 12 to 24 bit 500 Hz]:::digital
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller STM32 DSP]:::digital
    MCU --> DSP_DISP[Display LCD and Storage SD Card]:::output
    MCU --> COMM[BLE WiFi USB Communication]:::output

    PSU[Medical Grade Power Supply 5V and 3.3V]:::power --> ISO
    BAT[Battery Backup 3.7V Li ion]:::power --> ISO

    classDef patient fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef protect fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef analog fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef isolation fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffd9ec,stroke:#990066,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Signal flow goes left to right. The **Isolation Barrier** (purple block) is the mandatory safety boundary between patient-connected analog circuitry and mains-powered digital/host circuitry. The **power rails** (grey) feed only the isolated side; the patient side is powered through the isolation barrier.

---

## 4.2 Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Internal Topology (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP1[Input V1 non inverting] --> A1[Op Amp A1 buffer]
    INP2[Input V2 non inverting] --> A2[Op Amp A2 buffer]
    A1 --> R1A[Feedback R1]
    R1A --> RG[External Gain Resistor RG]
    RG --> R1B[Feedback R1]
    R1B --> A2
    A1 --> DA_TOP[Top input of difference amp]
    A2 --> DA_BOT[Bottom input of difference amp]
    DA_TOP --> A3[Unity Gain Difference Amp A3 and A4]
    DA_BOT --> A3
    A3 --> OUT[Single Ended Output Vout]

    classDef input fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class INP1,INP2 input
    class A1,A2,A3 amp
    class R1A,R1B,RG res
    class DA_TOP,DA_BOT,OUT out
```

---

## 4.3 Hardware Design Decision-Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Clinical Requirement]):::start --> SPEC[Define Signal Bandwidth Amplitude and SNR]:::step
    SPEC --> SAFECHK{Patient Contact Type?}:::decision
    SAFECHK --> B[Type B Body surface]:::branch
    SAFECHK --> BF[Type BF Body floating]:::branch
    SAFECHK --> CF[Type CF Cardiac floating]:::branch
    B --> ISO1[Single Isolation Barrier]:::step
    BF --> ISO2[Reinforced Isolation 2 MOPP]:::step
    CF --> ISO3[Defibrillation Proof + 5 kV Isolation]:::step
    ISO1 --> SEL[Select INA Op Amp and ADC]:::step
    ISO2 --> SEL
    ISO3 --> SEL
    SEL --> SIM[SPICE Simulation]:::step

    PCB --> TEST[Safety and EMC Pre Compliance Test]:::step
    TEST --> DONE([Prototype Ready for Clinical Trial]):::end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern (KTU 2024 — PEBRT415 ESE):**
> - **Part A:** 2 short-answer questions × 3 marks = 6 marks.
> - **Part B:** 1 main question × 14 marks (with internal choice of two 14-mark options, of which the student answers one).
> - **Total Module Contribution:** 20 marks.
> - **Cognitive Levels:** Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Design (L5), Evaluate (L6).
> - All numerical questions expect a final boxed answer with units.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define the term "Instrumentation Amplifier" and state any two reasons why it is preferred over a simple op-amp difference amplifier for biomedical signal acquisition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) is a precision differential amplifier with **high input impedance**, **high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR)**, and an **externally adjustable gain** set by a single resistor $R_G$. It is built using a three-op-amp topology: two input buffers followed by a difference amplifier.

**Two reasons it is preferred over a simple op-amp difference amplifier:**

1. **High input impedance on both inputs** — A simple difference amplifier has input impedance set by the input resistors, which loads the high-impedance electrode and attenuates the bio-signal. The INA's input buffers draw negligible current.
2. **Single-resistor gain control** — Gain can be varied by changing only $R_G$, without affecting the CMRR. In a simple difference amplifier, changing any one of the four feedback resistors unbalances the network and degrades CMRR.

> (1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two reasons. Total: 3 marks.)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**What is the purpose of an isolation barrier in a biomedical device hardware? Name two commonly used isolation components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **isolation barrier** is a galvanic (ohmic) separation between the patient-connected analog front-end and the mains-powered host/display circuitry. Its purpose is **patient protection**: it prevents any fault current from the mains side (e.g., a power supply short) from flowing through the patient, who may have low-body-resistance paths (e.g., during cardiac catheterization or surgery).

**Two commonly used isolation components:**

1. **Isolation Amplifier (e.g., ISO124, ADuM3150)** — Analog signal isolation with 5 kV withstand.
2. **Optocoupler (e.g., 6N137, MOC3021)** — Digital signal isolation using an LED-phototransistor pair.
3. *(Bonus, 1 mark if mentioned)* **Isolated DC-DC converter** (e.g., RECOM R-78 series) for power isolation.

> (1 mark for purpose, 1 mark each for two components. Total: 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Main Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> Each main question below carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Answer EITHER Question B1 OR Question B2.

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete hardware architecture of a **single-channel ECG acquisition system**, clearly identifying the function of each block. State typical values of gain, bandwidth, and sampling rate used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **first-order active low-pass filter** (Sallen-Key, 2nd-order Butterworth) suitable for use as an anti-aliasing filter in an ECG system with cutoff frequency $f_c = 150$ Hz. Show all component calculations and verify the design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — B1(a)**

**Block Diagram of ECG Hardware (7 marks):**

The complete ECG signal chain is:

**Electrode → Protection Network → INA (AD620, gain = 10) → High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz) → Second Gain Stage (gain = 100) → Low-Pass Filter (150 Hz) → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → Isolation Barrier (ISO124) → ADC (12-bit, 500 Hz) → MCU (STM32) → Display/Storage**

**Function of each block (mention any 5, 1 mark each; neatness = 1 mark):**

| Block | Function | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode (Ag/AgCl) | Transduces ionic skin potential to electronic voltage | Skin-electrode impedance 5 kΩ |
| Protection Network | Limits defibrillator / ESD current to safe level | 10 kΩ series + TVS diode |
| INA (AD620) | High-CMRR differential amplification | Gain 10, CMRR > 100 dB |
| HPF (0.05 Hz) | Removes DC electrode offset | fc = 0.05 Hz |
| Gain Stage (100) | Amplifies the mV signal to ~1 V full scale | Gain 100 |
| LPF (150 Hz) | Anti-aliasing; limits bandwidth | fc = 150 Hz |
| Notch (50 Hz) | Removes mains hum | f0 = 50 Hz, Q = 5 |
| Isolation (ISO124) | Patient safety | 5 kV isolation |
| ADC | Digitizes the conditioned signal | 12-bit, 500 Hz sampling |
| MCU (STM32) | Digital signal processing, display, storage | ARM Cortex-M4 |

**Total Gain = 10 × 100 = 1000**. An ECG of 1 mV becomes 1 V at ADC input — perfect for a 0–3.3 V ADC range.

**Sampling Rate:** 500 Hz is chosen as it satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ with margin (Nyquist would require 300 Hz for 150 Hz signal).

---

#### **Model Solution — B1(b)**

**Design a 2nd-order Sallen-Key Butterworth LPF with $f_c = 150$ Hz. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall the Sallen-Key transfer function for unity-gain LPF:**

$$
H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s C_1 (R_1 + R_2) + s^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}
$$

For a **Butterworth response**, the damping factor $\zeta$ must be $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, which gives equal $C_1 = C_2 = C$ and $R_1 = R_2 = R$ (simplest design choice).

**Step 2 — Simplify the design equations:**

With $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose a convenient capacitor and compute the resistor:**

Choose $C = 100\ \text{nF}$ (standard value, easy to obtain).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 150 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&\approx 10.61\ \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the standard 5% value $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and recompute the exact cutoff:**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 159.15\ \text{Hz}
$$

This is well within the ECG anti-aliasing requirement (the standard ECG bandwidth is 0.05–150 Hz; 159 Hz cutoff is acceptable).

**Step 5 — Verify Butterworth response:**

For equal R and C, the Q-factor is:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2 - (R_1/R_2)} = \frac{1}{2 - 1} = 0.707
$$

This is the **Butterworth Q-factor** (maximally flat passband). ✅ Design verified.

**Final Design Values:**
- $C_1 = C_2 = 100\ \text{nF}$
- $R_1 = R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$
- $f_c = 159\ \text{Hz}$ (close to target 150 Hz)
- Topology: Unity-gain Sallen-Key LPF using a single op-amp (e.g., OPA227, TL082).

> (1 mark for topology choice, 2 marks for component calculation, 1 mark for verification, 1 mark for component selection rationale, 2 marks for final values with units.)

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **differential gain** of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. If a designer requires a gain of 500 using an INA with internal resistor $R_1 = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$, calculate the required value of the external gain-setting resistor $R_G$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **IEC 60601-1 patient safety standard** in detail. List the three protection classes (B, BF, CF) and the corresponding leakage current limits under normal and single-fault conditions. State at least one clinical example for each class. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — B2(a)**

**Step 1 — Three-Op-Amp INA Topology Recap (1 mark):**

The INA consists of two input buffers (op-amps $A_1$, $A_2$) and a unity-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$).

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the input signals at the non-inverting terminals of $A_1$ and $A_2$. The current through the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ is:

$$
I_{R_G} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

This same current flows through the feedback resistors $R_1$ of both input buffers. The output voltages of $A_1$ and $A_2$ are:

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + I_{R_G} \cdot R_1 \quad ; \quad V_{o2} = V_2 - I_{R_G} \cdot R_1
$$

The differential output of the first stage is:

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + 2 I_{R_G} R_1 = (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)
$$

The unity-gain difference amplifier gives:

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)}
$$

Hence the **differential gain** is:

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given $A_d = 500$ and $R_1 = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 &= 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 25000}{R_G} \\
499 &= \frac{50000}{R_G} \\
R_G &= \frac{50000}{499} \approx 100.2\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard **100 Ω** resistor gives a gain of:

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{50000}{100} = 501
$$

which is within 0.2% of the target. ✅

---

#### **Model Solution — B2(b)**

**IEC 60601-1 — General Medical Electrical Equipment Safety Standard (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** IEC 60601-1 is the internationally accepted standard governing the basic safety and essential performance of medical electrical equipment. It classifies medical devices by the **type of patient contact** to limit leakage current that could flow through the patient.

**Three Protection Classes (4 marks):**

| Class | Description | Leakage Current (Normal) | Leakage Current (Single Fault) | Clinical Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type B** (Body) | Earthed; not floating | **< 100 µA** | **< 500 µA** | Hospital bed, examination lamp, MRI scanner (non-patient-circuit) |
| **Type BF** (Body, Floating) | Patient circuit is isolated from earth; applied part floats | **< 100 µA** | **< 500 µA** | ECG machine, SpO₂ pulse oximeter, NIBP cuff, ultrasound TENS unit |
| **Type CF** (Cardiac, Floating) | Highest isolation; suitable for direct cardiac contact | **< 10 µA** | **< 50 µA** | Defibrillator (when applied to chest), pacemaker programmer, intracardiac catheter |

**Means of Patient Protection (MOPP) (2 marks):**
- **One MOPP** = 1500 V AC isolation, 1 mm creepage.
- **Two MOPP** = 4000 V AC isolation, reinforced creepage/clearance.

Type BF requires 1 MOPP; Type CF requires 2 MOPP. Defibrillator-proof applied parts must survive a 5 kV discharge without functional damage.

> (1 mark for introduction, 4 marks for class table, 2 marks for MOPP and isolation requirements. Total: 7 marks.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Module 2 (Hardware Design) — Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "isolation"** in the ECG block diagram. Many students draw a 6–7 block signal chain but omit the isolation barrier between the analog front-end and the ADC/MCU. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing "Type B" with "Type BF" leakage limits.** Type B and BF both have 100 µA normal limit — but **Type CF is much stricter (10 µA)**. **Loss of 1 mark if mixed up.**
> 3. **Writing INA gain formula as $A_d = 1 + R_1/R_G$** (without the factor of 2). The correct formula is $A_d = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Not converting cutoff frequency from rad/s to Hz** in filter design (or vice versa). Always use $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$, not $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$. **Loss of 1 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting to choose standard component values (E12/E24 series)** in design problems. A calculated $R = 10.61\ \text{k}\Omega$ must be rounded to **10 kΩ** with a note on the resulting cutoff. **Loss of 0.5–1 mark.**
> 6. **Not stating CMRR in dB.** Always convert $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$. A numeric answer of "10000" without dB is incomplete. **Loss of 0.5 mark.**
> 7. **No mention of IEC 60601-1 standard** in any safety-related question. KTU examiners expect this standard to be cited by name. **Loss of 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 — Hardware Design — One-Page Recap (read this 10 minutes before the exam):**
>
> ✅ Hardware design of a biomedical device = analog front-end + digital back-end + isolation + power management.
>
> ✅ Canonical signal chain: **Electrode → Protection → INA → HPF → Gain → LPF → Notch → Isolation → ADC → MCU → Display**.
>
> ✅ **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** uses 3-op-amp topology; gain $A_d = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$; single external resistor $R_G$ sets gain; CMRR > 100 dB.
>
> ✅ **Active Filters:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R C)$ for both LPF and HPF. Sallen-Key (2nd-order) gives Butterworth response with $Q = 0.707$ for equal R, C.
>
> ✅ **Twin-T Notch Filter** removes 50/60 Hz mains; null frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi R C)$; Q enhanced by positive feedback.
>
> ✅ **ADC:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$; 12-bit, 3.3 V → 0.806 mV LSB. Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. ECG uses 500 Hz; EEG uses 256–512 Hz.
>
> ✅ **Patient Safety (IEC 60601-1):** Type B = 100 µA; Type BF = 100 µA (floating); Type CF = 10 µA (cardiac, floating). Always use isolation barrier.
>
> ✅ **CMRR$_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$**. A 1% resistor mismatch in a simple difference amp drops CMRR from 80 dB to ~40 dB — use INA chips.
>
> ✅ **Protection Network:** series resistor 10 kΩ + TVS diode (e.g., SMBJ5.0A) — must clamp defibrillator pulses (< 5 kV).
>
> ✅ **Key ICs to know:** AD620 / INA128 (INA), OPA227 / TL082 (op-amp), ADS1292 (medical ADC), STM32 / MSP430 (MCU), ISO124 / ADuM1411 (isolation), RECOM R-78 (isolated DC-DC).
>
> ✅ **High-Yield Numbers:**
> - ECG signal: 0.5 – 5 mV, 0.05 – 150 Hz
> - EEG signal: 10 – 100 µV, 0.5 – 40 Hz
> - EMG signal: 50 µV – 5 mV, 20 – 500 Hz
> - Typical total gain: 1000× (ECG), 10,000× (EEG)
> - Power: 3.3 V / 5 V rails; battery-powered for patient side.
>
> ✅ **Always cite IEC 60601-1 in safety-related questions** and remember the three protection classes B, BF, CF.
>
> ✅ **Standard component values:** E12 series (10, 12, 22, 33, 47, 68) and E24 series — round your design calculations to these values.
>
> ✅ **PCB Design Tip:** Use 4-layer board with dedicated ground plane; maintain ≥ 8 mm creepage between patient side and mains side for IEC compliance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Design** in the context of biomedical devices is the systematic, planned, and documented engineering activity of defining the architecture, modules, interfaces, data structures, and algorithms necessary to implement the **software component** of a medical device, ensuring it meets specified **functional**, **performance**, **safety**, and **regulatory** requirements as mandated by **IEC 62304:2006/AMD1:2015** (Medical device software — Software life cycle processes) and **ISO 14971** (Risk Management).

In modern healthcare technology, a biomedical device is rarely a pure hardware artifact. Devices such as **infusion pumps**, **pacemakers**, **patient monitors**, **ventilators**, **MRI machines**, and **blood glucose meters** all rely on embedded or application-level software. The software dictates device behaviour, alarm logic, signal processing, dose calculation, and wireless communication. Because failure can lead directly to **patient injury or death**, software design in this domain is treated as a **safety-critical engineering discipline**, not a casual coding activity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Software as a Hospital Pharmacy's Prescription Verification System**

Imagine a hospital pharmacy where every prescription must go through a multi-stage verification chain before medication reaches the patient. The pharmacist is the **hardware**, but the rule-book, the cross-checking logic, the dosing tables, the allergy database, the audit log, and the alarm system that catches a "10× overdose" before it is dispensed — **all of this is the "software design."**

If the design is sloppy:
- The check might happen *after* the drug is given (no real-time constraint).
- A junior pharmacist (sub-component) might be given authority to override the senior (poor module hierarchy).
- The prescription pad interface (User Interface) might confuse the doctor (poor Human Factors).
- There may be no record of *who* approved *what* (poor traceability).

A **good software design** is therefore not about clever algorithms alone; it is about creating an **organised, traceable, modular, safe, and verifiable** structure that never lets a hazardous state go undetected. This is the essence of **Software Design for Biomedical Devices**.

## 1.3 Why Software Design is Special in Biomedical Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three reasons software design carries exceptional weight in biomedical devices:**

1. **Regulatory scrutiny** — Bodies like the **US FDA**, **EU MDR 2017/745**, and India's **CDSCO** treat software as a separate, classifiable risk element. Class III devices (e.g., implantable defibrillators) require the **highest level of software documentation**.
2. **Closed-loop control with the human body** — A defect in dose-control software delivers the wrong drug, the wrong shock, or the wrong radiation dose directly into a patient.
3. **Long product lifecycles** — A pacemaker implanted today may run software originally designed 10 years ago, demanding **maintainability** and **rigorous configuration management**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardised by IEC 62304

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **SOUP** | Software Of Unknown Provenance — third-party, off-the-shelf, or legacy software that is not fully verifiable. |
| **Item** | A uniquely identifiable component of the medical device software. |
| **Software Safety Class A** | No injury or damage to health is possible. |
| **Software Safety Class B** | Non-serious injury is possible. |
| **Software Safety Class C** | Death or serious injury is possible. |
| **Risk** | Combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm. |
| **Hazard** | Potential source of harm. |
| **Traceability** | Ability to follow the life-cycle of a requirement from origin to implementation, and through verification. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Software Safety Classification (IEC 62304)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before a single line of code is written, the device manufacturer must assign the software to one of three safety classes. This single decision cascades through the entire project — it determines the documentation, the design rigour, the testing depth, and the regulatory submission.**

### 2.1.1 Decision Logic (Structured Bulleted Steps)

- **Step 1 — Identify the possible hazardous situation** arising from a software failure.
- **Step 2 — Estimate the severity** of the resulting harm (negligible, minor, serious, catastrophic).
- **Step 3 — Estimate the probability** that the software failure will lead to that harm.
- **Step 4 — Apply the IEC 62304 matrix:**

| Severity of Harm | Probability of Harm | Resulting Class |
|---|---|---|
| No injury | Any | **Class A** |
| Non-serious injury | Low | **Class B** |
| Non-serious injury | High | **Class C** |
| Serious or fatal | Any | **Class C** |

- **Step 5 — The higher the class, the more stringent the design controls**, documentation, and verification activities.

### 2.1.2 Real-World Example

> [!NOTE]
> A **blood pressure cuff** with software that simply displays a reading has no injury potential if the algorithm fails — **Class A**.
> A **ventilator** that stops delivering breaths because of a watchdog timer bug can cause death — **Class C**.
> An **insulin pump** whose dose calculation overflows can cause hypoglycaemic shock — **Class C**.

## 2.2 The Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) for Medical Devices

The KTU-aligned SDLC has **five distinct phases**, each with mandatory deliverables:

1. **Software Development Planning (Section 5.1 of IEC 62304)**
   - Deliverable: *Software Development Plan* describing scope, standards, tools, deliverables, and schedule.
2. **Software Requirements Analysis (Section 5.2)**
   - Deliverable: *Software Requirements Specification (SRS)* — what the software shall do.
3. **Software Architectural Design (Section 5.3)**
   - Deliverable: *Software Architecture Document (SAD)* — how the software is structured.
4. **Software Detailed Design (Section 5.4)**
   - Deliverable: *Software Detailed Design Document (SDDD)* — module-level design.
5. **Software Unit Implementation & Verification (Section 5.5)**
   - Deliverable: *Source code, unit test records*.

This is followed by **Software Integration & Integration Testing (5.6)** and **Software System Testing (5.7)**.

## 2.3 Software Architecture for Biomedical Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture** is the "blueprint of the blueprint" — it decides how the system is decomposed into **modules**, how they communicate, what data flows between them, and what happens when something fails.

### 2.3.1 Common Architectural Styles in Medical Device Software

- **Layered Architecture** — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) → Drivers → Middleware → Application → UI.
- **Event-driven / Real-time Architecture** — Used in patient monitors and infusion pumps; tasks respond to interrupts and timers.
- **State-machine Architecture** — Used in devices with discrete operating modes (e.g., defibrillator: *Standby → Analyse → Charge → Shock → Post-shock*).
- **Pipeline Architecture** — Used in signal-processing chains (ECG filtering, image reconstruction in MRI).
- **Modular / Component-based Architecture** — Each function (alarm, dosing, logging) is an isolated, testable module.

### 2.3.2 Design Principles That MUST Be Followed

- **Modularity** — Each function lives in one module with a single responsibility.
- **Loose coupling, high cohesion** — Modules share minimal data; each module is internally focused.
- **Defensive programming** — Every input is validated; every pointer is bounds-checked; every division checks for zero.
- **Watchdog and self-test** — Hardware/software watchdogs detect deadlock or runaway code; **POST (Power-On Self-Test)** verifies integrity at every boot.
- **Fail-safe defaults** — On any unrecognised state, the device reverts to a clinically safe state (e.g., ventilator enters "idle" not "max airflow").
- **Traceability matrix** — Every requirement in the SRS must be traceable to a design element, a unit test, and a system test.

## 2.4 The Role of Risk Management in Software Design

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 14971 is the master risk-management standard for medical devices, and it explicitly calls out software as a source of hazards.** The design process is therefore inseparable from risk analysis.

A useful analytic tool is the **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** applied to software modules. For each module, the analyst identifies:
- **Failure modes** — What can the software do wrong?
- **Effects** — What is the patient-visible consequence?
- **Severity (S)** — 1 to 10.
- **Occurrence (O)** — 1 to 10.
- **Detectability (D)** — 1 to 10 (higher = harder to detect).

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is then computed as:

$$
\text{RPN} \;=\; S \;\times\; O \;\times\; D
$$

The design team must drive the RPN down through design mitigations — for example, adding input validation reduces **O**; adding self-tests reduces **D**; and re-architecting may reduce **S**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield, exam-ready summary** of every quantitative concept you must remember for Module 2 — Software Design.

| Concept / Metric | Formula or Expression | Variable Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | S = Severity, O = Occurrence, D = Detectability (each 1–10) | Threshold: RPN $\geq 50$ is unacceptable | FMEA-based design prioritisation |
| Safety Integrity Level (SIL) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \leq 10^{-n}$ per hour | n = 1, 2, 3, 4 | SIL 3: $10^{-8} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-7}$ per hour | IEC 61508, related to IEC 62304 Class C |
| Software Safety Class | $C = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability})$ | See matrix in §2.1.1 | Class A, B, or C | IEC 62304 §4.3 |
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $\text{PFD} \approx \lambda \cdot t_{CE}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t_{CE}$ = channel-equivalent downtime | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-8}$ per hour | Used for Class C software |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | Hours | Reliability budgeting |
| Watchdog Timeout | $t_{WD} = k \cdot t_{max\,task}$ | $k = 1.5$ to $2.0$ safety factor | $t_{max\,task}$ = longest legitimate task time | Real-time design |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | E = edges, N = nodes, P = connected components | $\leq 10$ per function | Code quality metric |
| Requirement Coverage | $C_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{tested}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | $R_{\text{tested}}$ = requirements with tests, $R_{\text{total}}$ = total | 100% for Class C | Verification completeness |
| Line Coverage | $C_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{executed}}}{L_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | $L_{\text{executed}}$ = lines executed during tests | $\geq 95\%$ for Class C | Unit testing |
| Branch Coverage | $C_{\text{branch}} = \dfrac{B_{\text{covered}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | $B_{\text{covered}}$ = branches hit | 100% for Class C | Unit testing |

## 2.6 The "Why" Behind the Equation

The cyclomatic complexity equation $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ is more than a number — it is a **design smell detector**. When $V(G) > 10$, the function almost certainly has too many decision paths, making it nearly impossible to test exhaustively. For a Class C medical device, untested code is a **regulatory red flag**. Therefore, the architect's design rule is: **decompose until no function exceeds $V(G) = 10$.**

The **RPN equation** is similarly purposeful. Severity, Occurrence, and Detectability are the three independent axes along which software risk lives. Multiplying them gives a single prioritisation metric, but more importantly, it shows the architect **where to attack** — if the RPN is high, the architect can either **reduce S** (better isolation), **reduce O** (defensive programming), or **reduce D** (self-tests, alarms).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section develops the **quantitative design metrics** step-by-step so the student can solve any numerical problem that appears in the KTU examination. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity from a Flow Graph

The cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ of a software module measures the number of linearly independent paths through its control-flow graph. It is derived from Euler's formula for planar graphs.

### Step 1 — Euler's formula for connected planar graphs

$$
V - E + F \;=\; 2
$$

where $V$ is the number of vertices, $E$ the number of edges, and $F$ the number of faces (including the outer face). Rearranging for the number of faces:

$$
F \;=\; 2 - V + E
$$

### Step 2 — Map the formula to software

In a control-flow graph of a program:
- $V$ corresponds to **nodes** (statements, decisions).
- $E$ corresponds to **edges** (control transfers).
- The "regions" enclosed by edges correspond to **faces** $F$.
- There is one outer region (the "outside" of the program flow).

For a **single connected component** ($P = 1$), substitute the relation back. Removing the outer face gives the number of "bounded regions," which equals the number of independent paths:

$$
V(G) \;=\; F \;=\; E - N + 2
$$

This is the classic formula, with $N$ replacing $V$ (number of nodes).

### Step 3 — Worked Example

Consider the following C-style pseudo-code fragment for an alarm-decision block:

```text
if (systolic > 180)            // decision A
    alarm_high();
else if (systolic < 90)        // decision B
    alarm_low();
else
    no_alarm();
```

The flow graph has:
- 1 entry node
- 1 exit node
- 2 decision nodes (A and B)
- Total nodes: $N = 4$
- Edges: entry→A, A→alarm_high, A→B, B→alarm_low, B→no_alarm, alarm_high→exit, alarm_low→exit, no_alarm→exit → $E = 8$
- Connected components: $P = 1$

Apply the formula:

$$
V(G) \;=\; E - N + 2P \;=\; 8 - 4 + 2(1) \;=\; 6
$$

Six independent paths exist. To test them exhaustively, the verification team must construct **at least six test cases**. This number is the design-driven test budget for the module.

### Step 4 — Architectural Decision

If $V(G) = 6$ exceeds the project threshold of 10 *and* the module is safety-critical, the architect must **refactor** by extracting one branch into a sub-function — this is called the *"extract method"* refactor. After refactoring, each function's $V(G)$ falls below 10, and the overall module becomes verifiable within the testing budget.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RPN Calculation for a Software Module

Consider a **bolus calculation** module inside an insulin pump. The FMEA team produces the following scoring:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calculated dose overflows int32 | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| Wrong patient weight read | 8 | 3 | 5 |
| Decimal-to-integer truncation | 7 | 5 | 7 |

### Step 1 — Compute RPN for each failure mode

$$
\text{RPN}_{1} \;=\; 9 \times 4 \times 6 \;=\; 216
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{2} \;=\; 8 \times 3 \times 5 \;=\; 120
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{3} \;=\; 7 \times 5 \times 7 \;=\; 245
$$

### Step 2 — Rank and Prioritise

Ranking from highest to lowest: RPN$_3$ (245) > RPN$_1$ (216) > RPN$_2$ (120). All three exceed the project threshold of 50.

### Step 3 — Apply Design Mitigations

For RPN$_3$ (decimal truncation): the architect adds a **pre-dispatch integer-overflow check** (reduces $O$ from 5 to 2) and an **independent post-calculation verification** in a separate safety module (reduces $D$ from 7 to 3).

New RPN$_3$:

$$
\text{RPN}_{3,\text{new}} \;=\; 7 \times 2 \times 3 \;=\; 42
$$

This is now below the threshold of 50, and the design is deemed acceptable for clinical release.

### Step 4 — Trace the Reduction

By mathematically tracing the design change, the team can demonstrate to the regulator that the new architecture **reduces risk by design** — a core expectation of ISO 14971 and IEC 62304.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Watchdog Timeout Calculation

In a real-time biomedical device, each periodic task must complete within its deadline. The watchdog timer must be set high enough to allow the **longest legitimate execution** but low enough to catch a runaway.

### Step 1 — Measure the worst-case task execution time

The longest task in the system is the *ECG QRS-detection algorithm* with measured worst-case execution time $t_{\max\,task} = 38\,\text{ms}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the safety factor

The standard KTU-aligned safety factor for Class C software is $k = 1.5$ to $2.0$. Choosing $k = 2.0$ for a critical task:

$$
t_{WD} \;=\; k \cdot t_{\max\,task} \;=\; 2.0 \times 38\,\text{ms} \;=\; 76\,\text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Design interpretation

If the watchdog has not been "kicked" within 76 ms, the timer assumes the firmware has hung, resets the microcontroller, and forces the device into its **safe state** (e.g., the ventilator enters idle mode, the infusion pump stops the motor, the pacemaker reverts to backup VVI mode). This is the engineering embodiment of the *fail-safe default* design principle.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Architectural Style to Choose

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is a **KTU-favourite table** that students should memorise. In an exam, the question is often of the form: *"For an ECG monitor that must respond within 50 ms to an arrhythmia, which architecture is most appropriate, and why?"* The matrix below gives the model answer.

| Device Type | Real-time? | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| ECG / Patient Monitor | Hard real-time | Event-driven with RTOS | Must respond to interrupts in <50 ms |
| Infusion Pump | Hard real-time | Layered + state-machine | Drug delivery is a discrete, auditable state |
| Implantable Pacemaker | Hard real-time + ultra-low power | Layered with dedicated interrupt handlers | Battery life and deterministic response are critical |
| Defibrillator | Hard real-time | State-machine (explicit) | The device has a small, well-defined state diagram |
| Blood-Glucose Meter (consumer) | Soft real-time | Modular layered | Not life-critical; modular structure is enough |
| MRI Image Reconstruction | Throughput-bound | Pipeline (parallel) | Each stage (FFT, k-space, image) is a pipeline stage |
| PACS / Hospital Information System | Soft real-time | Client–server modular | Data integrity and access control dominate |

## 3.5 Verification Coverage Equation Derivation

For Class C software, the regulator expects 100% requirement coverage. The metric is:

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; \frac{R_{\text{tested}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

If $R_{\text{total}} = 142$ and $R_{\text{tested}} = 138$:

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; \frac{138}{142} \times 100\% \;=\; 97.18\%
$$

This is **below** the 100% requirement for Class C. The four missing test cases must be authored and executed before submission, or the device cannot be released.

## 4.3 Software Safety Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Identify Possible Harm from Software Failure]
    Q1 --> Q2[Can It Cause Death or Serious Injury?]

    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Probability of Occurrence]
    Q4 -->|High| CLASSC
    Q4 -->|Low| CLASSB[Class B Moderate Rigor]

```

## 4.4 FMEA-Driven Design Refinement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IDENT[Identify Failure Mode] --> SCORE[Score Severity Occurrence Detectability]
    SCORE --> CALC[Calculate RPN]
    CALC --> CHK{RPN Above Threshold?}
    CHK -->|Yes| MITIG[Apply Design Mitigation]
    MITIG --> RECALC[Recalculate RPN]
    RECALC --> CHK
    CHK -->|No| ACCEPT[Accept and Document]
```

```mermaid
    SYSREQ --> SYSTEST[System Testing]

```

> [!NOTE]
