5. **Test methods and procedures** for each requirement
6. **Acceptance criteria** derived from design inputs
7. **Sample size and statistical justification**
8. **Data analysis and reporting format** with traceability to DHF

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with internal choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Verification, Sample Size & Statistical Analysis

**(a) Describe in detail the components of a Design Verification Plan as required by ISO 13485:2016 and FDA 21 CFR 820.30. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Model Solution:**

A Design Verification Plan is a controlled document that demonstrates the design output meets the design input. The major components are:

1. **Purpose & Scope** — Identifies the specific design outputs being verified (e.g., firmware v1.2, mechanical sub-assembly SB-23A) and limits the verification to the prototype stage.
2. **References** — Lists ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR 820.30, ISO 14971 (risk management file), applicable product standards (e.g., IEC 60601-1 for electromedical devices), and prior versions of the DVP.
3. **Responsibilities** — Names the verification lead, Quality Assurance approver, Regulatory Affairs reviewer, and Manufacturing witness.
4. **Test Articles & Equipment** — Lists prototypes by serial number, software version, and test equipment with calibration certificates (e.g., oscilloscope cal. due 12-Jan-2025).
5. **Test Methods** — Detailed step-by-step procedures for each requirement (e.g., for electrical safety: apply 1500 V AC for 60 s, measure leakage current).
6. **Acceptance Criteria** — Quantitative thresholds derived *directly* from design inputs (e.g., leakage current ≤ 0.1 mA).
7. **Sample Size & Statistical Plan** — Sample size with statistical justification; choice of $Z$-test, t-test, ANOVA, etc.
8. **Test Environment** — Controlled temperature ($23 \pm 2^\circ C$), humidity ($50 \pm 10\%$ RH), per ISO 554.
9. **Data Analysis** — How data will be statistically analyzed, including acceptance / rejection rules.
10. **Reporting** — Format and content of the **Design Verification Report (DVR)**, which is appended to the **Design History File (DHF)**.

**(b) A pulse oximeter is required to measure SpO₂ with an accuracy of ±2% across the range 70–100%. From prior prototypes, the standard deviation of the measurement error is σ = 1.0%. Using 95% confidence and a margin of error E = 0.4%, determine the required sample size. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Select the formula (variable data):**

$$n = \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the values:**

$Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ (95% confidence, two-tailed)
$\sigma = 1.0\%$
$E = 0.4\%$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$n = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 1.0}{0.4} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$1.96 \times 1.0 = 1.96$$

**Step 5 — Divide by denominator:**

$$\frac{1.96}{0.4} = 4.90$$

**Step 6 — Square:**

$$n = (4.90)^{2} = 24.01$$

**Step 7 — Round up conservatively:**

$$n = 25 \text{ units (minimum)}$$

**Engineering recommendation:** A KTU-accepted DVP would round up to **$n = 30$** for safety margin and dropout allowance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — V&V Distinction, $C_{pk}$, and Diagnostic Metrics

**(a) Explain the difference between Design Verification and Design Validation. Prepare a comparative table highlighting the key differences across 8 parameters. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Model Solution:**

Verification and validation are two distinct activities in design control, and their confusion is one of the most common FDA Form 483 observations.

| Parameter | Design Verification | Design Validation |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Definition (ISO 13485) | Outputs meet **inputs** | Device meets **user needs** |
| Fundamental question | Are we building the device **right**? | Are we building the **right** device? |
| Stage in lifecycle | After design output, before validation | After verification, on production-equivalent units |
| Test environment | Bench, in-silico, lab, dimensional | Clinical, simulated-use, human factors |
| Sample type | Engineering prototypes | Production-equivalent / final finished device |
| Sample size | Statistical, often n ≥ 30 | Smaller (15–30 representative users) |
| Regulatory clause | FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g) | FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g) (also) |
| Example | Insulin pump flow rate test | Diabetic patient self-administers correctly |
| Document | Design Verification Plan (DVP) | Design Validation Plan (DVPn) |
| Failure consequence | Design iteration; CAPA | Often full design change or even project halt |

**(b) A manufacturer of infusion pumps measures flow rate on 25 production units. The mean flow is μ = 99.8 mL/hr with σ = 0.6 mL/hr. The specification is 100 ± 2 mL/hr. Calculate the process capability index $C_{pk}$ and comment on whether the process is acceptable for a medical device. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the specification limits:**

$USL = 102$ mL/hr
$LSL = 98$ mL/hr
$\mu = 99.8$ mL/hr
$\sigma = 0.6$ mL/hr

**Step 2 — Compute the upper capability index:**

$$C_{pkU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{102 - 99.8}{3 \times 0.6} = \frac{2.2}{1.8} = 1.222$$

**Step 3 — Compute the lower capability index:**

$$C_{pkL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{99.8 - 98}{3 \times 0.6} = \frac{1.8}{1.8} = 1.000$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.222, \ 1.000) = 1.000$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- A $C_{pk}$ of **1.00** corresponds to approximately **2,700 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, which is **NOT acceptable** for a medical device.
- Medical device industry standard requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (preferred $\geq 1.67$).
- **Action:** Re-center the process (e.g., retune the pump motor controller so that $\mu$ shifts to 100.0 mL/hr) AND/OR reduce variability (e.g., tighter component tolerances, six-sigma design). After corrective action, re-verify with a fresh DVP execution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing V&V:** Students often write "Validation = bench test" and "Verification = clinical test." This is the **opposite** of truth. Memorize: Verification = *Did we meet our own specs?* Validation = *Did we meet patient/user needs?*
> 2. **Forgetting to round up sample size:** Always round $n$ **up** (never down) — auditors penalize this heavily.
> 3. **Missing acceptance criteria:** A DVP without quantitative acceptance criteria is **invalid**. Saying "device should perform well" guarantees zero marks.
> 4. **Ignoring traceability:** Every DVP test must trace back to a specific Design Input — examiners specifically check for this in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Forgetting calibration status of equipment:** Always mention calibrated test equipment in your DVP — it is a **mandatory** element per ISO 17025.
> 6. **Not linking to standards:** Always cite the relevant standard (e.g., IEC 60601-1 for electromedical safety, ISO 10993 for biocompatibility, ISO 14971 for risk management) — a DVP that fails to cite standards is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Takeaways — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **DVP Definition:** Documented protocol confirming **outputs meet inputs** (Verification), distinct from **device meets user needs** (Validation).
- **Regulatory Anchors:** FDA **21 CFR 820.30(g)**, ISO **13485:2016 Clause 7.3.7**, ISO **14971** (risk), IEC **60601-1** (electromedical safety), IEC **62304** (software), IEC **62366-1** (usability), ISO **10993** (biocompatibility).
- **10 Mandatory DVP Elements:** Purpose, references, responsibilities, test articles, equipment, methods, acceptance criteria, sample size, data analysis, reporting.
- **V-Model:** DVP sits on the right arm of the V-Model, mapping back to the Design Input on the left arm. **Every** input must have a verification test, and every test must trace back to an input.
- **Sample Size Formula (Variable):** $n = \left( \frac{Z \sigma}{E} \right)^{2}$ — round **up** at the end.
- **Sample Size Formula (Attribute):** $n = \frac{Z^{2} p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$ — use $p = 0.5$ for conservative estimate.
- **Process Capability:** $C_{pk} = \min \left( \frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$. **Acceptance:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (preferred for medical devices).
- **Diagnostic Metrics:** Sensitivity $= \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, Specificity $= \frac{TN}{TN+FP}$, Accuracy $= \frac{TP+TN}{\text{Total}}$.
- **Bias & Precision:** Bias = systematic error (accuracy); Precision = random error (repeatability, %R&R).
- **CI Value at 95%:** $Z = 1.96$; at 99%: $Z = 2.576$. Memorize these — they appear in **every** statistical question.
- **Output of DVP:** A formal **Design Verification Report (DVR)** with results, deviations, and CAPA, filed in the **Design History File (DHF)**.
- **Continuous Improvement:** Failed verification triggers a **CAPA cycle** — re-design → re-verify → re-validate → re-release.
- **Audit Traps:** FDA Form 483, ISO 13485 audit non-conformities are routinely issued for: (1) DVP without pre-approved acceptance criteria, (2) post-hoc data manipulation, (3) untraceable tests, (4) uncalibrated equipment, (5) V&V confusion.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always start a 14-mark V&V answer with a **comparative table** — it is the single most efficient way to earn the first 3–4 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Protocols in Biomedical Device Design

In the context of biomedical device design, **Protocols** refer to a formally defined set of rules, conventions, data structures, and communication procedures that govern the **exchange of information** between medical devices, between devices and information systems (such as Hospital Information Systems, Electronic Health Records), and between devices and external monitoring/control systems. Protocols ensure that physiological signals, patient data, control commands, and device status information are transmitted in a standardized, secure, reliable, and interoperable manner across heterogeneous hardware and software platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme for PEBRT415, Module 3 focuses on the communication and interoperability **protocols** that form the nervous system of modern connected medical devices — including wired protocols (USB, Ethernet), short-range wireless (Bluetooth, BLE, ZigBee), and medical-specific standards (IEEE 11073, HL7, DICOM).

In a broader engineering sense, the term **"protocol"** in biomedical engineering encompasses:

1. **Communication Protocols** – the rules for data exchange (e.g., IEEE 11073, HL7, DICOM).
2. **Interface Protocols** – electrical/mechanical handshaking between subsystems.
3. **Clinical Protocols** – standardized procedures for safe device use on patients.
4. **Regulatory/Testing Protocols** – methodologies to validate safety, efficacy, and compliance (e.g., ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1 testing protocols).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Universal Language of a Hospital":**
> Imagine a multinational hospital where doctors, nurses, machines, and record systems all speak different native languages. A **protocol** is the agreed-upon **universal translator and rulebook** everyone agrees to follow. When a heart-rate monitor (made by Company A) wants to send patient data to a tablet (made by Company B), and that data must eventually reach a central Electronic Medical Record (EMR), a **protocol** is what guarantees the message arrives correctly, in the right format, at the right time, and only to authorized people. Without protocols, devices become "islands" — they can measure but cannot share.

## 1.3 Why Protocols are Critical in Biomedical Engineering

| Aspect | Role of Protocol |
|---|---|
| **Interoperability** | Allows devices from different manufacturers to communicate seamlessly. |
| **Patient Safety** | Standardized error-checking prevents data corruption that could cause misdiagnosis. |
| **Data Integrity** | Ensures physiological signals (ECG, SpO₂) are transmitted without loss. |
| **Cybersecurity** | Encryption and authentication protect sensitive health data (HIPAA / DISHA compliance). |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | FDA / CDSCO / CE Mark require adherence to protocol standards. |
| **Scalability** | Enables integration of new sensors and devices into existing hospital networks. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

- **Standard Frequency Bands (Medical Telemetry):** **WMTS bands 608–614 MHz, 1395–1400 MHz, 1427–1432 MHz** in the US; MICS 402–405 MHz for implantable devices.
- **Bluetooth LE Range:** up to **~100 m** (outdoor) and **~10–30 m** (indoor).
- **ZigBee Data Rate:** **250 kbps** in the 2.4 GHz band.
- **HL7 Message Length:** typically **< 1 MB** per message.
- **IEEE 11073 Optimized Exchange Protocol (OXA) latency target:** **< 1 second** for vital signs.
- **DICOM Transfer Syntax default:** Little Endian Implicit VR.
- **Maximum SAR for body-worn devices (FCC):** **1.6 W/kg** averaged over 1 g of tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are essential values that frequently appear in KTU numerical and short-answer questions on biomedical protocols.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Biomedical Protocols

Protocols used in biomedical device design can be classified along **multiple dimensions**:

### A. By Physical Layer (Transmission Medium)
1. **Wired Protocols**
   - **USB (Universal Serial Bus)** – widely used for connecting diagnostic devices to PCs.
   - **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** – backbone of hospital networks.
   - **RS-232 / RS-485** – legacy serial protocols still used in older medical instruments.
   - **SPI / I²C** – chip-level protocols used inside the device for inter-IC communication.
2. **Wireless Protocols**
   - **Bluetooth Classic & Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** – most common for wearable sensors.
   - **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** – low-power mesh networks for hospital monitoring.
   - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** – high bandwidth for imaging and large data.
   - **NFC (Near Field Communication)** – short-range device pairing, smart cards.
   - **LoRa / NB-IoT** – long-range, low-power for remote patient monitoring.
3. **Implantable Protocols**
   - **MICS (Medical Implant Communication Service)** – 402–405 MHz.
   - **Body Area Network (BAN) protocols** – IEEE 802.15.6.

### B. By Application Layer (Medical Data Standards)
1. **IEEE 11073 (Personal Health Devices / PHD)** – optimized for point-of-care and personal health devices.
2. **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** – hospital-level clinical and administrative data.
3. **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** – medical imaging.
4. **Continua Health Alliance Framework** – end-to-end interoperability.
5. **FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** – modern RESTful successor to HL7 v2.

## 2.2 Layered Architecture (OSI / ISO Model Mapping)

Most biomedical protocols are best understood via a layered model analogous to the **OSI 7-layer model**:

| OSI Layer | Function | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| 7 – Application | End-user data semantics | HL7 v2.x, DICOM Upper Layer, IEEE 11073-20601 |
| 6 – Presentation | Encoding, encryption | ASCII, UTF-8, ASN.1, Base64 |
| 5 – Session | Dialog control | IEEE 11073 Session |
| 4 – Transport | Reliable delivery | TCP, BLE L2CAP |
| 3 – Network | Addressing/routing | IP, IPv6 for IoMT |
| 2 – Data Link | Framing, error detection | BLE LL, IEEE 802.15.4 MAC, Ethernet MAC |
| 1 – Physical | Electrical/optical signals | RF carrier, voltage levels on USB |

## 2.3 IEEE 11073 Protocol Family (Detailed)

The **IEEE 11073** family, also known as **X73** or **Medical / Health Device Communication Standards**, is the cornerstone protocol for personal health devices.

- **IEEE 11073-20601** – Application Layer Optimized Exchange Protocol (Common Upper Layer).
- **IEEE 11073-104xx** – Device Specializations (e.g., 10404 for pulse oximeter, 10406 for ECG, 10407 for blood pressure, 10408 for thermometer, 10415 for weighing scale, 10441 for cardiovascular fitness).
- **IEEE 11073-30200** – Cable-connected (USB) transport.
- **IEEE 11073-30300** – Infrared (IrDA) transport.
- **IEEE 11073-30400** – Bluetooth HCI transport.

### IEEE 11073 Communication Model

The model defines three agents:
1. **Manager** (e.g., smartphone, gateway, hospital workstation).
2. **Agent** (the medical device / sensor).
3. **Association** (logical connection between them).

The state machine has 4 primary states:
- **Disconnected**
- **Connected (Unassociated)**
- **Associating**
- **Operating**

## 2.4 Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — the Workhorse of Wearables

BLE (introduced in Bluetooth 4.0, 2010) is the de-facto standard for biomedical wearables.

### BLE Stack Layers
- **Application Layer** – User-defined profiles (e.g., custom ECG streaming).
- **GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)** – Defines Services and Characteristics.
- **ATT (Attribute Protocol)** – Read/Write/Notify/Indicate operations.
- **L2CAP (Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol)** – Multiplexing.
- **HCI (Host Controller Interface)** – Bridge between host and controller.
- **Link Layer (LL)** – Advertising, scanning, connection.
- **Physical Layer (PHY)** – 2.4 GHz GFSK modulation, 40 channels (3 advertising, 37 data).

### BLE GATT for Medical Devices
A typical medical BLE device exposes GATT services such as:
- **Heart Rate Service (UUID 0x180D)** – contains Heart Rate Measurement Characteristic.
- **Health Thermometer Service (UUID 0x1809)**.
- **Pulse Oximeter Service (UUID 0x1822)**.
- **Glucose Service (UUID 0x1808)**.
- **Custom Service** – for proprietary sensor data (e.g., EMG, EEG).

## 2.5 ZigBee / IEEE 802.15.4

ZigBee is optimized for **low data rate**, **low power**, and **mesh networking** — ideal for hospital ward monitoring (multiple patients on one network).

- **Frequency Band:** 2.4 GHz (worldwide), 915 MHz (US), 868 MHz (EU).
- **Data Rate:** 250 kbps at 2.4 GHz.
- **Range:** 10–100 m.
- **Network Topologies:** Star, Mesh, Tree.
- **Node Types:** Coordinator, Router, End Device.
- **Power:** Sleep current ~1 µA → coin-cell operation for years.

## 2.6 HL7 and FHIR

- **HL7 v2.x** – Pipe-and-hat encoding (`|`, `^`, `~`, `&`, `\`). Most widely deployed hospital messaging standard.
- **HL7 v3 (CDA)** – XML-based clinical document architecture.
- **FHIR (HL7 FHIR R4/R5)** – RESTful, JSON/XML, modular "Resources" (Patient, Observation, Device, etc.). The future of healthcare interoperability.

## 2.7 DICOM

- Used in **radiology, cardiology, ophthalmology, pathology** imaging.
- Provides standards for:
  - Image formats (CT, MRI, ultrasound, X-ray).
  - Network protocols (DICOM Upper Layer over TCP/IP, port **104**).
  - Workflow (Modality Worklist, Storage, Query/Retrieve, Print).
  - Security (TLS profiles, digital signatures).

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for solving KTU numericals and short answers on this topic. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Path Loss (Free-space, Friis) | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| Received Power | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB}$ | dBm | Friis transmission equation |
| SNR (Shannon) | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ | bits/s | $B$ = bandwidth |
| Data Rate (BLE) | $1$ Mbps (LE 1M PHY), $2$ Mbps (LE 2M PHY), $500$ kbps (LE Coded) | bps | |
| Data Rate (ZigBee) | $250$ kbps (2.4 GHz) | bps | |
| BLE Channels | $40$ channels ($\# 0{-}39$); Adv = $37,38,39$; Data = $0{-}36$ | — | Channel spacing 2 MHz |
| Beacon Interval (BLE) | $20$ ms – $10.24$ s | ms/s | Configurable |
| Connection Interval | $7.5$ ms – $4$ s | ms | |
| MICS Band | $402{-}405$ MHz | MHz | For implants |
| WMTS Band | $608{-}614, 1395{-}1400, 1427{-}1432$ | MHz | For medical telemetry |
| SAR Limit (FCC) | $1.6$ W/kg | W/kg | Over 1 g tissue |
| DICOM Port | $104$ | — | TCP |
| HL7 v2 Port | $2575$ (MLLP) | — | TCP |
| FHIR Default Port | $443$ (HTTPS) | — | RESTful |

> [!NOTE]
> When writing LaTeX inside markdown tables, never use the `|` character for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to avoid breaking the table.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM):** BLE + IEEE 11073 + FHIR pipeline → wearable → smartphone → cloud EMR.
- **Smart ICU:** ZigBee mesh monitors SpO₂, ECG, BP, temperature at every bed, routed to a central nurse station.
- **Imaging Suite:** DICOM integrates CT/MRI machines with PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System).
- **Implantable Devices:** MICS band enables data from pacemakers to be read by external programmers.
- **Ambulance Telemedicine:** 4G/5G + HL7 streams pre-hospital vitals to receiving hospital.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss (Used for Wireless Protocol Link Budget)

When designing a wireless biomedical protocol link, the **link budget** is critical to ensure that the received signal is above the receiver sensitivity. The Friis equation gives the free-space path loss.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the isotropic radiator. Power density at distance $d$ from an isotropic transmitter of power $P_t$:

$$S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Replace isotropic with a directive antenna of gain $G_t$ (transmit):

$$S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4 \pi d^2}$$

**Step 3:** The received power at a receive antenna of effective aperture $A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 \, G_r}{4 \pi}$:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4 \pi d^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2 \, G_r}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^2$$

**Step 5:** Convert to dB. The free-space path loss $PL$ in dB is defined as $PL_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_t / P_r)$ for unity gains. This yields:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\lambda = c / f$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $f$ in Hz:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{c}\right)$$

**Step 7:** With $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**, the constant evaluates to $20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4 \pi \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} \times 10^6\right) = 32.44$:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$$

This is the **standard Friis formula** used in KTU link-budget problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BLE Connection Interval and Throughput

A common KTU numerical problem asks for the maximum throughput of a BLE link given connection parameters.

### Given
- Connection interval $T_{CI}$ (in seconds, range $0.0075$ to $4$ s).
- Number of packets per connection event $N$.
- Payload bytes per packet $L$ (max 247 bytes with ATT MTU).
- Bit rate of PHY $R$ (1 Mbps, 2 Mbps, or 500 kbps coded).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Time for one connection event:

$$T_{event} = N \cdot \left(T_{preamble} + T_{access\,addr} + T_{header} + T_{payload} + T_{IFS}\right)$$

**Step 2:** For LE 1M PHY with $L$-byte payload, the on-air packet time (excluding IFS) is:

$$T_{packet} = \frac{8(L + 6 + 2) + 80}{10^6} \; \text{seconds}$$

where 6 = LL header + MIC overhead, 2 = CRC, 80 bits = preamble + access address (40 bits each).

**Step 3:** Throughput (useful payload only):

$$Throughput = \frac{N \cdot L \cdot 8}{T_{CI}} \; \text{bits/s}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum theoretical throughput with one packet per event, $L=247$ bytes, $T_{CI}=7.5$ ms, $R=2$ Mbps:

$$T_{packet} = \frac{8(247+6+2) + 80}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{2080}{2 \times 10^6} = 1.04 \text{ ms}$$

$$Throughput = \frac{247 \times 8}{0.0075} = 263.5 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5:** In practice, with 2 Mbps PHY, connection event spacing 7.5 ms, and ACK structure, sustained throughput is ~200–250 kbps.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Capacity Bound for Telemetry Channel

The **maximum error-free data rate** over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:

$$C = B \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \; \text{bits/s}$$

For a Bluetooth channel with $B = 1$ MHz and $S/N = 20$ dB (i.e., $S/N = 100$):

$$C = 10^6 \cdot \log_2(101) = 10^6 \cdot 6.658 \approx 6.66 \text{ Mbps}$$

This explains why BLE's **actual** 1–2 Mbps PHY is well below Shannon's bound — it uses simple FSK rather than capacity-approaching codes.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) — Common in BP Cuff Protocols

A frequently tested KTU formula in the BP measurement device protocol context:

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$$

where $SBP$ = systolic, $DBP$ = diastolic BP. The IEEE 11073-10407 blood pressure device specialization transmits $SBP$, $DBP$, and $MAP$ as numeric observations.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection for Common Biomedical Applications

| Application | Data Rate Need | Power Need | Mobility | Recommended Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wearable fitness band | Low (kbps) | Ultra-low | High | BLE (GATT) |
| Continuous ECG (Holter) | Medium (250 kbps) | Low | High | BLE 2M PHY / Wi-Fi |
| Hospital ward monitoring (mesh) | Low (kbps) | Low | Stationary | ZigBee / IEEE 802.15.4 |
| CT/MRI image transfer | Very high (Mbps–Gbps) | N/A (plugged) | Stationary | DICOM over Ethernet |
| Implanted pacemaker telemetry | Very low | Ultra-low | Internal | MICS (402–405 MHz) |
| Ambulance-to-hospital | High (Mbps) | Vehicle powered | Mobile | 4G/5G + HL7/FHIR |
| Glucose meter dock | Low (kbps) | Battery | Stationary | USB / BLE |
| Smart pill / ingestible sensor | Very low | Battery | Internal | MICS / Body-channel |
| Endoscope live video | High (Mbps) | Mains | Mobile | Wi-Fi / proprietary RF |
| ICU ventilator | High reliability | Mains | Stationary | HL7 / Ethernet |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam answer for "Compare and contrast protocols" type questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Biomedical Protocol Stack Architecture

```mermaid

        H1["HL7 v2 / FHIR R4"]
```
