# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a protocol. List any two biomedical communication protocols.**

**Model Answer:**
A protocol is a formal set of rules and conventions that govern the exchange of data between two or more entities in a communication system. In biomedical device design, protocols ensure interoperability, safety, and security of medical data.
Two examples:
1. **IEEE 11073** — for personal health device communication.
2. **HL7** — for hospital-level clinical and administrative data exchange.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two examples = 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the OSI layers involved when a Bluetooth Low Energy pulse oximeter sends SpO₂ data to a smartphone.**

**Model Answer:**
The relevant layers are:
1. **Application Layer (7)** — GATT service for Pulse Oximeter (UUID 0x1822).
2. **Presentation Layer (6)** — UTF-8 / IEEE-11073-10404 data encoding.
3. **Transport Layer (4)** — BLE L2CAP.
4. **Data Link Layer (2)** — BLE Link Layer.
5. **Physical Layer (1)** — 2.4 GHz GFSK modulation.

*(½ mark per layer × 5 = 2.5; round to 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the IEEE 11073 protocol family in detail, mentioning its layered architecture, device specializations, and the manager–agent communication model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable ECG device transmits data over Bluetooth Low Energy. The link uses LE 2M PHY with a connection interval of 7.5 ms. The maximum ATT MTU is 247 bytes and only one packet is sent per connection event. Calculate the theoretical maximum throughput. If the ECG sampling rate is 500 Hz with 16-bit samples, comment on whether this link is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** IEEE 11073 (X73) is a family of standards from the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBS) for medical / health device communication. It is optimized for personal health devices (PHD).

2. **Architecture (2 marks):** The standard is structured in three parts:
   - **11073-20601 (Common Upper Layer / Optimized Exchange Protocol):** Defines generic service model, communication model, and information model. It is transport-independent.
   - **11073-104xx (Device Specializations):** Define device-specific metrics, e.g., 10404 pulse oximeter, 10406 ECG, 10407 BP monitor, 10408 thermometer, 10415 weighing scale.
   - **11073-30xxx (Transport Profiles):** Define how to carry 20601 over a specific transport: 30200 USB, 30300 IrDA, 30400 Bluetooth, 30500 ZigBee.

3. **Manager–Agent Model (2 marks):**
   - **Agent:** The medical device / sensor (e.g., SpO₂ sensor).
   - **Manager:** The receiving host (e.g., smartphone, gateway).
   - Communication is initiated by the Agent, which advertises its presence and negotiates an association with the Manager.
   - The **Domain Information Model (DIM)** uses object-oriented concepts — MDS (Medical Device System), Metric objects, and Numeric/Enumeration/Real-time Sample Array (RT-SA) types.

4. **Communication States (2 marks):**
   The protocol state machine: Disconnected → Connected (Unassociated) → Associating → Operating. In the Operating state, services like GET, SET, EVENT REPORT, and ACTION are used. Configuration is exchanged using **MDC/MDS objects** and **MIB (Management Information Base)**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the throughput:**
On-air packet duration for LE 2M PHY (1 M symbols/s × 2 = 2 Mbit/s? — actually LE 2M PHY is 2 megabits/s = 2 × 10⁶ bit/s):

$$T_{packet} = \frac{8 \times (L + 6 + 2) + 80}{2 \times 10^6}$$

Substituting $L = 247$ bytes:

$$T_{packet} = \frac{8(247 + 6 + 2) + 80}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{8 \times 255 + 80}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{2040 + 80}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{2120}{2 \times 10^6} = 1.06 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Effective throughput (1 packet per connection event):**

$$Throughput = \frac{L \times 8}{T_{CI}} = \frac{247 \times 8}{0.0075} = \frac{1976}{0.0075} \approx 263.5 \text{ kbps}$$

*(4 marks for derivation, 1 mark for numerical value)*

**Step 3 — Required data rate for ECG:**
With 500 Hz sampling × 16 bits = 8000 bits/s = 8 kbps (raw). With protocol overhead (~30–40%), real link demand ~ 12 kbps. This is **far below** the 263.5 kbps capacity. **Conclusion:** The link is more than sufficient; in practice, a lower connection interval or smaller MTU could save power. *(2 marks for comparison and conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Compare wired and wireless protocols used in biomedical device communication, with at least two examples of each. Justify which category is more suitable for a wearable continuous ECG monitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless telemetry link operates at 2.4 GHz with a transmit power of 0 dBm, transmit antenna gain of 2 dBi, and receiver antenna gain of 0 dBi. The receiver sensitivity is −90 dBm. Calculate the maximum range in free space, assuming negligible cable loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Wired Protocols | Wireless Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| Examples | USB, RS-232, Ethernet, SPI/I²C | BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, MICS |
| Mobility | Limited (tethered) | High (untethered) |
| Power | Higher (interface active) | Lower (BLE/ZigBee) |
| Data rate | Can be very high (Ethernet Gbps) | Limited by spectrum |
| Security | Physically contained | Needs encryption |
| EMI susceptibility | Low | High |
| Use case | Imaging, fixed bedside | Wearables, ambulatory |

**Justification for wearable ECG (4 marks):**
A wearable continuous ECG monitor (Holter) requires the patient to move freely, often for 24–72 hours. **Wireless protocols** — specifically **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — are most suitable because:
1. **Mobility** — no cables to restrict patient movement.
2. **Low power** — BLE sleep currents ~1 µA allow multi-day battery operation.
3. **Adequate bandwidth** — BLE 2M PHY (theoretical 263 kbps) easily handles ECG data (8 kbps raw).
4. **Smartphone integration** — universal BLE in mobile phones enables display and forwarding to EMR.
5. **Safety** — no mains-isolated wired connection while patient is ambulatory.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the link budget equation:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB}$$

**Step 2 — For successful reception, $P_r \geq P_{rx,min}$:**

$$P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB} \geq P_{rx,min}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for maximum path loss:**

$$PL_{dB,max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,min} = 0 + 2 + 0 - (-90) = 92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Friis free-space path loss formula:**

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$$

With $f = 2400$ MHz:

$$20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.380 = 67.6 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d$:**

$$92 = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 67.6 + 32.44$$
$$92 = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 100.04$$
$$20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) = -8.04$$
$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = -0.402$$
$$d_{km} = 10^{-0.402} \approx 0.396 \text{ km} \approx 396 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum free-space range is **~396 m**. In real indoor hospital environments with walls, body absorption, and multipath fading, this reduces to ~30–50 m. *(2 marks for numerical answer + 1 mark for engineering comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions in Friis formula** — $d$ must be in km and $f$ in MHz for the +32.44 constant. Mixing units is the #1 cause of full-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing HL7 versions** — v2 (pipe encoding, port 2575) is **not** the same as v3 (XML) or FHIR (REST/JSON, port 443).
> 3. **Wrong OSI layer attribution** — IEEE 11073 is an **Application Layer** standard, not a transport. BLE LL is Data Link, not Transport.
> 4. **Confusing BLE 1M vs 2M PHY** — they are 1 Mbps and 2 Mbps bit rates respectively; throughput must be recomputed.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering conclusion** — KTU expects a 1–2 line "in real hospital environment…" comment for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Protocols in Biomedical Device Design**

- [ ] **Protocol** = formal rules for biomedical data exchange; ensures interoperability, safety, security.
- [ ] **IEEE 11073** family: **20601** (common), **104xx** (device specs), **30xxx** (transports). Manager–Agent model. States: Disconnected → Unassociated → Associating → Operating.
- [ ] **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy):** 2.4 GHz, 40 channels, GATT-based services. PHY: 1M / 2M / Coded 500 kbps. Connection interval 7.5 ms – 4 s. Max ATT MTU = 247 bytes.
- [ ] **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4):** 250 kbps, mesh, 2.4 GHz, ultra-low power. Best for hospital ward monitoring.
- [ ] **MICS band:** **402–405 MHz** — reserved for **implanted** medical devices.
- [ ] **WMTS bands:** 608–614, 1395–1400, 1427–1432 MHz — for medical telemetry.
- [ ] **HL7 v2.x:** Pipe-and-hat encoding, port 2575 (MLLP), most widely deployed hospital messaging.
- [ ] **FHIR:** RESTful, JSON/XML, port 443, modern successor to HL7 v2.
- [ ] **DICOM:** Port 104, for medical imaging (CT, MRI, ultrasound, X-ray).
- [ ] **Friis FSPL:** $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$.
- [ ] **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- [ ] **BLE throughput** with LE 2M PHY, 247-byte MTU, 1 packet per 7.5 ms event ≈ **263.5 kbps**.
- [ ] **SAR limit (FCC):** **1.6 W/kg** averaged over 1 g tissue for body-worn devices.
- [ ] **MAP** = DBP + (SBP − DBP)/3 — used in BP device data.
- [ ] **OSI mapping** for biomedical protocols must be correctly stated in exam answers.
- [ ] **Real-world design rule:** choose **lowest-power protocol that meets the data rate & latency** requirements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Transfer Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Transfer Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Design Transfer** is the phase of the medical device design control process in which the device design is correctly translated into production specifications, manufacturing processes, quality system requirements, and distribution procedures to ensure that the designed product can be consistently and reliably manufactured at scale.

According to **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h)** and **ISO 13485:2016, Section 7.3.8**, Design Transfer ensures that the device design is correctly translated into production specifications and that all design outputs become the basis for the production and quality system. Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures to ensure that the device design is correctly transferred to production.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** identifies Design Transfer as a critical output of **Module 3: Design Process & Controls**, sitting between Design Verification/Validation and the Production phase in the design control lifecycle.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Design Output** | The documented result of each design phase (drawings, specifications, BOMs) |
| **Design Input** | The physical and performance requirements of a device as the basis for design |
| **Design Transfer** | Translation of design outputs into production-capable specifications |
| **DMR (Device Master Record)** | Compilation of all documents that describe the device and its production |
| **DHF (Design History File)** | Compilation of all records demonstrating design development conformance |
| **Process Validation** | Confirmation that a process consistently produces a result meeting specifications |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Handover" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Master Chef to Franchise Handover**
> 
> Imagine a **Master Chef** who has developed a unique dish in their personal kitchen — perfect taste, perfect presentation, perfect timing. The dish was tested with focus groups (validation) and the recipe was refined (verification). Now, the chef wants to open **100 franchise restaurants** worldwide.
> 
> The **Design Transfer Process** is everything that happens between "this works in my kitchen" and "this works in every franchise kitchen across the world." It is writing the recipe down to exact gram-precise measurements, training franchise chefs, ensuring every oven reaches the same temperature, sourcing the same ingredients from approved suppliers, and setting up a quality check at every location.
> 
> If the Master Chef simply photocopies the recipe and hands it to franchisees without testing ovens, training chefs, or specifying ingredient suppliers — the dish will fail in 90% of locations. That is exactly what Design Transfer prevents in medical devices.

## 1.4 Why Design Transfer is Critical in Biomedical Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A medical device that **works once in a lab** but **fails at scale** can directly cause **patient injury or death**. Regulatory bodies (FDA, EU MDR, CDSCO) therefore mandate Design Transfer as a **legally enforceable** design control step.

- **Patient Safety**: Inconsistent production of a pacemaker, infusion pump, or stent can be fatal
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Failure to perform Design Transfer = FDA 483 observations, warning letters, recalls
- **Cost Control**: Late discovery of manufacturing issues = expensive redesigns and lost market share
- **Quality Assurance**: Ensures the "designed device" is identical to the "manufactured device"

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Control Lifecycle — Position of Design Transfer

The FDA's **Design Control** framework (outlined in 21 CFR 820.30 and harmonized with ISO 13485) follows this structured sequence:

1. **Design & Development Planning**
2. **Design Input**
3. **Design Output**
4. **Design Review** (at each phase)
5. **Design Verification** (does the design meet the inputs?)
6. **Design Validation** (does the design meet user needs and intended use?)
7. **Design Transfer** ← *(This module's focus)*
8. **Design Changes** (post-transfer control)

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification** asks: *"Are we building the product right?"*
> **Validation** asks: *"Are we building the right product?"*
> **Transfer** asks: *"Can we build it the same way every time?"*

## 2.2 Core Elements of Design Transfer — The "6 Pillars"

Design Transfer is not a single document — it is a **multi-element system**. KTU examiners frequently test knowledge of these six pillars:

### Pillar 1: Production Specifications
- Detailed **drawings**, **BOMs (Bill of Materials)**, **tolerances**, **material specifications**
- Every dimension must be **manufacturable to ± specified tolerance**
- All components must be **sourced from qualified suppliers**

### Pillar 2: Manufacturing Process Definition
- **Process flow diagrams** (PFMEA — Process Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
- **Work instructions** for each manufacturing step
- Identification of **critical-to-quality (CTQ)** parameters

### Pillar 3: Equipment & Tooling Qualification
- **IQ (Installation Qualification)** — is equipment installed correctly?
- **OQ (Operational Qualification)** — does equipment operate within design specs?
- **PQ (Performance Qualification)** — does equipment consistently produce acceptable output?
- **IQ/OQ/PQ** is a core FDA expectation for any production process

### Pillar 4: Quality System Integration
- Transfer design outputs into the **Quality Management System (QMS)**
- Update **DMR (Device Master Record)**, **DHR (Device History Record)**
- Implement **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** mechanisms

### Pillar 5: Training & Personnel Competency
- Production operators must be **trained** on the new device
- Documented **competency assessments** for each critical process

### Pillar 6: Pilot Production & First Article Inspection
- Run a **pilot batch** under full production conditions
- Conduct **First Article Inspection (FAI)** on multiple units
- Compare pilot output to design intent — close the loop

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Matrix

| Element | Formula / Standard | Unit / Metric | KTU Relevance |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | High |
| Process Capability (Shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Dimensionless | High |
| Design Transfer Effectiveness | $DTE = \dfrac{N_{\text{conforming pilot units}}}{N_{\text{total pilot units}}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) | Medium |
| Production Yield | $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{produced}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Medium |
| Toleranced Dimension | $D_{\text{actual}} = D_{\text{nominal}} \pm \Delta D$ | mm, µm, etc. | High |
| Acceptance Sampling (ANSI/ASQ Z1.4) | $AQL$ (Acceptable Quality Level) | % defective | Medium |
| Reliability Target | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Tables**: All absolute values and `min` functions above use `\vert` or `\min` notation to preserve markdown table integrity — never use `|` inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical device companies (e.g., Medtronic, Stryker, Siemens Healthineers, GE Healthcare), Design Transfer is the **bridge between R&D and Manufacturing**. Engineers who understand this process can:

- Transition from R&D roles to **Operations/Manufacturing Engineering** roles
- Lead **New Product Introduction (NPI)** projects
- Become **Quality/Regulatory** professionals (high-demand career path)
- Work in **Contract Manufacturing Organizations (CMOs)** for medical devices

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Design Transfer Procedure — Step-by-Step

Since Design Transfer is a **procedural/conceptual** topic (not heavily formulaic), the "derivation" here is the **logical procedure** that must be followed. The KTU Board Examiners test this as a **sequential reasoning** question.

### Step 1: Identify Design Outputs
- Compile all design outputs from the prior design phases
- These include **drawings, schematics, BOMs, software code, test reports, verification/validation reports, labeling, packaging specs**
- Confirm all outputs have been **approved via Design Review**

### Step 2: Develop Production-Ready Specifications
- Convert R&D drawings into **manufacturing-grade drawings** with GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing)
- Define **raw material specifications** (grade, supplier, certifications)
- Specify **incoming inspection criteria** for every component

### Step 3: Map the Manufacturing Process Flow
- Create a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** for every assembly
- Identify **Critical Process Parameters (CPPs)** and **Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs)**
- Conduct **PFMEA (Process Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**

### Step 4: Qualify Equipment, Tooling, and Environment
- Perform **IQ/OQ/PQ** on all production equipment
- Validate **cleanroom conditions** (ISO Class 7, 8) if applicable
- Validate **sterilization processes** (ISO 11135 for EtO, ISO 11137 for gamma)

### Step 5: Establish Quality Controls
- Define **in-process inspection** points
- Define **final product release tests**
- Implement **statistical process control (SPC)** for CTQ parameters

### Step 6: Update the Quality System Documentation
- Update the **Device Master Record (DMR)**
- Generate **Device History Record (DHR) templates**
- Update **CAPA** and **complaint handling** procedures

### Step 7: Train Production Personnel
- Conduct **Training Needs Analysis (TNA)**
- Execute **documented training** for every operator
- Record **competency assessments**

### Step 8: Execute Pilot Production
- Run a **pilot batch** (typically 50–500 units depending on device class)
- Conduct **First Article Inspection (FAI)**
- Compare pilot output against **design intent**

### Step 9: Review and Approve Transfer
- Conduct a **formal Design Transfer Review** with cross-functional team
- Document the **Design Transfer Report** — the formal closure document
- Obtain **sign-off from QA/Regulatory/Manufacturing/Engineering leadership**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Process Capability for a Stent Diameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: A cardiovascular stent manufacturing line produces stents with a target diameter of **3.00 mm**. The **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** is **3.05 mm** and the **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)** is **2.95 mm**. After running a pilot of **100 units**, the mean diameter is **$\mu = 2.99$ mm** and the standard deviation is **$\sigma = 0.012$ mm**. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and assess transfer readiness.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate $C_p$ (Process Capability Index)**

$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

Substitute values:

$$C_p = \dfrac{3.05 - 2.95}{6 \times 0.012}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{0.10}{0.072}$$

$$C_p \approx 1.389$$

**Step 2: Calculate $C_{pk}$ (Shifted Process Capability)**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

Compute the upper term:

$$\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{3.05 - 2.99}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.036} \approx 1.667$$

Compute the lower term:

$$\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2.99 - 2.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.036} \approx 1.111$$

Take the minimum:

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.667,\ 1.111) = 1.111$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard**: For medical device manufacturing, $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is generally accepted, and $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is preferred for safety-critical devices like cardiovascular stents.
> 
> Here, $C_p = 1.389$ and $C_{pk} = 1.111$.
> 
> The mean is **shifted toward the LSL** (2.99 mm, below the 3.00 mm target). While $C_p$ is acceptable, $C_{pk} = 1.111$ is **below the medical device threshold of 1.33**.
> 
> **Conclusion**: The process is **not yet ready for Design Transfer**. The production team must re-center the process ($\mu \rightarrow 3.00$ mm) and/or reduce $\sigma$ before re-piloting.

**Step 4: Design Transfer Effectiveness (DTE)**

Assume the pilot produced **92 conforming units** out of **100**:

$$DTE = \dfrac{N_{\text{conforming pilot units}}}{N_{\text{total pilot units}}} = \dfrac{92}{100} = 0.92$$

> A DTE ≥ **0.95** is typically required for transfer sign-off. Here, **0.92 is below threshold** — confirming the need for process improvement.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Design Transfer Readiness

| Criterion | Threshold | Pilot Result | Status |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| $C_{pk}$ | ≥ 1.33 | 1.111 | ❌ FAIL |
| $DTE$ | ≥ 0.95 | 0.92 | ❌ FAIL |
| First Article Inspection | 0 critical defects | 3 critical defects | ❌ FAIL |
| IQ/OQ/PQ Completion | 100% | 100% | ✅ PASS |
| Operator Training | 100% personnel | 100% | ✅ PASS |
| DMR Updated | Complete | Complete | ✅ PASS |

**Decision**: **Transfer NOT approved.** Process must be re-engineered and re-piloted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Design Transfer Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Outputs Approved] --> B[Compile Design Outputs - Drawings, BOMs, Specs]
    B --> C[Develop Production-Ready Specifications]
    C --> D[Map Manufacturing Process Flow - PFMEA]
    D --> E[Qualify Equipment - IQ, OQ, PQ]
    E --> F[Establish Quality Controls - SPC, Inspections]
    F --> G[Update QMS Documentation - DMR, DHR]
    G --> H[Train Production Personnel]
    H --> I[Execute Pilot Production]
    I --> J[First Article Inspection - FAI]
    J --> K{Pilot Conformance Check}
    K -->|Pass Cpk 133, DTE 95| L[Design Transfer Review - Cross-Functional]
    K -->|Fail| M[Process Improvement Loop]
    M --> D
    L --> N[Transfer Report Sign-Off - QA, RA, Mfg, Eng]
    N --> O[End: Device Released to Production]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 6 Pillars of Design Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DT[Design Transfer Process] --> P1[Pillar 1: Production Specifications]
    DT --> P2[Pillar 2: Manufacturing Process Definition]
    DT --> P3[Pillar 3: Equipment and Tooling Qualification]
    DT --> P4[Pillar 4: Quality System Integration]
    DT --> P5[Pillar 5: Training and Personnel Competency]
    DT --> P6[Pillar 6: Pilot Production and FAI]
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> OUT[Transfer Report and Sign-Off]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Cross-Functional Design Transfer Review

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant RD as R and D Engineering
    participant MFG as Manufacturing Engineering
    participant QA as Quality Assurance
    participant RA as Regulatory Affairs
    participant OPS as Production Operations
    RD->>MFG: Handoff of Design Outputs
    MFG->>MFG: Develop Process Flow and Work Instructions
    MFG->>QA: Submit Process Validation Protocol
    QA->>QA: Execute IQ, OQ, PQ Protocols
    QA->>RA: Confirm Regulatory Compliance
    QA->>OPS: Release Training Materials
    OPS->>OPS: Conduct Pilot Production Run
    OPS->>QA: Submit First Article Inspection Report
    QA->>RD: Request Design Transfer Review Meeting
    RD->>MFG: Present Pilot Data and Cpk Results
    MFG->>QA: Confirm Process Stability
    QA->>RA: Confirm DMR and DHR Updated
    RA->>RD: Approve Transfer Report
    RD->>OPS: Authorize Full Production Release
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Device Lifecycle with Design Transfer Gate

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DesignInput
    DesignInput --> DesignOutput
    DesignOutput --> DesignVerification
    DesignVerification --> DesignValidation
    DesignValidation --> DesignTransfer
    DesignTransfer --> FullProduction
    FullProduction --> PostMarketSurveillance
    PostMarketSurveillance --> DesignChanges
    DesignChanges --> DesignVerification
    FullProduction --> [*]
    DesignTransfer --> TransferFailed : Pilot Fails
    TransferFailed --> DesignVerification : Re-engineer
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Design Transfer as per FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h). List any two elements of the Design Transfer process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Design Transfer is the phase of design control in which the device design is correctly translated into production specifications, manufacturing processes, quality system requirements, and distribution procedures, ensuring the design can be consistently and reliably manufactured at scale, as mandated by FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h) and ISO 13485:2016.

**Two Elements (1 mark):**
1. Development of production specifications (drawings, BOMs, tolerances)
2. Qualification of equipment through IQ/OQ/PQ protocols

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) in Design Transfer? State the minimum acceptable $C_{pk}$ value for medical device manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role (2 marks):** $C_{pk}$ quantifies the ability of a manufacturing process to produce output within specification limits, accounting for both process spread and centering. It is a critical metric in Design Transfer because it objectively demonstrates that the production process can consistently meet design specifications. A high $C_{pk}$ provides statistical confidence that the manufactured device will match the validated design.

**Minimum Value (1 mark):** The minimum acceptable $C_{pk}$ for medical device manufacturing is **1.33**, with **1.67** preferred for safety-critical devices.

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the six core elements (pillars) of the Design Transfer process for a medical device. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram.

**Model Answer:**

The six core elements of Design Transfer, as per FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h) and ISO 13485:2016, are:

**Pillar 1: Production Specifications (1 mark)**
Translation of R&D outputs into manufacturing-grade drawings with GD&T, complete Bill of Materials (BOMs), raw material specifications with qualified supplier identification, and incoming inspection criteria for every component.

**Pillar 2: Manufacturing Process Definition (1 mark)**
Creation of process flow diagrams for each assembly, identification of Critical Process Parameters (CPPs) and Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs), and execution of PFMEA (Process Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) to identify and mitigate production risks.

**Pillar 3: Equipment and Tooling Qualification (1 mark)**
Execution of IQ (Installation Qualification), OQ (Operational Qualification), and PQ (Performance Qualification) protocols on all production equipment. For sterile devices, validation of sterilization processes (ISO 11135 EtO, ISO 11137 gamma) and cleanroom environment (ISO 14644).

**Pillar 4: Quality System Integration (1 mark)**
Integration of design outputs into the Quality Management System, including updating the Device Master Record (DMR), generating Device History Record (DHR) templates, and implementing CAPA mechanisms for production-scale defect management.

**Pillar 5: Training and Personnel Competency (1 mark)**
Conducting Training Needs Analysis (TNA), executing documented training for every production operator on the new device's processes, and recording competency assessments for each critical process step.

**Pillar 6: Pilot Production and First Article Inspection (1 mark)**
Running a pilot batch (typically 50–500 units) under full production conditions, performing First Article Inspection (FAI) on multiple units, calculating $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ to verify process capability, and computing Design Transfer Effectiveness (DTE).

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
*(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram — The 6 Pillars of Design Transfer)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A syringe barrel production line has a target inner diameter of 10.00 mm with USL = 10.05 mm and LSL = 9.95 mm. Pilot data from 100 units yields $\mu = 10.02$ mm and $\sigma = 0.015$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and comment on Design Transfer readiness.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate $C_p$ (2 marks)**

$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{10.05 - 9.95}{6 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.090} \approx 1.111$$

**Step 2: Calculate $C_{pk}$ (3 marks)**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

Upper term:

$$\dfrac{10.05 - 10.02}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.03}{0.045} \approx 0.667$$

Lower term:

$$\dfrac{10.02 - 9.95}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.045} \approx 1.556$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.667,\ 1.556) = 0.667$$

**Step 3: Comment on Transfer Readiness (2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Conclusion**: With $C_{pk} = 0.667$, the process is **statistically incapable**. The minimum acceptable $C_{pk}$ for medical devices is **1.33**, and for safety-critical components like syringe barrels, **1.67 is preferred**. The process mean is shifted high (10.02 mm vs 10.00 mm target), and the standard deviation (0.015 mm) is too large for the tight tolerance window. **Design Transfer must be REJECTED.** The process requires re-centering and variance reduction before re-piloting.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the regulatory requirements for Design Transfer under FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h) and ISO 13485:2016. How do these frameworks differ in their treatment of Design Transfer?

**Model Answer:**

**FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h) — Design Transfer (3 marks):**
- The U.S. FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR) explicitly mandates Design Transfer as a **distinct subsection** of the design controls (820.30)
- Requires manufacturers to "establish and maintain procedures to ensure that the device design is correctly translated into production specifications"
- Emphasizes **production processes**, **equipment qualification**, and **quality system integration**
- The transfer must be **documented** in the Design History File (DHF)
- Failure to perform Design Transfer is a **citable deficiency** (Form 483 observation)

**ISO 13485:2016, Section 7.3.8 — Design and Development Transfer (3 marks):**
- The international standard treats Design Transfer as part of **Design and Development** (Section 7.3)
- Requires the organization to "transfer design and development outputs to manufacturing" in a manner that is "suitable for manufacture"
- Emphasizes **risk management** integration (per ISO 14971)
- Requires verification that outputs are **suitable for production** before full release
- Documentation requirements are integrated into the **Design and Development File**

**Key Differences (1 mark):**
- FDA treats Design Transfer as a **standalone, explicitly named** control; ISO 13485 embeds it as a **sub-element** of design and development
- ISO 13485 has stronger **risk management** language (linkage to ISO 14971)
- FDA has stronger **legal enforceability** (Warning Letters, Consent Decrees); ISO 13485 is enforced via **Notified Body audits** and CE Marking
- Both harmonize in principle — manufacturers complying with ISO 13485 generally satisfy FDA expectations

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of IQ/OQ/PQ in Design Transfer. What is the significance of PFMEA, and how does it differ from DFMEA?

**Model Answer:**

**IQ/OQ/PQ in Design Transfer (4 marks):**

**IQ (Installation Qualification)** verifies that equipment is installed correctly per manufacturer specifications — proper utilities (power, compressed air, water), correct location, calibration, and software installation. *Example: Verifying that a CNC machine is leveled and connected to the correct power supply.*

**OQ (Operational Qualification)** verifies that equipment operates within its designed operational ranges — testing boundary conditions, alarm systems, and control parameters. *Example: Running a CNC machine at minimum and maximum RPM to confirm stable operation.*

**PQ (Performance Qualification)** verifies that equipment consistently produces acceptable output under simulated production conditions — typically run with actual product or representative samples, often in triplicate. *Example: Machining 30 stent components and confirming all meet dimensional specifications.*

Together, IQ/OQ/PQ provides **documented evidence** that the manufacturing process is capable and reproducible — a core requirement of Design Transfer sign-off.

**PFMEA vs DFMEA (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DFMEA (Design FMEA) | PFMEA (Process FMEA) |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Focus** | Design of the product | Manufacturing of the product |
| **Performed by** | R&D / Design Engineering | Manufacturing / Process Engineering |
| **When** | During design phase | During Design Transfer / process development |
| **Failure Modes** | Design flaws (wrong material, geometry) | Process flaws (wrong temperature, operator error) |
| **Output** | Design changes / risk reduction | Work instructions, process controls, SPC |
| **KTU Relevance** | Module 2 (Design Inputs/Outputs) | Module 3 (Design Transfer) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction**: DFMEA asks *"What if the design itself fails?"* while PFMEA asks *"What if the manufacturing process fails to produce the design as intended?"* Both are mandatory for medical device compliance.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Design Transfer Questions**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Transfer with Validation**: Students often write that "Design Transfer validates the device." Wrong! Validation happens *before* transfer. Transfer ensures the *validated design* can be *manufactured consistently*. **2–3 mark loss** on definitional questions.
> 
> 2. **Skipping IQ/OQ/PQ**: In Part B 14-mark answers, students often list 4–5 elements of transfer but **forget equipment qualification**. This is a dedicated 3–4 mark component. Always include it.
> 
> 3. **Misquoting $C_{pk}$ Thresholds**: Writing $C_{pk} \geq 1.00$ (general industry) instead of **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** (medical device) costs **1 mark** in numerical problems.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Mean-Centering**: Students often calculate only $C_p$ and forget $C_{pk}$. The KTU examiner will specifically test $C_{pk}$ because it captures process *centering*, which is critical in transfer decisions. **2-mark loss** typical.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting Documentation**: Never omit DMR, DHF, DHR, and CAPA references in long answers. KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for documentation awareness.
> 
> 6. **Conflating DFMEA with PFMEA**: These are tested as a deliberate **trick question** in 14-mark answers. Use a comparison table to be safe.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design Transfer Process**

- **Definition**: Translation of validated design into production-ready manufacturing specifications (FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h), ISO 13485:2016 §7.3.8)
- **Position in Lifecycle**: Comes *after* Design Validation, *before* Full Production
- **Six Pillars**: Production Specs, Process Definition, IQ/OQ/PQ, QMS Integration, Training, Pilot Production
- **IQ/OQ/PQ**: Equipment qualification triad — Installation, Operational, Performance
- **$C_{pk}$ Threshold**: ≥ 1.33 (general medical device), ≥ 1.67 (safety-critical)
- **$C_{pk}$ Formula**: $C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$
- **$C_p$ Formula**: $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$
- **DTE Metric**: $\geq 0.95$ for transfer sign-off
- **DFMEA vs PFMEA**: Design failures vs Process failures
- **Key Documents**: DMR (Device Master Record), DHF (Design History File), DHR (Device History Record)
- **Regulatory Bodies**: FDA (USA), Notified Bodies (EU via MDR), CDSCO (India)
- **Sterilization Standards**: ISO 11135 (EtO), ISO 11137 (Gamma)
- **Cleanroom Standards**: ISO 14644 (Class 7, 8 typical for medical devices)
- **KTU High-Yield Keywords**: Design Transfer, IQ/OQ/PQ, $C_{pk}$, PFMEA, DMR, DHR, Pilot Production, First Article Inspection
- **Key Risk**: Process mean-shifting (low $C_{pk}$ despite good $C_p$) — the most common transfer failure mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid**: *"Design Transfer = making the lab success repeatable on the factory floor."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Software-Hardware Test Plan (SHTP)** for medical devices is a formal, documented engineering artifact that defines the systematic strategy, scope, resources, schedule, procedures, and acceptance criteria used to verify and validate (V&V) the integrated software and hardware subsystems of a medical device. It is a mandatory deliverable under international regulatory frameworks — most notably **IEC 62304** (Medical Device Software Life-Cycle Processes), **IEC 60601-1** (Medical Electrical Equipment Safety), and **ISO 13485** (Quality Management Systems for Medical Devices). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A medical device test plan is **NOT** a generic software test plan. It must explicitly address **patient safety**, **risk management per ISO 14971**, **software safety classification (Class A/B/C)**, and **hardware-software interaction faults**, because a single undetected bug can lead to patient harm or even death. ### 1.2 Core Components of a Test Plan (Master Checklist) | S.No | Component | Purpose | |------|-----------|---------| | 1 | Test Plan Identifier & Version | Traceability and document control | | 2 | Scope (In/Out) | Defines what is and is NOT tested | | 3 | References & Standards | IEC 62304, IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971 | | 4 | Test Items | Hardware modules, software units, integrated system | | 5 | Software Safety Class | Class A, B, or C per IEC 62304 | | 6 | Test Strategy | Black-box, white-box, hazard-based | | 7 | Pass/Fail Criteria | Quantifiable acceptance thresholds | | 8 | Test Environment | Lab equipment, simulators, patient simulators | | 9 | Test Deliverables | Test cases, reports, traceability matrix | | 10 | Roles & Responsibilities | Test engineers, clinical experts, QA | ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are a **chief airworthiness inspector certifying a new commercial aircraft** before its first commercial flight. You do not just "fly it once and see." You have a **certification test plan** that lists every system — engines, avionics, landing gear, oxygen masks, evacuation slides — and specifies how each will be stressed, under what conditions, with what pass criteria, and who is authorized to sign off. A **Software-Hardware Test Plan for a medical device works identically**, except the "passengers" are **patients whose lives depend on the device** — for example, an insulin pump, a defibrillator, a ventilator, or a pacemaker. The test plan is the regulatory-grade equivalent of the aircraft's certification document. ### 1.4 Why a Separate Plan Exists for Medical Devices A consumer smartphone can tolerate a software crash (user restarts the app). A **medical device cannot**, because: - **Patient harm** may result (e.g., wrong drug dose delivered by infusion pump). - **Regulatory bodies** (US FDA, EU MDR, CDSCO in India) demand documented evidence of safety. - **Hardware-software interaction bugs** are especially dangerous — e.g., sensor noise misinterpreted by software leading to therapy overdose. - **Audit trail** must show that every requirement was traced to a test case, and every test case was executed with a documented result. > [!NOTE] > **Key Regulatory Mapping for KTU Exam:** > - **IEC 62304** → Governs **software** development and testing. > - **IEC 60601-1** → Governs **hardware** electrical safety and essential performance. > - **ISO 14971** → Governs **risk management**, which drives test prioritization. > - **ISO 13485** → Governs the **quality system** that wraps around the test plan. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Test Plan Framework ### 2.1 The Hierarchical Testing Pyramid (IEC 62304 Aligned) Medical device testing follows a **strict pyramid hierarchy** (V-Model). Each level corresponds to a design phase on the left and a test phase on the right. ``` System-Level Validation Test ← Verifies user needs, clinical use / \ Integration Test (HW + SW) ← Verifies module interactions / \ Software Unit Test | Hardware Unit Test ← Verifies individual components ``` **Step 1 — Software Unit Testing:** - Tests individual functions, modules, or classes. - **White-box** testing (code path coverage). - For Class C software, **100% structural coverage** (branch + MC/DC) is required. **Step 2 — Hardware Unit Testing:** - Verifies individual PCBs, sensors, actuators, power supplies against hardware specifications. - Includes **EMC pre-compliance tests**, **thermal tests**, **power-on self-test (POST)** verification. **Step 3 — Integration Testing:** - **Hardware-Software Integration (HSI)** — the most critical stage for medical devices. - Tests interfaces: ADC readings → software algorithms, software commands → actuator drivers. - Uses **interface simulators** (e.g., patient simulators, signal injectors). **Step 4 — System-Level Validation:** - Verifies the **complete device** against clinical/user requirements. - Performed in a **simulated use environment** (and in some cases, on actual patients via clinical trials). ### 2.2 Software Safety Classification (IEC 62304) — Drives Test Rigor | Safety Class | Definition | Required Documentation | Required Test Coverage | |--------------|------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Class A** | No injury or damage to health is possible | Software system test | Functional testing | | **Class B** | Non-serious injury is possible | Software system + integration test | Statement coverage + integration test | | **Class C** | Death or serious injury is possible | All of the above + detailed design docs | **MC/DC (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage) ≥ 100%** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The higher the safety class, the **stricter** the testing and documentation requirements. A **Class C** device (e.g., pacemaker, ventilator, defibrillator) requires **MC/DC coverage** — a metric that ensures every condition in a decision has independently affected the outcome. ### 2.3 Test Plan Document Structure (Master Template) Below is the **canonical structure** of a Software-Hardware Test Plan as required by IEC 62304 Section 5.7: **1. Introduction & Scope** - 1.1 Purpose - 1.2 Scope of Testing (In/Out) - 1.3 Definitions and Acronyms - 1.4 References **2. Test Items** - 2.1 Software Modules List - 2.2 Hardware Subsystems List - 2.3 Integrated System Description - 2.4 Software Safety Classification (A/B/C) **3. Test Strategy** - 3.1 Testing Levels (Unit / Integration / System) - 3.2 Test Types (Functional, Performance, Security, Usability, EMC, Safety) - 3.3 Test Approach (Manual / Automated / Combined) **4. Pass/Fail Criteria** - 4.1 Functional Acceptance Thresholds - 4.2 Defect Severity Classification (Critical / Major / Minor) - 4.3 Exit Criteria for Each Test Phase **5. Test Environment** - 5.1 Hardware Test Equipment (Oscilloscopes, patient simulators, multimeters) - 5.2 Software Test Tools (Static analyzers, unit test frameworks) - 5.3 Test Fixtures and Harnesses **6. Test Deliverables** - 6.1 Test Cases and Test Procedures - 6.2 Test Data - 6.3 Test Logs and Defect Reports - 6.4 Traceability Matrix - 6.5 Test Summary Report **7. Schedule, Resources, and Responsibilities** ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Test Metrics & Formulas Although this is a process-oriented topic, the following **quantitative metrics** are routinely examined in KTU papers. $$ \text{Test Coverage (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of test cases executed}}{\text{Total number of test cases planned}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Statement Coverage (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of statements executed}}{\text{Total number of statements}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Branch Coverage (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of branches executed}}{\text{Total number of branches}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Defect Density} = \frac{\text{Number of confirmed defects}}{\text{Size of software (KLOC or FP)}} $$ $$ \text{Test Effectiveness (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Defects found in testing}}{\text{Defects found in testing + Defects found in field}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{MTBCF (Mean Time Between Critical Failures)} = \frac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of critical failures}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** The FDA explicitly reviews test coverage and defect density metrics during **510(k) and PMA submissions**. A pacemaker manufacturer with low MC/DC coverage will face regulatory rejection. These metrics are the **currency of trust** between a manufacturer and a regulator. ### 2.5 Risk-Based Test Prioritization (ISO 14971 Driven) Not all tests are equally important. The test plan must rank test cases by **risk severity × probability**, formalized via a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**. $$ \text{RPN} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Occurrence (O)} \times \text{Detectability (D)} $$ Test cases addressing hazards with **high RPN** are executed **first** and with **maximum rigor**. This is the **only mathematically defensible way** to allocate finite testing resources in a Class C medical device. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Traceability Matrix ### 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure: Building a Medical Device Test Plan Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** that a KTU examiner expects to see when a student answers a "design a test plan" question. **Step 1 — Identify Regulatory Scope and Applicable Standards** - Determine device classification (Class I, II, or III per FDA / EU MDR). - Identify software safety class (A, B, or C per IEC 62304 Clause 4.3). - List applicable standards: **IEC 62304, IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971, ISO 13485, IEC 62366 (Usability)**. **Step 2 — Decompose the System into Test Items** - Software: List every module, function, and software-of-unknown-provenance (SOUP). - Hardware: List every PCB, sensor, actuator, power subsystem, and mechanical assembly. - Interface: List every **hardware-software interface** (ADC channels, GPIO, communication buses like SPI/I²C/UART). **Step 3 — Derive Test Cases from Requirements** - Each user requirement → at least one system test case. - Each software requirement → at least one unit test case. - Each hardware requirement → at least one hardware test case. - This produces a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** — the **backbone** of regulatory submissions. **Step 4 — Define Test Levels and Their Acceptance Criteria** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unit Test Exit Criteria} &= \text{Statement Coverage} \geq 100\% \text{ (Class A)} \\ &\quad \text{+ Branch Coverage} \geq 100\% \text{ (Class B)} \\ &\quad \text{+ MC/DC Coverage} \geq 100\% \text{ (Class C)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Integration Test Exit Criteria} &= \text{All HW-SW interfaces exercised} \\ &\quad \text{+ No open Critical/Major defects} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{System Test Exit Criteria} &= \text{All user requirements verified} \\ &\quad \text{+ All risk control measures verified} \\ &\quad \text{+ EMC and safety tests passed} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Specify Test Environment and Tools** - For a defibrillator, the test plan must specify: **patient simulator (e.g., Fluke Impulse 7000DP)**, **defibrillator analyzer**, **oscilloscope with isolated probes**, and **automated test rig** for energy discharge. - For software: **unit test framework (e.g., Unity, CppUTest, GoogleTest)**, **static analysis tool (e.g., LDRA, Polyspace)**, **code coverage tool**. **Step 6 — Define Defect Classification and Severity** - **Critical (Severity 1):** Causes patient harm or death, or device failure with no fallback. Must be fixed before release. - **Major (Severity 2):** Causes degraded performance or work-around required. Must be fixed before release. - **Minor (Severity 3):** Cosmetic or documentation. Can be deferred with justification. **Step 7 — Build the Traceability Matrix** The **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is the most important deliverable. It establishes a bidirectional link between **requirements → design → implementation → test cases → defects**. $$ \text{Traceability Chain} = \text{Requirement} \leftrightarrow \text{Design Item} \leftrightarrow \text{Code Unit} \leftrightarrow \text{Test Case} \leftrightarrow \text{Test Result} $$ A bidirectional link is **mandatory** under IEC 62304 Clause 5.1.6. If a requirement has **no test case**, the requirement is **unverified** and the device **cannot be released**. **Step 8 — Define Schedule, Roles, and Resources** - Identify test engineers, clinical/regulatory experts, independent V&V team. - Note: For **Class C devices**, the FDA often expects an **independent V&V team** (separate from the design team). **Step 9 — Specify Test Deliverables** - Test Plan (this document) - Test Design Specification - Test Case Specification - Test Procedure Specification - Test Log - Anomaly/Defect Report - Test Summary Report - Traceability Matrix **Step 10 — Define Exit Criteria for the Entire Test Phase** - All critical and major defects closed. - Test coverage targets met. - Risk control measures verified. - Regulatory checklist (FDA / CE / CDSCO) signed off. - Test Summary Report approved by QA. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Test Plan for a Digital Infusion Pump (Class C) Let us illustrate the above with a **worked numerical/qualitative example** — designing a test plan for a **Class C digital infusion pump**. **Device Description:** A bedside infusion pump that delivers IV fluids and drugs at controlled rates (0.1 – 1000 mL/h). It has a touchscreen, a stepper-motor-driven syringe pump, pressure and air-in-line sensors, and embedded firmware (Class C software). **Step 1 — Regulatory Identification:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Device Class (FDA)} &= \text{Class II} \\ \text{Software Safety Class (IEC 62304)} &= \text{Class C (death possible from overdose or air embolism)} \\ \text{Applicable Standards} &= \text{IEC 62304, IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-2-24, ISO 14971, ISO 13485} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Test Item Decomposition:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Software Test Items} &= \begin{cases} \text{1. Flow rate control algorithm} \\ \text{2. Drug library lookup module} \\ \text{3. Alarm manager} \\ \text{4. Touchscreen UI} \\ \text{5. Communication stack (Wi-Fi/HL7)} \end{cases} \\ \text{Hardware Test Items} &= \begin{cases} \text{1. Stepper motor driver PCB} \\ \text{2. Pressure sensor signal conditioning} \\ \text{3. Air-in-line ultrasonic sensor} \\ \text{4. Battery and power supply} \\ \text{5. Touchscreen controller} \end{cases} \\ \text{Interface Test Items} &= \begin{cases} \text{1. Sensor ADC → flow algorithm} \\ \text{2. Algorithm → motor PWM command} \\ \text{3. UI → alarm manager} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Sample Test Cases (Illustrative):** | Test ID | Type | Requirement | Test Description | Pass Criteria | |---------|------|-------------|------------------|---------------| | TC-UNIT-001 | Unit | Flow algorithm shall set rate to 50 mL/h | Inject setpoint 50; read PWM duty | Duty = 50% ± 2% | | TC-HW-001 | Hardware | Pressure sensor accuracy | Apply 100 mmHg; read ADC | ADC value within ±5% of expected | | TC-INT-001 | Integration | Air-in-line alarm | Inject 0.1 mL air bubble | Alarm fires within 2 seconds | | TC-SYS-001 | System | Bolus delivery | Program 5 mL bolus in 30 s | Delivered volume = 5 ± 0.1 mL | | TC-SAF-001 | Safety | Power failure during infusion | Cut power at t=10 s | Audible alarm within 1 s; resume state saved | **Step 4 — Coverage Targets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unit Test} &: \text{MC/DC} \geq 100\% \text{ (Class C requirement)} \\ \text{Integration Test} &: \text{All HW-SW interfaces exercised} \\ \text{System Test} &: \text{All 87 user requirements have at least one passing test} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Defect Severity Assignment (Sample):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Critical Example} &: \text{Flow rate 10x programmed value (overdose)} \rightarrow \text{BLOCK release} \\ \text{Major Example} &: \text{False air-in-line alarm during normal operation} \rightarrow \text{FIX before release} \\ \text{Minor Example} &: \text{UI text typo} \rightarrow \text{DEFER with justification} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Test Environment Definition:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Hardware Tools} &= \text{Fluke IDA-6 infusion device analyzer, oscilloscope, pressure calibrator} \\ \text{Software Tools} &= \text{Unity + CppUTest, LDRA static analysis, VectorCAST} \\ \text{Test Fixtures} &= \text{Syringe load simulator, environmental chamber (temp/humidity)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — RTM Excerpt:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{REQ-001} &\to \text{DESIGN-FlowAlgo} \to \text{CODE-flow.c} \to \text{TC-UNIT-001} \to \text{PASS} \\ \text{REQ-014} &\to \text{DESIGN-AirSensor} \to \text{CODE-air.c} \to \text{TC-INT-001} \to \text{PASS} \end{aligned} $$ This exhaustive trace satisfies **IEC 62304 Clause 5.1.6** and is the **minimum evidence** the FDA expects in a 510(k) submission. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 V-Model for Medical Device Verification & Validation ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef designNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef testNode fill:#059669,stroke:#059669,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef linkNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px URS[User Requirements]:::designNode SRS[System Requirements]:::designNode ARCH[System Architecture]:::designNode SWD[Software Detailed Design]:::designNode HWD[Hardware Detailed Design]:::designNode CODE[Software Code]:::linkNode HW[Hardware Build]:::linkNode SYSV[System Validation Test]:::testNode SYST[System Integration Test]:::testNode SWIN[SW Integration Test]:::testNode HWIN[HW Integration Test]:::testNode UNIT[Software Unit Test]:::testNode HWUT[Hardware Unit Test]:::testNode URS -. Verifies .-> SYSV SRS -. Verifies .-> SYST ARCH -. Verifies .-> SWIN ARCH -. Verifies .-> HWIN SWD -. Verifies .-> UNIT HWD -. Verifies .-> HWUT URS ==> SRS SRS ==> ARCH ARCH ==> SWD ARCH ==> HWD SWD ==> CODE HWD ==> HW ``` ### 4.2 Test Plan Development Workflow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef inputNode fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff classDef processNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff classDef outputNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff INPUT[Inputs
Risk File, SRS, SDS, Hazard Analysis]:::inputNode P1[Step 1: Identify Reg Standards
IEC 62304, IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971]:::processNode P2[Step 2: Classify Software
Class A / B / C]:::processNode P3[Step 3: Decompose Test Items
SW + HW + Interface]:::processNode P4[Step 4: Derive Test Cases
from Requirements]:::processNode P5[Step 5: Define Coverage Targets
MC/DC for Class C]:::processNode P6[Step 6: Specify Environment
Tools, Fixtures, Simulators]:::processNode P7[Step 7: Build RTM
Requirements to Test]:::processNode P8[Step 8: Define Exit Criteria
Defect Closure + Coverage]:::processNode OUTPUT[Output
Approved Test Plan Document
+ Test Cases + RTM]:::outputNode INPUT ==> P1 ==> P2 ==> P3 ==> P4 ==> P5 ==> P6 ==> P7 ==> P8 ==> OUTPUT ``` ### 4.3 Hardware-Software Integration Test Architecture (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef sensorNode fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff classDef swNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff classDef actNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff classDef testNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000 S1[Pressure Sensor]:::sensorNode S2[Air-in-Line Sensor]:::sensorNode S3[Touchscreen]:::sensorNode A1[Stepper Motor]:::actNode A2[Buzzer / Speaker]:::actNode A3[LCD Display]:::actNode SW1[Signal Conditioning SW]:::swNode SW2[Control Algorithm SW]:::swNode SW3[Alarm Manager SW]:::swNode SW4[UI Handler SW]:::swNode T1[Sensor Simulator]:::testNode T2[Patient Simulator]:::testNode T3[Logic Analyzer]:::testNode T1 --> S1 T1 --> S2 T2 --> S3 S1 --> SW1 --> SW2 S2 --> SW1 --> SW3 S3 --> SW4 SW2 --> A1 SW3 --> A2 SW3 --> A3 SW4 --> A3 T3 -. Monitors .-> SW2 T3 -. Monitors .-> SW3 ``` ### 4.4 Risk-Based Test Prioritization Matrix (RPN Driven) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef highNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff classDef medNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000 classDef lowNode fill:#22c55e,stroke:#22c55e,color:#ffffff H1[Overdose due to flow algorithm bug
RPN = 9 x 4 x 2 = 72]:::highNode H2[Air embolism missed by sensor
RPN = 9 x 3 x 2 = 54]:::highNode H3[False alarm causing alarm fatigue
RPN = 6 x 5 x 4 = 120]:::highNode H4[UI typo in drug library
RPN = 3 x 2 x 6 = 36]:::medNode H5[Display brightness variation
RPN = 2 x 3 x 7 = 42]:::lowNode H1 --> TEST1[Priority 1: MC/DC + HALT test] H2 --> TEST2[Priority 1: Fault injection test] H3 --> TEST2 H4 --> TEST3[Priority 2: UI review test] H5 --> TEST4[Priority 3: Cosmetic verification] ``` ### 4.5 Traceability Matrix Architecture (Linking Layers) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef reqNode fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff classDef desNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff classDef codeNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff classDef testNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000 classDef resNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff R1[User Requirement
UR-001 Deliver 50 mL/h]:::reqNode R2[System Requirement
SR-014 Motor PWM control]:::reqNode D1[Design
Flow Algorithm Module]:::desNode D2[Design
Motor Driver Circuit]:::desNode C1[Code
flow.c]:::codeNode C2[Code
pwm_driver.c]:::codeNode TC1[Test Case
TC-UNIT-001]:::testNode TC2[Test Case
TC-INT-002]:::testNode RES1[Result
PASS]:::resNode DEF1[Defect
DEF-007]:::resNode R1 --> D1 --> C1 --> TC1 --> RES1 R2 --> D2 --> C2 --> TC2 --> DEF1 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a Software-Hardware Test Plan for a medical device. List any four components of the test plan as per IEC 62304.* **Model Answer:** A Software-Hardware Test Plan is a formal regulatory document that defines the strategy, scope, resources, procedures, and acceptance criteria used to verify and validate the integrated software and hardware of a medical device. It ensures patient safety and regulatory compliance. Four key components (per IEC 62304 Clause 5.7) are: 1. **Test items** — software modules, hardware subsystems, interfaces 2. **Test approach / strategy** — black-box, white-box, hazard-based testing 3. **Pass/Fail criteria** — quantifiable acceptance thresholds 4. **Test deliverables** — test cases, traceability matrix, test summary report *Marking Tip: Define the term (1 mark) + list 4 components (2 marks).* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the concept of MC/DC coverage and state which safety class of software (per IEC 62304) requires it.* **Model Answer:** **MC/DC (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage)** is a structural coverage metric that requires every Boolean condition in a decision to be shown to independently affect the decision outcome. It is stricter than branch coverage because it demands that each condition's effect be demonstrated **in isolation**, not just within a combination. $$ \text{MC/DC} = \frac{\text{Number of independent condition-outcome pairs covered}}{\text{Total number of independent condition-outcome pairs}} \times 100 $$ **Safety Class:** MC/DC coverage is required for **Class C** software per IEC 62304 — i.e., software where a failure could lead to **death or serious injury** (e.g., pacemaker firmware, ventilator control software, defibrillator logic). *Marking Tip: Define MC/DC (2 marks) + state Class C (1 mark).* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the V-Model as applied to verification and validation of a medical device. Identify the four testing levels and the corresponding design activities. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a Software-Hardware Test Plan for a **digital blood pressure monitor (Class II device, Class B software)**. Your plan must include: scope, software safety class justification, test items (SW + HW + interface), test strategy, pass/fail criteria, and a sample traceability matrix for at least three requirements. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution (Part A) **Part (a) — V-Model Explanation (7 marks):** The **V-Model** maps each design phase on the left arm of the "V" to a corresponding testing phase on the right arm. It is the standard lifecycle model mandated (implicitly) by IEC 62304 and IEC 60601-1 for medical device development. The four testing levels and their corresponding design activities are: 1. **Software Unit Test ↔ Software Detailed Design** - Design: Each function/module specified in detail. - Test: Each function tested in isolation with known inputs and expected outputs. - **Class B requirement:** Statement + Branch coverage ≥ 100%. 2. **Software Integration Test ↔ Software Architecture Design** - Design: Interaction of modules (function calls, data flow). - Test: Verify modules work together, including data passing and error propagation. 3. **Hardware-Software Integration Test ↔ System Architecture Design** - Design: How software interfaces with sensors, actuators, communication buses. - Test: Inject simulated sensor signals; verify software correctly interprets them and commands hardware correctly. 4. **System Validation Test ↔ User Requirements** - Design: User needs and intended use. - Test: Verify the complete device meets user needs in a simulated clinical environment. *(Award 1.5 marks per level × 4 levels = 6 marks; remaining 1 mark for V-model definition and explanation of "verification" vs "validation" distinction.)* **Key Distinction (1 mark):** - **Verification** = "Are we building the product RIGHT?" (Design → Tests) - **Validation** = "Are we building the RIGHT product?" (User needs → Tests) **Part (b) — Test Plan for Digital Blood Pressure Monitor (7 marks):** **1. Scope (1 mark):** - **In scope:** BP measurement algorithm (oscillometric), cuff inflation/deflation hardware, LCD display, user keypad, memory storage, and HW-SW interface (pressure sensor ADC). - **Out of scope:** Clinical accuracy validation (separate clinical trial), wireless connectivity (separate test plan). **2. Software Safety Class Justification (1 mark):** - **Class B** — A software failure could cause **non-serious injury** (e.g., incorrect BP reading leading to missed hypertension diagnosis or unnecessary medication). However, it is unlikely to cause death directly, so **not Class C**. **3. Test Items (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Software} &: \text{BP algorithm, user interface, memory manager, battery monitor} \\ \text{Hardware} &: \text{Cuff pump, pressure transducer, ADC, LCD, keypad matrix} \\ \text{Interface} &: \text{ADC readings} \to \text{BP algorithm}, \text{Algorithm} \to \text{LCD display} \end{aligned} $$ **4. Test Strategy (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unit Test} &: \text{Statement + Branch coverage} \geq 100\% \text{ (Class B)} \\ \text{Integration Test} &: \text{All HW-SW interfaces exercised with boundary conditions} \\ \text{System Test} &: \text{Simulated arm with known BP values (e.g., Fluke BP Pump 2)} \end{aligned} $$ **5. Pass/Fail Criteria (1 mark):** - Systolic/Diastolic accuracy: ±3 mmHg vs reference standard. - Cuff inflation time: ≤ 10 seconds to 180 mmHg. - Display refresh: ≤ 1 second. **6. Sample Traceability Matrix (1 mark):** | Req ID | Requirement | Design Item | Test Case | Result | |--------|-------------|-------------|-----------|--------| | UR-001 | Measure BP from 0–250 mmHg | Oscillometric algorithm | TC-SYS-001 | PASS | | SR-005 | Cuff auto-deflate on measurement end | Motor driver + valve | TC-INT-003 | PASS | | SR-012 | Low battery alarm at 20% | Battery monitor SW | TC-UNIT-014 | PASS | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Describe the **Software Safety Classification (A, B, C)** per IEC 62304. With a suitable example for each class, explain how the classification affects the depth of testing and documentation required. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **portable ECG monitor** is being developed as a **Class II device with Class C software**. Design the **integration test strategy** for this device, including: identification of HW-SW interfaces, types of integration tests, pass/fail criteria, and a justification for using an **independent V&V team**. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution (Part B) **Part (a) — Software Safety Classification (7 marks):** | Class | Definition | Example | Test Coverage Required | |-------|------------|---------|------------------------| | **A** | No injury or damage to health possible | Software for hospital bed positioning | Functional test only | | **B** | Non-serious injury possible | Software for electronic thermometer, BP monitor | Statement + Branch coverage | | **C** | Death or serious injury possible | Software for pacemaker, ventilator, defibrillator, infusion pump | **MC/DC ≥ 100%** + detailed design docs | **Effect on Testing (2 marks):** - **Class A** requires only system-level functional testing. Unit testing is minimal. - **Class B** requires unit, integration, and system testing. Statement and branch coverage must be demonstrated. - **Class C** requires **all of the above PLUS** detailed design documentation, **MC/DC coverage**, and **independent V&V team**. **Effect on Documentation (2 marks):** - **Class A:** Software system test documentation only. - **Class B:** Software system + integration test documentation. - **Class C:** **All** documentation including software architecture, detailed design, unit test, integration test, system test, and traceability matrix. *(Distribute marks: 1.5 per class for definition + example; 2 marks for testing impact; 1.5 marks for documentation impact.)* **Part (b) — Integration Test Strategy for Portable ECG Monitor (7 marks):** **1. HW-SW Interface Identification (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Interfaces} &= \begin{cases} \text{1. ECG electrode inputs} \to \text{Analog front end (AFE)} \to \text{ADC} \to \text{DSP filter} \\ \text{2. DSP filtered ECG} \to \text{Heart rate (HR) algorithm} \to \text{LCD display} \\ \text{3. HR algorithm} \to \text{Arrhythmia detector} \to \text{Visual + audible alarm} \\ \text{4. Patient keypad} \to \text{UI manager} \to \text{Memory (ECG storage)} \\ \text{5. Battery monitor} \to \text{Power management SW} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **2. Types of Integration Tests (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Type 1: Top-Down Integration} &: \text{Test ECG processing chain top-down with stubs} \\ \text{Type 2: Bottom-Up Integration} &: \text{Test drivers and HW first, integrate SW upward} \\ \text{Type 3: Continuous Integration} &: \text{Each commit triggers automated HW-in-the-loop test} \\ \text{Type 4: Fault Injection Test} &: \text{Inject lead-off, saturation, and 50/60 Hz noise; verify safe behavior} \end{aligned} $$ **3. Pass/Fail Criteria (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HR Accuracy} &: \pm 2 \text{ BPM vs reference over range } 30 - 250 \text{ BPM} \\ \text{Arrhythmia Detection Latency} &: \leq 5 \text{ seconds from onset to alarm} \\ \text{Lead-Off Detection} &: \text{Alarm within } 1 \text{ second of electrode disconnect} \\ \text{EMC Susceptibility} &: \text{No false alarm under } \pm 2 \text{ kV ESD discharge} \end{aligned} $$ **4. Independent V&V Team Justification (1.5 marks):** For **Class C software**, an **independent V&V team** (separate from the design team) is required because: - It eliminates **confirmation bias** — designers may unconsciously skip tests for code they "know" is correct. - It provides **regulatory defensibility** — the FDA explicitly recommends independence for high-risk software per FDA Guidance "General Principles of Software Validation" (2002). - It ensures **objective test coverage assessment** (MC/DC, branch coverage) without conflict of interest. - It allows **third-party scrutiny** of hazard traceability per ISO 14971. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "Verification" and "Validation."** Verification = "building the product right" (testing design). Validation = "building the right product" (testing user needs). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for confusing these. > 2. **Do NOT state "100% testing" is possible.** Always state coverage metrics (statement/branch/MC/DC) — testing can never prove the absence of defects, only their presence (Dijkstra's principle). > 3. **Do NOT omit the traceability matrix.** It is the **single most important deliverable** for regulatory submissions. A test plan without a traceability matrix is incomplete. > 4. **Do NOT assign the wrong safety class.** Infusion pumps, ventilators, pacemakers, and defibrillators are **Class C**, not Class B. BP monitors and thermometers are **Class B**. > 5. **Do NOT forget the HW-SW interface test** — this is the most failure-prone area in medical devices and is a **favourite KTU question** topic. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices:** - [ ] **Definition:** A regulatory document defining scope, strategy, items, criteria, environment, and deliverables for testing medical device software and hardware per **IEC 62304 Clause 5.7**. - [ ] **Applicable Standards:** **IEC 62304** (software lifecycle), **IEC 60601-1** (electrical safety), **ISO 14971** (risk management), **ISO 13485** (QMS), **IEC 62366** (usability). - [ ] **V-Model:** Four levels — Unit, Integration (SW + HW), System Integration, System Validation. Each level maps to a design phase. - [ ] **Software Safety Classes:** - **Class A** — No injury possible → Functional test only - **Class B** — Non-serious injury → Statement + Branch coverage - **Class C** — Death/serious injury → **MC/DC ≥ 100%** + detailed design docs + independent V&V team - [ ] **Key Test Metrics:** Test Coverage, Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, MC/DC, Defect Density, Test Effectiveness, MTBCF, RPN. - [ ] **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM):** Bidirectional link Requirement ↔ Design ↔ Code ↔ Test Case ↔ Result. **Mandatory** under IEC 62304 Clause 5.1.6. - [ ] **Defect Severity Levels:** Critical (block release), Major (fix before release), Minor (defer with justification). - [ ] **HW-SW Interface Testing:** Most failure-prone area. Use patient simulators, signal injectors, and HALT (Highly Accelerated Life Test). - [ ] **Independent V&V Team:** Required for **Class C** software. Eliminates bias, provides regulatory defensibility. - [ ] **Risk-Based Test Prioritization:** RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detectability. High-RPN hazards tested first with maximum rigor. - [ ] **Verification vs Validation:** Verification = "Are we building the product RIGHT?" Validation = "Are we building the RIGHT product?" - [ ] **Exit Criteria:** All critical/major defects closed + coverage targets met + risk controls verified + regulatory checklist signed off.

topics.title: System Testing - Subsystem and Full Device
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Testing - Subsystem and Full Device

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Testing** in the context of biomedical device design refers to the systematic, protocol-driven evaluation of a complete medical device to ensure that it meets all specified functional, performance, safety, and regulatory requirements. It is performed after individual component and subsystem tests have been completed successfully and forms the final gate before clinical evaluation and market release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** System testing is the process of exercising a complete biomedical device (hardware + software + firmware + accessories) under defined operating, environmental, and stress conditions to demonstrate that the integrated system conforms to the **User Needs**, **Design Inputs**, **Intended Use**, and applicable **Regulatory Standards** (IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR 820.30).

The system testing phase is broadly classified into two hierarchical levels:

1. **Subsystem Testing** – Verification of individual functional blocks/modules of the biomedical device (e.g., sensor front-end, signal conditioning, power management, display unit, communication module) in isolation, ensuring each meets its design input specifications.

2. **Full Device Testing** – Integration of all subsystems, followed by verification and validation of the *complete* biomedical device as it would be used by the end user (clinician, patient, caregiver) in a simulated or real clinical environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – V-Model Mapping**
> In the **V-Model of medical device development**, **subsystem testing** corresponds to the lower half of the right arm (unit → integration → subsystem), while **full device testing** corresponds to **system-level verification & validation**, which feeds directly into **clinical evaluation** at the apex.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **defibrillator** (a life-saving biomedical device). You would never just assemble it and hand it to a doctor. Instead:

| Engineering Stage | Real-World Analogy |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Subsystem Test** | Testing each organ of the human body individually — heart, lungs, kidneys — in isolation to make sure each organ is healthy. |
| **Integration Test** | Stitching the organs together and checking if they communicate (does oxygenated blood from the lungs reach the heart properly?). |
| **Full Device Test** | Putting the *whole person* on a treadmill and asking them to run — does the integrated body perform the intended task? |
| **Clinical Validation** | Letting the person live their normal life and confirming they can actually play football (intended use). |

**Subsystem testing** is the "microscopic" view — we zoom into each block of the block diagram. **Full device testing** is the "macroscopic" view — we step back and watch the entire device operate end-to-end, exactly as a clinician or patient will use it.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** *“Test the bricks, then the wall, then the building.”*
> - **Bricks** = Components/Units
> - **Wall** = Subsystem
> - **Building** = Full Device

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Regulatory Benchmarks

The following metrics are standardized across global regulatory frameworks and are **mandatory knowledge** for KTU examination answers:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** typically required ≥ **2000 hours** for non-life-supporting devices; ≥ **10,000 hours** for life-supporting devices (per IEC 60601-1 guidance).
- **Basic Safety (Class I, II, III)** — patient leakage current ≤ **100 µA** (Type B), ≤ **500 µA** (Type BF), ≤ **500 µA** (Type CF) under normal and single-fault conditions.
- **EMC compliance:** EN/IEC 60601-1-2 (Electromagnetic disturbances).
- **Usability:** IEC 62366-1 — *usability engineering file* is a regulatory submission requirement.
- **Risk Management:** ISO 14971 — testing must demonstrate residual risk is acceptable.
