## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Test Pyramid for Biomedical Devices

The biomedical device testing hierarchy follows a strict bottom-up pyramid. Skipping a level is considered a **design control violation** under **FDA 21 CFR 820.30 (Design Control)** and will be flagged by KTU examiners.

```
                ▲ Clinical Validation (Human Use / Simulated Use)
               /  \
              /Full \      ← System-Level Testing (Full Device)
             /Device \
            /---------\
           / Integration \   ← Subsystem Integration Testing
          /   Testing    \
         /-----------------\
        /  Subsystem Tests  \  ← Block-Level Verification
       /   (Module-Level)    \
      /________________________\
     /  Unit / Component Tests   \  ← Resistors, ICs, Sensors
    /________________________________\
```

### 2.2 Subsystem Testing – The Why and How

A **subsystem** is a functionally cohesive cluster of components that performs a specific sub-function within the biomedical device. Common subsystems include:

- **Signal Acquisition Subsystem** (electrodes, amplifiers, filters, ADC)
- **Signal Processing / Algorithm Subsystem** (DSP, microcontrollers, firmware)
- **Display & User Interface Subsystem** (LCD, LEDs, buzzer, touchscreen)
- **Power Management Subsystem** (battery, charger, isolation, DC-DC converters)
- **Communication Subsystem** (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, USB, NFC, wired protocols)
- **Safety / Protection Subsystem** (fuses, isolation barriers, watchdog timers, alarms)

#### 2.2.1 Why Subsystem Testing?

- **Fault Localization** – Easier to pinpoint a defect when a single subsystem is isolated.
- **Compliance per Module** – Each subsystem can be benchmarked against its own design input specification.
- **Parallel Testing** – Multiple subsystems can be tested simultaneously, reducing total project time.
- **Risk Reduction** – ISO 14971 risk control measures are often implemented at the subsystem level (e.g., adding a redundant alarm subsystem).

#### 2.2.2 How is Subsystem Testing Performed?

1. **Test Plan Definition** – Define test cases, pass/fail criteria, environmental conditions, sample size.
2. **Test Bench Setup** – Build a rig that mimics the subsystem's I/O (signal generators, dummy loads, environmental chambers).
3. **Functional Verification** – Apply inputs, capture outputs, compare to specification.
4. **Boundary / Stress Tests** – Push subsystem beyond normal operating ranges (e.g., over-voltage, EMI injection, temperature extremes).
5. **Regression Testing** – Re-run all test cases after a design change to ensure no new defects are introduced.

---

### 2.3 Full Device Testing – The Why and How

**Full device testing** is the moment of truth. All previously tested subsystems are now integrated into the final housing with mechanical, electrical, and software integration complete.

#### 2.3.1 Categories of Full Device Tests

| Test Category | Purpose | Example (ECG Monitor) |
|---------------|---------|------------------------|
| **Functional Verification** | Confirm device meets all design input specifications | Lead-off detection, HR calculation accuracy |
| **Performance Verification** | Quantify accuracy, precision, range | QRS detection sensitivity ≥ 90% |
| **Safety Testing** | Electrical, mechanical, thermal safety | Patient leakage current ≤ 100 µA |
| **EMC Testing** | Immunity & emissions | ESD ±8 kV contact, ±15 kV air |
| **Environmental Testing** | Temperature, humidity, altitude, vibration | Operate at 0–40 °C, 15–95% RH |
| **Usability / Human Factors** | Per IEC 62366-1 | Nurse can arm alarm in ≤ 5 s |
| **Software Validation** | Per IEC 62304 | All alarm states triggered correctly |
| **Sterilization / Disinfection** | If reusable | withstands 1000 cycles of 70% IPA |
| **Biocompatibility** | Per ISO 10993 | Cuff material non-cytotoxic |
| **Packaging / Transit** | ISTA 2A, ASTM D4169 | Survives 1 m drop |
| **Reliability / Accelerated Life** | Predict service life | MTBF ≥ 10,000 h |

#### 2.3.2 Verification vs Validation – The Most Confused Pair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question – Always comes for 7–14 marks.**

- **Verification** – *"Are we building the device RIGHT?"* — Does the device conform to its design inputs and specifications? (Document-based, on the bench.)
- **Validation** – *"Are we building the RIGHT device?"* — Does the device meet the user's needs and intended use? (Use-based, with simulated or real users.)

> **Simple Rule:** Verification = Specs. Validation = User Needs.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU ESE calculations. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate from Mean Time Between Failures | failures / hour |
| 2 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential distribution) | dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 3 | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$ | Sample size for verification (Gaussian) | $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ for 95% confidence |
| 4 | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | Sample size for attribute data (proportion) | $E$ = margin of error |
| 5 | $S_{ens} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Sensitivity (true positive rate) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_{spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Specificity (true negative rate) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | dimensionless |
| 8 | $NPV = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FN}$ | Negative Predictive Value | dimensionless |
| 9 | $A_{accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall diagnostic accuracy | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{leak} = \dfrac{V}{X_{c}} = V \cdot \omega C$ | Patient leakage current estimate | Amperes, $X_c = 1/(\omega C)$ |
| 11 | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities (Six Sigma) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $A_{vailability} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System availability | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 13 | $t_{acc} = t_{use} \cdot AF^{n}$ | Accelerated life test time (Arrhenius) | hours; $AF$ = acceleration factor |
| 14 | $\eta = 1 - \alpha$ | Confidence level where $\alpha$ is significance | $\eta = 0.95$ for 95% CI |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- $\lambda \cdot t \ll 1$ ensures linear reliability approximation.
- $E$ (margin of error) is typically 5% (0.05) for medical devices.
- For finite populations, apply **finite population correction**: multiply $n$ by $\dfrac{N}{N + n - 1}$.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-clinical Bench Testing** (in vitro) reduces animal and human trial costs.
- **HALT/HASS** (Highly Accelerated Life Test / Stress Screening) catches infant-mortality defects before shipment.
- **DFMEA → Subsystem Test** traceability ensures every identified failure mode has a test.
- **Software regression suites** (per IEC 62304) keep firmware bugs out of released devices.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Sample Size for Subsystem Verification Test

**Problem Statement:** A pulse oximeter subsystem must demonstrate that its SpO₂ measurement has an accuracy of ±2% with 95% confidence. Historical standard deviation $\sigma = 1.2\%$. Calculate the minimum sample size required for verification testing.

**Given:**
- Confidence level $\eta = 95\% \Rightarrow Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$
- Margin of error $E = 2\%$
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 1.2\%$

**Step 1 – Recall the two-sided Gaussian confidence interval formula:**

$$
n = \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^{2}
$$

This comes from inverting the confidence interval $E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

**Step 2 – Substitute the values into the formula:**

$$
n = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 1.2}{2.0} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute the numerator:**

$$
1.96 \times 1.2 = 2.352
$$

**Step 4 – Divide by $E$:**

$$
\frac{2.352}{2.0} = 1.176
$$

**Step 5 – Square the result:**

$$
n = (1.176)^{2} = 1.382976
$$

**Step 6 – Always round UP for sample size (conservative estimate):**

$$
n = \lceil 1.383 \rceil = 2
$$

But wait — applying the **finite population correction** and engineering safety margin, we typically multiply by a factor of 1.5–2.0:

$$
n_{final} = 1.5 \times 2 = 3
$$

> **Result:** A minimum of **3 samples** is required to verify the SpO₂ accuracy claim with 95% confidence. In practice, KTU expects you to round up conservatively.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Reliability at Time *t* for a Biomedical Subsystem

**Problem Statement:** A wearable ECG patch has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. The device is worn for 7 days continuously. Calculate the reliability at the end of the wear period.

**Step 1 – Convert wear time to hours:**

$$
t = 7 \times 24 = 168 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 – Apply the exponential reliability function:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3 – Substitute the values:**

$$
R(168) = e^{-(0.0002)(168)}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute the exponent:**

$$
\lambda t = 0.0002 \times 168 = 0.0336
$$

**Step 5 – Evaluate the exponential:**

$$
R(168) = e^{-0.0336} \approx 0.9670
$$

> **Result:** The probability that the ECG patch operates without failure over 7 days is **96.70%**. This is well above the typical 95% requirement for wearable medical devices.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – System Availability for a Patient Monitor

**Problem Statement:** A bedside patient monitor has $MTBF = 8760$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours. Calculate the steady-state availability.

**Step 1 – Write the availability formula:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values:**

$$
A = \frac{8760}{8760 + 4}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute denominator:**

$$
8760 + 4 = 8764
$$

**Step 4 – Compute ratio:**

$$
A = \frac{8760}{8764} \approx 0.99954
$$

> **Result:** The patient monitor is available **99.954%** of the time, equivalent to about **4 hours of downtime per year** — a typical Class II medical device target.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing the Right Test

| Design Goal | Test Type | Sample Size Formula | Pass Criterion |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|----------------|
| Prove ±2% sensor accuracy | Subsystem Verification | $n = (Z\sigma/E)^2$ | All samples within ±2% |
| Prove alarm triggers at HR < 50 bpm | Full Device Validation | Binomial exact test | 95% of trials detect alarm |
| Prove 5-year service life | Accelerated Life Test | Arrhenius model | Zero failures at $t_{use}$ |
| Prove no skin irritation | Biocompatibility (ISO 10993) | Per protocol | Pass cytotoxicity, sensitisation |
| Prove usability for nurses | Summative Usability Test | $n \ge 15$ per IEC 62366-1 | All critical tasks completed |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 – Sensitivity & Specificity of an Algorithm Subsystem

**Problem Statement:** A cardiac arrhythmia detection algorithm is tested on a database of 1000 ECG recordings. 600 are truly arrhythmic (TP+FN) and 400 are normal (TN+FP). The algorithm flags 580 arrhythmic and 420 normal cases. The confusion matrix shows: TP = 550, FN = 50, FP = 30, TN = 370. Compute sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy.

**Step 1 – Sensitivity:**

$$
S_{sens} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{550}{600} = 0.9167
$$

**Step 2 – Specificity:**

$$
S_{spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{370}{400} = 0.9250
$$

**Step 3 – Positive Predictive Value:**

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{550}{580} = 0.9483
$$

**Step 4 – Negative Predictive Value:**

$$
NPV = \frac{TN}{TN+FN} = \frac{370}{420} = 0.8810
$$

**Step 5 – Overall Accuracy:**

$$
A = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{550+370}{1000} = 0.9200
$$

> **Result:** Sensitivity 91.67%, Specificity 92.50%, PPV 94.83%, NPV 88.10%, Overall Accuracy 92.00%. For a life-supporting device, IEC 60601-1-8 and clinical guidelines typically require sensitivity ≥ 90% and specificity ≥ 85% — both thresholds are met.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – V-Model of Biomedical Device Development (with Testing Phases)

```mermaid
graph TD
    userNeeds[User Needs] --> srs[System Requirements SRS]
    srs --> subsysSpec[Subsystem Design Specs]
    subsysSpec --> detailDes[Detailed Design Schematics Code]
    detailDes --> unitTest[Unit Testing]
    unitTest --> subsysTest[Subsystem Testing]
    subsysTest --> intTest[Integration Testing]
    intTest --> fullDevTest[Full Device System Testing]
    fullDevTest --> clinVal[Clinical Validation]
    clinVal --> release[Market Release]
    srs -. verifies .-> subsysTest
    userNeeds -. validates .-> fullDevTest
    userNeeds -. validates .-> clinVal
    srs -. verifies .-> fullDevTest
```

---

```mermaid
graph LR
    sigAcq[Signal Acquisition Subsystem] --> sigProc[Signal Processing Subsystem]

    sigProc --> displayUI[Display and UI Subsystem]
    pwrMgmt --> sigAcq

    testRig1[Test Bench 1 Sensors and Amplifiers] -. tests .-> sigAcq
    testRig2[Test Bench 2 DSP and Firmware] -. tests .-> sigProc
    testRig3[Test Bench 3 Battery and EMC] -. tests .-> pwrMgmt

    testRig5[Test Bench 5 Leakage and Failsafe] -. tests .-> safety
    testRig6[Test Bench 6 LCD and Touch HMI] -. tests .-> displayUI
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Verification vs Validation Decision Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    startQ[Is the question about specifications or user needs] --> specPath[Is it about specifications]
    startQ --> userPath[Is it about user needs and intended use]
    specPath --> verify[VERIFICATION]
    userPath --> valid[VALIDATION]

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram – Full Device Test Sequence (End-to-End)

    setup --> perfVer[Performance Verification]

```
