# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Operational Qualification (OQ). List any four tests performed during OQ. **[3 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Operational Qualification (OQ) is the documented verification that an equipment or system operates as intended across its entire operating range and at its specified operational limits, when installed in its intended environment. OQ confirms that every control, alarm, and safety feature functions correctly under controlled, non-production conditions.

**Four tests performed during OQ:**

1. **Functional Tests:** Verification of all control panel buttons, knobs, and software menus.
2. **Operational Range Tests:** Operating the device at the minimum, normal, and maximum design limits.
3. **Challenge / Worst-Case Tests:** Testing under voltage fluctuations (±10 %), temperature extremes, and EMC conditions.
4. **Alarm and Interlock Tests:** Triggering each alarm condition (high/low pressure, sensor disconnect, low battery) and verifying the response time.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between IQ and PQ in any three aspects. **[3 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Installation Qualification (IQ) | Performance Qualification (PQ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Verifies correct installation, presence, and configuration | Verifies consistent reproducibility in real production |
| **Environment** | Site / controlled commissioning area | Actual production / clinical environment |
| **Operator** | Vendor / Validation Engineer | End-user (clinician, technician) |
| **Material Used** | Calibration standards, test fixtures | Real production / patient material |
| **Output Metric** | Pass / Fail checklist | Cpk, CV %, statistical trend |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Full Validation Lifecycle of a Biomedical Device

**[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Installation Qualification (IQ)** protocol. Describe any **five key activities** performed during IQ with examples for a **ventilator** used in an ICU. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **Operational Qualification (OQ)** protocol. Compare OQ with **Performance Qualification (PQ)** in a tabular form covering any **six parameters**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Installation Qualification (IQ) of a Ventilator — [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 Mark):** IQ is the documented verification that the ventilator has been correctly delivered, installed, and configured in accordance with the manufacturer's specifications, the User Requirements Specification (URS), and applicable regulatory standards (IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485).

**Five Key IQ Activities (5 × 1 Mark = 5 Marks):**

1. **Verification of Delivery and Documentation:**
   Check serial number (e.g., SN: VNT-2024-0123), model (e.g., Maquet Servo-i), accessories (breathing circuits, humidifier, test lung, power cord, O₂ hose), manuals, and software license keys against the purchase order.

2. **Verification of Installation Environment:**
   Measure ICU room temperature (18–25 °C), humidity (30–65 %), and verify clean air supply. Confirm UPS power backup is operational, earth grounding resistance is < 1 Ω, and emergency power switching is functional.

3. **Verification of Medical Gas Connections:**
   Confirm O₂ inlet pressure = **4 bar ± 0.5 bar**, Medical Air = **4 bar ± 0.5 bar**. Perform leak tests on the high-pressure hoses using soap solution or pressure decay test.

4. **Calibration of Critical Sensors:**
   Verify factory calibration certificates for the **flow sensor** (accuracy ± 5 % of reading), **pressure transducer** (± 1 cmH₂O), and **FiO₂ sensor** (± 2 %). All certificates must be traceable to NIST / NABL standards.

5. **Hardware and Software Verification:**
   Confirm firmware version (e.g., **v2.4.7**), language pack, alarm presets, and default parameters. Test the touchscreen, rotary knob, and emergency stop button.

6. **Safety and Alarm System Check (Bonus):**
   Verify that audible alarms ≥ 65 dB at 1 m, visual alarms (red/yellow/cyan) flash at the specified frequency, and that the **high-priority alarm** (e.g., disconnection) triggers within 5 seconds.

**Deliverable:** Signed **IQ Report** by the Validation Committee (QA, Engineering, ICU Head, Regulatory).

---

#### (b) Operational Qualification vs. Performance Qualification — [7 Marks]

**OQ Definition (1 Mark):** OQ is the documented verification that the ventilator operates correctly across its entire operating range and at its worst-case limits, in a controlled environment, performed by validation engineers.

**PQ Definition (1 Mark):** PQ is the documented verification that the ventilator consistently performs as intended in the actual ICU environment, operated by trained clinical staff, on real patients, with statistical proof of reproducibility.

**Comparison Table (5 Marks — 1 Mark per row × 5 selected rows):**

| Parameter | Operational Qualification (OQ) | Performance Qualification (PQ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment** | Controlled commissioning lab | Real ICU environment with patients |
| **Operator** | Validation engineer | Trained clinician / ICU nurse |
| **Material** | Calibrated test lung, reference gas | Real patient lungs, real clinical gas |
| **Test Focus** | Worst-case limits, alarms, safety | Reproducibility, repeatability, Cpk |
| **Output Metric** | Pass / Fail per parameter | Cpk ≥ 1.33, trend chart |
| **Document** | OQ Report | PQ Report + Validation Summary Report (VSR) |

---

### Question B (Option 2): Statistical Validation and GAMP Categorization

**[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define the **Process Capability Index (Cpk)**. In a PQ study of a **blood glucose meter**, 30 measurements on real patient blood samples yielded a mean $\mu = 105$ mg/dL and standard deviation $\sigma = 4$ mg/dL. The specification limits are LSL = 70 mg/dL and USL = 180 mg/dL. Calculate Cpk and interpret whether the device is acceptable for market release. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **GAMP 5 risk-based categorization** of software-controlled biomedical devices. For a **Configurable Patient Monitor with HL7 interface**, identify the GAMP category and list the IQ/OQ/PQ documentation required. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Cpk Calculation for Glucose Meter — [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Process Capability Index (Cpk) is a statistical measure of the capability of a process to produce output within specification limits. It is defined as:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Given Data (1 Mark):**

$$
\mu = 105\ \text{mg/dL},\quad \sigma = 4\ \text{mg/dL},\quad LSL = 70\ \text{mg/dL},\quad USL = 180\ \text{mg/dL}
$$

**Step 1: Calculate $z_U$ (2 Marks):**

$$
z_{U} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{180 - 105}{3 \times 4} = \frac{75}{12} = 6.25
$$

**Step 2: Calculate $z_L$ (1 Mark):**

$$
z_{L} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{105 - 70}{3 \times 4} = \frac{35}{12} = 2.917
$$

**Step 3: Take the minimum (1 Mark):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(6.25,\ 2.917) = 2.917
$$

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** Since $C_{pk} = 2.917 \geq 1.67$ (preferred for life-critical devices), the blood glucose meter is **highly capable** and is **acceptable for market release** with a very low defect rate (approximately 0.0018 ppm).

---

#### (b) GAMP 5 Categorization for a Patient Monitor — [7 Marks]

**GAMP 5 Definition (2 Marks):** GAMP 5 (Good Automated Manufacturing Practice, ISPE) is a risk-based framework for validating automated systems used in the pharmaceutical and medical device industry. It categorizes software into five categories (G1 to G5) based on complexity and risk.

**Five GAMP Categories (3 Marks):**

1. **G1 — Infrastructure Software:** Operating systems, network firmware.
2. **G2 — Non-Configured COTS:** Off-the-shelf firmware (e.g., alarm firmware).
3. **G3 — Non-Configured Custom Code:** Simple custom scripts.
4. **G4 — Configured Software:** Off-the-shelf product configured to user needs (e.g., LIMS, EMR, Patient Monitor with HL7 interface).
5. **G5 — Custom / Bespoke Software:** Fully custom applications (e.g., AI/ML diagnostic software).

**Classification and IQ/OQ/PQ Documentation for the Patient Monitor (2 Marks):**

- The Patient Monitor with HL7 interface is a **G4 Configured System** because it is built on a commercial platform but configured to the hospital's specific parameters and HL7 messaging.

**Required Documentation (2 Marks):**
1. **IQ:** HL7 interface configuration file, server credentials, network topology diagram, calibration certificates of SpO₂, ECG, NIBP modules.
2. **OQ:** Functional verification of all alarm limits, HL7 message format (ORU, ADT), audit trail logging, role-based access.
3. **PQ:** Three consecutive shifts of clinical use, statistical analysis of alarm false-positive rate, data-integrity verification in the EMR, ALCOA+ compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IQ and PQ environments** — IQ is *not* done in production; PQ *must* be done in production. Marks are deducted if you swap the environment.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the "three-batch rule"** in PQ — this is a *favorite* KTU 14-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Skipping statistical analysis in PQ** — answers that describe PQ *without* Cpk / CV are incomplete and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Using "IQ/OQ/PQ validates the device" loosely** — IQ/OQ/PQ *qualifies the equipment*, while V&V (Verification & Validation) *qualifies the design*. KTU examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 5. **Cpk formula errors** — students often forget the factor of **3σ** (using only $\sigma$). This single error loses the entire 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Missing regulatory anchors** — always cite **21 CFR 820, ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1, and GAMP 5** to score full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

### A. Definitions
- **IQ:** "Is it *installed* correctly?" — checks delivery, environment, calibration, utilities.
- **OQ:** "Does it *operate* correctly?" — checks functional range, alarms, safety, worst-case.
- **PQ:** "Does it *perform* reliably in production?" — checks reproducibility with real material, real operator, statistical proof.

### B. The V-Model Flow
- **URS → FS → DS → IQ → OQ → PQ → VSR → CPV → Market Release**
- Each stage has a **signed deliverable** before the next can begin.

### C. Statistical Anchor (PQ)
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ — minimum for medical devices.
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ — preferred for life-supporting devices.
- **Three consecutive successful batches** is the FDA / EU GMP PQ benchmark.

### D. Regulatory Anchors
- **FDA 21 CFR 820.70 / 820.75** — Process and Installation Quality.
- **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.5.6** — Process validation.
- **EU MDR 2017/745 Annex I, II** — Conformity assessment.
- **GAMP 5 (ISPE, 2018)** — Software risk-based validation.
- **IEC 60601-1** — Electrical safety of medical equipment.

### E. GAMP 5 Categories
- **G1:** OS, network.
- **G2:** Non-configured COTS.
- **G3:** Custom simple scripts.
- **G4:** Configured COTS (most hospital IT systems).
- **G5:** Custom bespoke (AI/ML, novel algorithms).

### F. Data Integrity (PQ focus)
- **ALCOA+:** Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate + Complete, Consistent, Enduring, Available.

### G. Re-Qualification Triggers
- Equipment relocation (IQ only).
- Software upgrade (IQ + OQ).
- Process / material change (PQ).
- Critical parameter change (Full IQ + OQ + PQ).

### H. Common Devices Validated via IQ/OQ/PQ
- Ventilators, defibrillators, infusion pumps, blood analyzers, autoclaves, MRI / CT, ECG machines, dialysis machines, surgical robots.

### I. Mnemonic for IQ/OQ/PQ
- **"I"** = **I**nstalled (delivery check).
- **"O"** = **O**perates (range check).
- **"P"** = **P**erforms (reproducibility check).

### J. Two-Bullet Final Summary
- **IQ, OQ, PQ** are the **three sequential qualification stages** of the V-Model, and skipping any one is a major regulatory non-compliance.
- **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the universal statistical proof of PQ acceptability for medical devices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labelling and Instructions for Use (IFU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Labelling and Instructions for Use (IFU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Labelling** in the context of medical devices is defined as all written, printed, or graphic matter that is present on the device, on its packaging, or accompanying the device, and which is intended to identify or describe the device, its intended purpose, the manufacturer, and other relevant safety and performance information.

According to **ISO 13485:2016 (Section 4.11)** and **21 CFR Part 801 (US FDA)**, labelling encompasses:
- The device label itself
- Accompanying documents
- Instructions for Use (IFU)
- Package inserts
- User manuals

**Instructions for Use (IFU)** is a comprehensive document provided by the manufacturer that contains detailed information necessary for the safe and proper use of a medical device. It is a critical component of the device's technical documentation and is mandated by regulatory authorities worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Labelling and IFU are classified as part of the **Regulatory Documentation and Risk Management** pillar of biomedical device design. Under the EU MDR 2017/745 and ISO 13485:2016, IFU is a **mandatory deliverable** for every medical device placed on the market, irrespective of its risk class (Class I, IIa, IIb, or III).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a biomedical device as a **complex pharmaceutical prescription** combined with a **power tool**:
- The **device label** is like the small sticker on a pill bottle — it tells you the name, dose, manufacturer, and expiry at a glance.
- The **IFU** is like the **detailed patient information leaflet** tucked inside the box — it tells you how to use it correctly, what side effects (warnings) to watch for, and what to do in case of an emergency.
- Just as you would not take a powerful medicine without reading the leaflet, a clinician should not use a sophisticated device like a ventilator, defibrillator, or infusion pump without consulting the IFU.

In essence, **labelling is the immediate identifier, while the IFU is the complete user manual** — together they form the *voice of the manufacturer* speaking to the user, even when the designer is not present.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Consider a car. The badge on the hood (label) tells you the make and model. The owner's manual in the glove compartment (IFU) tells you how to operate every feature, the safety warnings, the maintenance schedule, and what to do in emergencies. Medical device labelling follows the exact same philosophy — but with stricter regulatory enforcement because lives are at stake.

### 1.3 Key Standards and Governing Bodies

| Standard / Regulation | Governing Body | Region |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 13485:2016** | ISO | International |
| **ISO 15223-1:2021** | ISO | International |
| **21 CFR Part 801** | US FDA | United States |
| **21 CFR Part 820** | US FDA (QSR) | United States |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | European Commission | European Union |
| **MDR Annex I, Chapter III** | EU | European Union |
| **MEDDEV 2.14/3** | EU | European Union |
| **GHTF/SG1/N70:2011** | Global Harmonization Task Force | International |

### 1.4 Critical Distinction: Label vs. Labelling vs. IFU

- **Label:** The written, printed, or graphic information on the immediate container of the device.
- **Labelling:** The broader term covering the label AND all accompanying printed material.
- **IFU:** A specific labelling component that provides detailed use instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant / Metric:** The **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** is a globally harmonized numeric or alphanumeric code that must appear on the device label. Under **21 CFR Part 830** and **EU MDR Article 27**, the UDI consists of a **UDI-DI (Device Identifier)** and a **UDI-PI (Production Identifier)**. This is the single most important identifier in modern medical device labelling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Medical Device Labelling

Labelling is a **multi-layered information system** designed to communicate with multiple stakeholders (patients, clinicians, regulators, procurement officers). The hierarchy is as follows:

- **Layer 1: Primary Packaging (Device Label)**
  - Manufacturer name and address
  - Device name and model number
  - Intended purpose (brief)
  - Sterilization status
  - Batch / Lot / Serial number
  - Manufacturing and Expiry date
  - UDI carrier (UDI-DI + UDI-PI)
  - CE mark or 510(k) clearance number
  - Storage conditions
  - Single-use symbol (if applicable)

- **Layer 2: Secondary Packaging (Outer Carton)**
  - All elements from Layer 1
  - Quantity of units
  - Country of origin
  - Importer / Distributor information
  - Barcode / GS1 / HIBCC codes
  - Languages required by the destination market

- **Layer 3: Instructions for Use (IFU)**
  - Detailed operating instructions
  - Indications and Contraindications
  - Warnings and Precautions
  - Adverse events
  - Clinical performance summary
  - Disposal information

- **Layer 4: Promotional / Marketing Material (Controlled)**
  - Must be consistent with the IFU and regulatory clearance
  - Cannot make unsupported claims

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Regulatory Source | Mandatory? | Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manufacturer name & address | ISO 13485 §4.11, 21 CFR 801.1 | **YES** | Text on label |
| Authorized Representative | EU MDR Article 11 | **YES (EU)** | Text + symbol |
| UDI Carrier | 21 CFR 830, EU MDR Art. 27 | **YES** | AIDC + HRI |
| CE Mark | EU MDR Art. 20 | **YES (EU)** | Symbol (Conformité Européenne) |
| Intended Purpose | EU MDR Annex I §23.1 | **YES** | Text in IFU |
| Indication for Use | 21 CFR 801.4 | **YES (US)** | Text in IFU |
| Sterility Symbol | ISO 15223-1 | If sterile | Symbol (STERILE) |
| Expiry Date | ISO 15223-1, 21 CFR 801.1 | **YES (if applicable)** | Use-by symbol with date |
| Batch Code | ISO 15223-1, 21 CFR 801.1 | **YES** | LOT symbol |
| Single Use Symbol | ISO 15223-1 | If applicable | Symbol (do not re-use) |
| Consult IFU Symbol | ISO 15223-1 | **YES** | Symbol (book with i) |
| Caution Symbol | ISO 15223-1 | **YES** | Symbol (triangle with !) |
| MR Conditional / Unsafe | ASTM F2503, ISO 15223-1 | For MR devices | Symbol |
| Biological Origin | ISO 15223-1 | If applicable | Symbol |
| Latex Content | 21 CFR 801.437 | If contains | Warning text |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use of Vertical Bars in Tables:** In all tables above, vertical bars inside cell content have been rendered as the standard pipe character used only for cell separation. For mathematical vertical bars (such as absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or set notation), use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic for IFU Content Development

The development of an IFU follows a structured engineering process aligned with the **Design Controls of 21 CFR 820.30** and **ISO 13485 Section 7.3**:

- **Step 1: Identify User Profile** — Determine whether the device is for healthcare professionals, lay users, or both. This drives the language complexity (lay language requirement under EU MDR).
- **Step 2: Map Use Environment** — Hospital, home, ambulance, MRI suite, etc.
- **Step 3: Define Indications and Contraindications** — From the clinical evaluation report.
- **Step 4: Extract Hazards from Risk Management File** — Per **ISO 14971:2019**.
- **Step 5: Translate Hazards into Warnings and Precautions** — Risk control measures communicated to the user.
- **Step 6: Detail Operating Instructions** — Step-by-step procedure with figures.
- **Step 7: Specify Cleaning, Disinfection, Sterilization** — If reusable.
- **Step 8: Specify Storage, Transport, and Disposal Conditions.**
- **Step 9: Cross-Check with Usability Engineering File** — Per **IEC 62366-1:2015**.
- **Step 10: Validate Translation Quality** — For multi-language markets.

### 2.4 Symbols vs. Text — The Modern Approach

Modern medical device labelling relies heavily on **harmonized symbols** (per **ISO 15223-1:2021**) to:
- Reduce translation costs
- Overcome language barriers
- Save label space
- Improve user comprehension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a symbol is used on a label, the manufacturer must provide the meaning of the symbol either in the IFU, on the packaging, or in a separate symbols glossary. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Procurement:** The UDI enables electronic health records (EHR) integration and recall management.
- **Regulatory Surveillance:** Labelling is the primary tool used by regulators to identify non-conforming devices in the field.
- **Patient Safety:** Clear warnings and IFU content directly reduce use errors, which is the leading cause of medical device adverse events.
- **Litigation Defense:** Properly drafted IFU serves as the manufacturer's primary legal defense in product liability cases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology Note:** Labelling and IFU is a regulatory-content topic, not a quantitative engineering topic. Therefore, instead of algebraic derivations, this section provides an **analytical step-by-step regulatory mapping** and a **comparative regulatory matrix** that aligns with how KTU examiners frame 14-mark analytical questions.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: FDA vs. EU MDR vs. ISO 13485 Labelling Requirements

| Requirement | 21 CFR Part 801 (US FDA) | EU MDR 2017/745 | ISO 13485:2016 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Legal Manufacturer Identity** | Required (801.1) | Required (Art. 10 §10.9) | Required (4.11 a) |
| **Intended Use Statement** | Required (801.4) | Required (Annex I §23.1) | Required (4.11 b) |
| **Warning Statements** | Required for specific devices | Required (Annex I §23.4) | Required if risk-related (4.11 d) |
| **Adequate Directions for Use** | Required (801.5) | Covered by IFU requirement | Covered by 4.11 c |
| **Prescription vs. OTC** | Rx-only labelling required | Not applicable in this form | Not applicable in this form |
| **UDI System** | 21 CFR Part 830 (mandatory) | Article 27 (mandatory) | Not mandated, but expected |
| **Symbols-Only Labelling** | Allowed with glossary | Allowed per ISO 15223-1 | Allowed per ISO 15223-1 |
| **Electronic IFU (eIFU)** | Allowed for certain devices | Allowed per Article 22 | Not specifically addressed |
| **Multi-Language Requirement** | English mandatory | Official languages of MS | As per destination market |
| **Importer / Distributor Info** | Required | Required (Art. 11) | Required (4.11 f) |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation: How an IFU is Derived from Design Inputs

The following aligned block presents the **structured logic flow** that a biomedical engineer follows from design input to IFU release:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Design Inputs} &\rightarrow \text{User Needs, Intended Use, Use Environment} \\
\text{Step 2: Risk Analysis (ISO 14971)} &\rightarrow \text{Hazard Identification, Risk Estimation} \\
\text{Step 3: Risk Control Measures} &\rightarrow \text{Inherent Safety, Protective, Information} \\
\text{Step 4: Information for Safety} &\rightarrow \text{Warnings, Precautions, Contra-indications} \\
\text{Step 5: Usability Engineering (IEC 62366-1)} &\rightarrow \text{User Interface Specification, Use Errors} \\
\text{Step 6: Clinical Evaluation (MEDDEV 2.7/1)} &\rightarrow \text{Indications, Contraindications, Adverse Events} \\
\text{Step 7: IFU Draft} &\rightarrow \text{Synthesis of all above inputs} \\
\text{Step 8: Validation} &\rightarrow \text{Summative Usability Testing, Readability Testing} \\
\text{Step 9: Translation} &\rightarrow \text{Certified Translation, Back-Translation} \\
\text{Step 10: IFU Release} &\rightarrow \text{Version Control, Distribution with Device}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explanation of Conversion Logic:** Each step above is a **regulatory input-output relationship**. For example, *Step 3 to Step 4* represents the application of the **risk control hierarchy** from ISO 14971:2019, which prioritizes inherent safe design over protective measures, and finally over **information for safety** (which is what the IFU represents). This is the **only** acceptable use of information as a risk control — meaning the IFU is a *last-line* safety mechanism after the device has been engineered to be as safe as possible.

### 3.3 The IFU Content Checklist — Detailed Analytical Walkthrough

Each sub-section below corresponds to a section that **must** appear in a compliant IFU under **EU MDR Annex I, Chapter III, Section 23**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{23.1} &\rightarrow \text{Name and trade name of the device} \\
\text{23.2} &\rightarrow \text{Name, registered trade name, address of manufacturer} \\
\text{23.3} &\rightarrow \text{Address of Authorized Representative (EU)} \\
\text{23.4 (a)} &\rightarrow \text{Intended purpose, indications, contra-indications} \\
\text{23.4 (b)} &\rightarrow \text{Target patient population and intended users} \\
\text{23.4 (c)} &\rightarrow \text{Clinical benefits to be expected} \\
\text{23.4 (d)} &\rightarrow \text{Residual risks, warnings, precautions} \\
\text{23.4 (e)} &\rightarrow \text{Necessary training required} \\
\text{23.4 (f)} &\rightarrow \text{Repeatable use-related warnings} \\
\text{23.4 (g)} &\rightarrow \text{Adverse events and reporting procedure} \\
\text{23.4 (h)} &\rightarrow \text{Installation and calibration instructions} \\
\text{23.4 (i)} &\rightarrow \text{Operating instructions} \\
\text{23.4 (j)} &\rightarrow \text{Cleaning, disinfection, sterilization instructions} \\
\text{23.4 (k)} &\rightarrow \text{Disposal instructions} \\
\text{23.4 (l)} &\rightarrow \text{Performance evaluation method} \\
\text{23.4 (m)} &\rightarrow \text{Storage and transport conditions} \\
\text{23.4 (n)} &\rightarrow \text{UDI carrier information}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The 23.4 sub-points are the **gold standard for IFU evaluation** in KTU long-answer questions. Memorizing this hierarchy is the single highest-yield activity for Module 4.

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Readability and Comprehension Test

Under **FDA Guidance: Applying Human Factors and Usability Engineering to Medical Devices (2016)** and **EU MDR Article 10(11)**, IFU must be tested for readability. The standard analytical approach is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Readability Index} &\rightarrow \text{Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level} \\
\text{Target Reading Level} &\rightarrow \text{Grade 6-8 for lay users (per FDA)} \\
\text{Comprehension Test} &\rightarrow \text{Summative usability test with target users} \\
\text{Pass Criterion} &\rightarrow \text{No use errors that lead to serious harm} \\
\text{Failure Mode} &\rightarrow \text{IFU must be revised, not just the device}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **engineering traceability chain** that justifies every word in an IFU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram: Medical Device Labelling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    regA[Regulatory Standards] --> sys1[ISO 13485]
    regA[Regulatory Standards] --> sys2[ISO 15223-1]
    regA[Regulatory Standards] --> sys3[21 CFR Part 801]
    regA[Regulatory Standards] --> sys4[EU MDR 2017 745]
    regA[Regulatory Standards] --> sys5[ISO 14971]
    regA[Regulatory Standards] --> sys6[IEC 62366-1]
    sys1[ISO 13485] --> proc1[Labelling Process]
    sys2[ISO 15223-1] --> proc1[Labelling Process]
    sys3[21 CFR Part 801] --> proc1[Labelling Process]
    sys4[EU MDR 2017 745] --> proc1[Labelling Process]
    sys5[ISO 14971] --> proc1[Labelling Process]
    sys6[IEC 62366-1] --> proc1[Labelling Process]
    proc1[Labelling Process] --> lay1[Layer 1 Primary Label]
    proc1[Labelling Process] --> lay2[Layer 2 Secondary Packaging]
    proc1[Labelling Process] --> lay3[Layer 3 IFU Document]
    proc1[Labelling Process] --> lay4[Layer 4 eIFU Web]
    lay1[Layer 1 Primary Label] --> ele1[Manufacturer Info]
    lay1[Layer 1 Primary Label] --> ele2[UDI Carrier]
    lay1[Layer 1 Primary Label] --> ele3[Sterility Status]
    lay1[Layer 1 Primary Label] --> ele4[Expiry Date]
    lay2[Layer 2 Secondary Packaging] --> ele5[Batch Code]
    lay2[Layer 2 Secondary Packaging] --> ele6[Distributor Info]
    lay3[Layer 3 IFU Document] --> ele7[Intended Use]
    lay3[Layer 3 IFU Document] --> ele8[Warnings and Precautions]
    lay3[Layer 3 IFU Document] --> ele9[Operating Instructions]
    lay3[Layer 3 IFU Document] --> ele10[Cleaning Sterilization]
    lay4[Layer 4 eIFU Web] --> ele11[Risk Management Links]
    lay4[Layer 4 eIFU Web] --> ele12[Revision History]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: IFU Development Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    st1[Stage 1 User Needs] --> st2[Stage 2 Risk Analysis]
    st2[Stage 2 Risk Analysis] --> st3[Stage 3 Risk Control]
    st3[Stage 3 Risk Control] --> st4[Stage 4 IFU Draft]
    st4[Stage 4 IFU Draft] --> st5[Stage 5 Usability Test]
    st5[Stage 5 Usability Test] --> st6[Stage 6 Readability Test]
    st6[Stage 6 Readability Test] --> st7[Stage 7 Translation]
    st7[Stage 7 Translation] --> st8[Stage 8 Regulatory Review]
    st8[Stage 8 Regulatory Review] --> st9[Stage 9 IFU Release]
    st9[Stage 9 IFU Release] --> st10[Stage 10 Post Market Update]
    st10[Stage 10 Post Market Update] --> st1[Stage 1 User Needs]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Symbol-Based vs. Text-Based Labelling Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp[Decision Input] --> q1{Device Used Internationally?}
    q1{Device Used Internationally?} -->|Yes| path1[Use ISO 15223-1 Symbols]
    q1{Device Used Internationally?} -->|No| q2{Destination Market Has Standardized Symbols?}
    path1[Use ISO 15223-1 Symbols] --> out1[Reduce Translation Cost]
    path1[Use ISO 15223-1 Symbols] --> out2[Improve User Recognition]
    path1[Use ISO 15223-1 Symbols] --> out3[Provide Symbols Glossary in IFU]
    q2{Destination Market Has Standardized Symbols?} -->|Yes| path1[Use ISO 15223-1 Symbols]
    q2{Destination Market Has Standardized Symbols?} -->|No| path2[Use Localized Text Only]
    path2[Use Localized Text Only] --> out4[Translate to All Official Languages]
    path2[Use Localized Text Only] --> out5[Increase Label Size]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric (e.g., `regA`, `sys1`, `lay1`) and prefixed with letters to prevent conflicts with Mermaid reserved keywords. All node labels are clean, uppercase alphanumeric text with no embedded markdown formatting.

### 4.4 Schematic of UDI Carrier Placement

The **UDI carrier** is the most scrutinized element of modern labelling. It consists of:
- **AIDC (Automatic Identification and Data Capture):** A machine-readable form (e.g., 2D Data Matrix, RFID)
- **HRI (Human Readable Interpretation):** The plain-text version of the AIDC code

Both must appear on the label, with the AIDC taking precedence for scanning and the HRI for backup readability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]:** Define the term "Labelling" as applicable to medical devices. List any **four** essential elements that must appear on a device label.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Labelling refers to all written, printed, or graphic information that is present on the medical device, its packaging, or accompanying the device, intended to identify the device and provide safety and performance information, as mandated by **ISO 13485:2016** and **21 CFR Part 801**.

**Four Essential Label Elements:**

- **Manufacturer name and address** — Identifies the legal entity responsible for the device.
- **Unique Device Identifier (UDI)** — Global identifier per **21 CFR Part 830** / **EU MDR Article 27**.
- **Batch / Lot or Serial number** — Enables traceability, per **ISO 15223-1**.
- **Expiry date or Use-by date** — Critical for sterile or shelf-life-limited devices.

> [!NOTE]
> Acceptable alternatives include: sterilization status, storage conditions, CE mark, intended purpose (brief), and prescription-only status.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]:** What is an **Instructions for Use (IFU)** document? Why is it considered a critical component of the medical device's risk control strategy under **ISO 14971**?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of IFU:** An Instructions for Use document is a comprehensive paper or electronic document provided by the manufacturer that contains detailed information for the safe and proper use of a medical device, including indications, contraindications, warnings, precautions, operating procedures, and maintenance instructions.

**Role in Risk Control Strategy (ISO 14971):** Under the **risk control hierarchy** of ISO 14971:2019, the manufacturer must first attempt to reduce risk through:
- **Inherent safe design**
- **Protective measures (e.g., guards, alarms)**
- **Information for safety (i.e., the IFU)**

The IFU serves as the **third and final layer** of risk control — communicating residual risks to the user. It is critical because:
- It transfers risk information from the manufacturer to the end user.
- It is a **regulatory mandatory deliverable** for every medical device.
- It forms the **legal contract of use** between manufacturer and user.
- It is a **post-market tool** for managing newly identified hazards.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse "warning labels" with the full IFU. The warning label is a brief summary; the IFU is the complete document. Also, information for safety is the **weakest** form of risk control — never present it as the primary control.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **hierarchical structure of medical device labelling** in detail. Differentiate between the primary packaging label, secondary packaging, and the IFU. Provide two mandatory elements for each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the **UDI system** under the US FDA and EU MDR frameworks. Explain the difference between UDI-DI and UDI-PI, and how they appear on the device label.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The hierarchical structure of medical device labelling is a **multi-layer system** designed to communicate information at different levels of detail to different stakeholders.

**Layer 1 — Primary Packaging (Device Label):**
This is the label affixed directly to the device or its immediate container. It must be compact, durable, and machine-readable.

*Two mandatory elements:*
- **Manufacturer's name and registered address** (per ISO 13485 §4.11 a).
- **UDI carrier (UDI-DI + UDI-PI)** in both AIDC and HRI formats (per 21 CFR §830 and EU MDR Art. 27).

**Layer 2 — Secondary Packaging (Outer Carton):**
This is the label on the box that contains multiple primary packages. It supplements the primary label with logistics and market-specific information.

*Two mandatory elements:*
- **Quantity of devices in the package.**
- **Importer / Distributor information** for the destination market (per EU MDR Art. 11).

**Layer 3 — Instructions for Use (IFU):**
This is the comprehensive user manual supplied with the device. It can be in paper or electronic form (eIFU).

*Two mandatory elements:*
- **Intended purpose and indications for use** (per EU MDR Annex I §23.4 a).
- **Warnings, precautions, and residual risks** (per EU MDR Annex I §23.4 d).

**Conclusion:** The three-layer hierarchy ensures that the most critical safety information is always available at the point of use, while comprehensive operating instructions are available in the accompanying IFU. This design complies with the **principle of progressive disclosure** of information.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

The **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** system is a globally harmonized framework that requires every medical device to carry a unique numeric or alphanumeric code to enable traceability and post-market surveillance.

**US FDA Framework (21 CFR Part 830):**
The FDA established the UDI system in 2013, with phased implementation based on device risk class. The system requires:
- Each device to have a **UDI** assigned by an FDA-accredited issuing agency (e.g., GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA).
- The UDI to be submitted to the **Global Unique Device Identification Database (GUDID)**.
- Direct marking for devices that remain in use for more than 30 days and are reusable.

**EU MDR Framework (Article 27):**
The EU MDR mandates the UDI system for all devices except custom-made and investigational devices. Key requirements:
- Each device must be identified by a UDI.
- The manufacturer must assign UDI-DI and maintain UDI-PI.
- The UDI is to be stored in the **Eudamed** database (the EU's equivalent of GUDID).

**Difference between UDI-DI and UDI-PI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UDI-DI (Device Identifier)} &\rightarrow \text{Static, identifies the specific device model or version} \\
\text{UDI-PI (Production Identifier)} &\rightarrow \text{Dynamic, identifies production data: lot, batch, serial, expiry}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Appearance on the Label:**
- The UDI must be presented in **two formats**: AIDC (e.g., 2D Data Matrix) and HRI (Human Readable).
- The AIDC format is machine-readable and takes precedence for scanning.
- The HRI format provides a backup in case of scan failure.
- Example UDI: `(01)00380726123456(11)210301(17)250101(10)A12345` where `(01)` is the UDI-DI, `(11)` is the manufacturing date, `(17)` is the expiry date, and `(10)` is the batch code — all forming the UDI-PI.

**Conclusion:** The UDI system is the **backbone of modern medical device post-market surveillance** and is a mandatory element on every compliant device label.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With reference to **EU MDR Annex I, Chapter III, Section 23**, list and explain **any eight** mandatory elements that must appear in the Instructions for Use (IFU) of a medical device.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **risk control hierarchy** under ISO 14971:2019 and describe how the IFU serves as a **residual risk communication tool**. Provide an example.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

The **EU MDR Annex I, Chapter III, Section 23** specifies the mandatory information to be supplied with a medical device. The following are eight mandatory IFU elements:

- **23.4 (a) — Intended purpose, indications, and contra-indications:** This defines the medical condition to be treated, the target patient population, and conditions under which the device must NOT be used.

- **23.4 (b) — Target patient population and intended users:** Specifies whether the device is for adult, pediatric, or specific patient groups, and whether the user is a healthcare professional or a layperson.

- **23.4 (c) — Clinical benefits to be expected:** The manufacturer must state the claimed clinical benefit, supported by clinical evaluation data (per MEDDEV 2.7/1 or EU MDR Annex XIV).

- **23.4 (d) — Residual risks, warnings, and precautions:** A summary of risks that could not be eliminated by design, along with the warnings the user must observe.

- **23.4 (g) — Adverse events and reporting procedure:** The user must be informed about possible adverse events and how to report them to the manufacturer and competent authority.

- **23.4 (h) — Installation and calibration instructions:** Required for devices that need setup, such as infusion pumps, ventilators, or imaging systems.

- **23.4 (j) — Cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization instructions:** Required for reusable devices, with validated methods and approved agents.

- **23.4 (n) — UDI carrier information:** The Unique Device Identifier must be referenced in the IFU and explained.

> [!NOTE]
> Other mandatory elements include: 23.4 (i) operating instructions, 23.4 (k) disposal instructions, and 23.4 (m) storage and transport conditions.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

The **risk control hierarchy** under **ISO 14971:2019** is a structured approach to reducing risk, applied in the following order of priority:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 1 (Strongest)} &\rightarrow \text{Inherent Safe Design} \\
\text{Level 2 (Intermediate)} &\rightarrow \text{Protective Measures} \\
\text{Level 3 (Weakest)} &\rightarrow \text{Information for Safety (IFU, Training, Labels)} \\
\text{Fallback} &\rightarrow \text{Residual Risk Communication in IFU}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Inherent Safe Design:** The manufacturer first attempts to eliminate the hazard or reduce risk by changing the device design itself. For example, using lower voltage to reduce electric shock risk.

**Step 2 — Protective Measures:** If inherent design cannot fully eliminate the risk, protective measures such as guards, alarms, or fail-safe mechanisms are added. For example, an audible alarm on a ventilator.

**Step 3 — Information for Safety:** If residual risk remains, the manufacturer communicates it to the user via the IFU, label, and training. This is the **weakest** form of risk control because it relies on user behavior.

**IFU as a Residual Risk Communication Tool:** The IFU is the formal channel through which the manufacturer transfers residual risk information to the user. It converts technical risk analysis (in the Risk Management File) into actionable user guidance.

**Example — Infusion Pump:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hazard} &\rightarrow \text{Over-infusion leading to patient overdose} \\
\text{Inherent Safe Design} &\rightarrow \text{Single-fault-tolerant flow sensor} \\
\text{Protective Measure} &\rightarrow \text{Hard and soft upper limits with alarm} \\
\text{Information for Safety (IFU)} &\rightarrow \text{Warning: Always verify drug concentration} \\
&\text{before starting infusion. Use only compatible} \\
&\text{IV sets approved by the manufacturer.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The IFU is the **last line of defense** in the risk control hierarchy. It must be written in clear, validated language and tested through summative usability studies (per IEC 62366-1) to ensure user comprehension.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** list more than the required number of points. If the question asks for "any eight," listing ten wastes time and may indicate rote learning.
> - **Always cite the regulatory reference** (e.g., "as per EU MDR Annex I §23.4 d") to score high on the **Application** level of Bloom's Taxonomy.
> - For 14-mark questions, **structure the answer with clear sub-headings** (Layer 1, Layer 2, etc.) and **box your final conclusion**.
> - For IFU content questions, **never skip the "warnings and precautions" section** — it is the most heavily weighted element.
> - For UDI questions, **always distinguish between AIDC and HRI** — confusing them is a 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> - For risk control questions, **always state the hierarchy order** (Inherent > Protective > Informational) — reversing it is a critical error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Labelling** = Device Label + Outer Packaging + IFU + Accompanying Documents.
- **IFU** is a **mandatory** document under ISO 13485, EU MDR Annex I, and 21 CFR Part 801.
- **ISO 15223-1:2021** is the master standard for **symbols** used in medical device labelling.
- The **UDI system** consists of **UDI-DI** (static, device model) and **UDI-PI** (dynamic, production data).
- The UDI must appear in **both AIDC and HRI formats** on the device label.
- **EU MDR Section 23.4** is the **gold standard checklist** for IFU content (14 mandatory sub-elements).
- The **risk control hierarchy** of ISO 14971 prioritizes: **Inherent Design > Protective Measures > Information for Safety (IFU)**.
- **eIFU** (Electronic IFU) is allowed for certain device classes under both FDA and EU MDR.
- All labels must use **official languages** of the destination market — English is mandatory for the US.
- Symbols on labels must be **explained** in the IFU or a symbols glossary.
- The **Manufacturer** and **Authorized Representative** (for EU) must be clearly identified.
- **Sterile**, **single-use**, **batch**, and **expiry date** symbols are harmonized under ISO 15223-1.
- **MR Safe / MR Conditional / MR Unsafe** symbols (per ASTM F2503) are mandatory for devices used in MRI.
- The IFU must be **validated through summative usability testing** per IEC 62366-1:2015.
- **Post-market updates** to the IFU are required whenever new hazards are identified.
- **Readability** must be at **Grade 6-8 reading level** for lay users per FDA guidance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Manufacture Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Design and Manufacture Process** for biomedical devices is a structured, regulated, and multidisciplinary engineering workflow that transforms a clinical or diagnostic need into a safe, effective, and commercially viable medical product. It systematically integrates **User Needs**, **Design Inputs/Outputs**, **Verification & Validation (V&V)**, **Design Transfer**, and **Risk Management** in compliance with global regulatory standards such as **ISO 13485 (Quality Management Systems)**, **ISO 14971 (Risk Management)**, **FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30 (Design Controls)**, and the **EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745)**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic addresses how mechanical, electronic, materials, and biomedical engineering principles converge to deliver patient-safe devices ranging from simple consumables (syringes, catheters) to complex active implants (pacemakers, neural stimulators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A biomedical device is NOT considered "designed and manufactured" until its **Design History File (DHF)** is complete, **biocompatibility (ISO 10993)** is established, and the **sterilization/cleaning process is validated**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a Custom Heart-Valve from Scratch**

Think of designing a biomedical device the same way you would design and build a **custom wheelchair for a specific patient**:

| Phase | Real-World Analogy | Biomedical Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Identify Need | Patient cannot climb stairs | User Needs (clinical requirement) |
| Brainstorm | Sketch many chair types | Concept Generation |
| Pick Best | Choose stair-climbing design | Design Selection / Feasibility |
| Draw Plan | Blueprints, material list | Design Inputs & Outputs |
| Build Prototype | 3D printed test chair | Prototype Fabrication (Rapid Prototyping) |
| Test Prototype | Have patient try it on stairs | Design Verification & Validation |
| Mass Produce | Set up factory line | Design Transfer to Manufacturing |
| Quality Check | Inspect each chair | Process Validation, QC, Sterilization |

Just as you would **never** sell a wheelchair without testing it on a real user, you can **never** commercialize a medical device without completing structured **V&V** and documenting the entire journey in a **DHF**.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical & Regulatory Constants

> The following standard metrics govern biomedical device design:

- **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL):** $\mathbf{10^{-6}}$ (probability of one viable microorganism per million sterilized units)
- **ISO 10993 biocompatibility tests:** Cytotoxicity, Sensitization, Irritation, Systemic Toxicity, Genotoxicity, Implantation
- **Risk Acceptability Matrix:** Severity × Probability (per **ISO 14971**)
- **Design Control Phases:** 8 mandatory elements per **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**
- **Documented Evidence:** Design History File (DHF) must be retained for the **lifetime of the device** (typically 2+ years post last sale)

---

### 1.4 Scope of Design & Manufacture

The process covers **three intertwined pillars**:

1. **Design Engineering** — Mechanical, electronic, software, materials, ergonomics
2. **Manufacturing Engineering** — Process selection, tooling, automation, sterilization
3. **Quality & Regulatory Engineering** — Compliance, risk, traceability, post-market surveillance

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD REFERENCE

### 2.1 The Eight Design Control Elements (FDA 21 CFR 820.30)

The **U.S. FDA** mandates a rigorous design control framework. Every Class II and Class III medical device marketed in the USA must comply. These are the **8 high-yield elements** every KTU student must know:

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "PDI-ROV-CT"**
> **P**lan, **D**esign Input, **R**eview, **O**utput, **V**erification, **V**alidation, **C**ontrol of Changes, **T**ransfer

| # | Element | Purpose | Key Output Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design & Development Planning** | Define responsibilities, stages, reviews, resources | Design & Development Plan (DDP) |
| 2 | **Design Input** | Capture physical, performance, regulatory, user requirements | Design Input Requirements (DIR) |
| 3 | **Design Output** | Translate inputs into specifications, drawings, software, procedures | Design Output (DO) documents |
| 4 | **Design Review** | Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary reviews at each stage | Design Review Report (DRR) |
| 5 | **Design Verification** | Confirm outputs meet inputs (bench testing) | Verification Test Reports |
| 6 | **Design Validation** | Confirm device meets user needs and intended use (clinical/simulated use) | Validation Test Reports / Clinical Evaluation |
| 7 | **Design Transfer** | Move design into production with proper procedures and training | Transfer Plan, DMR (Device Master Record) |
| 8 | **Design Changes & DHF** | Control all changes; maintain Design History File | Change Control Records, DHF Index |

---

### 2.2 Verification vs. Validation — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked distinction in KTU ESE and a guaranteed 7-mark question in Module 4.**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | "Did we build the device **right**?" | "Did we build the **right** device?" |
| **Compares** | Design Output vs Design Input | Final Device vs User Needs |
| **Performed At** | Sub-system / component level | Finished device in use environment |
| **Methods** | Bench tests, simulations, dimensional checks | Clinical evaluation, simulated use, animal studies |
| **Example** | Tensile test of titanium stent struts meets 600 MPa spec | Cardiologist successfully deploys the stent in a silicone heart model |
| **Standard Reference** | 21 CFR 820.30(f) | 21 CFR 820.30(g) |

---

### 2.3 Stages of the Product Development Lifecycle (KTU Framework)

A complete biomedical device design-and-manufacture pipeline typically has **five stages**:

**Stage 1 — Concept & Feasibility (Phase 0)**
- Identify clinical problem
- Literature/patent survey
- Initial risk analysis (Preliminary Hazard Analysis – PHA)
- Proof-of-concept prototypes (often 3D printed)

**Stage 2 — Design & Development (Phase 1–2)**
- Detailed engineering design (CAD, FEA, circuit design)
- Materials selection (biocompatibility matrix – **ISO 10993**)
- Risk management file (RMF) per **ISO 14971**
- Design verification (bench, EMC, electrical safety, software)

**Stage 3 — Process & Manufacturing Development (Phase 2)**
- Design for Manufacturability (DFM) / Design for Assembly (DFA)
- Process validation (IQ – Installation Qualification, OQ – Operational Qualification, PQ – Performance Qualification)
- Tooling, fixtures, production line setup

**Stage 4 — Clinical Validation & Regulatory Submission (Phase 3)**
- Design Validation (clinical or simulated-use)
- Regulatory submission: FDA **510(k)** (predicate-based) or **PMA** (high-risk), or **CE Marking** under EU MDR
- Sterilization validation (per **ISO 11135** EtO, **ISO 11137** gamma, **ISO 17665** steam)

**Stage 5 — Launch & Post-Market Surveillance**
- Production release
- Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF) / Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)
- CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) per **21 CFR 820.100**
- Vigilance reporting (adverse events)

---

### 2.4 Manufacturing Process Selection for Biomedical Devices

The **Why** behind process selection: Medical devices demand extreme precision, biocompatibility, repeatability, and sterility. A designer must consider:

- **Material compatibility** (e.g., PEEK cannot be sterilized by gamma in all cases due to discoloration)
- **Tolerance capability** (implants need $\pm 5\ \mu m$ tolerances)
- **Surface finish** (orthopedic implants require **Ra < 0.1 µm** for osseointegration)
- **Production volume** (implants <10k/yr; disposables >10M/yr)
- **Regulatory compatibility** (process must be validatable per **IQ/OQ/PQ**)

### KTU Reference Table — Common Manufacturing Processes

| Process | Typical Tolerance | Best For | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CNC Machining** | $\pm 5\ \mu m$ | Metal implants (Ti, 316L SS, Co-Cr) | High cost for complex shapes |
| **Injection Molding** | $\pm 50\ \mu m$ | Polymer disposables (syringes) | High tooling cost |
| **Extrusion** | $\pm 100\ \mu m$ | Tubing (catheters, vascular grafts) | Limited to constant cross-section |
| **Blow Molding** | $\pm 200\ \mu m$ | Hollow containers, IV bottles | Lower precision |
| **Thermoforming** | $\pm 300\ \mu m$ | Trays, blister packs, prosthetics sockets | Limited depth/feature ratio |
| **3D Printing (SLA/SLS/FDM)** | $\pm 50$–$200\ \mu m$ | Patient-specific implants, surgical guides | Material limitations, post-processing |
| **Microfabrication (MEMS)** | $\pm 1\ \mu m$ | Sensors, Lab-on-Chip, micropumps | Cleanroom, high capex |
| **Laser Welding/Cutting** | $\pm 20\ \mu m$ | Hermetic sealing of pacemaker cans | Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) |
| **Electron Beam Welding** | $\pm 10\ \mu m$ | Titanium implants | Vacuum chamber required |
| **Anodization / Passivation** | $0.1$–$10\ \mu m$ | Surface finishing of Ti/SS implants | Adds steps |

---

### 2.5 Materials for Biomedical Devices

| Material Class | Examples | Typical Applications | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metals** | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Co-Cr-Mo | Bone plates, screws, stents | Strength, fatigue resistance |
| **Polymers** | UHMWPE, PEEK, PMMA, Silicone, PVC | Acetabular cup, sutures, tubing | Flexibility, biocompatibility |
| **Ceramics** | Alumina, Zirconia, Hydroxyapatite | Hip femoral head, dental crowns | Hardness, wear resistance |
| **Composites** | Carbon-fiber PEEK, Bioglass-polymer | Spinal cages, dental | Tailorable stiffness |
| **Biodegradables** | PLA, PGA, Mg alloys, Zn alloys | Scaffolds, bone pins, vascular stents | Temporary support then resorb |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Material selection MUST reference **ISO 10993** biocompatibility data. A material "being used in another medical device" is NOT sufficient justification. A biocompatibility test plan is mandatory.

---

### 2.6 Sterilization Methods (Manufacturing-Critical Step)

| Method | Standard | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam (Autoclave)** | ISO 17665 | Metal instruments, glass | $121$–$134^\circ C$ — not for heat-sensitive polymers |
| **Ethylene Oxide (EtO)** | ISO 11135 | Polymers, electronics, optical | Toxic residue, long cycle ($>$24 h) |
| **Gamma Radiation** | ISO 11137 | Bulk disposables | Polymer degradation (UHMWPE) |
| **Electron Beam (e-beam)** | ISO 11137 | Similar to gamma, faster | Limited penetration depth |
| **Hydrogen Peroxide Plasma** | ISO 22441 | Heat/moisture sensitive devices | Lower penetration |
| **Dry Heat** | ISO 20857 | Powders, oils | Slow, high temperature |
| **Filtration (0.22 µm)** | — | Liquids, antibiotics | For liquid products only |

The probability of a surviving microbe must be $\mathbf{\le 10^{-6}}$ (SAL = $10^{-6}$) for terminally sterilized devices.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK & PROCESS MODEL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this is a process-driven (non-calculus) topic, the "derivations" below represent the **logical derivation of the design-to-manufacture workflow** with each decision criterion explicitly justified.

---

### 3.1 Logical Derivation: From Clinical Need to Manufactured Device

**Step 1 — Identify the User Need (UN)**
The process begins with an unmet clinical, surgical, or diagnostic need.
- **Example:** A surgeon needs a *bioresorbable vascular stent* that disappears after 24 months.
- **Output Document:** User Needs Document (UND)

**Why this step?** Without a clearly defined UN, subsequent validation has nothing to test against.

---

**Step 2 — Translate UN into Design Inputs (DI)**
Design Inputs are **engineering-quantified, testable, and traceable** requirements derived from the UN.

- **Example Conversion:**
- UN: "Stent must dissolve in 24 months" → DI: "Mg alloy stent must retain 80% radial strength at 6 months and 100% degradation by 24 ± 2 months in vivo"
- UN: "Surgeon must deploy easily" → DI: "Deployment force < 5 N; trackable through 6 Fr catheter"

**Why this step?** Vague wishes cannot be verified. Only quantified inputs can be tested objectively.

---

**Step 3 — Generate Design Concepts**
Multiple concepts are proposed (typically 3–10) using techniques such as:
- Morphological analysis
- TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)
- Brainstorming / SCAMPER
- Biomimicry

Each concept is **scored against a weighted Pugh Matrix**:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot c_{ij}
$$

where $S_i$ is the score of concept $i$, $w_j$ is the weight of criterion $j$ (sum to 1), and $c_{ij}$ is the rating of concept $i$ against criterion $j$ (typically $-1, 0, +1$).

**Why this step?** Avoids committing to a single concept too early. Reduces downstream design rework.

---

**Step 4 — Risk Management (Parallel & Iterative)**
Per **ISO 14971**, a Risk Management File (RMF) is built concurrently with design.

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Probability of Occurrence}
$$

If Risk > Acceptable threshold → implement **risk control measure** → re-evaluate. Iterate until **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) is achieved.

**Why this step?** Patient safety is non-negotiable. The RMF is a **living document** updated with every design change.

---

**Step 5 — Detailed Design & Design Output (DO)**
CAD models, finite element analysis (FEA), circuit schematics, software code, material specifications, sterilization method, packaging design, and labeling.

**Output Document:** Device Master Record (DMR) — the recipe for building one device.

**Why this step?** Design outputs must contain all information needed to manufacture, package, and label the device correctly.

---

**Step 6 — Design Verification (Did we build it right?)**
Objective evidence that the design output meets the design input.

$$
\text{Verification Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Number of DI requirements verified by test}}{\text{Total number of DI requirements}} \times 100\%
$$

Acceptance criterion: **100%** of DIs must be verified with documented evidence.

**Example:** Stent strut tensile strength $\ge 600$ MPa → tested per **ASTM F2477** → result 712 MPa → PASS.

---

**Step 7 — Design Validation (Did we build the right thing?)**
Testing in **simulated-use or clinical environment** to confirm the device meets user needs and intended use.

- Simulated-use testing on anatomical models
- Animal studies (per **ISO 10993-2**)
- Clinical investigation (per **ISO 14155**)
- Human factors / usability testing (per **IEC 62366-1**)

**Why this step?** A device that meets every DI on paper may still fail in real hands (ergonomics, sterility, training needs).

---

**Step 8 — Design Transfer to Manufacturing**
The validated design is translated into a **manufacturing process flow**, including:
- Production line layout
- Work instructions
- Inspection & test procedures
- Tooling and fixtures
- Sterilization process
- Packaging specifications

**Process Validation (IQ/OQ/PQ)** must be completed:

$$
\text{Process Capability Index: } C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

For medical devices, $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ is generally the minimum acceptable; $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ is preferred for critical-to-quality (CTQ) parameters.

**Why this step?** A reproducible, validated process is the only legal basis for commercial release.

---

**Step 9 — Regulatory Submission**
For **USA (FDA)**, depending on risk class:
- **Class I** — General controls (mostly exempt)
- **Class II** — **510(k)** clearance, demonstrating substantial equivalence to a predicate
- **Class III** — **PMA (Pre-Market Approval)**, full clinical evidence

For **EU (CE Marking)** under **MDR 2017/745**, classification follows **Rule 1–22** with risk classes I, IIa, IIb, III.

**Why this step?** Without regulatory clearance, the device cannot be legally sold.

---

**Step 10 — Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)**
After launch, the manufacturer must:
- Collect complaints and adverse events
- Issue **Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCA)** if needed
- Update **Risk Management File** with real-world data
- Submit **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** for higher-risk devices
- Conduct **PMCF** studies

**Why this step?** Some risks only emerge in the general population — the design loop is **never truly closed**.

---

### 3.2 Decision Logic: Choosing a Manufacturing Process

The selection of a manufacturing process for a biomedical device follows this logical flow:

1. **Determine Material** → driven by biocompatibility (ISO 10993) and mechanical needs.
2. **Determine Production Volume** →
 - < 100 units/yr → CNC / 3D Printing
 - $100$ – $10{,}000$ units/yr → CNC / Investment Casting
 - $10{,}000$ – $1{,}000{,}000$ units/yr → Injection Molding
 - $> 1{,}000{,}000$ units/yr → High-cavitation Injection Molding / Stamping
3. **Determine Tolerances** → tighter than $\pm 50\ \mu m$ → CNC / MEMS.
4. **Determine Surface Finish** → implant-grade → post-process (electropolishing, anodization).
5. **Determine Sterilization Compatibility** → e.g., polymers sensitive to gamma require EtO.
6. **Cost Analysis** → Unit cost = Tooling cost / Volume + Material + Labor + QC + Sterilization + Packaging.

This **systematic logic** is what KTU examiners reward in Part B answers.
