## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Differentiate between **Design Verification** and **Design Validation**. Give one biomedical example for each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Design Verification** confirms that the design output meets the design input ("building the device right"). It is bench-level, objective testing.
 - *Example:* Tensile testing a bioresorbable stent strut to confirm it meets the input specification of $\ge 500$ MPa ultimate tensile strength per **ASTM F2477**.
- **Design Validation** confirms that the final device meets the user needs and intended use ("building the right device"). It is performed in simulated-use or clinical settings.
 - *Example:* A vascular surgeon successfully deploys the stent in a silicone anatomical model of a tortuous coronary artery without kinking, confirming the user need of "easy deliverability."
- Both are mandatory under **21 CFR 820.30** and must be completed before market release.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is a **Design History File (DHF)**? List four documents it must contain.

**Model Answer:**
A **Design History File (DHF)** is a complete, traceable compilation of records that documents the design history of a medical device, as mandated by **21 CFR 820.30(e)**.

It must contain (any four):

1. **Design and Development Plan (DDP)**
2. **Design Input Requirements (DIR)**
3. **Design Output documents (drawings, specs, software)**
4. **Design Review Reports (DRR)**
5. **Design Verification Test Reports**
6. **Design Validation Reports**
7. **Design Transfer Records**
8. **Risk Management File (per ISO 14971)**

The DHF must be **retained for the lifetime of the device** (or 2 years after the last device is released, whichever is longer per FDA).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 4 Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design Process & Controls

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **eight elements of Design Controls** as per **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**. Why are design reviews mandatory at multiple stages? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The eight elements of design control per **21 CFR 820.30** are:

1. **Design and Development Planning** — Defines the project scope, deliverables, responsibilities, resources, and review schedule.
2. **Design Input** — Physical, performance, regulatory, and user requirements, including risk control measures. Must be **complete, unambiguous, and verifiable**.
3. **Design Output** — Drawings, specifications, software code, manufacturing procedures, labeling, and packaging.
4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented review at the end of each design phase by **multi-disciplinary team** (engineering, QA, regulatory, clinical, manufacturing).
5. **Design Verification** — Objective evidence that design outputs meet design inputs (bench testing, FEA correlation, EMC).
6. **Design Validation** — Confirmation that the device meets user needs and intended use (simulated use, animal, clinical, human factors).
7. **Design Transfer** — Translation of the design into production-scale manufacturing with proper work instructions and inspection.
8. **Design Changes & DHF** — Formal change control and maintenance of the Design History File.

**Why multiple Design Reviews are mandatory:**
- Catch errors early when correction cost is low.
- Ensure cross-functional alignment (clinical + engineering + manufacturing + regulatory).
- Provide documented evidence for regulatory auditors.
- Force explicit acceptance of risk at each stage (risk-based gate decision).

**Diagrams:** A block diagram showing the inter-relationships of these elements earns full marks.

---

**(b)** A startup intends to develop a **wearable continuous glucose monitor (CGM)** for diabetic patients. Outline the **complete design and manufacture process** from concept to post-market, indicating the relevant ISO/FDA standards at each stage. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Activity | Applicable Standard |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Concept & Feasibility | Identify user need (non-invasive CGM); concept sketches; patent search | ISO 14971 (Preliminary Risk Analysis) |
| 2. Design Input | Sensor accuracy $\pm 10$%, battery life 14 days, biocompatible skin patch | ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility) |
| 3. Detailed Design | CAD of patch, sensor electrode design, BLE circuit, mobile app | IEC 60601-1 (Medical Electrical Safety) |
| 4. Design Output | Gerber files, BOM, firmware code, sterilization spec | IEC 62304 (Medical Device Software) |
| 5. Design Verification | EMC, electrical safety, sensor calibration per ASTM standards | 21 CFR 820.30(f) |
| 6. Design Validation | Simulated-use wear trial on 30 patients; human factors | IEC 62366-1 (Usability) |
| 7. Design Transfer | Injection mold tooling for patch; automated assembly line | ISO 13485 (QMS) |
| 8. Process Validation | IQ/OQ/PQ of patch assembly; EtO sterilization validation | ISO 11135 (EtO Sterilization) |
| 9. Regulatory Submission | FDA 510(k) for Class II device; CE Marking under EU MDR | 21 CFR 807 / EU MDR 2017/745 |
| 10. Post-Market | PMS, complaint handling, CAPA, PSUR | 21 CFR 803 (Medical Device Reporting) |

**Key Justification:**
The CGM is a Class II device because it is a non-life-sustaining diagnostic. The 510(k) pathway with a predicate device is suitable. Risk management is parallel, and the software is "Moderate" level of concern per FDA, requiring detailed documentation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — Manufacturing Process & Materials

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare the **manufacturing processes** suitable for producing (i) a **titanium orthopedic bone plate** and (ii) a **disposable PVC IV catheter**. Justify your choice based on material, tolerance, volume, and sterilization. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Titanium Bone Plate | PVC IV Catheter |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Ti-6Al-4V (biocompatible alloy) | PVC (flexible polymer) |
| **Production Volume** | Low (1k – 50k units/yr) | Very High (millions/yr) |
| **Tolerance Required** | $\pm 10$ – $50\ \mu m$ | $\pm 100$ – $200\ \mu m$ |
| **Best Process** | **CNC Machining** from forged Ti billet | **Injection Molding** with multi-cavity mold |
| **Justification** | CNC offers the precision, surface finish (Ra $< 0.8\ \mu m$), and flexibility for patient-specific plates; volume too low for stamping/molding | Injection molding offers unit cost $< \$0.05$, tight tolerance, and high repeatability; PVC flows easily in heated molds |
| **Surface Treatment** | Anodization / Passivation for osseointegration | None (disposable) |
| **Sterilization** | Gamma radiation (ISO 11137) or steam (ISO 17665) | EtO gas (ISO 11135) — PVC heat-sensitive |
| **Quality Check** | 100% dimensional + fatigue per ASTM F382 | Statistical sampling per ISO 2859-1 |
| **Cost per Unit** | \$30 – \$300 | \$0.05 – \$0.50 |

The fundamental difference is **material** driving the process: metals require subtractive forming (CNC), polymers lend themselves to formative/shape processes (injection molding).

---

**(b)** Discuss the **sterilization methods** available for medical devices. Why is **Ethylene Oxide (EtO)** preferred for polymer-based implants and devices with electronics? Mention relevant ISO standards. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Available Sterilization Methods:**

1. **Steam (Autoclave)** — $121$–$134^\circ C$, ISO 17665. Best for metal instruments; not for heat-sensitive polymers or electronics.
2. **Ethylene Oxide (EtO) Gas** — Low temperature ($37$–$63^\circ C$), ISO 11135. Penetrates packaging; compatible with polymers and electronics.
3. **Gamma Radiation** — Cobalt-60 source, ISO 11137. Penetrates bulk; can degrade UHMWPE and some polymers.
4. **Electron Beam (e-beam)** — ISO 11137. Similar to gamma but faster; lower penetration.
5. **Hydrogen Peroxide Plasma** — Low temp, ISO 22441. For heat/moisture-sensitive devices.
6. **Dry Heat** — ISO 20857. Slow, high temp, for powders/oils.
7. **Filtration (0.22 µm)** — For liquid pharmaceuticals only.

**Why EtO is preferred for polymers and electronics:**

- **Low temperature** ($37$–$63^\circ C$): Prevents melting/deformation of polymers (PVC, PE, silicone) and damage to heat-sensitive electronic components.
- **Penetrates packaging**: Allows sterilization of pre-packaged devices in sealed pouches.
- **Compatible with a wide range of materials**: Polymers, metals, ceramics, and assembled electronics.
- **Achieves SAL $= 10^{-6}$** when properly validated.

**Limitations of EtO:**
- Toxic and potentially carcinogenic — requires **aeration time** (typically 7–14 days) for residue dissipation.
- Long cycle (24–96 hours) — slow throughput.
- Requires validation of **EtO residue limits** per **ISO 10993-7**.

**Key ISO Standards Recap:**
- **ISO 11135** — EtO sterilization process validation
- **ISO 11137** — Radiation sterilization
- **ISO 17665** — Steam sterilization
- **ISO 10993-7** — EtO residual limits
- **ISO 22441** — H₂O₂ plasma sterilization

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse Verification with Validation — this costs up to **3 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **ISO / ASTM standard number** when describing a test — naming the test without the standard loses **1–2 marks**.
> - **Do NOT** claim "FDA approves all medical devices" — only **Class III** requires **PMA**; **Class II** is **510(k)** clearance.
> - **Do NOT** use vague quantities — KTU rewards specific tolerances (e.g., **$\pm 10\ \mu m$**), specific temperatures (e.g., **$121^\circ C$** for 15 min at 15 psi for autoclave), and specific **SAL values ($10^{-6}$)**.
> - In **design control** answers, always mention the **multi-disciplinary nature** of the design review team — single-person review is **not acceptable**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 Things You MUST Remember Before the KTU ESE Exam**

- **Design Control** is **mandatory** for FDA Class II and III; 8 elements per **21 CFR 820.30**.
- **Verification** = Output vs Input (building it right). **Validation** = Device vs User Needs (building the right device).
- **DHF** = complete design history, retained for device lifetime + 2 years.
- **DMR** = the manufacturing "recipe" for one device.
- **Risk Management** is **continuous** and follows **ISO 14971**.
- **Biocompatibility testing** is per **ISO 10993** series (cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation, etc.).
- **Sterilization SAL** must be $\mathbf{10^{-6}}$ for terminally sterilized devices.
- **EtO (ISO 11135)** is preferred for polymer and electronic devices (low temperature).
- **Gamma (ISO 11137)** is preferred for bulk polymer disposables; can degrade UHMWPE.
- **Autoclave (ISO 17665)** is for metal instruments ($121^\circ C$, 15 psi, 15 min minimum).
- **CNC machining** is the go-to process for metal implants with tight tolerances ($\pm 5\ \mu m$).
- **Injection molding** is the go-to for high-volume polymer disposables.
- **3D printing (additive manufacturing)** is used for patient-specific implants and surgical guides.
- **FDA classes**: I (low), II (moderate, 510(k)), III (high, PMA).
- **CE Marking** under **EU MDR 2017/745** with classes I, IIa, IIb, III.
- **Process validation** follows **IQ (Installation) → OQ (Operational) → PQ (Performance)**.
- **Process capability** $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ minimum; $\ge 1.67$ for critical-to-quality (CTQ) parameters.
- **Post-market surveillance** is **mandatory** and feeds back into risk management and design improvements.
- **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action) per **21 CFR 820.100** is required for any non-conformity.
- **Human factors / usability engineering** per **IEC 62366-1** is required for design validation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Assurance (QA)** in the context of biomedical device design is a comprehensive, systematic set of planned and documented activities implemented within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** to ensure that a medical device consistently fulfills its intended use, meets user requirements, and complies with applicable regulatory standards throughout the **Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC)**.

According to **ISO 13485:2016** (the international standard for medical device QMS), QA is part of the broader quality management approach that emphasizes **prevention of defects** rather than detection, covering all phases from design, manufacturing, distribution, installation, and post-market surveillance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> Quality Assurance is the integrated totality of actions, procedures, processes, and systems that establish confidence that a biomedical device will reliably satisfy its design specifications, safety requirements, and performance expectations as defined by its intended clinical use.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a five-star restaurant:

| Restaurant Analogy | Biomedical QA Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Master Recipe Book** (standard recipes for every dish) | **Design History File (DHF)** & Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) |
| **Sourcing fresh, certified ingredients** | **Supplier Qualification & Incoming Material Inspection** |
| **Following recipe steps exactly every time** | **Process Validation & Work Instructions** |
| **Tasting the dish before serving** | **In-Process Quality Control (QC) Checks** |
| **Customer feedback forms** | **Post-Market Surveillance & Complaint Handling** |
| **Health Inspector visiting monthly** | **FDA / Notified Body Audits (ISO 13485)** |
| **Investigating a food poisoning incident** | **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Actions)** |

In this analogy, **Quality Assurance is the entire kitchen's culture and processes** — the recipe, the training, the equipment, the hygiene. **Quality Control (QC)** is just the act of tasting each dish before serving. QA prevents the bad dish from ever being cooked; QC catches it just before it leaves the kitchen.

### 1.3 Key Quality Terminology — Foundation Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinctions Every KTU Student Must Know:**

- **Quality Assurance (QA):** Proactive, process-oriented, **PREVENTION**-focused. "Build quality in."
- **Quality Control (QC):** Reactive, product-oriented, **DETECTION**-focused. "Inspect quality out."
- **Quality Management System (QMS):** The complete organizational infrastructure governing QA/QC (e.g., ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR Part 820).
- **Design Controls:** A subset of the QMS that governs the **design and development** phase of medical devices.
- **Verification:** "Are we building the product **RIGHT**?" — Confirms design output meets design input.
- **Validation:** "Are we building the **RIGHT** product?" — Confirms the device meets user needs and intended use.
- **Regulatory Body:** Government authority enforcing device safety (e.g., **US FDA**, **CDSCO-India**, **EU Notified Bodies** under **MDR 2017/745**).

### 1.4 Governing Regulatory Standards & Numerical Benchmarks

| Standard / Regulation | Scope | Issuing Body |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 13485:2016** | QMS for medical devices | International Organization for Standardization |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Risk Management for medical devices | ISO |
| **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** | US Quality System Regulation (QSR) | US FDA |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | European Medical Device Regulation | European Commission |
| **IEC 62304** | Software lifecycle for medical device software | IEC |
| **ISO 62366-1:2015** | Usability Engineering | ISO |
| **BIS IS 13485** | Indian adoption of ISO 13485 | Bureau of Indian Standards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Point:** For KTU 2024 exams, the **most frequently cited** standards are **ISO 13485** (QMS), **ISO 14971** (Risk), and **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** (Design Controls). Memorize the clause numbers in ISO 13485 — they appear directly in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Quality System Framework — Layered Architecture

The Quality Assurance framework for biomedical devices can be decomposed into **five hierarchical layers**, each building upon the one below:

**Layer 1 — Regulatory Foundation (Why QA Exists)**
- Patient safety is the supreme priority (ethical & legal mandate).
- Devices failing in the field can cause **death or serious injury** (ISO/IEC terminology: **Serious Deterioration of State of Health**).
- Regulatory bodies mandate documented evidence of safety, efficacy, and quality.

**Layer 2 — Quality Management System (The Container)**
- ISO 13485 mandates the QMS must include: **document control, record control, management responsibility, resource management, product realization, measurement/analysis/improvement**.
- A QMS without design controls cannot legally bring a Class II/III device to market in the US/EU.

**Layer 3 — Design Controls (The Engineering Core)**
- Governed by **21 CFR 820.30** (FDA) and **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3**.
- The **8 design control elements** are:
  1. Design and Development Planning
  2. Design and Development Inputs
  3. Design and Development Outputs
  4. Design and Development Review
  5. Design and Development Verification
  6. Design and Development Validation
  7. Design and Development Transfer
  8. Design and Development Changes (including Design History File)

**Layer 4 — Risk Management (The Decision Engine)**
- Per **ISO 14971**, risk is defined as the **combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm**.
- The **Risk Management Process** is iterative: **Analysis → Evaluation → Control → Residual Risk Evaluation → Risk Management Report**.

**Layer 5 — Post-Market Activities (The Feedback Loop)**
- Post-Market Surveillance (PMS), complaint handling, vigilance reporting, trending, and CAPA.
- A device is never "finished" — it is monitored for its entire commercial life.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality Engineering Metrics

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = P \times S$ | Risk Magnitude (ISO 14971 conceptual) | $R$ = Risk, $P$ = Probability of harm, $S$ = Severity |
| 2 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index | $USL$ = Upper Spec Limit, $LSL$ = Lower Spec Limit, $\sigma$ = standard deviation |
| 3 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability (Centred Process, accounts for shift) | $\mu$ = process mean |
| 4 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities (Six Sigma) | — |
| 5 | $\sigma\text{-level} = \text{NORMSINV}(1.5/\text{DPMO}) + 1.5$ | Six Sigma Level Conversion | NORMSINV = inverse normal CDF |
| 6 | $\text{AOQL} = \dfrac{h \cdot p \cdot (N - d)}{N}$ | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | $h$ = avg items inspected in sample, $N$ = lot size, $d$ = defectives accepted, $p$ = process fraction defective |
| 7 | $\text{Process Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{D}{U}$ | First-pass yield | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units |
| 8 | $\text{Hazard Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Failure Rate}_{\text{new}}}{\text{Failure Rate}_{\text{reference}}}$ | Reliability comparison | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat for Markdown:** In the formula table above, vertical bars are intentionally absent to preserve table syntax. The $C_{pk}$ absolute-value form has been algebraically expanded into two separate inequalities to ensure clean rendering.

### 2.3 Design Control Sub-Process — Granular Breakdown

**Design Input (Clause 7.3.3):**
- Functional requirements
- Performance requirements
- Applicable regulatory requirements
- Risk management outputs (from ISO 14971)
- Usability requirements (from IEC 62366)
- Outputs of clinical evaluation
- Biocompatibility requirements (per **ISO 10993** series)

**Design Output (Clause 7.3.4):**
- Drawings, specifications, software source code
- Manufacturing, inspection, labeling, packaging specifications
- Work instructions, training materials
- **Design Input → Design Output must be traceable (Design Trace Matrix).**

**Design Review (Clause 7.3.5):**
- Documented, systematic, held at suitable design stages
- Participants: cross-functional team (engineering, QA, regulatory, clinical, manufacturing)
- Output: review minutes, action items, approvals

**Design Verification (Clause 7.3.6):**
- Performed against design inputs
- Includes: bench testing, software verification, type testing
- Typically done on **production-equivalent units** (not prototypes if design is finalized)

**Design Validation (Clause 7.3.7):**
- Performed against **user needs and intended use**
- Must include **production units** or **equivalent** under simulated/actual use conditions
- Clinical evaluation or usability testing

**Design Transfer (Clause 7.3.8):**
- Ensures design outputs become production specifications
- Includes transfer of knowledge to manufacturing
- Validates production processes can build the design

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quality Assurance systems in biomedical devices are not bureaucratic overhead — they are **engineered safety nets**. Practical utilities include:

- **Liability Protection:** Documented design controls are the legal defense during product liability litigation.
- **Time-to-Market Acceleration:** A mature QMS reduces regulatory back-and-forth (FDA 510(k) average review ~ 90 days vs. months of deficiency letters without proper documentation).
- **Defect Cost Reduction:** Industry data shows that fixing a defect in the design phase costs roughly **$1**, in production it costs **$10–100**, and in the post-market phase it can cost **$1,000+** in recalls, lawsuits, and brand damage.
- **Global Market Access:** ISO 13485 certification is the global "passport" for medical device market entry in 150+ countries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$

The $C_{pk}$ metric quantifies how well a process can produce output within specification limits, accounting for both **process spread** and **process centering**. The derivation proceeds from the Gaussian process model.

**Step 1 — Model the Process as Gaussian.**
For a stable process, the output $X$ follows a normal distribution:

$$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\ \sigma^2)$$

**Step 2 — Express the Specification Window.**
The tolerance region between the Lower Specification Limit ($LSL$) and the Upper Specification Limit ($USL$) is:

$$\Delta_{\text{spec}} = USL - LSL$$

**Step 3 — Express the Process Spread.**
A "well-behaved" process is conventionally bounded by $\pm 3\sigma$ from the mean, giving a total natural width of:

$$\Delta_{\text{process}} = 6\sigma$$

**Step 4 — Define the Naive Capability $C_p$.**
The first capability index compares the specification window to the process spread:

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{spec}}}{\Delta_{\text{process}}}$$

**Step 5 — Account for Process Centering.**
$C_p$ assumes the process mean $\mu$ is perfectly centered at the midpoint of the specifications. In reality, processes drift. Define the centering offset:

$$\delta = \mu - \text{Midpoint of Specs} = \mu - \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute One-Sided Distances.**
The two one-sided distances from the mean to the nearest specification limit are:

$$d_{\text{upper}} = USL - \mu$$

$$d_{\text{lower}} = \mu - LSL$$

**Step 7 — Form the Two One-Sided Capability Indices.**
Each index measures how many "sigma widths" (of $3\sigma$ each) fit between the mean and the nearest limit:

$$C_{\text{pu}} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{\text{pl}} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 8 — Take the Minimum (Worst-Case Side).**
The process is only as capable as its weaker side. Therefore:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\bigl(C_{\text{pu}},\ C_{\text{pl}}\bigr)
= \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the Result.**
- $C_{pk} = 1.00$ → Process is barely capable (0.27% out-of-spec).
- $C_{pk} = 1.33$ → Industry baseline for many medical devices.
- $C_{pk} = 1.67$ → Capable process (parts-per-thousand defect level).
- $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ → Six Sigma world-class process.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — $C_{pk}$ for a Pacemaker Battery Voltage

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A pacemaker's battery voltage at end-of-service is specified as $LSL = 2.40\text{ V}$ and $USL = 2.80\text{ V}$. A production run of $n = 50$ units yields sample mean $\mu = 2.62\text{ V}$ and sample standard deviation $\sigma = 0.05\text{ V}$. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and interpret the result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{2.80 - 2.40}{6 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.30} = 1.333$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{pu}} &= \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2.80 - 2.62}{3 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{0.18}{0.15} = 1.200
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{pl}} &= \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2.62 - 2.40}{3 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{0.22}{0.15} = 1.467
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(1.200,\ 1.467) = 1.200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**
- $C_p = 1.333$ suggests the process spread is acceptable.
- $C_{pk} = 1.200 < C_p$ indicates the process is **shifted off-centre** toward the upper specification limit (the battery is running "hot").
- **Engineering Action:** Adjust the manufacturing process to bring $\mu$ closer to the midpoint $\dfrac{2.40 + 2.80}{2} = 2.60\text{ V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always report $C_{pk}$ with the **side that is limiting** and **comment on the offset direction**. A $C_{pk}$ answer without interpretation loses 2 of the 7 marks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Verification vs. Validation Comparison

| Dimension | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | "Did we build it right?" | "Did we build the right thing?" |
| **Reference** | Design **Inputs** | User **Needs** & Intended Use |
| **Standards** | 21 CFR 820.30(f), ISO 13485 Cl. 7.3.6 | 21 CFR 820.30(g), ISO 13485 Cl. 7.3.7 |
| **Sample Type** | Production-equivalent | Production units (under simulated/actual use) |
| **Typical Method** | Bench tests, software unit tests, type testing | Clinical evaluation, usability studies, animal studies |
| **Output Type** | Test reports, pass/fail metrics | Clinical/lab evidence of safety & effectiveness |
| **Stage** | Throughout design and development | Late in development, after design freeze |
| **Failure Implication** | "Design does not meet specifications" | "Device does not meet user need" |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Risk Management per ISO 14971

| Step | Activity | Output Document |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Analysis | Risk analysis worksheet, FMEA |
| 2 | Risk Evaluation | Risk evaluation matrix (probability × severity) |
| 3 | Risk Control | Risk control measures & verification |
| 4 | Residual Risk Evaluation | Acceptability of residual risk |
| 5 | Risk Management Report | Overall residual risk acceptability statement |
| 6 | Production & Post-Production Information | Updates to risk file based on field data |

### 3.5 CAPA — Corrective and Preventive Action Workflow

The CAPA system (governed by **21 CFR 820.100** and **ISO 13485 Clause 8.5.2/8.5.3**) operates as a closed-loop problem solver:

**Step 1 — Identification:** A non-conformance, complaint, audit finding, or trend is detected.

**Step 2 — Containment:** Immediate action to stop further defective output (e.g., quarantine of a batch).

**Step 3 — Root Cause Analysis:** Techniques include:
- **5 Whys Analysis**
- **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** — categories: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment.
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**

**Step 4 — Corrective Action:** Eliminate the **root cause** of the detected non-conformance.

**Step 5 — Preventive Action:** Eliminate the **potential root cause** so the problem does not recur elsewhere.

**Step 6 — Verification of Effectiveness:** Confirm the actions actually solved the problem (data trending over time).

**Step 7 — Documentation:** Update procedures, training records, design outputs, and risk file as needed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quality Management System (QMS) — Master Architecture Flow

This Mermaid diagram maps the **complete QMS architecture** as defined by ISO 13485 and the US FDA Quality System Regulation, showing how Quality Assurance is embedded at every lifecycle stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["REGULATORY FOUNDATION"]:::root
    node2["ISO 13485 QMS"]:::layer2
    node3["FDA 21 CFR 820"]:::layer2
    node4["EU MDR 2017 745"]:::layer2
    node5["DESIGN CONTROLS PHASE"]:::layer3
    node6["Design Planning"]:::sub
    node7["Design Inputs"]:::sub
    node8["Design Outputs"]:::sub
    node9["Design Review"]:::sub
    node10["Design Verification"]:::sub
    node11["Design Validation"]:::sub
    node12["Design Transfer"]:::sub
    node13["RISK MANAGEMENT ISO 14971"]:::layer3side
    node14["MANUFACTURING AND CONTROLS"]:::layer4
    node15["Receiving Inspection"]:::sub
    node16["In Process QA"]:::sub
    node17["Final Release Testing"]:::sub
    node18["Statistical Process Control"]:::sub
    node19["POST MARKET PHASE"]:::layer5
    node20["Complaint Handling"]:::sub
    node21["Vigilance Reporting"]:::sub
    node22["CAPA System"]:::sub
    node23["Management Review"]:::top
    node24["Documentation and Records DHF DMR DHR"]:::doc

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node2 --> node14
    node3 --> node14
    node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8 --> node9 --> node10 --> node11 --> node12
    node5 --> node13
    node14 --> node15
    node14 --> node16
    node14 --> node17
    node14 --> node18
    node14 --> node19
    node19 --> node20
    node19 --> node21
    node19 --> node22

    node23 --> node14
    node24 --> node5
    node24 --> node14
    node24 --> node19

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer2 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

```

### 4.2 Risk Management Process per ISO 14971

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    riskA["STEP 1 RISK ANALYSIS"]:::ra
    riskB["STEP 2 RISK EVALUATION"]:::re
    riskC["STEP 3 RISK CONTROL"]:::rc
    riskD["STEP 4 RESIDUAL RISK EVAL"]:::rd
    riskE["STEP 5 RISK MANAGEMENT REPORT"]:::re2
    riskF["STEP 6 PROD AND POST PROD INFO"]:::rf
    outA["Intended Use and Foreseeable Misuse"]:::out
    outB["Hazard Identification and Severity"]:::out
    outC["Risk Control Measures Implemented"]:::out
    outD["Acceptability of Residual Risk"]:::out
    outE["Overall Residual Risk Acceptable?"]:::out
    outF["Updates to Risk File From Field Data"]:::out

    riskA --> riskB --> riskC --> riskD --> riskE
    riskE --> riskF
    riskF -.->|"loop back to update"| riskA
    riskA --- outA
    riskB --- outB
    riskC --- outC
    riskD --- outD
    riskE --- outE
    riskF --- outF

    classDef ra fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef re fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef rc fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef rd fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef re2 fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef rf fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#db2777,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#ffffff,stroke:#6b7280,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 CAPA — Closed-Loop Problem Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    capa1["NON CONFORMANCE OR EVENT DETECTED"]:::c1
    capa2["CONTAINMENT Immediate Action"]:::c2
    capa3["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS 5 Whys Fishbone FTA"]:::c3
    capa4["CORRECTIVE ACTION Eliminate Detected Cause"]:::c4
    capa5["PREVENTIVE ACTION Eliminate Potential Cause"]:::c5
    capa6["VERIFY EFFECTIVENESS Data Trending"]:::c6
    capa7["DOCUMENTATION Update SOPs Training Risk File"]:::c7
    capa8["FEEDBACK TO QMS AND MANAGEMENT REVIEW"]:::c8

    capa1 --> capa2 --> capa3 --> capa4 --> capa5 --> capa6 --> capa7 --> capa8
    capa8 -.->|"trigger new CAPA if ineffective"| capa1

    classDef c7 fill:#db2777,stroke:#831843,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef c8 fill:#374151,stroke:#111827,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**"Differentiate between Quality Assurance and Quality Control in the context of medical device manufacturing."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process-oriented, **prevention** of defects | Product-oriented, **detection** of defects |
| **Approach** | Proactive — build quality in | Reactive — inspect quality out |
| **Activities** | SOPs, training, process validation, design controls, audits | Inspections, tests, sampling, acceptance criteria |
| **Timing** | Throughout the entire product life cycle | Primarily during manufacturing and post-production |
| **Documentation** | Quality Manual, QMS procedures, design history file | Inspection records, test reports, batch records |

**Key distinction (1 mark):** QA is the **system**; QC is the **measurement** within the system.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**"List and briefly explain the five stages of the risk management process as defined in ISO 14971."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Risk Analysis (1 mark):** Identify hazards, characterize harm, estimate probability and severity for each hazardous situation.
2. **Risk Evaluation (0.5 mark):** Compare estimated risk against acceptability criteria defined in the risk management plan; decide if risk reduction is needed.
3. **Risk Control (0.5 mark):** Implement measures in the following priority order — inherent safety by design, protective measures in the device, information for safety (labeling/training).
4. **Residual Risk Evaluation (0.5 mark):** Assess risk after control measures; determine if it is acceptable; perform risk/benefit analysis if not.
5. **Risk Management Report & Post-Production Information (0.5 mark):** Document overall residual risk acceptability; update the risk file with production and post-market data; review at planned intervals.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** Students answer ONE full question from the module. Each question is 14 marks and contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Design Controls & QMS for a New Blood Glucose Monitor**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) List the eight elements of design controls as per FDA 21 CFR 820.30 and ISO 13485 Clause 7.3. Explain in detail the significance of **Design Input** and **Design Output**, with a suitable example for a **portable blood glucose monitor**. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**The Eight Design Control Elements (2 marks):**

1. Design and Development Planning
2. Design and Development Inputs
3. Design and Development Outputs
4. Design and Development Review
5. Design and Development Verification
6. Design and Development Validation
7. Design and Development Transfer
8. Design and Development Changes (controls over design changes; DHF)

**Design Input — Definition & Significance (2.5 marks):**

Design Input is the **foundation** of the design process. It consists of the **physical, performance, regulatory, and user requirements** that form the basis for device design. Per ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.3, design inputs must be:
- Documented
- Reviewed for adequacy
- Approved by designated individuals
- Traceable to outputs

**Example for a portable blood glucose monitor:**
- Measurement range: **40–400 mg/dL** (clinical requirement)
- Accuracy: ±15% or ±15 mg/dL (per **ISO 15197:2013**)
- Sample volume: ≤ 1 µL
- Test time: ≤ 10 seconds
- Operating temperature: **10–40°C**
- Battery life: ≥ 1000 tests
- Biocompatibility of strip materials (per **ISO 10993-1**)
- Electromagnetic compatibility (per **IEC 60601-1-2**)
- Intended user: lay person / patient (drives usability requirements per IEC 62366)
- Sterility / non-sterility requirements
- Labeling language requirements (e.g., US, EU, India)

**Design Output — Definition & Significance (2.5 marks):**

Design Output is the **result of the design effort** — the documented description of the device that allows it to be manufactured, inspected, used, and serviced. Per ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.4, outputs must be:
- Documented
- Expressed in terms that can be verified against design inputs
- Approved before release

**Examples of design output for the blood glucose monitor:**
- Engineering drawings of the device housing and PCB
- Bill of Materials (BOM)
- Test strip chemical formulation
- Software source code and algorithm for glucose calculation
- Labeling and Instructions for Use (IFU)
- Manufacturing Work Instructions
- Packaging specifications
- Biocompatibility test reports
- Risk management file (per ISO 14971)

**Traceability (the link):** A **Design Traceability Matrix (DTM)** must map **each design input** to the **design output(s)** that satisfies it, and to the **verification & validation** activities that prove it. Without this, an FDA auditor will issue a 483 observation.

---

**(b) Explain the difference between **Verification and Validation** with a clinical-grade example. Describe a typical **Design History File (DHF)** and its contents for the same blood glucose monitor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Verification vs. Validation (3.5 marks):**

| Dimension | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question** | Are we building the device **right**? | Are we building the **right** device? |
| **Reference** | Design **Inputs** | User **Needs & Intended Use** |
| **When** | During design and development | At design freeze, before market release |
| **How** | Bench testing, software unit/integration tests, type testing, dimensional inspection | Clinical study, usability study, simulated-use testing, animal study |
| **Sample** | Production-equivalent units | Production units under actual/simulated use |
| **Output** | Pass/fail test reports, specifications compliance | Evidence of clinical safety and effectiveness |

**Example for the blood glucose monitor:**

- **Verification (3.5 marks continued):** Bench test 30 strips from 3 production lots against a reference glucose analyzer using blood samples of known concentration (40, 75, 150, 400 mg/dL). Plot the bias vs. reference; confirm **99% of results fall within ±15 mg/dL for values < 75 mg/dL and within ±15% for values ≥ 75 mg/dL** per **ISO 15197**. The output is a test report showing compliance with the design input specification.

- **Validation:** A usability study (per IEC 62366) with 30 lay users (representative of intended population) performing self-testing using the device at home, unsupervised, with only the IFU as guidance. Document use errors, comprehension of results, near-patient scenarios. The output is a usability validation report demonstrating the device meets the user need.

**Design History File (DHF) (3.5 marks):**

The DHF is a **compilation of records** describing the design history of a finished device. It is mandated by **21 CFR 820.30(j)**. Contents include:

1. Design and Development Plan
2. User Needs Document
3. Design Input Requirements Specification
4. Design Output Drawings, Specifications, Software Documentation
5. Design Review Minutes (each formal review)
6. Verification Test Protocols and Reports
7. Validation Protocols and Reports (including clinical/usability)
8. Design Transfer Records (handover to manufacturing)
9. Design Change Records
10. Risk Management File (per ISO 14971) — hazards, FMEA, risk control verification
11. Design Traceability Matrix
12. Labeling and IFU
13. Biocompatibility, EMC, electrical safety test reports
14. Shelf-life / sterilization validation (if applicable)
15. Design History File Index

**Why the DHF matters (closing sentence):** The DHF is the **single most important document** in a regulatory audit. If the FDA asks for the DHF during an inspection and it is incomplete, the company may receive a Form 483 and risk product seizure or import alert.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Risk Management, CAPA & Process Capability**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the **ISO 14971 risk management process**. Identify the four main techniques used during risk analysis. For an **infusion pump**, list five potential hazards and their corresponding risk control measures. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**ISO 14971 Process — Block Diagram (2 marks):**

A clean process diagram should show: **Risk Analysis → Risk Evaluation → Risk Control → Residual Risk Evaluation → Risk Management Report → Production & Post-Production Information** with a feedback loop from the last step back to the first (see Section 4.2 for the Mermaid equivalent).

**Four Risk Analysis Techniques (2 marks):**

1. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA):** Top-down deductive analysis of how component failures combine to cause a top-level hazard event.
2. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA):** Bottom-up inductive analysis of individual failure modes, their effects, severity, probability, and detectability. Computes **Risk Priority Number (RPN = S × O × D)**.
3. **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP):** Uses guide words (No, More, Less, As Well As, etc.) to systematically explore process deviations.
4. **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA):** Early-stage identification of hazards based on device class and energy sources, used at the start of design.

**Five Hazards and Risk Controls for an Infusion Pump (3 marks):**

| # | Hazard | Risk Control Measure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Over-infusion** (drug overdose) | Software dose-limit checks; redundant flow sensors; alarm on flow deviation > 10% |
| 2 | **Free-flow** (uncontrolled gravity flow) | Mechanical anti-free-flow clamp; automatic stop on door open |
| 3 | **Air-in-line** (embolism) | Ultrasonic air-in-line sensor; alarm and stop pump at > 0.1 mL air |
| 4 | **Battery failure mid-infusion** | Low-battery alarm ≥ 30 min before depletion; backup capacitor for safe shutoff |
| 5 | **Electromagnetic interference** causing incorrect rate | EMC compliance testing per **IEC 60601-1-2**; shielding and software filtering |

---

**(b) A manufacturer of a Class III orthopedic implant is monitoring the **surface roughness (Ra)** of a polished femoral stem. Specifications are $LSL = 0.05\ \mu m$, $USL = 0.25\ \mu m$. A production sample yields $n = 50$ units with mean $\bar{x} = 0.18\ \mu m$ and standard deviation $s = 0.03\ \mu m$. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, interpret the result, and describe the **CAPA workflow** that would be triggered if the process began to drift. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Process Capability Computation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \dfrac{0.25 - 0.05}{6 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.18} = 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{pu}} &= \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s} = \dfrac{0.25 - 0.18}{3 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.09} = 0.778
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{pl}} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} = \dfrac{0.18 - 0.05}{3 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.09} = 1.444
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(0.778,\ 1.444) = 0.778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

$C_{pk} = 0.778 < 1.00$ indicates the process is **NOT capable** of meeting specifications as currently configured. The limiting side is the **upper** specification (Ra is too high — the surface is too rough), with the process mean sitting 0.07 µm below the USL versus 0.09 µm of natural spread. The process needs **immediate improvement** (not just re-centring), since even shifting the mean to the midpoint 0.15 µm would only give:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk}^{\text{best}} &= \dfrac{0.05}{3 \times 0.03} = 0.556
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — the limit is the USL distance. After re-centering, $C_{pk}$ would equal $C_p = 1.111$ (an improvement). The drift must be eliminated through **variation reduction** (better polishing process control) and **centering adjustment**.

**Step 3 — CAPA Workflow Triggered by Drift (3 marks):**

1. **Detection (SPC Triggers):** The control chart (X-bar, R) shows points outside control limits or 7 consecutive points on one side. A non-conformance is logged.

2. **Containment:** Quarantine the suspect batch of femoral stems; perform 100% inspection of inventory; assess need for recall of distributed product.

3. **Root Cause Analysis:** Use 5-Whys, Fishbone, and FMEA. Example causes — worn polishing wheel, contaminated slurry, operator training lapse, temperature/humidity variation, tool wear.

4. **Corrective Action:** Replace worn tools, recalibrate polishing equipment, retrain operators, update the preventive maintenance schedule.

5. **Preventive Action:** Add an SPC rule to the polishing station; install real-time Ra in-line measurement; add an alert when any sample approaches the USL.

6. **Verify Effectiveness:** Monitor Cpk for the next 30 production batches. Target Cpk ≥ 1.33 before closing the CAPA.

7. **Documentation:** Update the Device History Record, Risk Management File (FMEA), Design Transfer documents, and report the closure in the next Management Review.

8. **Vigilance Reporting:** If any non-conforming device reached the market and posed a serious risk, file a **Medical Device Report (MDR)** with the FDA (within 30 days) and a **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)** in the EU.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation (loses 2–3 marks):** "Verification is testing; validation is also testing" — WRONG. State the exact question each answers ("right way" vs "right thing") and the reference (inputs vs. user needs).
> 2. **Forgetting Design Traceability (loses 1–2 marks):** Always mention that a Design Traceability Matrix (DTM) links inputs → outputs → V&V activities. This is a KTU-favoured keyword.
> 3. **Skipping the $C_{pk}$ interpretation (loses 1 mark):** The numerical value alone is incomplete. Always say whether the process is capable, which side is limiting, and the corrective direction.
> 4. **Wrong ISO 14971 step count (loses 0.5 mark):** There are 6 steps in the ISO 14971:2019 risk management process (Analysis, Evaluation, Control, Residual Risk Evaluation, Risk Management Report, Production & Post-Production Information). Do not list 5 or 7.
> 5. **Not citing the standard (loses 0.5–1 mark):** Always cite the specific standard clause: ISO 13485:2016 Cl. 7.3, 21 CFR 820.30, ISO 14971:2019, IEC 62366-1:2015. Examiners reward this.
> 6. **Forgetting the feedback loop in CAPA (loses 1 mark):** Always mention "verification of effectiveness" and "feedback to the QMS / management review."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Assurance (Module 4)**

- **QA = Prevention; QC = Detection.** Both are essential, neither replaces the other.
- **ISO 13485:2016** is the **international QMS standard** for medical devices; 8 clauses you must know exist (4–8 are the technical ones).
- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** is the **US Quality System Regulation**; 21 CFR 820.30 covers Design Controls.
- **EU MDR 2017/745** has replaced the older MDD 93/42/EEC; CE Marking under MDR requires notified body involvement for most devices.
- **Design Controls have 8 elements** — memorize them in the order: Plan → Inputs → Outputs → Review → Verification → Validation → Transfer → Changes.
- **Verification = "Are we building the product right?"** (against design inputs, bench tests, software tests).
- **Validation = "Are we building the right product?"** (against user needs, clinical/usability studies).
- **Design History File (DHF)** is the regulatory record of the design process; 21 CFR 820.30(j) mandates it.
- **ISO 14971:2019** is the **risk management standard**; risk = probability × severity; the 6-step process is iterative.
- **CAPA = Corrective AND Preventive Actions** — distinguish "fix what is wrong" from "fix what might go wrong"; close the loop with effectiveness verification.
- **Process Capability Indices:**
  - $C_p$ = spread only (assumes centered process).
  - $C_{pk}$ = spread + centering (the real-world metric).
  - $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is the typical minimum for medical device manufacturing.
- **Six Sigma: $C_{pk} = 2.00$** corresponds to ~3.4 DPMO.
- **Risk Control Hierarchy (inherent order of priority):** Inherent safety by design → Protective measures in the device → Information for safety (labeling, training).
- **Vigilance & Post-Market:** FDA MDR (30 days), EU FSCA/FSN, India CDSCO adverse event reporting — all are legal obligations once the device is on the market.
- **Documentation is Evidence:** A QMS without documented evidence is not a QMS — it is a wish. Everything must be in writing, dated, and signed.
- **KTU 2024 Favourite Keywords:** "traceability," "intended use," "residual risk," "design transfer," "validation under simulated use conditions," "post-market surveillance feedback loop" — drop these in every answer.
- **Numerical Skill Required:** Be fluent with $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, DPMO, and the AQL/AOQL concept. Practice at least 2 numerical problems per standard.
- **Closing Mindset:** In biomedical design, the QA system is the **moral and legal contract** between the manufacturer and the patient. Every document you sign defends a life.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Audits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Audits in Biomedical Device Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Audit**, in the context of biomedical device design and manufacturing, is a **systematic, independent, and documented examination** of a Quality Management System (QMS), design controls, manufacturing processes, or regulatory compliance status, conducted to verify conformity with predetermined requirements such as **ISO 13485:2016**, **FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (QSR)**, **EU MDR 2017/745**, or other applicable standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An audit is a structured evaluation process used to determine whether the design, development, and manufacturing activities of a medical device conform to planned arrangements, regulatory requirements, and the organization's own QMS, and whether these arrangements are effectively implemented and maintained.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **hospital safety inspection**. Before a new MRI machine is allowed to operate, an independent inspector walks through the facility, checks installation logs, verifies staff training records, examines calibration certificates, and tests emergency shutdown systems. The inspector does NOT fix problems — they only **document** what they find and report it.

A **biomedical device audit** works the same way:
- The **auditor** = the independent inspector
- The **QMS / Design History File (DHF)** = the hospital's documentation system
- The **audit findings** = the inspector's report
- The **Corrective Action (CA)** = the hospital fixing the issues found

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** An audit is NOT a test of devices — it is a test of the **process** that produced the devices. Even a perfectly functioning device can "fail" an audit if its design and manufacturing records are incomplete or non-compliant.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **QMS** | Quality Management System |
| **DHF** | Design History File |
| **DMR** | Device Master Record |
| **CAPA** | Corrective and Preventive Action |
| **CFR** | Code of Federal Regulations |
| **Notified Body** | Organization accredited to audit medical device manufacturers for CE marking |
| **MDSAP** | Medical Device Single Audit Program |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The standard reference period for biomedical device audits is **ISO 13485:2016** and **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** (currently transitioning to QMSR — Quality Management System Regulation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Audits

Biomedical device audits are broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. Internal Audits (First-Party Audits)
- Conducted by the **organization's own personnel** or hired consultants.
- Purpose: To evaluate the effectiveness of the QMS before external audits.
- Frequency: Typically **at least once per year** for each QMS process (per ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.4).
- Performed by **independent, trained auditors** who are NOT responsible for the area being audited.

### B. External Audits
External audits are further divided into:

1. **Second-Party Audits** — Conducted by **customers** or by parties having a contractual relationship with the organization.
2. **Third-Party Audits** — Conducted by **independent certification bodies** (e.g., Notified Bodies, ISO registrars, FDA inspectors).

### C. Regulatory Audits
- Conducted by **regulatory authorities** (FDA, EU Competent Authorities, CDSCO India).
- Mandatory for market authorization and continued compliance.
- May be **announced, semi-announced, or unannounced** (e.g., EU MDR allows unannounced audits).

### D. Supplier Audits
- Conducted at **suppliers and subcontractors** to verify their capability and quality.
- Critical for components like sterile packaging, electronic components, software, and sterile barriers.

### E. Process / Product Audits
- **Process Audit:** Examines a specific manufacturing or design process.
- **Product Audit:** Examines a specific device for conformity to specifications.

### F. Design Audit
- A focused review of the **Design History File (DHF)** for completeness, traceability, and regulatory compliance.
- Conducted at **design review milestones** and prior to design transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Audit Independence Principle:** The auditor must be **independent of the activity being audited** to ensure objectivity. An engineer cannot audit their own design work.

## 2.2 The Audit Process (Audit Cycle)

The biomedical device audit follows a structured **6-stage cycle**:

### Stage 1: Audit Planning
- Define **audit scope, objectives, and criteria**.
- Select auditors and form the **audit team**.
- Develop the **audit plan** and checklists.
- Notify the auditee in advance (for announced audits).

### Stage 2: Document Review (Stage 1 Audit)
- Review QMS documentation, procedures, work instructions.
- Examine **Design History File (DHF)** contents.
- Verify that documented procedures exist before on-site evaluation.

### Stage 3: On-Site Audit Activities
- **Opening Meeting:** Confirm scope, schedule, and confidentiality.
- **Data Collection:** Interviews, observation, document examination.
- Use techniques like **sampling, tracing, and verification**.

### Stage 4: Audit Findings & Classification

Findings are classified into three categories:

| Classification | Description | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical Nonconformity** | Immediate regulatory risk; product safety compromised; systemic failure | Immediate correction; possible product recall; regulatory notification |
| **Major Nonconformity** | Significant deviation from QMS; possible product impact; absence of required procedure | Root cause analysis; CAPA within 30–60 days |
| **Minor Nonconformity** | Isolated incident; limited product impact; housekeeping issue | Documented correction; CAPA within 90 days |
| **Observation / OFI** | Opportunity for improvement; not a nonconformity | Continuous improvement suggestion |

### Stage 5: Audit Report
- Document **objective evidence, findings, conclusions, and recommendations**.
- Distribute to relevant stakeholders.
- Maintain as controlled record per QMS requirements.

### Stage 6: Follow-Up Audit (Closure)
- Verify that **Corrective Actions (CA)** have been implemented and are effective.
- **Close out** findings only after evidence of effectiveness is provided.

## 2.3 Audit Sampling (Statistical Foundation)

Audit sampling is a critical technique used when it is impractical to examine 100% of records.

$$n = \frac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot p \cdot q / (Z^2 \cdot E^2)}$$

Where:
- $n$ = sample size
- $N$ = total population size (e.g., number of DHF records)
- $p$ = estimated proportion of nonconformities (typically 0.5 for unknown)
- $q = 1 - p$
- $Z$ = Z-score for confidence level (e.g., 1.96 for 95% confidence)
- $E$ = acceptable margin of error (e.g., 0.05)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Audit Mathematics:**

| Formula | Application |
|---|---|
| $n = \frac{N}{1 + (N-1) p q / (Z^2 E^2)}$ | Sample size for audit record review |
| $\text{Audit Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Issues Found} \cap \text{Real Issues}}{\text{Total Real Issues}}$ | Sensitivity of audit detection |
| $\text{CAPA Closure Rate} = \frac{\text{CAPAs Closed on Time}}{\text{Total CAPAs Due}} \times 100\%$ | Follow-up performance metric |
| $\text{Audit Coverage} = \frac{\text{Processes Audited}}{\text{Total QMS Processes}} \times 100\%$ | Completeness of internal audit program |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In a Notified Body audit, the auditor may sample 5–10 Design History Files out of 50 — the sampling must be statistically justified and risk-based per **ISO 19011:2018** (Guidelines for Auditing Management Systems).

## 2.4 Risk-Based Approach to Audits

Modern audit philosophy emphasizes **risk-based thinking** (ISO 13485:2016, Clause 0.2):

- **Higher-risk devices** (Class III, implantable, life-sustaining) receive **more frequent and more thorough audits**.
- **Lower-risk devices** (Class I non-sterile, non-measuring) receive proportionally reduced audit attention.
- Auditors use **risk matrices** to determine audit depth.

$$\text{Audit Priority} = f(\text{Device Risk}, \text{Process Complexity}, \text{Historical Nonconformities}, \text{Supplier Criticality})$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Audit Sample Size Calculation

### Problem Statement
A notified body auditor needs to review **Design History Files (DHFs)** for a Class II medical device manufacturer. The company has **N = 200** DHFs on file. The auditor wants **95% confidence** with a **margin of error of 5%**. Assuming no prior knowledge of nonconformity rate, calculate the required audit sample size.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the given parameters.

$$N = 200, \quad Z = 1.96, \quad E = 0.05, \quad p = 0.5, \quad q = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Write the audit sampling formula.

$$n = \frac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot \frac{p \cdot q}{Z^2 \cdot E^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator term inside the fraction.

$$(N-1) = 200 - 1 = 199$$

**Step 4:** Compute $p \cdot q$.

$$p \cdot q = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$$

**Step 5:** Compute $Z^2 \cdot E^2$.

$$Z^2 = (1.96)^2 = 3.8416$$

$$E^2 = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025$$

$$Z^2 \cdot E^2 = 3.8416 \times 0.0025 = 0.009604$$

**Step 6:** Compute the full denominator.

$$1 + \frac{199 \times 0.25}{0.009604} = 1 + \frac{49.75}{0.009604}$$

**Step 7:** Perform the division.

$$\frac{49.75}{0.009604} \approx 5180.13$$

**Step 8:** Add 1 to the denominator.

$$\text{Denominator} = 1 + 5180.13 = 5181.13$$

**Step 9:** Compute the final sample size.

$$n = \frac{200}{5181.13} \approx 0.0386$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The calculated sample size is approximately **0.04**, which is less than 1. This indicates that with a population of 200 and a 5% margin of error at 95% confidence, statistical theory suggests very few samples are needed. However, **regulatory auditors do not rely solely on statistics** — they use a **minimum sample of 5 records per process** as a practical floor, plus **risk-based selection** of additional files (e.g., complaints, CAPAs, design changes).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: CAPA Closure Rate Calculation

### Problem Statement
A medical device manufacturer's quality dashboard shows the following Q1 audit results:
- Total CAPAs issued = **45**
- CAPAs closed within deadline = **32**
- CAPAs overdue = **13**

Calculate the **CAPA Closure Rate** and interpret it.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.

$$\text{CAPA Closure Rate} = \frac{\text{CAPAs Closed on Time}}{\text{Total CAPAs Due}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\text{CAPA Closure Rate} = \frac{32}{45} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$\text{CAPA Closure Rate} = 0.7111 \times 100\% \approx 71.11\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A CAPA closure rate of **71.11%** is **below the typical industry benchmark of 85–90%**. This indicates systemic weakness in the corrective action process and may be flagged as a **Major Nonconformity** in a regulatory audit. The FDA considers chronic CAPA delays as evidence of an **ineffective QMS**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Audit Risk Priority Scoring

### Problem Statement
An internal audit program must prioritize 4 QMS processes. The risk-based priority score is computed as:

$$\text{Priority} = (\text{Device Risk}) \times (\text{Process Complexity}) \times (\text{Historical NCs})$$

| Process | Device Risk (1–5) | Process Complexity (1–5) | Historical NCs (1–5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design Control | 5 | 5 | 4 |
| Production Control | 4 | 4 | 3 |
| Supplier Management | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| Document Control | 2 | 2 | 1 |

Determine the **audit order**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute priority for Design Control.

$$P_{\text{Design}} = 5 \times 5 \times 4 = 100$$

**Step 2:** Compute priority for Production Control.

$$P_{\text{Production}} = 4 \times 4 \times 3 = 48$$

**Step 3:** Compute priority for Supplier Management.

$$P_{\text{Supplier}} = 3 \times 3 \times 2 = 18$$

**Step 4:** Compute priority for Document Control.

$$P_{\text{Document}} = 2 \times 2 \times 1 = 4$$

**Step 5:** Rank the processes in descending order of priority.

$$\text{Design Control (100)} > \text{Production Control (48)} > \text{Supplier Mgmt (18)} > \text{Document Control (4)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The audit program should audit **Design Control first** as it carries the highest risk-weighted priority. This aligns with FDA's heightened focus on **21 CFR 820.30 (Design Controls)** for Class II and Class III devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Audit Lifecycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Audit Planning Scope, Criteria, Team] --> B[Stage 1 Document Review QMS, DHF, SOPs]
    B --> C[Stage 2 On-Site Audit Opening Meeting, Interviews, Observation]
    C --> D[Findings Classification Critical, Major, Minor, OFI]
    D --> E[Audit Report Objective Evidence, Conclusions]
    E --> F[CAPA Initiation Root Cause Analysis]
    F --> G[Follow-Up Audit Verify Effectiveness]
    G --> H[Closure Approved or Escalation]
    H --> I[Continuous Improvement Feedback to QMS]
    I --> A
```

## 4.2 Audit Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Audit[Biomedical Device Audits] --> Internal[Internal Audits]
    Audit --> External[External Audits]
    Audit --> Regulatory[Regulatory Audits]
    Audit --> Supplier[Supplier Audits]
    Audit --> Design[Design Audits]
    External --> Second[Second-Party Customer]
    External --> Third[Third-Party Certification Body]
    Regulatory --> FDA[FDA Inspection]
    Regulatory --> EU[EU Notified Body]
    Regulatory --> MDSAP[MDSAP Audit]
    Design --> DHR[Design History Review]
    Design --> VnV[Verification and Validation Review]
```

## 4.3 Audit Finding Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Finding[Audit Finding] --> NC{Nonconformity?}
    Finding --> OFI[Observation Opportunity for Improvement]
    NC --> Critical[Critical Immediate Safety Risk]
    NC --> Major[Major Systemic Deviation]
    NC --> Minor[Minor Isolated Issue]
    Critical --> Action1[Immediate Stop Ship Recall Notification]
    Major --> Action2[CAPA within 30-60 Days]
    Minor --> Action3[CAPA within 90 Days]
    OFI --> Action4[Document for Improvement]
```

## 4.4 Risk-Based Audit Process Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Risk Inputs Device Class, Process, History] --> Matrix[Risk Matrix Calculation]
    Matrix --> Plan[Audit Plan Generation]
    Plan --> Execute[Execute Audit Sampling, Interviews]
    Execute --> Analyze[Analyze Findings Severity]
    Analyze --> Report[Generate Audit Report]
    Report --> CAPA[Trigger CAPA Process]
    CAPA --> Verify[Verify CAPA Effectiveness]
    Verify --> Close[Close Audit Loop]
    Close --> Input
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams map the **functional workflow** of an audit program. The cyclic nature of the audit process emphasizes that audits are not one-time events but a **continuous loop** feeding back into QMS improvement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
**Define the term "Audit" as applied to a biomedical device Quality Management System. List any THREE types of audits.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
An **audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining objective evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria (such as ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR Part 820, or internal procedures) are fulfilled (1 mark).

**Three types of audits (1/2 mark each):**
1. Internal Audit (First-Party)
2. External Audit (Second-Party / Third-Party)
3. Regulatory Audit (e.g., FDA inspection, Notified Body audit)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2]**
**Differentiate between a "Major Nonconformity" and a "Critical Nonconformity" in the context of a medical device audit.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Major Nonconformity (1.5 marks) | Critical Nonconformity (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Impact | Significant deviation from QMS; may affect product quality | Immediate patient safety risk; product non-compliant |
| Example | Missing documented procedure for design verification | Use of non-sterile components in sterile device |
| Action | CAPA within 30–60 days | Immediate correction; possible recall; regulatory notification |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six stages of the audit process** as per ISO 19011:2018, with specific reference to biomedical device QMS audits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class III medical device manufacturer has **N = 500** DHFs. An auditor requires **98% confidence** (Z = 2.33) with a **margin of error of 3%**. Calculate the minimum sample size and explain why statistical sampling is necessary in audits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The six stages of the audit process as per **ISO 19011:2018** (1 mark per stage, with biomedical device examples):

1. **Initiating the Audit** — Define audit scope (e.g., design controls for Class III implants), objectives, criteria (ISO 13485, 21 CFR 820), and select the audit team (1 mark).

2. **Preparing Audit Activities** — Develop the audit plan, assign tasks, prepare working documents (checklists, sampling plans) for the biomedical device process being audited (1 mark).

3. **Conducting the Audit** — Hold the opening meeting, communicate during the audit, collect and verify information through interviews, observation, and document review of the **Design History File (DHF)**, **Device Master Record (DMR)**, and CAPA records (1 mark).

4. **Preparing Audit Findings** — Generate audit findings with classification (Critical, Major, Minor, OFI) supported by objective evidence; prepare conclusions (1 mark).

5. **Distributing the Audit Report** — Issue the report within the agreed timeframe to the auditee and relevant regulatory stakeholders (1 mark).

6. **Completing the Audit** — Verify that corrective actions have been implemented and are effective; close out findings formally (1 mark).

7. **Conducting Audit Follow-Up** *(also accepted)* — Periodic verification of CAPA effectiveness as part of the **continuous audit cycle** (1 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.

$$N = 500, \quad Z = 2.33, \quad E = 0.03, \quad p = 0.5, \quad q = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Write the formula.

$$n = \frac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot \frac{p \cdot q}{Z^2 \cdot E^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $Z^2 \cdot E^2$.

$$Z^2 = (2.33)^2 = 5.4289$$

$$E^2 = (0.03)^2 = 0.0009$$

$$Z^2 \cdot E^2 = 5.4289 \times 0.0009 = 0.004886$$

**Step 4:** Compute the correction factor.

$$\frac{(N-1) \cdot p \cdot q}{Z^2 \cdot E^2} = \frac{499 \times 0.25}{0.004886} = \frac{124.75}{0.004886} \approx 25533.36$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator.

$$1 + 25533.36 = 25534.36$$

**Step 6:** Compute $n$.

$$n = \frac{500}{25534.36} \approx 0.0196$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation and minimum sampling rule.

$$\text{Statistical minimum} \approx 0.02 \text{ (1 record)}$$

$$\text{Practical regulatory minimum} = 5 \text{ records per process} + \text{risk-based oversampling}$$

**Step 8:** Necessity of statistical sampling (3 marks):
- **Resource limitation:** Auditors cannot review 100% of records; sampling makes audits feasible (1 mark).
- **Risk-based decision making:** Statistical confidence ensures findings are representative (1 mark).
- **Regulatory acceptance:** ISO 19011 and FDA recognize sampling as a valid audit tool provided justification is documented (1 mark).

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of audit findings** (Critical, Major, Minor, Observation) with **two examples each** from a biomedical device design context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **risk-based approach to auditing** as mandated by ISO 13485:2016. Describe how a manufacturer's audit frequency and depth should vary for a **Class I device** versus a **Class III implantable device**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Critical Nonconformity (1.5 marks):**
- Definition: A nonconformity that represents an immediate and unacceptable risk to patient safety or product quality, or a systemic regulatory violation.
- **Example 1:** A sterile barrier packaging process with no validation evidence, and the device is currently being shipped (0.75 mark).
- **Example 2:** Software in a Class III pacemaker with no documented verification and validation, and known field complaints of malfunction (0.75 mark).

**2. Major Nonconformity (1.5 marks):**
- Definition: A nonconformity that indicates a significant breakdown in the QMS, or the absence of a required procedure.
- **Example 1:** The CAPA procedure requires root cause analysis using 5-Why or fishbone, but 70% of CAPAs have no documented root cause (0.75 mark).
- **Example 2:** Design changes made after design transfer without following the change control procedure (0.75 mark).

**3. Minor Nonconformity (1.5 marks):**
- Definition: An isolated incident that does not indicate a systemic breakdown.
- **Example 1:** A single training record missing an employee signature for one course (0.75 mark).
- **Example 2:** A work instruction that references an outdated revision number of a related document (0.75 mark).

**4. Observation / OFI (1.5 marks):**
- Definition: A situation that does not constitute a nonconformity but has potential for improvement.
- **Example 1:** The design review procedure is followed, but design reviews are often delayed, suggesting a need for better scheduling (0.75 mark).
- **Example 2:** Trend analysis of complaints is performed quarterly, but increasing the frequency to monthly might yield earlier detection of issues (0.75 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Risk-Based Auditing Principle (3 marks):**
The risk-based approach, mandated by **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 0.2**, requires the organization to:
- Identify risks and opportunities associated with QMS processes (1 mark).
- Allocate audit resources proportionate to the **risk to patient safety** and **product risk classification** (1 mark).
- Use **post-market surveillance data, complaint trends, and CAPA history** to dynamically adjust audit focus (1 mark).

**Class I vs. Class III Audit Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Class I Device (e.g., elastic bandage) (2 marks) | Class III Implantable (e.g., pacemaker) (2 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Audit Frequency** | Annual; shorter on-site duration | Annual + unannounced; extended on-site duration |
| **Audit Depth** | Focus on basic QMS, labeling, UDI | Deep dive into Design Controls, Risk Management (ISO 14971), Biocompatibility, Clinical Evaluation |
| **Sampling** | Small sample of records (5–10) | Large sample, including DHF, V&V, biocompatibility, software lifecycle |
| **Regulatory Scrutiny** | Self-certification / minimal | FDA PMA approval; EU MDR Class III conformity assessment; higher Notified Body involvement |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Audit" with "Inspection":** An audit reviews the **QMS and processes**, NOT individual products. Students often lose 1–2 marks by describing product testing instead of process evaluation.
> 2. **Misclassifying Findings:** Writing "minor" when the example clearly demonstrates patient safety risk will result in zero credit for that sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting Internal Choice:** In Part B, you must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Writing parts of both without choosing is penalized.
> 4. **Skipping Steps in Numerical Problems:** Even if the final answer is correct, omitting intermediate steps in the sampling formula will cost 2–3 marks. Always show **all six calculation steps**.
> 5. **Citing outdated standards:** Reference **ISO 13485:2016** (current), not the 2003 version. FDA 21 CFR Part 820 is transitioning to **QMSR** — mention both if relevant.
> 6. **Forgetting Auditor Independence:** In any internal audit answer, mention that the auditor must NOT be from the department being audited (ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.4).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Audit Definition:** Systematic, independent, documented examination of QMS conformity to ISO 13485 / 21 CFR 820.
- ✅ **Types of Audits:** Internal (First-Party), External (Second/Third-Party), Regulatory, Supplier, Design.
- ✅ **Six Stages of Audit:** Initiating → Preparing → Conducting → Findings → Report → Follow-Up.
- ✅ **Finding Classification:** **Critical** (safety risk, immediate) → **Major** (systemic, 30–60 days CAPA) → **Minor** (isolated, 90 days CAPA) → **OFI** (improvement).
- ✅ **Auditor Independence Rule:** Auditors must NOT audit their own work (ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.4).
- ✅ **Audit Frequency:** At least once per year for each QMS process.
- ✅ **Sampling Formula:** $n = N / [1 + (N-1) p q / (Z^2 E^2)]$; use practical minimum of **5 records** per process.
- ✅ **CAPA Closure Rate Benchmark:** 85–90% is industry standard; below 80% = Major NC.
- ✅ **Risk-Based Auditing:** Higher-risk devices (Class III) get more frequent, deeper, and unannounced audits.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** ISO 13485:2016, ISO 19011:2018, FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (→ QMSR), EU MDR 2017/745, MDSAP.
- ✅ **Design Audit Focus:** DHF completeness, traceability, design controls per 21 CFR 820.30.
- ✅ **Unannounced Audits:** Allowed under EU MDR for Notified Bodies; FDA also conducts "for-cause" unannounced inspections.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post Market Surveillance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Post Market Surveillance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** is defined under the **EU Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR 2017/745, Article 2(60))** as *"all activities carried out by manufacturers, in cooperation with other economic operators, with the aim of establishing and keeping up to date a systematic and proactive process to collect, record, analyse and act upon data on the quality, performance and safety of a device throughout its lifetime."*

In the context of the **Indian regulatory framework** governed by the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** under the **Medical Device Rules (MDR) 2017**, PMS is a continuous, mandatory, post-licensing vigilance system that requires manufacturers of medical devices to monitor, document, and report any adverse events, device deficiencies, or safety concerns that emerge after the device has been placed in the Indian market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PMS is a **lifecycle activity** — it begins the *moment* a device is released and continues until the device is permanently withdrawn from service. It is not a one-time submission; it is an **ongoing regulatory obligation**.

The corresponding global and national regulatory bodies that govern PMS include:

| Regulatory Authority | Jurisdiction | Key PMS Document |
|---|---|---|
| **US FDA** (21 CFR 803) | United States | Medical Device Reporting (MDR) |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | European Union | Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR) |
| **PMDA** | Japan | Post-Marketing Surveillance (PMS) |
| **CDSCO** | India | Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI) |
| **TGA** | Australia | Adverse Event Reports |
| **Health Canada** | Canada | Mandatory Problem Reporting (MPR) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Car Dashboard After You Drive Off the Showroom Floor"**
>
> Imagine you buy a brand new car. Before delivery, the manufacturer has tested it in labs, on test tracks, and in crash simulations. But the **real world** is messier — different drivers, different climates, different fuel qualities, unforeseen road conditions. So once the car is in customers' hands, the manufacturer installs:
>
> 1. **A dashboard warning system** → equivalent to *Adverse Event Reporting* (lights up when something is wrong)
> 2. **A service centre feedback loop** → equivalent to *Complaint Handling System*
> 3. **A recall mechanism** → equivalent to *Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)* when a defect is found
