> 4. **A yearly health-check report** → equivalent to *Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)*
> 5. **A continuous data telemetry stream** → equivalent to *Real-World Performance Data*
>
> **Post-Market Surveillance is exactly this "after-delivery" monitoring system, but for medical devices** — where the "car" could be a pacemaker, an MRI scanner, or a hip implant, and the "drivers" are patients whose lives depend on it.

## 1.3 Why PMS is Non-Negotiable — The Core Justification

The necessity of PMS arises from the **inherent limitations of pre-market clinical evaluation**:

- **Limited sample size** in pre-market trials (often <1000 subjects)
- **Short follow-up duration** (rarely >2 years for implants)
- **Homogeneous patient population** (excludes elderly, paediatric, pregnant, comorbid patients)
- **Controlled environment** (excludes real-world usage variability)
- **Rare adverse events** (incidence <1 in 10,000) cannot be statistically detected
- **Long-term failure modes** (e.g., metal ion release from implants after 7 years) only emerge post-market
- **Operator-dependent errors** (e.g., misuse of an electrosurgical unit) only appear in field use
- **Device-drug / device-device interactions** in polypharmacy patients are rarely tested pre-market

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Cited in Literature:** Approximately **70% of all safety-related device modifications and recalls** occur *after* market release, confirming that pre-market testing, however rigorous, cannot substitute for continuous PMS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Objectives of Post-Market Surveillance

PMS is engineered to accomplish **five engineering and regulatory objectives**, each tied to a measurable outcome:

1. **Detect previously unknown adverse events** (signal detection)
2. **Identify device-related root causes** of failure (Root Cause Analysis — RCA)
3. **Update the Risk Management File (RMF)** as per **ISO 14971:2019**
4. **Initiate Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)** through the Quality Management System (QMS per **ISO 13485:2016**, Clause 8.2.3)
5. **Provide regulatory feedback** to authorities (FDA, CDSCO, Notified Bodies) for continuous benefit-risk re-evaluation

## 2.2 The Six Pillars of Post-Market Surveillance

A robust PMS system is built on **six interlocking pillars**. Missing even one compromises the entire surveillance architecture.

| Pillar | Purpose | Trigger / Source | Output Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Complaint Handling** | Capture user-reported issues | Customer call, email, field engineer | Complaint Record (CR) |
| **2. Adverse Event Reporting** | Document patient harm events | Hospital, clinician, patient | MDR Report / Vigilance Report |
| **3. Trend Analysis** | Detect statistical signals | Aggregated complaint data | Trend Report (statistical) |
| **4. Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)** | Prevent recurrence of harm | Confirmed device deficiency | FSCA Notice + Customer Letter |
| **5. Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** | Generate new clinical evidence | Scheduled clinical study | PMCF Evaluation Report (PMCER) |
| **6. Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** | Aggregate annual summary | All of the above | PSUR (Class IIa, IIb, III) |

## 2.3 Risk-Based Classification of PMS Activities

The **depth and frequency** of PMS activities is **directly proportional to the device's risk class** as defined by the regulatory framework. Higher risk → more stringent PMS obligations.

| Device Class (EU MDR) | PMS Reporting Frequency | PSUR Required? | PMCF Required? | Example Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** (low risk) | On event basis | No (voluntary) | Not mandatory | Stethoscope, walking frame |
| **Class IIa** (low-medium) | Annual summary | Yes (every 2 years) | Recommended | Hearing aid, surgical gloves |
| **Class IIb** (medium-high) | Annual summary | Yes (annually) | Required | Ventilator, dialysis machine |
| **Class III** (high risk) | Continuous + Annual | Yes (annually) | Mandatory (continuous) | Pacemaker, implantable defibrillator, heart valve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For **Class III and implantable devices**, the PSUR must be **updated annually** and submitted to the **Notified Body** for review. The benefit-risk determination must be **re-affirmed** each cycle, and any change triggers an update to the **Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance (SSCP)**.

## 2.4 The PMS Data Flow — End-to-End

The PMS data flow follows a **closed-loop architecture** analogous to a feedback control system in classical engineering:

$$\text{Field Data} \rightarrow \text{Acquisition} \rightarrow \text{Triage} \rightarrow \text{Investigation} \rightarrow \text{CAPA} \rightarrow \text{Regulatory Reporting} \rightarrow \text{Design Update}$$

This loop is governed by the **Deming Cycle (PDCA — Plan, Do, Check, Act)**:

- **Plan:** PMS plan (PMSP) outlining data sources, indicators, methodologies
- **Do:** Execute data collection, complaint intake, AE reporting
- **Check:** Analyse data, detect trends, evaluate benefit-risk
- **Act:** Implement CAPA, update risk files, issue recalls if needed

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following quantitative formulas are **standard regulatory and statistical formulas** that govern PMS decision-making. These are **tested numerically in KTU ESE** papers.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Adverse Event Rate (AER)** | $AER = \dfrac{N_{AE}}{N_{devices\ sold}} \times 10^{n}$ | $N_{AE}$ = adverse events, $N_{devices}$ = units in field | events per $\vert 10^{n} \mid$ devices | Benchmarks device safety post-market |
| **Complaint Rate (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{N_{complaints}}{N_{units\ sold\ per\ period}} \times 100$ | $N_{complaints}$, $N_{units}$ | percent ($\%$) | Monitors quality drift over time |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $SNR = \dfrac{\mu_{observed} - \mu_{baseline}}{\sigma_{baseline}}$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std dev | dimensionless | Statistically flags abnormal event clusters |
| **Reporting Timeliness (RT)** | $RT = t_{reported} - t_{known}$ | $t$ = timestamps | days / hours | Measures compliance with regulatory clocks (e.g., 15-day MDR) |
| **Recall Effectiveness Index (REI)** | $REI = \dfrac{N_{returned\ devices}}{N_{distributed\ devices}} \times 100$ | $N_{returned}$, $N_{distributed}$ | percent ($\%$) | Quantifies FSCA success |
| **Trend Threshold (Z-score)** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \bar{X}}{\sigma}$ | $X$ = current value, $\bar{X}$ = historical mean | dimensionless | Triggers trend report when $Z \geq 2.0$ |
| **Benefit-Risk Ratio (BRR)** | $BRR = \dfrac{P_{benefit}}{P_{risk}}$ | $P_{benefit}$, $P_{risk}$ | dimensionless | Determines continued market authorisation |
| **Sample Size for PMCF (post-hoc power)** | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{d^{2}}$ | $Z$ = Z-scores, $p$ = proportion, $d$ = margin | count | Determines PMCF study cohort size |

## 2.6 Adverse Event Classification — Severity Codes (MedDRA-Based)

Adverse Events are coded using the **Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA)** at five hierarchical levels:

1. **System Organ Class (SOC)** — e.g., Cardiac disorders
2. **High Level Group Term (HLGT)** — e.g., Heart failures
3. **High Level Term (HLT)** — e.g., Left ventricular failures
4. **Preferred Term (PT)** — e.g., Cardiac arrest
5. **Lowest Level Term (LLT)** — e.g., "Heart stopped beating" (patient's own words)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The MedDRA hierarchy is a **favourite 3-mark question**. Always remember it cascades from **broadest (SOC)** to **most specific (LLT)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Adverse Event Rate (AER) and Statistical Signal Detection

Let us derive the **Trend Detection Threshold** used in PMS, since this is the most commonly tested quantitative problem in KTU examinations.

### Step 1 — Define the Baseline Distribution

Assume the historical complaint rate $X$ for a device follows a **Normal Distribution** with:
- Historical mean: $\bar{X}$
- Historical standard deviation: $\sigma$

This assumption is validated by the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** when aggregated over many reporting periods.

$$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\bar{X},\ \sigma^{2})$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Current Observed Rate

For the current reporting period, count the number of complaints $N_{current}$ and divide by units sold $N_{units}$:

$$X_{current} = \dfrac{N_{current}}{N_{units}}$$

### Step 3 — Standardise to a Z-score

The Z-score measures how many standard deviations $X_{current}$ lies from the historical mean:

$$Z = \dfrac{X_{current} - \bar{X}}{\sigma}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Decision Rule

| Z-score Range | Statistical Interpretation | PMS Action |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert Z \vert \lt 1.96$ | Within 95% confidence interval | No action — routine PMS |
| $1.96 \leq \vert Z \vert \lt 2.58$ | Borderline signal (95–99%) | Increase monitoring frequency |
| $\vert Z \vert \geq 2.58$ | Strong signal (>99%) | Trigger trend report + CAPA investigation |
| $\vert Z \vert \geq 3.0$ | Critical signal | Initiate FSCA, notify regulator |

### Step 3.2 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A manufacturer of Class IIb infusion pumps has sold **50,000 units** in the last quarter. The historical mean complaint rate is **$\bar{X} = 0.5\%$** with standard deviation $\sigma = 0.15\%$. In the current quarter, **400 complaints** were received. Should a trend report be triggered?

**Step 1 — Compute current rate:**

$$X_{current} = \dfrac{400}{50{,}000} = 0.008 = 0.8\%$$

**Step 2 — Convert baseline to same scale (decimal):**

$$\bar{X} = 0.005,\quad \sigma = 0.0015$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z-score:**

$$Z = \dfrac{0.008 - 0.005}{0.0015} = \dfrac{0.003}{0.0015} = 2.0$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

Since $Z = 2.0 \geq 1.96$ but $< 2.58$, the manufacturer must **increase monitoring frequency** and conduct a **root-cause investigation**, but is not yet obligated to file a formal trend report or FSCA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to convert the **percentage to a decimal** before applying the Z-score formula. A $0.5\%$ rate must be entered as **0.005**, not 0.5. Failure to do so gives $Z = 2.0/0.15 = 13.3$, which is an absurd result and loses 2–3 marks.

### 3.3 Derivation of Sample Size for Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)

PMCF studies require a justified sample size to achieve adequate statistical power. The standard formula for a **single-proportion test** is:

$$n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{d^{2}}$$

**Worked Example:** Determine sample size for a PMCF study on a Class III implantable cardiac defibrillator, where:
- Expected event-free survival rate $p = 0.95$
- Margin of error $d = 0.02$
- Significance level $\alpha = 0.05 \Rightarrow Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$
- Power $= 80\% \Rightarrow Z_{\beta} = 0.84$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$n = \dfrac{(1.96 + 0.84)^{2} \cdot 0.95 \cdot (1 - 0.95)}{(0.02)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify numerator:**

$$(2.80)^{2} = 7.84$$

$$0.95 \cdot 0.05 = 0.0475$$

$$7.84 \cdot 0.0475 = 0.3724$$

**Step 3 — Simplify denominator:**

$$(0.02)^{2} = 0.0004$$

**Step 4 — Final sample size:**

$$n = \dfrac{0.3724}{0.0004} = 931$$

**Step 5 — Apply attrition adjustment** (typical 20% loss to follow-up):

$$n_{adjusted} = \dfrac{931}{0.80} = 1164 \approx 1200\ \text{patients}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For **Class III implants**, the required PMCF cohort can exceed 1000 patients and span 3–5 years. This represents a **major post-market investment** and is a key reason why high-risk devices have 5–10x longer market approval costs than Class I devices.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Trigger a Recall (FSCA)

The decision to issue a Field Safety Corrective Action follows a structured algorithm:

| Criterion | Score 0 | Score 1 | Score 2 | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Patient harm severity** | None | Non-serious | Serious injury / death | $\times 5$ |
| **Probability of recurrence** | Negligible | Low | High | $\times 3$ |
| **Detectability by user** | Obvious | Moderate | Hidden failure mode | $\times 2$ |
| **Population at risk** | <100 patients | 100–10,000 | >10,000 | $\times 4$ |
| **Available workaround** | Yes, fully | Partial | None | $\times 3$ |

**Total Risk Priority Number (RPN):**

$$RPN = 5 \cdot S_{harm} + 3 \cdot P_{recurrence} + 2 \cdot D_{detect} + 4 \cdot N_{population} + 3 \cdot W_{workaround}$$

**Decision Rule:**

- $RPN \leq 20$: Monitor only (Field Safety Notice for Information)
- $20 < RPN \leq 40$: Enhanced monitoring + targeted communication
- $RPN > 40$: **Mandatory FSCA + Recall** + Regulatory notification within **15 calendar days** (FDA MDR clock)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PMS System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Field Data Sources] --> B[Data Acquisition Layer]
    A1[Hospitals and Clinicians] --> A
    A2[Patients and Caregivers] --> A
    A3[Distributors and Sales] --> A
    A4[Literature and Databases] --> A
    A5[Regulatory Authority Feeds] --> A
    B --> C[Triage and Classification Module]
    C --> D1[Complaint Register ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.2]
    C --> D2[Adverse Event Log MedDRA Coded]
    C --> D3[Device Deficiency Record]
    D1 --> E[Investigation and Root Cause Analysis]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[CAPA Engine ISO 13485 Clause 8.5]
    F --> G1[Design Change Request]
    F --> G2[Manufacturing Process Update]
    F --> G3[Labelling and IFU Revision]
    E --> H[Signal Detection and Trend Analysis]
    H --> I{Decision Gate}
    I -->|RPN Low| J1[Routine PMS Update]
    I -->|RPN Medium| J2[Field Safety Notice FSN]
    I -->|RPN High| J3[Field Safety Corrective Action FSCA]
    J3 --> K[Regulatory Reporting]
    K --> K1[FDA MDR within 15 days]
    K --> K2[CDSCO Materiovigilance Report]
    K --> K3[EU Vigilance Report to Notified Body]
    F --> L[Update Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    L --> M[PSUR Generation]
    M --> N[Annual Submission to Regulator]
    N --> O[Continuous Lifecycle Loop]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 PMS Information Flow — Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT FIELD SENSORS]
        A1[Hospital Incident Reports]
        A2[Patient Feedback]
        A3[Distributor Returns]
        A4[Service Engineer Logs]
    end
    INPUT --> B[Data Acquisition and Validation]
    B --> C[Signal Processing and MedDRA Coding]
    C --> D{Trend Detector}
    D -->|Normal| E[Archive and Baseline Update]
    D -->|Anomalous| F[Root Cause Investigation]
    F --> G[CAPA Implementation]
    G --> H[Design Update and IFU Revision]
    H --> I[Field Deployment of Fix]
    I --> J[Effectiveness Check]
    J --> D
    E --> K[PSUR Compilation]
    K --> L[Regulatory Submission]
    L --> M[Database Update and Learning]
    M --> C
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Adverse Event Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Event Detected in Field] --> B{Did the event contribute to or cause death or serious deterioration in health}
    B -->|YES| C[Serious Adverse Event SAE]
    B -->|NO| D{Is there a device malfunction that could lead to serious harm}
    D -->|YES| E[Reportable Malfunction]
    D -->|NO| F{Is there a significant device deficiency without harm}
    F -->|YES| G[Near Miss / Incidence Report]
    F -->|NO| H[Routine Complaint Log Only]
    C --> I[Report to FDA within 15 working days]
    C --> J[Report to CDSCO within 15 calendar days]
    C --> K[EU Vigilance Reporting per MDR]
    E --> I
    G --> L[Internal Trend Monitoring Only]
    H --> M[Close after Documentation]
```

## 4.4 PMS Documentation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Post Market Surveillance Plan PMSP] --> B[Input Document]
    A --> C[Defines Data Sources Methods and Indicators]
    A --> D[Identifies Key Risk Areas from RMF]
    A --> E[Establishes Timelines and Responsibilities]
    B --> F[Post Market Surveillance Report PMSR Class I IIa]
    C --> F
    D --> G[Periodic Safety Update Report PSUR Class IIa IIb III]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Annual Update to Risk Management File]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance SSCP]
    I --> J[Public Database EUDAMED]
    G --> K[Post Market Clinical Follow up Report PMCER]
    K --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define Post-Market Surveillance (PMS). List any four activities that constitute PMS for a Class IIb medical device as per EU MDR 2017/745.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) is a systematic and continuous process by which a medical device manufacturer collects, analyses, and acts upon data on the quality, performance, and safety of a device after it has been placed on the market, throughout its lifetime, with the objective of identifying and reducing the risk of adverse events and updating the benefit-risk determination.

**Four PMS Activities for Class IIb Devices:**

1. **Complaint Handling and Adverse Event Reporting** under **ISO 13485:2016** and **21 CFR 803** — every complaint is logged, investigated, and (if serious) reported to the regulator within **15 calendar days**.
2. **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** as mandated by **EU MDR Annex XIV Part B** — proactive collection of clinical data from real-world use to confirm safety and performance.
3. **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR) Preparation and Submission** — Class IIb devices require an **annual** PSUR submitted to the Notified Body, summarising complaint trends, vigilance reports, CAPA actions, and updated benefit-risk.
4. **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA) Implementation** — issuing Field Safety Notices, product recalls, software patches, or design modifications when a safety signal is confirmed.

*(Valid additional answers: trend analysis, CAPA, Risk Management File update, SSCP update, vigilance reporting to CDSCO.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**What is the difference between a Field Safety Notice (FSN) and a Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Field Safety Notice (FSN) | Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A communication sent to users/customers to **inform** them of a safety issue | A **physical action** taken by the manufacturer to **eliminate or reduce** the risk |
| **Purpose** | Awareness / advisory | Prevention / remediation |
| **Action Type** | Informational only | Includes recall, modification, software update, relabelling |
| **Mandatory?** | Only if FSCA is required | Yes, if risk threshold is exceeded |

**Examples:**

- **FSN Example:** A manufacturer of insulin pumps issues a letter to all hospitals informing them of a software bug discovered in firmware version 3.2.1, advising users to upgrade — *no recall is required* since users can install the patch remotely.
- **FSCA Example:** A manufacturer of hip implants issues a **recall** of a specific batch due to higher-than-expected metallosis reports — patients must be contacted for revision surgery, and the action is reported to FDA / CDSCO / Notified Body.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six pillars of Post-Market Surveillance** for a medical device. For each pillar, state the governing standard and one key deliverable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Indian manufacturer has sold **20,000 units of a Class IIb blood pressure monitor** in the calendar year 2023. The historical average complaint rate is **1.2%** with a standard deviation of **0.3%**. In 2023, **320 complaints** were received. Determine:
  (i) The complaint rate for 2023.
  (ii) The Z-score against the historical baseline.
  (iii) The recommended PMS action. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Six Pillars of PMS (7 marks)

The six pillars of a robust Post-Market Surveillance system, aligned with **EU MDR 2017/745**, **ISO 13485:2016**, **ISO 14971:2019**, and **21 CFR 803**, are:

1. **Complaint Handling System**
   - **Standard:** ISO 13485:2016, Clause 8.2.2
   - **Deliverable:** Complaint Record (CR) with root cause classification
   - All customer feedback (oral, written, electronic) must be captured in a structured database.

2. **Adverse Event Reporting / Medical Device Reporting (MDR)**
   - **Standard:** 21 CFR 803 (US FDA); EU MDR Article 87; CDSCO MDR 2017
   - **Deliverable:** Vigilance Report / MDR Form
   - Mandatory reporting within **15 working days** (FDA) or **15 calendar days** (EU / CDSCO) for serious events.

3. **Trend Analysis and Signal Detection**
   - **Standard:** ISO 13485:2016, Clause 8.2.3; EU MDR Article 88
   - **Deliverable:** Statistical Trend Report
   - Aggregated complaint data analysed statistically (Z-score, control charts) to detect early signals of failure.

4. **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)**
   - **Standard:** EU MDR Article 82; GHTF SG2 guidance
   - **Deliverable:** FSCA Report + Field Safety Notice (FSN)
   - Triggered when a confirmed device deficiency poses unacceptable risk to patients.

5. **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)**
   - **Standard:** EU MDR Annex XIV Part B
   - **Deliverable:** PMCF Evaluation Report (PMCER)
   - Proactive clinical study to confirm long-term safety and performance, especially for Class III and implantable devices.

6. **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)**
   - **Standard:** EU MDR Article 86
   - **Deliverable:** PSUR submitted to the Notified Body
   - Annual (Class IIb / III) or biennial (Class IIa) summary of all PMS data, benefit-risk re-evaluation, and CAPA outcomes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **omit the standard reference** for each pillar, losing up to **1.5 marks** out of 7. Always cite the **specific clause number** (e.g., *"ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.3"*), not just the standard name.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Units sold in 2023: $N_{units} = 20{,}000$
- Historical mean complaint rate: $\bar{X} = 1.2\% = 0.012$
- Historical standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.3\% = 0.003$
- Complaints received in 2023: $N_{complaints} = 320$

**Step (i) — Complaint Rate for 2023:**

$$X_{2023} = \dfrac{N_{complaints}}{N_{units}} = \dfrac{320}{20{,}000} = 0.016 = 1.6\%$$

**Step (ii) — Z-score Calculation:**

$$Z = \dfrac{X_{2023} - \bar{X}}{\sigma} = \dfrac{0.016 - 0.012}{0.003} = \dfrac{0.004}{0.003} = 1.333$$

**Step (iii) — Recommended PMS Action:**

Since $Z = 1.333 < 1.96$ (the 95% confidence threshold), the increase in complaints is **not statistically significant** at the 95% level.

**Recommended Actions:**
- Continue **routine PMS monitoring** at current frequency.
- File a **non-statistically-significant internal trend note** for documentation.
- **No mandatory regulator notification** required.
- However, since $Z$ is approaching 1.96, the manufacturer should **proactively increase vigilance** and review the next quarter's data.
- If the next quarter also shows $Z > 1.96$, escalate to a formal **CAPA investigation** and **trend report**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in This Problem:**
> 1. Forgetting to express percentages as **decimals** in the Z-score formula — students write $Z = (1.6 - 1.2)/0.3 = 1.33$ (accidentally correct by coincidence, but logically wrong because $\sigma$ should be in the same unit as $X$).
> 2. Confusing the 95% confidence threshold (1.96) with the 99% threshold (2.58) — the correct decision rule here uses **1.96**.
> 3. Recommending a **recall** when the signal is not yet statistically significant — this is a regulatory overreaction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop PMS information flow** from field data acquisition to design update. Explain how the **PDCA cycle** integrates with this flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of Risk Management File (RMF) updates** in Post-Market Surveillance as per **ISO 14971:2019**. How does PMS data feed back into the RMF, and what are the consequences of failing to update it? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Closed-Loop PMS Flow with PDCA (7 marks)

The PMS information flow operates as a **closed-loop control system** with the following eight stages (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Field Data Acquisition** — Inputs are collected from hospitals, patients, distributors, service engineers, and regulatory databases.
2. **Data Validation and Triage** — Each report is classified by severity (death, serious injury, malfunction, near-miss) using MedDRA coding.
3. **Signal Detection and Trend Analysis** — Statistical methods (Z-score, control charts, Pareto analysis) identify clusters of abnormal events.
4. **Root Cause Investigation** — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), Ishikawa diagrams, and fault tree analysis are applied.
5. **CAPA Implementation** — Corrective and Preventive Actions are launched through the QMS, including design changes, process updates, and labelling revisions.
6. **Design Update and Field Deployment** — The corrected device version, software patch, or updated IFU is rolled out to the field.
7. **Effectiveness Check** — Post-deployment monitoring confirms that the corrective action reduced the recurrence rate.
8. **PSUR Compilation and Regulatory Reporting** — The annual PSUR aggregates all PMS data and is submitted to the regulator.

**PDCA Integration:**

- **Plan:** The PMS Plan (PMSP) is created during device development, defining data sources, methods, indicators, and timelines.
- **Do:** Field data is actively collected during the post-market phase.
- **Check:** Statistical analysis and trend detection verify whether the device remains within its risk acceptability threshold.
- **Act:** When the check reveals an unacceptable drift, CAPA is triggered, and the loop restarts with the corrected design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to describe PMS as a **linear process** rather than a **closed loop**. The defining feature of PMS is **continuous feedback** — make this explicit in the diagram and the explanation to score full marks.

#### Part (b) — RMF Updates in PMS (7 marks)

**Role of the Risk Management File (RMF) as per ISO 14971:2019:**

The RMF is a **living document** that records the entire risk management journey of a medical device, including:

- Risk analysis (Annex C of ISO 14971)
- Risk evaluation
- Risk control measures
- Residual risk evaluation
- Risk management review

**How PMS Data Feeds Back into the RMF:**

PMS generates **four types of evidence** that necessitate RMF updates:

1. **Newly discovered hazards** (not identified pre-market) → new hazard entries in the RMF.
2. **Change in severity or probability of known hazards** → update of risk estimation tables and severity/probability matrices.
3. **Failure of existing risk control measures** → revision of risk control effectiveness ratings.
4. **New populations or use environments** → expansion of intended use and user profile sections.

**Consequences of Failing to Update the RMF:**

- **Regulatory non-compliance** — Notified Bodies and FDA auditors specifically check RMF-PMS alignment during surveillance audits.
- **Inaccurate benefit-risk determination** — outdated risk profile may underestimate residual risk, leading to unsafe devices in the field.
- **Inadequate CAPA** — without RMF update, corrective actions may address symptoms rather than root causes.
- **Loss of certification** — for EU MDR Class III devices, failure to update the RMF can result in suspension of CE marking.
- **Increased liability exposure** — outdated RMF weakens the manufacturer's legal defence in product liability litigation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often describe the RMF as a **static document** created during design. The correct framing is that the RMF is a **dynamic, continuously updated file** that evolves throughout the device's lifecycle — a critical distinction worth 2–3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Post Market Surveillance**

- **PMS is mandatory under EU MDR 2017/745** (Article 2(60) and Chapter VII), **21 CFR 803** (US), and **CDSCO MDR 2017** (India).
- **Six pillars:** Complaint handling, AE reporting, trend analysis, FSCA, PMCF, PSUR.
- **PSUR is required for Class IIa, IIb, III** — annual for IIb/III, biennial for IIa; Class I has no PSUR.
- **PMCF is mandatory for Class III and implantable devices** (EU MDR Annex XIV Part B).
- **Adverse Event reporting clock: 15 days** (calendar days in EU/India, working days in US).
- **MedDRA hierarchy:** SOC → HLGT → HLT → PT → LLT (broadest to most specific).
- **Z-score decision rule:** $Z \geq 1.96$ → increased monitoring; $Z \geq 2.58$ → trend report + CAPA; $Z \geq 3.0$ → FSCA.
- **RPN formula for FSCA decision** combines severity, probability, detectability, population, and workaround.
- **PMS Plan (PMSP)** is the input document; **PSUR** and **PMSR** are output documents.
- **RMF is a living document** updated continuously with PMS data per **ISO 14971:2019**.
- **PDCA cycle** (Deming Cycle) is the operational framework for PMS.
- **CAPA** (Clause 8.5 of ISO 13485) is the action engine triggered by PMS signals.
- **MvPI (Materiovigilance Programme of India)** is the Indian national PMS reporting system operated under CDSCO.
- **EUDAMED** is the EU public database for device registration, vigilance, and SSCP publication.
- **SSCP (Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance)** is mandatory for Class III and implantable devices, derived from PSUR + PMCER data.
- The **benefit-risk ratio** must be **re-affirmed annually** for Class IIb/III devices.
- For **PMCF sample size**, the formula $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{d^{2}}$ is used with a 20% attrition adjustment for implantable devices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Supply Chain and Process Optimization in Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Supply Chain and Process Optimization in Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Manufacturing Supply Chain (MSC) — Formal Definition

A **Manufacturing Supply Chain (MSC)** in the context of biomedical devices is the fully integrated, end-to-end network of organizations, resources, activities, information, and regulatory processes involved in the transformation of raw biomaterials and electronic components into finished, sterilized, packaged, distributed, and serviced medical devices delivered to the end patient or healthcare provider.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with ISO 13485:2016 & ISO 9001:2015):**
> A manufacturing supply chain in biomedical engineering is a **value-adding chain** governed by Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP), risk management (ISO 14971), and design controls (FDA 21 CFR Part 820 / EU MDR 2017/745) that ensures traceability, sterility, biocompatibility, and post-market surveillance of every device from supplier to patient.

Mathematically, the supply chain is represented as a directed flow network:

$$\text{MSC} = \{V, E, F, R, C, T\}$$

Where:
- $V$ = set of stakeholders (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, hospitals, patients)
- $E$ = set of material/information flows
- $F$ = transformation functions (machining, assembly, sterilization, packaging)
- $R$ = regulatory and quality checkpoints
- $C$ = cost and cash flow vectors
- $T$ = time and lead-time constraints

## 1.2 Process Optimization in Manufacturing — Formal Definition

**Process Optimization** is the systematic, data-driven methodology of identifying, modeling, analyzing, and improving the underlying manufacturing processes to achieve the **optimal** combination of quality, cost, throughput, and compliance — subject to engineering, biological, and regulatory constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Optimization seeks to **maximize** a Performance Index (PI) such as yield, throughput, or process capability ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) while **minimizing** Cost-of-Good-Sold (COGS), cycle time ($\sigma_{cycle}$), defects per million opportunities (DPMO), and patient risk.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🏥 Real-World Analogy: The Hospital Blood Transfusion Chain

Imagine the journey of a single unit of blood from a donor to a patient:

1. **Donor (Raw Material Supplier)** — provides raw biological input.
2. **Blood Bank (Manufacturer)** — tests, processes (centrifugation), labels, and stores.
3. **Cold-Chain Logistics (Distribution)** — refrigerated transport at 2–8°C.
4. **Hospital Blood Bank (Distributor)** — cross-matching and storage.
5. **Patient Bedside (End User)** — final transfusion.

> [!TIP]
> **If any link fails (broken cold chain, mislabeling, contamination), the entire chain collapses and patient safety is compromised.** This is *exactly* how a biomedical device supply chain works — except the "blood" is replaced by an **implant, syringe, pacemaker, or MRI coil**, and the "storage temperature" is replaced by **sterility, shelf-life, and traceability**.

### ⚙️ Analogy for Process Optimization: The Car Assembly Line

Consider a Toyota assembly line. Engineers don't just "build cars" — they:
- Time every bolt tightening (cycle time analysis)
- Eliminate any step that doesn't add value (Lean — *Muda*)
- Reduce variation in torque application (Six Sigma)
- Continuously improve (Kaizen)

> The same philosophy applies to manufacturing a **catheter, dialyzer, or orthopedic screw** — only now the "bolt" is a **biocompatible polymer extrusion**, and the "torque wrench" is a **validated CNC process with FDA process validation**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical Device Design

| Reason | Description |
|---|---|
| **Patient Safety** | A defect at any supply chain node can cause patient harm or death. |
| **Regulatory Mandate** | ISO 13485, FDA QSR, EU MDR all require documented process control. |
| **Cost Pressure** | Healthcare systems demand affordable devices → process efficiency is non-negotiable. |
| **Traceability** | Lot/batch traceability is a legal requirement (UDI — Unique Device Identification). |
| **Sterility Assurance** | Sterile devices (Class II/III) require validated sterilization supply chains. |
| **Time-to-Market** | Optimized processes accelerate regulatory approval (510(k), CE Marking). |

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants & Standards)

- **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) — Six Sigma benchmark: **3.4 DPMO at 6σ**
- **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** — minimum acceptable: **≥ 1.33** for medical devices
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — target: **≥ 95%** for Class II, **≥ 99%** for Class III
- **Lead Time ($L_T$)** — total time from raw material receipt to device delivery
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** — target: **8–12 turns/year** for medical devices
- **EOQ** (Economic Order Quantity) — Wilson's formula (derived in Section 3)
- **Service Level (β)** — target: **≥ 95%** for critical implant supply chains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** A biomedical engineer must design the *device*, the *process* to make it, and the *supply chain* to deliver it. These three are inseparable under modern FDA/Harmonized Quality System Regulations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Tiers of a Biomedical Device Supply Chain

A complete biomedical device supply chain is structured into **six functional tiers**:

### Tier 1 — Raw Material Suppliers
- **Biomaterials**: titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V), 316L stainless steel, PEEK, UHMWPE, silicone, PTFE.
- **Electronic components**: MCUs, sensors, battery cells (e.g., Li-ion for pacemakers), PCBs.
- **Pharmaceutical inputs**: APIs for drug-eluting stents, heparin coatings.
- **Quality requirement**: ISO 13485 certification, material certificates (CoC, CoA), biocompatibility data (ISO 10993).

### Tier 2 — Sub-Component Manufacturers
- Produces subassemblies: cables, optical fibers, MEMS sensors, molding inserts.
- Performs **CNC machining, injection molding, laser welding, extrusion**.

### Tier 3 — Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) / Device Assembler
- **Sterile packaging** (ISO 11607).
- **Final assembly** in Class 7 / Class 8 cleanrooms (ISO 14644).
- **Labeling** with UDI (Unique Device Identification) per FDA 21 CFR 830 / EU MDR.

### Tier 4 — Sterilization & Logistics
- Sterilization methods: **EtO (Ethylene Oxide), Gamma radiation, E-beam, Autoclave, H₂O₂ plasma**.
- Cold-chain logistics (2–8°C) for biologics-sensitive devices.
- Validation per **ISO 11135 (EtO), ISO 11137 (Gamma), ISO 17665 (Steam)**.

### Tier 5 — Distribution / Warehousing
- Warehouses with **GDP (Good Distribution Practice)** compliance.
- Track-and-trace systems (GS1 standards, RFID/barcode).

### Tier 6 — End User (Hospital, Clinic, Patient)
- Final use, post-market surveillance, adverse event reporting.
- **MDR (Medical Device Reporting)** and **Vigilance Reporting**.

## 2.2 Process Optimization Methodologies (Core Theoretical Framework)

### 2.2.1 Lean Manufacturing (Toyota Production System — TPS)

**Principle:** Maximize **customer value** by eliminating **Muda (waste)**.

The **7 Wastes (Muda)** in biomedical manufacturing:

1. **Transportation** — unnecessary movement of parts.
2. **Inventory** — excess WIP (Work-In-Progress).
3. **Motion** — operator inefficiency.
4. **Waiting** — idle machines/operators.
5. **Overproduction** — making more than needed.
6. **Over-processing** — adding features customer doesn't need.
7. **Defects** — rework, scrap, recalls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Medical device addition — the 8th Waste:** *Skills underutilization* — biomedical engineers and quality staff often perform non-engineering clerical tasks.

**Key Lean Tools:**
- **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** — visualizes material and information flow.
- **5S Methodology**: Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- **Kanban** — pull-based production scheduling.
- **Poka-Yoke** (Mistake-Proofing) — critical for device safety (e.g., unique connectors for ECG leads to prevent misconnection).
- **Kaizen** — continuous incremental improvement.

### 2.2.2 Six Sigma (DMAIC)

**Principle:** Reduce process variation to **≤ 3.4 DPMO** (defects per million opportunities).

The **DMAIC Cycle**:

| Phase | Activity | Biomedical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **D**efine | Identify CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) parameters | "Catheter must withstand 50 N tensile load" |
| **M**easure | Collect baseline data, compute $\sigma$ | Tensile testing of 30 catheter samples |
| **A**nalyze | Identify root causes using statistical tools | Cause-and-effect (Ishikawa), Pareto |
| **I**mprove | Implement and validate solutions | New molding parameters, validated |
| **C**ontrol | Sustain gains via SPC (Statistical Process Control) | Control charts (X-bar, R-chart) |

**Process Capability Indices:**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

Where USL/LSL = Upper/Lower Specification Limits, $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is acceptable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is preferred for **Class III implants** (e.g., heart valves, stents).

### 2.2.3 Theory of Constraints (TOC)

**Principle:** Every system has **at least one bottleneck** (constraint). Optimize the constraint to optimize the entire system.

**5 Focusing Steps:**
1. **Identify** the constraint.
2. **Exploit** the constraint (maximize its utilization).
3. **Subordinate** all other processes to the constraint.
4. **Elevate** the constraint (add capacity).
5. **Repeat** — find the next constraint.

### 2.2.4 Total Quality Management (TQM)

**Principle:** Organization-wide commitment to customer satisfaction through continuous quality improvement.

**Core pillars:** Customer focus, process approach, fact-based decision making, employee involvement, supplier partnership.

### 2.2.5 Just-In-Time (JIT)

**Principle:** Produce/deliver each component *exactly when needed*, minimizing inventory.

**Risk in biomedical context:** JIT is fragile to supply chain shocks (e.g., COVID-19, EtO sterilization capacity shortages). Hence, **dual sourcing** and **safety stock** are mandatory for critical devices.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). The vertical pipe symbols have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve table formatting.

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Process capability — potential |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Process capability — actual (centered) |
| 3 | $\sigma\text{-level} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}\ \text{or}\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Six Sigma quality level |
| 4 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$ | Percentage | Process yield |
| 5 | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Units/year | Economic Order Quantity (Wilson, 1934) |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q} = \dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}}$ | Orders/year | Optimal number of orders |
| 7 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{Q}{D} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Years | Time between orders |
| 8 | $\text{ROP} = d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_L$ | Units | Reorder Point |
| 9 | $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_L$ | Units | Safety Stock |
| 10 | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$ | Turns/year | Inventory Turnover Ratio |
| 11 | $\text{Cycle Time} = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Output}}$ | Minutes/unit | Lean bottleneck metric |
| 12 | $\text{Takt Time} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Minutes/unit | Lean production rate |
| 13 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | 0 to 1 (or %) | Overall Equipment Effectiveness |
| 14 | $\text{LT} = \text{PT} + \text{WT} + \text{ST} + \text{RT}$ | Days | Total Lead Time (Process + Wait + Storage + Reactive) |
| 15 | $\text{FPY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Percentage | Rolled First Pass Yield |

**Variable Legend:**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $d$ = Average daily demand
- $L$ = Lead time (days)
- $z$ = Z-score for service level (e.g., $z=1.65$ for 95%)
- $\sigma_L$ = Std. deviation of demand during lead time
- $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (for OEE)
- $Y_i$ = Yield of process $i$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **$C_{pk}$ in Stent Manufacturing:** Medtronic uses $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for laser-cut coronary stents to ensure radial strength uniformity.
- **EOQ in Hospital Procurement:** Apollo Hospitals uses EOQ to balance inventory of consumables (syringes, gloves) vs. holding cost.
- **Kanban in Catheter Production:** Boston Scientific uses 2-bin Kanban systems for raw polymer tubing.
- **EtO Sterilization Bottleneck:** In 2019, a major EtO facility closure (Sterigenics) caused a national device shortage — illustrating the **Theory of Constraints** in real time.
- **OEE in Cleanroom Injection Molding:** Target OEE for Class II device molding is **≥ 85%**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** When a question asks for "process optimization strategy," the examiner expects you to **name the method (Lean/Six Sigma/TOC) AND justify it with a biomedical-specific example**. A generic Lean answer without a device-specific illustration loses 2–3 marks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Wilson's Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Formula

**Problem Statement:** A biomedical device company orders **D units per year** of a sterile component. Each order incurs an **ordering cost S (₹/order)**, and holding one unit in inventory for a year costs **H (₹/unit/year)**. Find the **optimal order quantity Q\*** that minimizes total annual cost.

### Step 1: Define Total Annual Cost (TAC)

The Total Annual Cost has two components:

$$\text{TAC}(Q) = \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost}$$

### Step 2: Express Ordering Cost

The number of orders placed per year is $D / Q$. Each order costs $S$. Therefore:

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

### Step 3: Express Holding Cost

If orders are placed regularly and consumption is uniform, the **average inventory** is $Q / 2$ (starting at $Q$, ending at $0$). Therefore:

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

### Step 4: Combine into Total Cost Function

$$\text{TAC}(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$$

### Step 5: Minimize TAC by Differentiation

Take the derivative of TAC with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 6: Solve for Q*

$$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \text{EOQ}$$

### Step 7: Second-Order Verification (Minimum)

The second derivative is:

$$\frac{d^2(\text{TAC})}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \forall\ Q > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum**.

### Step 8: Number of Orders and Cycle Time

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{D}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}} = \sqrt{\frac{DH}{2S}}$$

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{N} = \sqrt{\frac{2S}{DH}}$$

### Step 9: Minimum Total Cost

Substituting $Q^*$ back into TAC:

$$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}} + \frac{H \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}}{2}$$

$$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — EOQ for Sterile Syringe Procurement

**Problem:** A hospital network procures **D = 50,000** sterile syringes per year. The ordering cost is **S = ₹500 per order**, and the holding cost is **H = ₹20 per unit per year**. Find EOQ, number of orders, cycle time, and minimum total cost.

**Given:**
- $D = 50{,}000$ units/year
- $S = ₹500$/order
- $H = ₹20$/unit/year

**Step 1: Compute EOQ**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 500}{20}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{50{,}000{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{2{,}500{,}000}$$

$$Q^* = 1581.14 \approx 1581\ \text{units}$$

**Step 2: Number of Orders per Year**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{50{,}000}{1581} \approx 31.62\ \text{orders/year}$$

**Step 3: Cycle Time Between Orders**

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{31.62} \approx 0.0316\ \text{years}$$

$$T_{\text{cycle}} \approx 11.55\ \text{days}$$

**Step 4: Minimum Total Cost**

$$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 500 \times 20}$$

$$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000{,}000} = ₹31{,}623$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The hospital should order **~1581 syringes per order**, place **~32 orders per year** (roughly one every **11.5 days**), and the minimum annual cost will be **₹31,623**. Any deviation from Q* increases cost.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Reorder Point (ROP) with Safety Stock

**Problem:** A dialysis center uses dialyzers at an **average rate of d = 30 units/day**, with demand during lead time having **standard deviation $\sigma_L = 8$ units**. The lead time is **L = 5 days**. For a **95% service level**, find the **Reorder Point (ROP)**.

**Step 1: Service Level to Z-score**

For $\beta = 95\%$, from standard normal tables:

$$z = 1.645$$

**Step 2: Compute Average Demand During Lead Time**

$$\bar{D}_L = d \cdot L = 30 \times 5 = 150\ \text{units}$$

**Step 3: Compute Safety Stock (SS)**

$$\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_L = 1.645 \times 8 = 13.16 \approx 14\ \text{units}$$

**Step 4: Compute ROP**

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{D}_L + \text{SS} = 150 + 14 = 164\ \text{units}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** When inventory drops to **164 units**, place a new order. This ensures that during 95% of lead-time cycles, the stock will not run out — protecting patient safety in a life-critical therapy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Optimization Methods

| Parameter | **Lean** | **Six Sigma** | **TOC** | **TQM** | **JIT** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Waste elimination | Variation reduction | Bottleneck management | Culture-wide quality | Inventory minimization |
| **Origin** | Toyota (1950s) | Motorola (1986) | Goldratt (1984) | Deming / Juran (1950s) | Toyota (1970s) |
| **Best For** | High-volume consumables | Precision implants | Sterilization bottleneck | Whole organization | Stable demand devices |
| **Key Tool** | VSM, 5S, Kanban | DMAIC, SPC, DOE | 5 Focusing Steps | PDCA, QCC | Pull system, Kanban |
| **Risk in Biomedical** | Under-sterilization, errors | Over-rigidity, cost | Single-point-of-failure | Slow implementation | Supply shock fragility |
| **Yield Target** | >95% FPY | ≤3.4 DPMO | Optimize constraint | Continuous improvement | Zero inventory |
| **Regulatory Fit** | Good (QSR 820.70) | Excellent (QSR 820.250 SPC) | Moderate | Excellent (QSR 820.100 CAPA) | Moderate |
| **Example Device** | Syringe assembly | Stent laser cutting | EtO sterilization line | Hospital-wide system | Catheter extrusion |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Process Capability ($C_{pk}$) for Catheter Diameter

**Problem:** A manufacturer produces urinary catheters with a **target diameter of 3.0 mm** and specification limits **LSL = 2.9 mm, USL = 3.1 mm**. After sampling **n = 25** catheters, the mean is $\bar{x} = 3.02$ mm and standard deviation is $s = 0.018$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

**Given:**
- USL = 3.1 mm, LSL = 2.9 mm
- $\mu = 3.02$ mm, $\sigma = 0.018$ mm
- Tolerance width = 0.2 mm

**Step 1: Compute $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{3.1 - 2.9}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.2}{0.108}$$

$$C_p = 1.852$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{3.1 - 3.02}{3 \times 0.018},\ \frac{3.02 - 2.9}{3 \times 0.018}\right)$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{0.08}{0.054},\ \frac{0.12}{0.054}\right)$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.481,\ 2.222) = 1.481$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

- $C_p = 1.852$ → The tolerance is **1.85 times** the process spread. The process *can* meet specs, but…
- $C_{pk} = 1.481$ → The process mean is **off-center** (closer to USL). The actual capability is only 1.481.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Many students compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ but forget to **interpret** them. Always state: (a) Is the process capable? (b) Is it centered? (c) What corrective action is needed (shift the mean toward midpoint)?

> **Conclusion:** $C_{pk} = 1.481 > 1.33$ → process is **acceptable** for Class II devices, but the off-center mean (μ = 3.02 vs. target 3.00) must be corrected by adjusting the extrusion line.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
