## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Manufacturing Supply Chain in the context of biomedical devices. List any FOUR regulatory standards governing it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Manufacturing Supply Chain (MSC)** in the biomedical device industry is the integrated network of organizations, resources, activities, and information systems that transform raw biomaterials and electronic components into finished, sterilized, packaged, and distributed medical devices delivered to the end patient, while ensuring safety, efficacy, traceability, and regulatory compliance.

**Four Regulatory Standards:**

1. **ISO 13485:2016** — Quality Management Systems for Medical Devices.
2. **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** — US Quality System Regulation (QSR) for Finished Devices.
3. **EU MDR 2017/745** — European Medical Device Regulation.
4. **ISO 14971:2019** — Application of Risk Management to Medical Devices.
5. *(Bonus)* **ISO 14644** — Cleanroom classification for medical device manufacturing.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the "7 Wastes (Muda)" of Lean Manufacturing with ONE biomedical-specific example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Waste | Definition | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transportation** | Unnecessary movement of materials | Moving packaged syringes across the plant before final EtO sterilization |
| 2 | **Inventory** | Excess WIP or finished goods | Storing 1 year's stock of orthopedic screws (risk of oxidation) |
| 3 | **Motion** | Inefficient operator movement | Technician walking 20 m to fetch each component (poor 5S) |
| 4 | **Waiting** | Idle time | Catheter extrusion halted due to QC hold on incoming polymer |
| 5 | **Overproduction** | Producing more than needed | Manufacturing 10,000 ECG electrodes when 5,000 are required |
| 6 | **Over-processing** | Adding unnecessary features | Mirror-polishing an internal (non-implanted) surface of a device |
| 7 | **Defects** | Rework, scrap, recalls | Misaligned laser cuts on stent struts (recall risk) |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **six tiers of a biomedical device manufacturing supply chain**. Explain the role of **UDI labeling** and **sterilization** in the chain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cardiac stent manufacturer places orders for **D = 80,000 units/year** of a cobalt-chromium alloy tube. The ordering cost is **S = ₹2,000/order** and the holding cost is **H = ₹50/unit/year**.

Compute:
1. **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**
2. **Number of orders per year**
3. **Cycle time between orders (in days, assuming 300 working days/year)**
4. **Minimum total annual cost**

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks — Six Tiers of Biomedical Device Supply Chain**

The biomedical device supply chain is organized into **six functional tiers**, each with a defined role and regulatory responsibility.

**Diagram (text-based representation):**

```
TIER 1: Raw Material Suppliers (Biomaterials, Electronics, APIs)
   ↓ (CoC, CoA, Biocompatibility data — ISO 10993)
TIER 2: Sub-Component Manufacturers (CNC, Molding, Extrusion)
   ↓ (ISO 13485 supplier qualification)
TIER 3: OEM Final Assembly (Cleanroom ISO Class 7/8 — ISO 14644)
   ↓ (UDI Labeling per FDA 21 CFR 830 / EU MDR Annex VI)
TIER 4: Sterilization & Logistics (EtO/Gamma/E-beam — ISO 11135/11137)
   ↓ (Validated sterilization, SAL 10⁻⁶)
TIER 5: Distribution / Warehousing (GDP, GS1, Track-and-Trace)
   ↓ (Cold-chain, RFID, Barcode)
TIER 6: End User (Hospital, Clinic, Patient)
   ↓ (Post-market surveillance, MDR/Vigilance)
   ↺ (Feedback to Tier 1 for continuous improvement)
```

**Role of UDI Labeling (Tier 3 → Tier 4):**
- The **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** is a globally unique numeric/alphanumeric code that identifies the device, its manufacturer, lot/batch, serial number, and expiration date.
- **Purpose:** Enables traceability from manufacturer → distributor → hospital → patient, allowing rapid recall of defective devices.
- **Regulations:** FDA 21 CFR 830 (USA), EU MDR Article 27 (Europe).
- **Example:** A pacemaker's UDI captures manufacturer ID, device model, lot number, and serial number — required on both the device label and the patient implant card.

**Role of Sterilization (Tier 4):**
- Sterilization is the **critical process** that achieves a **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) of 10⁻⁶** — meaning probability of a viable microorganism being present is **1 in a million**.
- **Methods:**
  - **Ethylene Oxide (EtO):** Low-temperature, suitable for polymers and electronics. Standard: **ISO 11135**.
  - **Gamma Radiation:** High penetration, used for syringes, gloves. Standard: **ISO 11137**.
  - **Steam (Autoclave):** For heat-stable metal instruments. Standard: **ISO 17665**.
  - **H₂O₂ Plasma:** For heat/moisture-sensitive devices.
- **Validation:** Requires **IQ (Installation Qualification), OQ (Operational Qualification), and PQ (Performance Qualification)** — a 3-batch validation per FDA guidance.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks — EOQ Numerical**

**Given:**
- $D = 80{,}000$ units/year
- $S = ₹2{,}000$/order
- $H = ₹50$/unit/year
- Working days = 300/year

**Step 1: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 80{,}000 \times 2{,}000}{50}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{320{,}000{,}000}{50}} = \sqrt{6{,}400{,}000}$$

$$Q^* = 2{,}529.8 \approx 2{,}530\ \text{units per order}$$

**Step 2: Number of Orders per Year**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{80{,}000}{2{,}530} \approx 31.62 \approx 32\ \text{orders/year}$$

**Step 3: Cycle Time Between Orders**

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{300\ \text{days}}{31.62} \approx 9.49\ \text{days/order}$$

**Step 4: Minimum Total Annual Cost**

$$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 80{,}000 \times 2{,}000 \times 50}$$

$$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{16{,}000{,}000{,}000} = ₹126{,}491$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The manufacturer should order **~2,530 tubes per order**, place **~32 orders per year** (one every **9.5 days**), and the minimum annual inventory cost is **₹1,26,491**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **DMAIC methodology of Six Sigma** in detail. State the **process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** with formulas and justify why $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is required for Class II medical devices.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dialysis membrane production line operates with specification limits **USL = 50 μm, LSL = 40 μm**. From a sample of **n = 30 membranes**, the mean pore diameter is **$\bar{x} = 46$ μm** and the standard deviation is **$s = 1.5$ μm**.

Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and comment on whether the process is **capable** and **centered** for a **Class II device**.

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks — DMAIC Methodology**

**Six Sigma** is a data-driven methodology aimed at reducing process variation to **≤ 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It uses the **DMAIC** (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control) cycle.

**1. DEFINE Phase:**
- Identify the **problem statement**, **project scope**, **customer (CTQ — Critical-to-Quality)**, and **VOC (Voice of Customer)**.
- Biomedical example: "Reduce variation in stent strut thickness to improve radial strength."

**2. MEASURE Phase:**
- Collect baseline data on CTQ parameters.
- Establish the **measurement system analysis (MSA)** — Gage R&R.
- Biomedical example: Measure strut thickness of 30 stents using calibrated optical micrometer.

**3. ANALYZE Phase:**
- Identify **root causes** of variation using **Ishikawa (fishbone) diagrams, Pareto charts, regression analysis, ANOVA**.
- Biomedical example: Pareto analysis reveals that 80% of strut variation comes from laser cutting parameters (peak power, pulse duration).

**4. IMPROVE Phase:**
- Design and run **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to find optimal parameter settings.
- Implement **pilot run**, validate against $C_{pk}$ target.
- Biomedical example: Set laser power to 80 W, pulse duration to 50 μs — pilot run yields $C_{pk} = 1.72$.

**5. CONTROL Phase:**
- Sustain improvements via **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**: **X-bar and R-charts**, **p-charts**, **c-charts**.
- Document the **control plan**, train operators, and create **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
- Biomedical example: X-bar/R-charts posted at the laser cutting workstation with documented reaction rules.

**Process Capability Indices:**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \quad \text{(Potential Capability — assumes centering)}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) \quad \text{(Actual Capability — accounts for off-centering)}$$

**Why $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for Class II devices:**
- **$C_{pk} < 1.00$**: Process is **incapable** — significant defect rate, not acceptable.
- **$1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$**: Process is **marginally capable** — acceptable only for non-critical Class I devices.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$**: Process is **capable** — defect rate ~63 ppm, acceptable for **Class II devices** (e.g., infusion pumps, dialysis machines, surgical drapes).
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$**: **Excellent** — preferred for **Class III implants** (pacemakers, heart valves, stents).

> The threshold $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ ensures a **3σ margin** from each specification limit, providing protection against minor process drifts and ensuring patient safety — a key requirement of **ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.4 (Monitoring and measurement of processes)** and **FDA QSR 21 CFR 820.250 (Statistical Techniques)**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks — $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ Numerical**

**Given:**
- USL = 50 μm, LSL = 40 μm
- $\mu = 46$ μm, $\sigma = 1.5$ μm

**Step 1: Compute $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{50 - 40}{6 \times 1.5} = \frac{10}{9} = 1.111$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_{pk}$ (Two Sub-Values)**

**Upper-side capability:**

$$C_{pk,\ upper} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{50 - 46}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{4}{4.5} = 0.889$$

**Lower-side capability:**

$$C_{pk,\ lower} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{46 - 40}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{6}{4.5} = 1.333$$

**Overall $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.889,\ 1.333) = 0.889$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

- **$C_p = 1.111 < 1.33$** → The process spread is **wider** than the specification window allows (i.e., $6\sigma = 9$ μm is close to the tolerance of 10 μm). The process is **not inherently capable** for Class II.
- **$C_{pk} = 0.889 < 1.33$** → The process is **not capable** for Class II. There is a high risk of producing membranes with pore size **above 50 μm** (poor selectivity).
- **Centering issue:** $\mu = 46$ μm vs. midpoint = $(40+50)/2 = 45$ μm. The mean is **1 μm above** the target, which causes the upper-side $C_{pk}$ to be the binding constraint.

**Step 4: Corrective Action**

1. **Shift the mean** downward by 1 μm (re-center the process to 45 μm).
2. **Reduce process variation** by tightening laser/drilling parameters, controlling humidity, and improving operator training.
3. **Target:** Achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ — this requires reducing $\sigma$ to **$\leq 1.25$ μm** AND centering at 45 μm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$.** $C_p$ ignores the process mean. $C_{pk}$ is what the regulator cares about.
> 2. **Always write units.** $C_{pk}$ is dimensionless, but the underlying measurements (μm, mm, N) must have units.
> 3. **Always interpret the answer.** A student who writes "$C_{pk} = 0.889$" without commenting on "process is incapable" loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Do not forget the decision rule** — $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for Class II, $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for Class III.
> 5. **Two-sided vs. one-sided specs** — for one-sided specs, use the appropriate half-formula. KTU 2024 most often uses two-sided.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Manufacturing Supply Chain (MSC)** = end-to-end network from raw biomaterials to patient, governed by **ISO 13485** and **FDA 21 CFR 820**.
- **Six Tiers:** Raw Material → Sub-Component → OEM Assembly → Sterilization → Distribution → End User.
- **Process Optimization** = maximizing quality/throughput while minimizing cost, defects, and risk.
- **Lean Manufacturing** = eliminate 7 Wastes (Muda) + 1 (skills underutilization). Tools: VSM, 5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen.
- **Six Sigma (DMAIC)** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. Target: **3.4 DPMO**.
- **TOC (Theory of Constraints)** = 5 Focusing Steps: Identify → Exploit → Subordinate → Elevate → Repeat.
- **TQM** = organization-wide quality culture; PDCA cycle.
- **JIT** = produce exactly when needed; risky for medical devices (need safety stock).
- **$C_p$** = $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ — potential capability.
- **$C_{pk}$** = $\min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ — actual capability.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** for Class II; **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** for Class III implants.
- **EOQ** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ (Wilson's formula, 1934).
- **ROP** = $d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_L$ (with safety stock).
- **TAC$_{\min}$** = $\sqrt{2DSH}$.
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** = $\dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$; target 8–12/year.
- **OEE** = $A \times P \times Q$; target **≥ 85%** for medical device lines.
- **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)** = $10^{-6}$ for terminally sterilized devices.
- **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** = mandatory per FDA 21 CFR 830 and EU MDR Art. 27.
- **Cleanroom classes** = ISO 14644 (Class 7 / Class 8 for typical device assembly).
- **Key Sterilization Standards** = ISO 11135 (EtO), ISO 11137 (Gamma), ISO 17665 (Steam).
- **Risk Management** = ISO 14971 — mandatory throughout the supply chain.
- **EtO Sterilization** is often the **bottleneck** in real supply chains (TOC application).
- **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** is a Lean tool with high biomedical value (prevent connector mis-mating).
- **For KTU 2024 numerical problems** — practice EOQ, $C_{pk}$, ROP, and OEE; examiners expect 3–4 such problems per ESE.
- **Biocompatibility** (ISO 10993) and **Biocompatibility Testing** must be referenced in any supply chain discussion.
- **Always link optimization method to device class** — Lean for disposables, Six Sigma for implants, TOC for sterilization, TQM organization-wide.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When answering a "process optimization" question, structure your answer as: **(1) Method name, (2) Definition, (3) Key tool/framework, (4) Biomedical-specific example, (5) Regulatory link (ISO 13485 / 21 CFR 820).** This 5-part structure consistently scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rapid Prototyping and 3D Printing (Case Study: Manufacturing Supply Chain)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rapid Prototyping and 3D Printing (Case Study: Manufacturing Supply Chain)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rapid Prototyping (RP) — Formal Definition

**Rapid Prototyping (RP)** is a class of *Additive Manufacturing (AM)* technologies used to fabricate a scaled, physical, three-dimensional model of a designed part directly from **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** data, typically by adding material layer-by-layer rather than subtracting it through machining. In the KTU 2024 scheme (PEBRT415 — Design of Biomedical Devices), RP is positioned as the bridge between *digital patient/tissue data* (MRI, CT, optical scan) and a *tangible anatomical model, surgical jig, or implant prototype*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is the umbrella term adopted by **ISO/ASTM 52900** for processes that join materials to make objects from 3D model data, *successively*, layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive and formative manufacturing methodologies. **RP** is the historical term for the *prototyping* application of AM; today the same machines are routinely used for *end-use production* of biomedical devices (e.g., titanium cranial plates, patient-specific acetabular cups, hearing-aid shells).

## 1.2 3D Printing — Formal Definition

**3D Printing** is the colloquial and widely-adopted term synonymous with Additive Manufacturing, especially for *low-cost, polymer-based* systems. Technically, the ISO/ASTM 52900 taxonomy lists **seven AM process categories**:

1. VAT Photopolymerisation (SLA, DLP)
2. Material Extrusion (FDM, FFF)
3. Powder Bed Fusion (SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM)
4. Material Jetting (PolyJet, MultiJet, Binder Jetting in part)
5. Binder Jetting
6. Sheet Lamination (LOM, UAM)
7. Directed Energy Deposition (DED)

For biomedical applications, the **first four** dominate clinical translation.

> [!NOTE]
> **STL File** (Standard Tessellation Language / Stereolithography format) is the *de facto* standard interface between CAD and AM machines. It approximates surfaces with a triangular mesh. Its successor **3MF (3D Manufacturing Format)** preserves colour, material, and lattice metadata and is increasingly preferred for medical workflows.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cake vs The Sculpture"

Imagine a marble sculptor carving a statue (subtractive manufacturing — waste a 90% marble block to reveal David) versus a baker piping frosting layer by layer onto a spinning cake to build a rose (additive manufacturing — place material *only where needed*).

- **Sculptor = CNC machining / milling** → wasteful, restricted to certain geometries, requires hardened tooling.
- **Baker = 3D printer** → near-zero waste (savings of 40-70 % raw material), can create *internal lattices* and *patient-specific curvatures* impossible to mill, no hard tooling required.
- **Recipe book = STL/3MF digital file** → identical output in any kitchen (printer) worldwide — this is the foundation of *distributed manufacturing* in the supply chain.

## 1.4 Case Study Framing — Manufacturing Supply Chain

The case study (typical KTU board question context) examines how a **traditional orthopaedic implant supply chain** (raw Ti bar → centralised factory → 6-12 week distribution → hospital) is *disrupted* by introducing 3D printing at the *point-of-care* (CT scan at hospital → digital planning → on-site printer → sterilised patient-specific implant in 24-48 h).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core syllabus mapping (Module 4):** *Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)*, *process selection matrix for biomedical polymers/ceramics/metals*, *supply chain impact — inventory, lead-time, mass customisation, regulatory pathway (FDA 510(k), CE-MDR)*.

| Key Term | Standard Metric (Biomedical) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Layer thickness (Z resolution)** | **25 – 200 µm** (SLA: 25 µm, FDM: 100-200 µm) |
| **Build volume** | typical desktop: 200 × 200 × 250 mm; industrial SLM: 500 × 500 × 500 mm |
| **Material utilisation efficiency** | **≥ 95 %** (vs 5-10 % for CNC of Ti) |
| **Time-to-clinic for patient-specific implant** | **24 – 72 h** (vs 4-12 weeks traditional) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Digital Thread — End-to-End Workflow

Every biomedical RP pipeline follows the same logical sequence, and the KTU board often tests the *flow* as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 — Image acquisition.** Patient undergoes CT (bone, metallic implants), MRI (soft tissue, cartilage), or intra-oral optical scan. Output: **DICOM** (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) volumetric data.

**Step 2 — Segmentation.** Thresholding + region-growing converts DICOM voxels to a 3D surface/triangle mesh in software (Mimics, 3D Slicer, Materialise, InVesalius). Output: editable mesh (often exported as **STL**).

**Step 3 — CAD refinement.** The mesh is smoothed, holes patched, and patient-matched features added (screw holes, articulation surfaces, lattice infill). This is **Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)**.

**Step 4 — Slicing & process planning.** The STL is virtually cut into 2D cross-sections (slice plane normal to build axis Z), support structures added, and machine code (G-code, CLI, or proprietary) generated. *Why slice?* Because AM builds 2-D layers — the slicer translates the 3-D into a stack of 2-D patterns.

**Step 5 — Build (additive fabrication).** Material is deposited/cured/fused per slice. The *Why*: it allows *internal* features (lattice, porosity, drug reservoirs) impossible with moulding.

**Step 6 — Post-processing.** Support removal, surface finishing (bead-blasting, electropolishing for Ti), thermal annealing (stress relief for SLM), sterilisation (autoclave, EtO, gamma).

**Step 7 — Quality assurance & clinical use.** Dimensional metrology (CT of the printed part, CMM), biocompatibility validation (ISO 10993), and delivery to OR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **digital thread** (Steps 1-3) is *file-based, near-instantaneous, and infinitely reproducible*; only Steps 5-6 are *physical* and *time/material consuming*. This asymmetry is the entire economic foundation of the supply-chain disruption narrative.

## 2.2 Process Selection — Engineering Logic

KTU examiners expect you to justify the choice of AM process using a **decision matrix** with axes: *material*, *resolution*, *biocompatibility*, *mechanical strength*, *sterilisation compatibility*, *cost*.

- **FDM** (thermoplastic extrusion): cheap (~$500 desktop, $5k industrial), uses ABS/PLA/PCL/PEEK, **low resolution (100-200 µm)**, ideal for *anatomical models, surgical planning, splints, orthoses*.
- **SLA** (UV-cured resin): high resolution (**25-50 µm**), optically clear resins for surgical guides, but limited biocompatibility (only specific resins certified for skin/mucosal contact, max 30 d).
- **SLS** (laser-sintered powder): nylon-12 (PA-12) — strong, no supports, used for *prosthetic sockets, custom器械*.
- **SLM / DMLS** (laser-melted metal): **Ti-6Al-4V**, CoCr, 316L SS — produces *load-bearing implants* (spinal cages, acetabular cups, cranial plates) with **>95 %** density and fatigue strength comparable to wrought alloy after HIP.
- **PolyJet** (UV-cured droplets): multi-material, **16 µm** resolution, ideal for *realistic organ models with varying stiffness* (tumour + soft tissue + bone in one print).
- **Bioprinting** (extrusion of cell-laden bioink): hydrogel + living cells → *tissue engineering scaffolds*, still largely pre-clinical.

## 2.3 Manufacturing Supply Chain — Traditional vs AM-Augmented

The case study asks: *how does AM transform the supply chain?*

**Traditional (subtractive + formative) supply chain** for, e.g., a knee implant:
- Raw material → centralised forging/machining facility (single geography) → 6-12 week QA → distributor warehouse → hospital consignment stock → surgery.
- **Inventory carrying cost = 25-30 % of product value per year.**
- **Minimum order quantity (MOQ) = hundreds of units** — one-size-fits-most with 5-7 sterile sizes.
- Long **lead time**, **mass production**, **geographic dependency**.

**AM-enabled supply chain**:
- Digital file in cloud → 3D print on demand at *hospital hub* or *regional bureau*.
- **Inventory = digital**, not physical; **MOQ = 1** (mass customisation).
- Lead time drops to **24-72 h** for patient-specific.
- Spare parts on demand for legacy devices (FDA published guidance 2017 — *3D Printing of Medical Devices at the Point of Care*).
- **Distributed manufacturing** — multiple regional printers reduce single-point-of-failure risk (a critical lesson from the 2020 PPE/mask crisis during COVID-19).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below is **mathematically verifiable** and has appeared in KTU biomedical/RP numerical questions. SI units unless stated.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **STL triangle count** | $N_t = \dfrac{2 V_{obj}}{A_{tri} \cdot h_z}$ | Triangles needed for given voxel size $h_z$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Build time (FDM, layer-wise)** | $T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot \left(t_{motion} + t_{deposition}\right)$ | Total print time | seconds |
| 3 | **Number of layers** | $N_{layers} = \left\lceil \dfrac{h_z^{part}}{h_{layer}} \right\rceil$ | Z-height of part divided by layer thickness | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Material volume in part** | $V_{part} = \dfrac{m_{part}}{\rho_{mat}}$ | Convert mass of print to volume | $m^3$ |
| 5 | **Material utilisation efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{build \, envelope}} \times 100\,\%$ | Fraction of material becoming part | % |
| 6 | **Cost per part (simplified)** | $C_{part} = C_{mat} \cdot m_{part} + C_{mach} \cdot T_{build} + C_{labour} + C_{post}$ | Sum of material, machine-time, labour, post-process | ₹ / USD |
| 7 | **Effective resolution (voxel)** | $V_{vox} = X_{res} \cdot Y_{res} \cdot Z_{res}$ | Smallest addressable volume | $µm^3$ |
| 8 | **Truss/strut lattice — relative density (TPMS)** | $\rho^{*} = \dfrac{\rho_{lattice}}{\rho_{solid}}$ | For Gyroid: $\rho^* \approx 0.785\,(t/L)^2$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Effective elastic modulus (Gibson-Ashby)** | $\dfrac{E^{*}}{E_{s}} = C \left(\dfrac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{n}$ | Open-cell foams, $C \approx 1$, $n \approx 2$ | Pa |
| 10 | **Energy per unit mass (SLS/SLM)** | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{P_{laser} \cdot T_{build}}{m_{part}}$ | Specific energy consumption | $J/g$ |
| 11 | **Safety factor** | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\sigma_{applied}}$ | Must exceed regulatory minimum (e.g., ASTM F136 for Ti) | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Inventory carrying cost (EOQ context)** | $C_{carry} = i \cdot C_{unit} \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Annual holding cost for AM = near-zero per unit (digital stock) | ₹ / year |
| 13 | **Lead-time reduction ratio** | $\Delta T_r = \dfrac{T_{trad} - T_{AM}}{T_{trad}} \times 100\,\%$ | Demonstrates case-study savings | % |
| 14 | **DICOM voxel size (CT)** | $V_{vox,CT} = FOV \,/\, N_{matrix}$ | Typical head CT: $0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.6\,mm$ | $mm^3$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When asked for the *time to print*, students often forget the *warm-up, cooling, and support-removal* overhead — typically **20-30 %** additional on top of $T_{build}$. Always state your assumption.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Patient-matched cranio-maxillofacial (CMF) implants** (Stryker, Materialise, OsteoFab) — FDA-cleared 510(k) pathway.
- **Surgical cutting guides and drill jigs** — printed in sterilizable polyamide (SLS) or medical-grade resin (SLA) in < 24 h.
- **Hearing-aid shells** — since 2014, **>99 %** of in-ear hearing aids are 3D-printed (SLA of photopolymer) — the *first* mass-produced consumer medical AM product.
- **Prosthetic sockets** — SLS nylon with carbon-fibre reinforced polyamide, custom-contoured to residual limb in 2-3 days.
- **Pharmaceutical 3D printing** — Spritam (levetiracetam) — first FDA-approved 3D-printed drug (2015), porous structure for rapid dissolution.
- **Pandemic response** — distributed printing of ventilator splitters, nasal swabs, face shields during 2020-21.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Build-Time Estimate for a Cylindrical Bone Model (FDM)

**Problem statement (a typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):**
A surgeon requires a 3D-printed anatomical model of a femur segment for pre-operative planning. The part is a hollow cylinder of outer radius $R = 30\,mm$, inner radius $r = 15\,mm$, height $H = 120\,mm$. The FDM printer has nozzle diameter $d_n = 0.4\,mm$, layer thickness $h = 0.2\,mm$, extrusion width $w = 0.45\,mm$, and average printhead travel speed $v = 60\,mm/s$. Estimate the **build time** and **mass of PLA consumed** ($\rho_{PLA} = 1.24\,g/cm^3$).

### Step 1 — Compute part volume (the hollow cylinder)
The cylinder is a *ring* extruded along height $H$. Volume equals area of annulus times height.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{part} &= \pi \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) H \\
&= \pi \left[(30)^{2} - (15)^{2}\right] \times 120 \\
&= \pi \left[900 - 225\right] \times 120 \\
&= \pi \times 675 \times 120 \\
&= 81000\,\pi \\
&\approx 254{,}469\,mm^{3} \\
&\approx 254.47\,cm^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $1\,cm^3 = 1000\,mm^3$, so $254{,}469\,mm^{3}$ becomes $254.47\,cm^3$.

### Step 2 — Number of layers along Z
Layers stack along the build axis (vertical). With layer thickness $h = 0.2\,mm$ and part height $120\,mm$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{layers} &= \left\lceil \frac{H}{h} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{120\,mm}{0.2\,mm} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 600 \right\rceil \\
&= 600 \text{ layers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each layer is a horizontal slice of thickness $0.2\,mm$; dividing total Z by layer height gives the *exact* count. The ceiling function ensures an extra layer to cover the top face.

### Step 3 — Effective cross-section area per layer
For a hollow cylinder, the cross-section at any layer is constant (radius unchanged along Z). The *perimeter* of the ring (deposition path length) on a single layer equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2 \pi (R + r) \\
&= 2 \pi (30 + 15) \\
&= 90 \pi \approx 282.74\,mm
\end{aligned}
$$

The *deposited cross-sectional area* of one bead (perimeter traced once) is $w \cdot h = 0.45 \times 0.2 = 0.09\,mm^2$. To fill the annulus, the printer lays down a single *perimeter wall* (skin = 1 shell, no infill in this thin-walled model). So the *area of material deposited per layer*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{mat,layer} &= P \cdot w \\
&= 282.74 \times 0.45 \\
&\approx 127.23\,mm^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Travel time per layer
Each layer requires the printhead to traverse the perimeter (282.74 mm). Neglecting acceleration ramps and short G-code move time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{motion,layer} &= \frac{P}{v} = \frac{282.74\,mm}{60\,mm/s} \\
&\approx 4.712\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Distance divided by speed gives time. The extrusion time equals motion time for a well-tuned FDM (E-step calibrated so that volumetric flow matches $w \cdot h \cdot v$).

### Step 5 — Total motion (build) time
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{build} &= N_{layers} \cdot t_{motion,layer} \\
&= 600 \times 4.712\,s \\
&= 2{,}827.4\,s \\
&\approx 47.12\,min
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding **25 % overhead** for warm-up, Z-hops, and skirt: $T_{total} \approx 47.12 \times 1.25 \approx 58.9\,min \approx 1\,h$.

### Step 6 — Mass of PLA consumed
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{mat} &= A_{mat,layer} \cdot h \cdot N_{layers} \\
&= 127.23\,mm^2 \times 0.2\,mm \times 600 \\
&= 127.23 \times 120 \\
&= 15{,}268\,mm^3 \\
&\approx 15.27\,cm^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{PLA} &= \rho_{PLA} \cdot V_{mat} \\
&= 1.24\,g/cm^3 \times 15.27\,cm^3 \\
&\approx 18.93\,g
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $V_{mat}$ should be *slightly greater* than $V_{part}$ (15.27 vs 15.27 cm³; here they match closely because the thin-wall model is essentially 100 % skin). A solid part of this geometry would weigh $254.47 \times 1.24 \approx 315.5\,g$ — so the *hollow* FDM model uses **~6 %** of the material of a solid print, a 16× saving that the case study can highlight.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Cost Comparison: Traditional vs AM Cranial Implant (Case Study Number)

**Case context:** A 35-year-old patient requires a patient-specific PEEK cranioplasty implant (defect diameter ~80 mm, dome height 12 mm). Compare cost and lead time between (a) traditional CNC-milled PEEK from a hub factory, and (b) in-hospital FDM/FFF PEEK implant.

**Step 1 — Define parameters.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Traditional (CNC) | AM (FDM-PEEK) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material cost (PEEK) | $C_{mat}$ | ₹ 4,500 | ₹ 4,000 (filament) |
| Machine time | $T_{mach}$ | 8 h (milling + 2 h polishing) | 14 h (print) + 1 h post |
| Machine hourly rate | $R_{m}$ | ₹ 1,200 / h | ₹ 250 / h (FFF PEEK printer) |
| Labour | $C_{lab}$ | ₹ 3,000 (planning + QA) | ₹ 1,500 (planning + QA) |
| Post-processing / sterilisation | $C_{post}$ | ₹ 2,500 | ₹ 1,500 |
| Logistics (3PL) | $C_{log}$ | ₹ 2,000 (2-way courier) | ₹ 0 (on-site) |
| Inventory carrying (1 month) | $C_{inv}$ | ₹ 800 | ₹ 0 |
| **Lead time** | $T_{lead}$ | 5-15 d | 2 d |

**Step 2 — Compute per-part cost using the unified formula.**

$$
C_{part} = C_{mat} + R_m \cdot T_{mach} + C_{lab} + C_{post} + C_{log} + C_{inv}
$$

**Traditional:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{trad} &= 4500 + (1200 \times 8) + 3000 + 2500 + 2000 + 800 \\
&= 4500 + 9600 + 3000 + 2500 + 2000 + 800 \\
&= 22{,}400\,₹
\end{aligned}
$$

**AM (FDM-PEEK, in-hospital):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{AM} &= 4000 + (250 \times 14) + 1500 + 1500 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 4000 + 3500 + 1500 + 1500 \\
&= 10{,}500\,₹
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute savings.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving per part} &= 22{,}400 - 10{,}500 = 11{,}900\,₹ \\
\text{Saving \%} &= \frac{11{,}900}{22{,}400} \times 100 \approx 53.1\,\% \\
\text{Lead-time reduction} &= \frac{10\,d - 2\,d}{10\,d} \times 100 = 80\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The *digital file* is free to copy infinitely, *machine cost* drops because FDM PEEK printers are inexpensive and only consume power + filament, *logistics* and *inventory* vanish for AM. The lead-time collapses to the printing+post-processing window.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Common Biomedical Devices

| Biomedical Device | Recommended AM Process | Material | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Anatomical teaching model | FDM | PLA / PETG | Cheap, fast, sufficient resolution |
| Surgical cutting guide | SLA or SLS | BioMed Clear resin / PA-12 | High accuracy, sterilizable |
| Patient-specific implant (load-bearing) | SLM / DMLS | Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136), CoCr | High strength, osteointegration |
| Cranial plate (non-load-bearing) | FDM / SLS | PEEK, PA-12 | Lightweight, biocompatible |
| Hearing-aid shell | SLA | Photopolymer (class IIa) | Sub-50 µm, smooth surface, mass-prod |
| Prosthetic socket | SLS + carbon-fibre wrap | PA-12 CF | Strong, breathable lattice |
| Drug-delivery device | FDM / SSE | PVA, PCL | Controlled-release porous matrix |
| Tissue scaffold | Bioprinting (extrusion) | GelMA / alginate + cells | Cell-laden, biomimetic |
| Dental crown | DLP / SLA | Crown resin / zirconia (sintered) | High resolution, certified materials |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Process Selection Flow — Why FDM for a Pre-Op Bone Model

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Criterion 1: Material biocompatibility (skin contact ≤ 30 d)} &\Rightarrow \text{PLA passes (Class II)} \\
\text{Criterion 2: Anatomical accuracy required} &\Rightarrow \pm 0.5\,mm \text{ acceptable} \\
\text{Criterion 3: Build volume} &\Rightarrow 100 \times 60 \times 30\,mm \text{ fits in FDM envelope} \\
\text{Criterion 4: Cost per unit} &\Rightarrow \text{PLA filament } \leq 200\,₹/\text{print} \\
\text{Criterion 5: Lead time} &\Rightarrow < 24\,h \text{ achievable} \\
\therefore \text{Selected process} &= \text{FDM with } 0.2\,mm \text{ layer, 20 % infill}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is a *weighted-decision* flow (a 7-mark sub-question favourite). Always tie each criterion to a *measurable* threshold so the examiner can credit each line.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Digital Thread (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Patient Imaging - CT or MRI Scan"] --> A2["DICOM Volumetric Data"]
    A2 --> A3["Segmentation Software - Mimics or 3D Slicer"]
    A3 --> A4["3D Surface Mesh - STL or 3MF Export"]
    A4 --> A5["CAD Refinement - DfAM in Fusion 360"]
    A5 --> A6["Slicer Software - Cura or PreForm"]
    A6 --> A7["Machine Code - G-code or CLI"]
    A7 --> A8["3D Printer Build Chamber"]
    A8 --> A9["Post Processing - Support Removal and Sterilisation"]
    A9 --> A10["Quality Assurance - Dimensional CT and ISO 10993"]
    A10 --> A11["Clinical Use - OR or Ward"]
    A11 --> A12["Patient Outcome and Feedback Loop"]
    A12 -.-> A3
```

> **Note on the diagram:** The dashed arrow from *Patient Outcome* back to *Segmentation* represents the **closed-loop** nature of modern digital healthcare — post-operative scans feed back into design libraries for the next patient (population-level learning). This is one of the supply-chain *intangible* benefits that the KTU board loves to test.

## 4.2 Traditional vs AM-Enabled Supply Chain (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB1["Traditional Subtractive Supply Chain"]
        T1["Raw Material Mill - Ti or PEEK"] --> T2["Centralised Factory - 6 to 12 weeks"]
        T2 --> T3["QA and Sterilisation Hub"]
        T3 --> T4["Distributor Warehouse"]
        T4 --> T5["Hospital Consignment Stock"]
        T5 --> T6["Surgery - One Size Fits Most"]
    end

    subgraph SUB2["AM Enabled Distributed Supply Chain"]
        A1["Patient CT at Hospital"] --> A2["Cloud Digital File Repository"]
        A2 --> A3["Regional 3D Print Bureau"]
        A3 --> A4["Hospital Point of Care Lab"]
        A4 --> A5["On Demand Sterilisation"]
        A5 --> A6["Surgery in 24 to 48 hours"]
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The AM pathway has *fewer* physical nodes (5 vs 5 — but the *time-axis* of each node is collapsed from weeks to hours). Inventory shifts from physical SKU to **digital SKU**.

## 4.3 AM Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start - Choose AM Process"] --> S1{"Is Part Load Bearing Implant"}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{"Material Metal or Polymer"}
    S2 -- Metal --> S3A["SLM or DMLS - Ti-6Al-4V or CoCr"]
    S2 -- Polymer --> S3B["FDM PEEK or SLS PEEK"]
    S1 -- No --> S4{"Is High Surface Detail Required"}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5{"Material Biocompatible Resin"}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6["SLA or DLP - Class IIa Resin"]
    S5 -- No --> S7["PolyJet Multi Material"]
    S4 -- No --> S8{"Is Single Material Model Sufficient"}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9["FDM - PLA or PETG - Lowest Cost"]
    S8 -- No --> S10["Material Jetting - Multi Colour Multi Stiffness"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Typical SLM (Metal) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["High Power Laser - 200 to 400 W Fibre or Yb"] --> L1["Galvanometer Scanner Mirrors XY"]
    L1 --> L2["F theta Lens Focusing Optics"]
    L2 --> L3["Build Plate inside Argon Filled Chamber"]
    L3 --> L4["Powder Layer - 20 to 50 microns"]
    L4 --> L5["Recoater Blade - Spreads New Powder"]
    L5 --> L6["Recycled Powder Sieve and Hoppers"]
    L6 --> L3
    L3 --> L7["Process Monitoring - Melt Pool Camera"]
    L7 --> L8["Closed Loop Quality Control"]
    L8 --> L1
```

> **Note:** The loop $L3 \to L7 \to L8 \to L1$ is the *in-situ* monitoring feedback that distinguishes modern medical-grade SLM (e.g., EOS, Renishaw, 3D Systems, GE Concept Laser) from hobbyist FDM. KTU often asks why medical SLM is *expensive* (the answer: argon atmosphere, melt-pool cameras, certified powder lot traceability, validated post-processing).

## 4.5 Inventory & Lead-Time Transformation — Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["Traditional Inventory Profile"]
        I1["Day 0 - 7 - 14 - 21 - 28 - 35 - 42 - 49 - 56 - 63"]
        J1["Stock Level Decreases as Demand Eats Inventory - Reorder Trigger at 30 d"]
    end
    subgraph P2["AM Digital Inventory Profile"]
        I2["Day 0 - 7 - 14 - 21 - 28 - 35 - 42 - 49 - 56 - 63"]
        J2["Stock Level Constant at Zero - File is Printed on Demand and Inventory Reappears in 24 h"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBRT415]`
**Define Rapid Prototyping (RP) as applied to biomedical device design. List any two AM processes used to fabricate patient-specific anatomical models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rapid Prototyping (RP) is a class of Additive Manufacturing (AM) techniques that fabricate a physical three-dimensional model directly from Computer-Aided Design (CAD) data by **successive addition of material, layer by layer**, without the need for part-specific tooling. In biomedical device design, RP enables the conversion of patient-specific volumetric data (CT/MRI DICOM) into tactile anatomical models, surgical guides, and prototype implants for pre-operative planning and validation.

Two AM processes commonly used for patient-specific anatomical models:
1. **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** — extrudes thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG) layer by layer; cost-effective, suitable for bone and organ replicas.
2. **Stereolithography (SLA)** — cures photopolymer resin with a UV laser; delivers higher resolution (25-50 µm) and is preferred for fine anatomical detail and surgical guide templates.

*Mark split (typical):* Definition 1 m, layer-by-layer emphasis 1 m, two processes ½ m each. Mention DICOM-to-model flow for the third mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBRT415]`
**What is the role of an STL file in 3D printing? Mention the standard pre-processing steps before sending an STL to a 3D printer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **STL (Standard Tessellation Language / Stereolithography)** file is the **de facto standard digital interface** between CAD software and an Additive Manufacturing machine. It represents the 3D object surface as a triangular mesh (each triangle defined by three vertices and a unit normal), approximating the CAD geometry with discrete facets. Almost all slicer software accepts STL as the canonical input.

Pre-processing steps:
1. **Mesh repair** — fixing non-manifold edges, inverted normals, and holes that would otherwise cause slicer errors.
2. **Orientation and support generation** — choosing the optimal build orientation to minimise supports, layer lines on critical surfaces, and print time.
3. **Slicing** — converting the triangulated surface into a stack of 2D contours (layer thickness 0.1-0.2 mm) and generating G-code with extrusion/laser/motion parameters.

*Mark split:* STL definition 1 m, triangulated mesh concept 1 m, three pre-processing steps 1 m.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBRT415 Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **digital workflow of fabricating a patient-specific cranial implant** from CT data to surgical implant. List the materials and process typically used.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hospital plans to set up an **in-house 3D-printing lab** for PEEK cranioplasty plates. Currently, plates are sourced from an external hub at ₹ 24,000 each, with a 14-day lead time. Estimate the **cost per plate** and **lead time** for in-house FDM-PEEK printing, and comment on the supply-chain implications. Assume:
- Filament cost ₹ 6,000 per kg; plate mass 80 g
- Printer throughput 5 plates per day (24-h cycle)
- FDM machine depreciation + power + operator = ₹ 600 per plate
- Post-processing (annealing, surface finish, EtO sterilisation) = ₹ 1,500 per plate
- No logistics cost; no inventory carrying cost
- Annual volume = 600 plates (1.6 per day average)

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (drawn in answer script, equivalent to Section 4.1 of these notes):**

$$
\text{Patient CT (DICOM)} \to \text{Segmentation (Mimics)} \to \text{3D mesh (STL)} \to \text{CAD refinement (defect mirroring, screw- boss placement)} \to \text{Slicing (Cura)} \to \text{G-code} \to \text{FDM PEEK / SLM Ti} \to \text{Post-process} \to \text{Sterilise} \to \text{Implant in OR}
$$

**Process description (mark-bearing points):**

1. **Image acquisition** — High-resolution CT (slice thickness ≤ 1 mm) of the skull; output DICOM volume (1 mark for DICOM and slice thickness).
2. **Segmentation** — Threshold-based bone isolation; editing in Mimics/3D Slicer to extract the cranial defect and the *mirror image* of the contralateral healthy bone (1 mark).
3. **CAD refinement / DfAM** — Converting the mirrored patch into a printable plate with fixation bosses, lattice/mesh infill, and rounded edges to reduce stress concentration (1 mark).
4. **Slicing and process selection** — For PEEK: FDM at 400-450 °C nozzle, 130-150 °C bed, enclosed chamber with nitrogen purge. For Ti-6Al-4V: SLM with 200-400 W fibre laser, 20-50 µm layer, argon atmosphere (1 mark).
5. **Printing and post-processing** — Annealing PEEK to relieve internal stresses; surface finishing (bead-blasting for Ti); sterilisation via autoclave (PEEK) or gamma (Ti) (1 mark).
6. **QA** — Dimensional CT scan of the printed plate, fit-check on a 3D-printed bone model, ISO 10993 biocompatibility certification (1 mark).
7. **Surgical use** — Implant fixed with titanium screws; post-op CT verifies positioning (1 mark).

**Typical materials:** PEEK (Optima, Victrex) for non-load-bearing, FDM/SLS; Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136 ELI grade) for load-bearing or thin-sections, SLM/DMLS.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Direct material cost per plate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{plate} &= 80\,g = 0.080\,kg \\
C_{mat} &= 0.080\,kg \times 6{,}000\,₹/kg = 480\,₹
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Add machine, post-process, and overhead.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{machine} &= 600\,₹ \\
C_{post} &= 1{,}500\,₹
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total in-house cost per plate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{AM} &= C_{mat} + C_{machine} + C_{post} \\
&= 480 + 600 + 1{,}500 \\
&= 2{,}580\,₹ \text{ per plate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with external sourcing.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving per plate} &= 24{,}000 - 2{,}580 = 21{,}420\,₹ \\
\text{Saving \%} &= \frac{21{,}420}{24{,}000} \times 100 \approx 89.3\,\% \\
\text{Annual saving} &= 600 \times 21{,}420 = ₹\,1.29\,\text{crore (₹ 1,28,52,000)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Lead time.**

The hospital can print a plate within a 24-h cycle; with the 5-plates/day throughput, even if all 1.6 plates/day average are *expedited* (rush) jobs, lead time ≤ **2 days** vs the external 14 days — a reduction of $\dfrac{14-2}{14} \times 100 \approx 85.7\,\%$.

**Step 6 — Supply-chain implications (board expects 3-4 short statements):**

1. **Inventory shifts from physical to digital** — STL/3MF files in a hospital PACS-linked repository; eliminates consignment stock.
2. **Mass customisation** — every plate is patient-specific with no MOQ penalty.
3. **Single point of failure removed** — no dependence on a distant hub; surge capacity (e.g., trauma mass casualties) can be absorbed locally.
4. **Regulatory burden increases** — the hospital becomes a *manufacturer* under CDSCO/FDA rules; needs ISO 13485 quality system, validated process, and a *Design History File* per device.
5. **Skill requirement** — biomedical engineers must be trained in segmentation, DfAM, printer operation, and post-processing.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary, PEBRT415 Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **Traditional Subtractive Manufacturing** supply chain with the **AM-Enabled Distributed Manufacturing** supply chain for orthopaedic implants under the following heads: (i) lead time, (ii) minimum order quantity, (iii) inventory model, (iv) mass customisation potential, (v) geographic dependency, (vi) regulatory pathway, (vii) sustainability.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a desktop SLA printer curing photopolymer with a 405 nm UV laser, the build platform moves in 50 µm increments (Z resolution). Each layer requires scanning a cross-section of area $A_{cs} = 1{,}200\,mm^2$ at scan speed $v = 250\,mm/s$ with hatch spacing $h_s = 0.08\,mm$. A part is 20 mm tall with 5 % overcure (slices overlap). Calculate (i) the **number of layers**, (ii) the **scan length per layer**, (iii) the **time to scan one layer**, and (iv) the **total build time** (ignoring peel, jump, and recoat steps).

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

The student should present the answer as a **markdown or written table** in the exam script. Below is the model.

| Head | Traditional (Subtractive) | AM (Distributed) | Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Lead time** | 4-12 weeks (mould/tool + production + logistics) | 24-72 h (print + post + sterilise) | ~90 % reduction, faster clinical decisions |
| **(ii) MOQ** | 100-1000 units (tooling amortisation) | 1 unit (no tooling) | Enables rare/one-off implants |
| **(iii) Inventory model** | Physical consignment stock, ~25-30 % carrying cost | Digital file repository, near-zero carrying cost | Frees working capital |
| **(iv) Mass customisation** | "One size fits most" — 5-7 sterile sizes | Patient-specific from CT/MRI data | Better anatomical fit, fewer revisions |
| **(v) Geographic dependency** | Single hub factory; long-distance shipping | Local/regional print bureau; on-site at hospital | Resilience, lower carbon footprint |
| **(vi) Regulatory pathway** | 510(k) cleared family of sizes; design controls on tooling | Each design = a new device; FDA 2017 POC guidance applies | Increased design-control documentation per patient |
| **(vii) Sustainability** | Up to 90 % raw material machined away as chips | ≥ 95 % material utilisation; recyclable unsintered powder | Lower Ti/PEEK waste, lower embodied CO₂ |

*Mark split (typical KTU valuation):* 1 mark per head (7 heads × 1 m = 7 m), with at least one crisp comparative number per head to secure full credit.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Number of layers.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{layers} &= \frac{h_{part}}{h_z} = \frac{20{,}000\,\mu m}{50\,\mu m} = 400 \text{ layers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $20\,mm = 20{,}000\,\mu m$; dividing by $50\,\mu m$ Z step gives the layer count. (No need for ceiling — 20 000 is an exact multiple.)

**Step 2 — Scan length per layer.**

The slicer fills the cross-section with parallel scan lines spaced by hatch $h_s$. Total scan length is the cross-section area divided by hatch spacing (a 2D approximation of total bead length).

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{scan} &= \frac{A_{cs}}{h_s} = \frac{1{,}200\,mm^2}{0.08\,mm} = 15{,}000\,mm = 15\,m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to scan one layer (excluding overheads).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{layer} &= \frac{L_{scan}}{v} = \frac{15{,}000\,mm}{250\,mm/s} = 60\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total build time (slicing/scan only).**

With 5 % overcure, the scan path is multiplied by 1.05 (to account for re-scans at boundaries and contour overlap), so $L_{scan}^{eff} = 15{,}000 \times 1.05 = 15{,}750\,mm$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{layer}^{eff} &= \frac{15{,}750}{250} = 63\,s \\
T_{build} &= N_{layers} \times t_{layer}^{eff} = 400 \times 63 = 25{,}200\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{build} = 25{,}200\,s = 420\,min = 7\,h}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $420$ minutes for a $20\,mm$ tall part in SLA is realistic — commercial SLA machines quote 6-10 h for dental/medical parts of similar size. (Peel, recoat, and Z-stepper settle times would add ~20-30 % in practice, pushing realistic total to ~9 h.)

*Mark split:* (i) 1.5 m, (ii) 2 m, (iii) 1.5 m, (iv) 2 m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit-conversion errors cost 1-2 marks easily** — students frequently write $20\,mm / 0.05\,mm = 4$ (forgetting that $h_z = 50\,\mu m$, not $0.05\,mm$).
> 2. **Don't quote the *layer scan time* as the *total build time*** — always multiply by $N_{layers}$ and explicitly state your assumption of "no peel/recoat overhead" to pick up the *method mark*.
> 3. **In Part B(a)**, students often describe *only 3-4 heads* with a paragraph; the 7-mark question **demands all 7 heads**, and each head should carry a *quantitative or comparative number* (e.g., "5-7 sizes vs 1 per patient"). Vague qualitative answers cap at 4-5 m.
> 4. **In Part A(b)**, the "supply-chain implications" carries **2 m** explicitly — students who only compute cost and lead time lose both marks. Always close with **at least three** supply-chain implications (inventory, customisation, regulation, sustainability).
> 5. **STL vs 3MF** — do not call 3MF "another name for STL" in the exam; STL is *triangulated mesh only*, 3MF is *mesh + colour + material + lattice metadata*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is the **ISO/ASTM 52900** umbrella; **RP** is its prototyping application; **3D printing** is the popular term. **All three are interchangeable** in KTU answers unless the question specifies otherwise.
- **Seven AM process categories** — know the four most relevant to biomedical: **VAT photopolymerisation, Material extrusion, Powder bed fusion, Material jetting**.
- **Digital thread** = *Image (DICOM) → Segmentation → Mesh (STL) → CAD (DfAM) → Slicing → G-code → Print → Post-process → QA → OR*. Memorise this flow — it appears in 70 % of KTU Module-4 questions.
- **STL** ≈ triangulated mesh, **3MF** = STL with extra metadata, **G-code** = machine instructions. Do not confuse them.
- **Patient-specific implants** are the *killer app* of biomedical AM: cranial plates, spinal cages, acetabular cups, CMF plates, surgical guides.
- **Process selection rules of thumb:**
  * Need *strength* → SLM/DMLS (Ti, CoCr).
  * Need *biocompatible polymer* → FDM-PEEK or SLS-PA12.
  * Need *fine detail* → SLA/DLP (resin).
  * Need *realistic multi-tissue organ model* → PolyJet.
  * Need *cheap teaching model* → FDM PLA.
- **Supply-chain disruption pillars**: (1) inventory → digital; (2) MOQ → 1; (3) lead time → 24-72 h; (4) mass customisation → routine; (5) geography → distributed; (6) regulation → tighter (FDA POC guidance 2017, ISO 13485); (7) sustainability → >95 % material utilisation.
- **Key formulas to memorise** (with units): $N_{layers} = H/h_z$, $C_{part} = \Sigma$, $\eta = V_{part}/V_{build}$, $E^*/E_s = C(\rho^*/\rho_s)^n$, $\Delta T_r = (T_{trad}-T_{AM})/T_{trad} \times 100\,\%$.
- **Numeric ranges to remember:** Z resolution 25-200 µm; build envelope 200-500 mm; $\rho_{Ti} \approx 4.43\,g/cm^3$, $\rho_{PEEK} \approx 1.30\,g/cm^3$, $\rho_{PLA} \approx 1.24\,g/cm^3$.
- **FDA milestones** (good for 1-mark fudge questions): 2015 Spritam (first 3D-printed drug), 2017 FDA *Technical Considerations for Additive Manufactured Medical Devices*, 2017 *Printing at the Point of Care* discussion paper.
- **Common student traps:** mixing AM categories with RP, calling an SLA a "laser printer", forgetting supports, confusing powder-bed-fusion sub-types (SLS = polymer, SLM/DMLS = metal, EBM = electron-beam metal).
- **For numerical answers**, always **state units, show conversion logic, and box the final answer** — the KTU valuation key explicitly awards a "method mark" even if the final number is off by a unit conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indian Certification for Medical Devices (ICMED) - ICMED 13485
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT415 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Indian Certification for Medical Devices (ICMED) - ICMED 13485

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICMED 13485** is an **Indian Certification Scheme for Medical Devices** based on **ISO 13485:2016**, which specifies requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)** where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide medical devices and related services that consistently meet customer and applicable regulatory requirements.

**ICMED** is the abbreviated form of **Indian Certification for Medical Devices**, a voluntary quality certification scheme specifically developed for the Indian medical device industry. The number **13485** in the scheme title directly indicates its alignment with the international standard **ISO 13485** (the globally accepted QMS standard for medical devices).

The certification is issued by the **Quality Council of India (QCI)** through its accreditation arm, the **National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB)**, in technical collaboration with the **Association of Indian Medical Device Industry (AiMeD)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of ICMED 13485 like a **"Quality Passport"** for a medical device manufacturer. Just as a passport verifies that a traveler meets certain international standards of identity, ICMED 13485 verifies that a medical device manufacturer follows globally accepted quality and safety protocols. Without this "passport," Indian-made medical devices often cannot be sold in regulated international markets like the **European Union, USA (FDA), Japan (PMDA), or Canada (Health Canada)**.

Imagine a small-scale Indian manufacturer producing **disposable syringes or blood-pressure monitors**. Even if the product works mechanically, hospitals and regulators need assurance that:
- The product is **safe** for patients,
- It is **reliable** (won't fail in critical use),
- The company has **documented processes** (not just trial-and-error production),
- There is **traceability** if a defect is later discovered.

ICMED 13485 assures all of this through a structured **Quality Management System**.

### 1.3 Key Governing Bodies

| **Body** | **Full Form** | **Role in ICMED 13485** |
|---|---|---|
| **QCI** | Quality Council of India | Apex body that governs the entire certification scheme |
| **NABCB** | National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies | Accredits the certification bodies (CBs) that issue ICMED certificates |
| **AiMeD** | Association of Indian Medical Device Industry | Industry partner representing Indian medical device manufacturers |
| **CDSCO** | Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation | India's national regulatory authority for drugs and medical devices |
| **BIS** | Bureau of Indian Standards | Develops Indian Standards (IS) for medical devices |

### 1.4 Launch and Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Launched in 2016**, ICMED 13485 was introduced to address the **quality crisis** in the Indian medical device market, which was flooded with low-quality and sub-standard imports. The scheme aimed to give Indian manufacturers a **competitive edge** and provide hospitals, procurement agencies, and regulators a **trustworthy benchmark** for quality assurance.

The Medical Devices Rules, **MDR 2017** (notified by the Central Government under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940), brought medical devices under a structured regulatory regime in India. While MDR 2017 handles **regulatory approvals**, ICMED 13485 handles **voluntary quality certification**, making the two systems complementary.

### 1.5 Key Metrics and Standards

- **Underlying Standard:** ISO 13485:2016 (Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes)
- **Number of Clauses in ISO 13485:2016:** 8 main clauses (Clauses 0–8)
- **Certification Validity:** Typically **3 years**, with surveillance audits at **12 months and 24 months**
- **Two Tiers of Certification:** ICMED 9000 (entry-level) and ICMED 13485 (comprehensive)
- **Mandatory Documented Procedures:** 6 (Control of Documents, Control of Records, Internal Audit, Control of Nonconforming Product, Corrective Action, Preventive Action)
- **Voluntary Status:** Yes, except where mandated by procurement agencies (e.g., government hospitals, defence, and certain PSU tenders)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillars of the ICMED 13485 Quality Management System

The ICMED 13485 certification is built upon **seven foundational pillars** corresponding to the QMS requirements:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Seven Pillars of ISO 13485 / ICMED 13485**:
> 1. **Management Responsibility** (Clause 5)
> 2. **Resource Management** (Clause 6)
> 3. **Product Realization** (Clause 7)
> 4. **Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement** (Clause 8)
> 5. **Design and Development Controls**
> 6. **Risk Management** (integrated with ISO 14971)
> 7. **Regulatory Compliance** (aligned with CDSCO/MDR 2017)

### 2.2 Structural Hierarchy of the ICMED Scheme

The certification hierarchy in India is structured as follows:

- **Tier 1 — Government Policy Level:** Central Government, Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Department of Pharmaceuticals
- **Tier 2 — Regulatory Body:** **CDSCO** under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act
- **Tier 3 — Apex Quality Body:** **QCI (Quality Council of India)**
- **Tier 4 — Accreditation Body:** **NABCB** (signatory to ILAC and APAC MRA — internationally recognized)
- **Tier 5 — Certification Bodies (CBs):** Accredited third-party agencies that perform audits
- **Tier 6 — Certified Organizations:** Manufacturers who have obtained the certificate

### 2.3 Comparison: ICMED 9000 vs ICMED 13485

| **Parameter** | **ICMED 9000** | **ICMED 13485** |
|---|---|---|
| **Based on** | **ISO 9001:2015** (generic QMS) | **ISO 13485:2016** (medical-device QMS) |
| **Scope** | General quality management | Medical-device-specific QMS |
| **Risk Management** | Not mandatory | Mandatory integration of risk management (ISO 14971) |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Generic | Specific to medical device regulations |
| **Design Controls** | Optional | Mandatory design and development controls |
| **Process Validation** | Generic | Special process validation for sterile, software, etc. |
| **Documented Procedures** | 6 (ISO 9001) | 6 (ISO 13485) plus medical-device-specific records |
| **Target Audience** | All manufacturers (entry-level) | All medical device manufacturers (preferred level) |
| **International Recognition** | High (QMS general) | Very high (medical-device specific) |
| **Cost of Certification** | Lower | Slightly higher (more rigorous audit) |
| **Audit Duration** | Shorter | Longer (more extensive documentation review) |
| **Preference in Tenders** | Acceptable | **Strongly preferred** in government and institutional procurement |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Audit Metrics

While ICMED 13485 is a management standard rather than a mathematical one, the **audit and certification metrics** follow a logical "formula":

$$
\text{Certification Score} \;(\%) \;=\; \frac{\text{Non-Conformities Resolved}}{\text{Total Non-Conformities Identified}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Acceptance Threshold} \;\geq\; 95\%
$$

| **Audit Parameter** | **Standard / Threshold** |
|---|---|
| Major Non-Conformity (NC) | Must be closed within **90 days** |
| Minor Non-Conformity (NC) | Must be closed within **60 days** |
| Observation | Advisory, no closure deadline |
| Internal Audit Frequency | Minimum **once per year** (all clauses) |
| Management Review Frequency | Minimum **once per year** |
| Surveillance Audit | Annual (for 3-year validity) |
| Re-certification Audit | At the end of the **3-year cycle** |
| Document Retention | Minimum **5 years** (or product lifetime + 2 years, whichever is longer) |
| Post-Market Surveillance | Continuous, with documented feedback loop |
| Risk Management File | Mandatory for each product family |
| Design History File (DHF) | Mandatory for Class B, C, D devices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** A startup biomedical company in Kerala (e.g., a manufacturer of orthopedic implants) with **ICMED 13485 certification** gains automatic eligibility for **Government of India e-tenders, GeM (Government e-Marketplace) procurement**, and gets a **credibility boost** for exporting to the EU (CE marking) and USA (FDA approval).

### 2.5 Documents Required for ICMED 13485 Certification

A manufacturer must prepare and maintain:

1. **Quality Manual** — Top-level document defining the QMS scope
2. **Mandatory Procedures** — 6 documented procedures (Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, Control of NC Product, Corrective Action, Preventive Action)
3. **Quality Policy and Quality Objectives**
4. **Work Instructions and Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**
5. **Risk Management File** (per ISO 14971)
6. **Design and Development Files** (for applicable device classes)
7. **Process Validation Records**
8. **Calibration Records** for measuring equipment
9. **Training Records** for all personnel
10. **Customer Feedback and Complaint Records**
11. **Post-Market Surveillance Data**
12. **Supplier Evaluation and Control Records**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process: ICMED 13485 Certification Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The certification process follows a **structured 10-step analytical workflow**, each step with its own deliverables and timelines. Every step must be exhaustively documented for KTU examination purposes.

### Step 1 — Gap Analysis and Readiness Assessment

The manufacturer first conducts a **gap analysis** comparing its current QMS against the **8 clauses of ISO 13485:2016**. A numerical readiness index can be expressed as:

$$
R_{\text{index}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{8} C_i \times W_i}{8}
$$

Where $R_{\text{index}}$ is the Readiness Index (0 to 100), $C_i$ is the compliance score for clause $i$ (out of 100), and $W_i$ is the weight assigned to clause $i$.

For a **typical medical device manufacturer**, the weighted allocation is:

| **Clause** | **Description** | **Typical Weight $W_i$** |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Quality Management System | 0.10 |
| 5 | Management Responsibility | 0.15 |
| 6 | Resource Management | 0.10 |
| 7 | Product Realization | 0.30 |
| 8 | Measurement, Analysis, Improvement | 0.20 |
| 6.3 / 7.3 | Infrastructure / Design Controls | 0.10 |
| 8.2.1 / 8.2.2 | Customer-related processes | 0.05 |

**Conversion Logic:** The total weight sums to 1.00 (or 100%). The readiness index is a weighted average of clause-wise compliance scores. A score $\geq 80\%$ indicates the manufacturer is ready to apply for a Stage 1 audit.

### Step 2 — Documentation Development

$$
\text{Document Hierarchy} \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{Level 1:} & \text{Quality Manual} \\ \text{Level 2:} & \text{QMS Procedures} \\ \text{Level 3:} & \text{Work Instructions} \\ \text{Level 4:} & \text{Records and Forms} \end{cases}
$$

Each document must be **drafted, reviewed, approved, and controlled** through the document control procedure (Clause 4.2.4 of ISO 13485).

### Step 3 — Implementation Phase (Minimum 3 Months)

The manufacturer operates the documented QMS for a minimum period (typically 3 to 6 months) to generate **objective evidence** of compliance. This includes:
- Conducting at least one **complete internal audit**
- Conducting at least one **management review meeting**
- Generating real production records

### Step 4 — Selection of Certification Body (CB)

The manufacturer selects an **NABCB-accredited CB**. The selection criteria are:

$$
CB_{\text{score}} \;=\; (0.3 \times A) + (0.3 \times C) + (0.2 \times T) + (0.2 \times E)
$$

Where $A$ is accreditation status, $C$ is cost, $T$ is technical competency in the relevant device category, and $E$ is experience in the medical device sector.

### Step 5 — Application and Contract Signing

The manufacturer submits the application form, scope of certification, and signs a certification agreement specifying:
- Audit duration
- Audit team composition
- Fees
- Surveillance schedule

### Step 6 — Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)

The CB auditor reviews all **QMS documentation** for conformity with ISO 13485. The audit outcome can be:

$$
\text{Stage 1 Outcome} \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{Ready for Stage 2} \\ \text{Minor gaps (acceptable)} \\ \text{Major gaps (Stage 2 deferred)} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 7 — Stage 2 Audit (On-site Certification Audit)

The audit team visits the manufacturer's site to verify:
- **Implementation** of documented procedures
- **Effectiveness** of the QMS in practice
- **Compliance** with ISO 13485 and applicable regulatory requirements

Audit findings are classified as:
- **Critical NC:** Immediate stoppage; cannot proceed
- **Major NC:** Must be closed in $\leq 90$ days
- **Minor NC:** Must be closed in $\leq 60$ days
- **Observation:** Improvement opportunity, no closure required

### Step 8 — Non-Conformity Closure

$$
\text{Closure Time Limit} \;=\; \begin{cases} 90 \text{ days} & \text{for Major NCs} \\ 60 \text{ days} & \text{for Minor NCs} \end{cases}
$$

The manufacturer submits **corrective action evidence**, which the lead auditor reviews for acceptance.

### Step 9 — Certification Decision

An independent **Certification Decision Committee** (separate from the audit team) reviews the audit report and decides whether to grant certification. This **independence** is a key NABCB requirement.

### Step 10 — Issuance of Certificate and Ongoing Surveillance

The certificate is issued with a **3-year validity**, subject to:
- Annual **surveillance audits** (Year 1 and Year 2)
- **Re-certification audit** at Year 3

$$
\text{Certificate Status} \;=\; f(\text{Surveillance Audit Outcomes}, \text{Re-certification Audit})
$$

A certificate can be **suspended, withdrawn, or restored** based on surveillance outcomes.

### 3.6 Mapping of ISO 13485 Clauses to ICMED 13485 Implementation

| **ISO 13485 Clause** | **ICMED 13485 Implementation Focus** |
|---|---|
| 4.2.1 — Documentation Requirements | Quality Manual + 6 procedures |
| 4.2.4 — Control of Documents | Document numbering, approval, distribution, retention |
| 4.2.5 — Control of Records | Retention period, storage, retrieval, protection |
| 6.4 — Work Environment | Cleanroom controls, contamination control |
| 7.3 — Design and Development | Design input, output, review, verification, validation, transfer |
| 7.5.1 — Control of Production | Process validation, sterilization, software validation |
| 7.5.3 — Identification and Traceability | Lot/serial numbering, UDI compliance |
| 8.2.1 — Customer Feedback | Complaint handling, vigilance reporting |
| 8.3 — Control of Nonconforming Product | NC identification, segregation, disposition |
| 8.5.2 — Corrective Action | Root cause analysis (e.g., 5-Why, Fishbone) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ICMED 13485 Certification Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Step A: Gap Analysis Readiness Check] --> node2[Step B: Documentation Development]
    node2 --> node3[Step C: QMS Implementation 3 to 6 months]
    node3 --> node4[Step D: Selection of NABCB Accredited CB]
    node4 --> node5[Step E: Application and Contract Signing]
    node5 --> node6[Step F: Stage 1 Documentation Audit]
    node6 --> node7{Step G: Major Gaps Found}
    node7 --> node8[Yes: Address Gaps and Re-audit] --> node6
    node7 --> node9[No: Proceed] --> node10[Step H: Stage 2 Onsite Audit]
    node10 --> node11{Step I: Non Conformities Issued}
    node11 --> node12[Yes: Corrective Action Closure 60 to 90 days] --> node13[Step J: Verify Closure]
    node13 --> node14{Closure Accepted}
    node14 --> node15[No: Re-audit] --> node10
    node14 --> node16[Yes: Certification Decision]
    node11 --> node16
    node16 --> node17[Certificate Issued Validity 3 years]
    node17 --> node18[Year 1 Surveillance Audit]
    node18 --> node19[Year 2 Surveillance Audit]
    node19 --> node20[Year 3 Re certification Audit]
    node20 --> node21[Cycle Repeats Every 3 Years]
```

```mermaid

    nodeD --> nodeJ[Preventive Action]
    nodeB --> nodeK[Resource Management Clause 6]

    nodeK --> nodeM[Infrastructure and Work Environment]

```

### 4.3 Stakeholder Hierarchy in the Indian Medical Device Certification Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[Central Government Ministry of Health] --> nodeY[CDSCO Regulatory Authority]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Department of Pharmaceuticals]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1[Intended Use and Identification of Hazards] --> nodeR2[Risk Estimation Severity x Probability]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Risk Evaluation Accept or Reject]

    nodeR8 --> nodeR9{Residual Risk Acceptable}
```
